He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their acquaintance, but he listened without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.

Soulanges gazed calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep around the immense ballroom, admiring the display of diamonds, rubies, gold, and shining hair, which almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cut glass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s indifference unsettled the lawyer. Unable to control his impatience, Martial approached Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. Upon seeing the Provencal, Soulanges cast him a covert glance and impertinently turned away his head. A solemn silence reigned in the room, where curiosity peaked. Eager faces wore strange mixed expressions; everyone anticipated an outbreak that men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly, the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor to hide his agitation. Spotting the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and the company thought Soulanges had publicly surrendered his position out of fear of the ridicule that follows a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened so inattentively that he missed her words spoken behind her fan:

“Martial, please don’t wear that ring you took from me. I have my reasons and will explain them when we leave. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”

“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.

“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”

Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. It was then that the little blue lady became the object of curiosity that agitated the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.

When the friends parted after their challenge, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the intoxication dancing produces in a woman and the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress, which enhances their attractions as much as it does women’s, Martial thought he might yield to the charm that drew his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he managed to hide his first glances towards the lady in blue from the Countess’s watchful eyes, he was soon caught in the act; and while he excused himself once for his absent-mindedness, he could not justify the unseemly silence that followed the most insinuating question a woman can ask:

“Do you like me very much this evening?”

The more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.

While Martial danced, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting the good humor of even the most indifferent, he resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed at liberty to ask her the name of the mysterious lady. He noticed a little space cleared between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several chairs vacant, forming rows of fortifications held by mothers or middle-aged women; the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade of shawls and wraps. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and from one to another, through compliments, he finally reached the empty space next to the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dripping wax, to Martial’s annoyance.

The Colonel, too tactful to speak suddenly to the little blue lady on his right, began by addressing a plain woman seated on the left:

“This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! Every woman here is pretty! You’re not dancing—because you don’t care for it, no doubt.”

This vapid conversation aimed to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; however, she, silent and absent-minded, paid no attention. The officer had prepared several phrases leading to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped for great things. But he was surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing at Madame de Vaudremont.

“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked hesitantly.

“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.

“And your husband is here, of course?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”

The mournful lady smiled sadly.

“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will ensure not to bring you back here. When so many are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball that your beauty may claim.”

“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”

The curt tone of her replies was so discouraging that the Colonel found himself compelled to withdraw. Martial, guessing what the officer’s last request had been and the refusal he had met with, began to smile, stroking his chin and making the diamond on his finger sparkle.

“What are you laughing at?” asked the Comtesse de Vaudremont.

“At the poor Colonel’s failure, who just put his foot in it----”

“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.

“I did not hear you.”

“If you can hear nothing this evening, at least you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, vexed.

“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.

“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”

“I thank you for telling me his name,” she said; “he seems an agreeable man.”

“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”

“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” asked the lady, looking inquiringly at the Colonel.

“On the very best.”

The unknown turned pale.

“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”

“I fancied Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering from the suppressed grief that had clouded her beauty.

“For a week past, the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he’s still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”

“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone that signified dismissal.

At that moment, the quadrille was coming to an end. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, telling himself, “She is married.”

“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe the fresh air from the garden, “how are you getting on?”

“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”

“What does that matter?”

“Oh, deuce take it! I’m a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no intention of courting a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me she did not intend to dance.”

“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”

“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile, I’m going to look for Soulanges; he might know the lady, as she seems interested in him.”

“You’ve lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”

“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I’m an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”

With these words, the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges sitting at a _bouillotte_ table. Though there was no friendship between the two soldiers beyond the superficial comradeship from the perils of war and service, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was alarmed to see the Colonel of Artillery, known to be prudent, playing a game that could lead to ruin. The heaps of gold and notes piled on the fateful cards showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood around the players at the table. Occasionally, a few words were spoken—_pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken_—but the five motionless men seemed to converse only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, approached him, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table, completely cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he didn’t even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, defying fortune rather than being grateful for her favors.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” He believed he was helping by pulling him away from the gaming table. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”

“What is that?” asked Soulanges.

“That you will answer a question.”

The Comte de Soulanges abruptly rose, carelessly gathering his winnings into his handkerchief, his expression so fierce that no one objected to his departure. In fact, the players seemed relieved to see his morose face disappear from the light of the gaming table.

“Those soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” remarked a diplomat who took Soulanges’ place. A single tired face turned to the newcomer and said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”

“My dear fellow,” Montcornet said to Soulanges, leading him aside, “the Emperor praised you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”

“The Master does not love the Artillery.”

“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said that men who married in Paris during the campaign are not in disgrace. Well?”

Soulanges looked confused.

“And now I hope you will tell me if you know a charming woman sitting under a huge candelabrum----”

At this, the Count’s face brightened; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” he said, his voice changed, “if anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull with this gold. Leave me, I beg you. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than----I hate everything I see. In fact, I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”

“My poor friend!” Montcornet replied gently, squeezing the Count’s hand. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you that Martial is thinking so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is quite smitten with that little lady?”

“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling with rage, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!”

He sank into an easy chair, and Montcornet slowly left, realizing Soulanges was too furious for pleasantries to soothe him.

Returning to the ballroom, Montcornet first noticed Madame de Vaudremont, who appeared agitated. He took a seat next to her.

“I wager something has vexed you?” he said.

“A mere trifle, General. I want to leave for a ball at the Grand Duchess of Berg’s, but I must first stop at the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is amusing himself by flirting with the dowagers.”

“That’s not the whole secret of your disturbance. I bet a hundred louis you will stay here the whole evening.”

“Impertinent man!”

“Then I’ve hit the truth?”

“Well, tell me, what am I thinking?” she asked, tapping his fingers with her fan. “I might reward you if you guess right.”

“I won’t accept the challenge; I have too much advantage.”

“You are presumptuous.”

“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet----”

“Of whom?” she exclaimed, feigning surprise.

“Of that candelabrum,” he replied, glancing at the fair stranger and scrutinizing the Countess.

“You have guessed it,” she said, hiding her face behind her fan. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as an old monkey, just told me that Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is flirting with that stranger, who sits here like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face, so cruelly beautiful and pale. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac, who only goes to a ball to watch everything while pretending to sleep, has made me miserable. Martial shall pay dearly for this trick. Urge him, since he is your friend, not to make me unhappy.”

“I just left a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that lady. And he is a man, madame, to keep his word. But I know Martial; such threats encourage him. Besides, we have wagered----” The Colonel lowered his voice.

“Can it be true?” asked the Countess.

“On my word of honor.”

“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” Madame de Vaudremont replied, her glance inviting.

“Will you do me the honor of dancing with me?”

“Yes; but the next quadrille. During this one, I want to find out what will come of this little intrigue and ascertain who the little blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”

Understanding Madame de Vaudremont wished to be alone, the Colonel retired, pleased to have made a good start.

At most entertainments, women like Madame de Lansac gather like old sailors watching younger mariners struggle with the tempest. At this moment, Madame de Lansac, interested in the unfolding drama, could easily guess the Countess’s agitation. The lady might fan herself gracefully, smile at the young men who bowed to her, and employ all the arts to hide her emotion, but the Duchess, a clear-sighted and mischievous figure from the previous century, could read her heart.

The old lady detected the slightest movements revealing the soul’s impressions. The faintest frown, quiver of cheeks, or curl of lips was like a book to her. From her large armchair, filled by her dress, the coquette of the past admired the younger coquette, seeing how bravely she disguised her annoyance and grief. Madame de Vaudremont felt as much sorrow as she feigned cheerfulness; she had believed Martial was a man of talent who could adorn her life with power, but now she realized her mistake, injurious to her reputation and self-esteem. Her sudden passion for Martial, though recent, caused her acute pain, akin to the amputation of a healthy limb.

The old Duchess, watching for an opportunity to speak to the Countess, hastily dismissed her Ambassador; in comparison to a lover’s quarrel, every other interest paled. Madame de Lansac shot a sardonic glance at the younger lady, making the Countess fear her fate was in the dowager’s hands. There are looks between women that are like torches at the climax of a tragedy. No one who did not know the Duchess could appreciate the terror her expression inspired in the Countess.

Madame de Lansac was tall, and her features led people to say, “That must have been a handsome woman!” She coated her cheeks with rouge, making wrinkles scarcely visible, but her eyes looked dim. She wore many diamonds and dressed tastefully. Her sharp nose promised epigrams, and her well-fitted teeth preserved a smile of irony reminiscent of Voltaire. The exquisite politeness of her manners softened her mischievous mind, making it impossible to accuse her of spitefulness.

The old woman’s eyes lit up, and a triumphant glance, accompanied by a smile that said, “I promised you as much!” crossed the room, bringing a blush of hope to the pale cheeks of the young woman languishing under the chandelier. The alliance between Madame de Lansac and the stranger did not escape the practiced eye of the Comtesse de Vaudremont, who sensed a mystery and was determined to uncover it.

At this moment, the Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned all the dowagers without success about the blue lady’s name, turned in despair to the Comtesse de Gondreville, who replied unsatisfactorily, “A lady whom the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”

By chance, the lawyer caught the glance of intelligence the Duchess sent to the stranger. Despite being on bad terms with her, he decided to speak. The Duchess, seeing the jaunty Baron approaching, smiled with sardonic irony and looked at Madame de Vaudremont, which made Montcornet laugh.

“If the old witch pretends to be friendly,” thought the Baron, “she is certainly going to play me some spiteful trick.” “Madame,” he said, “you have, I am told, undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”

“Do you take me for a dragon?” said the old lady. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added sweetly, reviving Martial’s hopes.

“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of all these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”

“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, whom you men do not know; she goes nowhere.”

“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature. May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you will tell me all I want to know, I promise to urge a petition for the restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands on the Emperor.”

The younger branch of the house of Navarreins bears the arms of Lansac, and the Duchess’s liaison with Louis XV had earned her husband the title of duke. The young lawyer’s suggestion to petition for an estate belonging to the elder branch was sheer trickery.

“Monsieur,” the old woman said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room has reached the same curiosity as yours. All eyes are involuntarily turned towards the corner where my protégée has modestly placed herself; she is reaping all the homage the women wished to deprive her of. Happy the man she chooses for her partner!” She interrupted herself, fixing her eyes on Madame de Vaudremont with a look that plainly said, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from the lips of your handsome Countess than from mine.”

There was such marked defiance in the Duchess’s attitude that Madame de Vaudremont rose, approached her, and took the chair Martial placed for her. Without acknowledging him, she said, “I can guess, madame, that you are talking of me; but I admit my lack of perspicacity; I do not know whether it is for good or evil.”

Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s pretty hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and replied in a low, compassionate tone, “Poor child!”

The women looked at each other. Madame de Vaudremont understood that Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious expression, saying, “Leave us.”

The Baron, displeased to see the Countess under the spell of the dangerous sibyl, gave one of those looks a man can give—potent over a blinded heart but ridiculous in the eyes of a woman beginning to criticize the man who attracted her.

“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” said Madame de Vaudremont, turning to give an ironical sidelong gaze at the lawyer.

Martial, a man of the world with wit and acumen, would not risk breaking with a woman favored at Court, whom the Emperor wished to see married. He counted on the jealousy he intended to provoke in her as the surest means of discovering the secret of her coolness and withdrew willingly, as a new quadrille began.

With an air of making room for dancing, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two ladies talk. He followed the glances they directed at the stranger. Comparing the Countess with the new beauty, made attractive by mystery, the Baron fell prey to the self-interest common to adventurous lady-killers, hesitating between a fortune within reach and indulging his caprice. The blaze of light highlighted his anxious, sullen face against the white silk hangings, making him resemble an evil genius. Observers likely remarked, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”

Meanwhile, the Colonel, leaning against the doorway between the ballroom and the cardroom, laughed undetected under his moustache. He enjoyed watching the dance’s turmoil, reading the agitation in the faces of the Countess and Martial, and wondering about the connection between Soulanges’ gloomy looks and the fair unknown’s expression, which alternated between hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles from many beautiful women. As a Colonel of the Imperial Guard, he was one of the best matches in the army.

It was nearly midnight. The conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, interests, rivalries, and scheming had reached a fervor that made a young man exclaim, “A fine ball!”

“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you are enduring a thousand deaths, I thought I might give you some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty means spoiling your future; it is like tearing the gown you must wear. My dear, it is not much later that we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, sweetheart, making clever enemies and friends without sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”

“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly exclaimed.

“My child, at your age, you must learn to choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is not wise enough to be a good husband, nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he is the man to squander your fortune; still, that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left him. In three years, he will be a wreck. Then he will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business acumen and be able to gossip agreeably, but he is too presumptuous to have any real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is calculating you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught a second time in the same trap? A new alliance should be a good speculation on your part, and in marrying again, you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Marechale!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As she spoke, both women naturally focused on Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.

“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “then you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin the happiness of wives. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passions. I came here to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, only toy with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve turned into a lovesick fool for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married for two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has experienced remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come and see the result of your actions.”

The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.

“There,” said Madame de Lansac, gesturing toward a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child brings her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”

The Countess looked away, lost in sad thoughts.

The Duchess led her to the door of the card-room, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!” 

The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. The players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amidst festivity, struck the Countess with a sense of foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not entirely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.

“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”

“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in her compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.

“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.

“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.

“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.

“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.

Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”

“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”

“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”

“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”

“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”

“But she loves her husband.”

“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”

“But she is married.”

“A whimsical objection!”

“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”

“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed eagerly. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”

“You deserve that I should send you to her.”

“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”

“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”

“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”

He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.

At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.

Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning. 

“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”

This thought stirred her pride more than her curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control of herself. The Colonel, misinterpreting her embarrassment, became more ardent. The old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues. The passions of the two couples were evident in the crowded rooms, reflected in the faces around them, amplifying their own feelings of impotence.

Finally, the Baron found a seat beside Madame de Soulanges. He admired her beauty up close, captivated by her fresh neck and graceful figure. At that time, women wore sashes tied under the bosom, a style that flattered those with perfect figures. As he stole glances at the Countess, Martial was enchanted by her perfection.

“You haven’t danced once this evening, madame,” he said in soft, flattering tones. “Not for lack of a partner, I presume?”

“I never go to parties; I’m quite unknown,” Madame de Soulanges replied coldly, unaware of her aunt’s signal to attract the Baron.

Martial, adjusting the diamond on his hand, felt a spark of hope as he caught the Countess’s eye. She blushed and looked at him with an indefinable expression.

“Do you enjoy dancing?” he asked to continue the conversation.

“Yes, very much, monsieur.”

Their eyes met, and the earnestness in her voice stirred something in him. “Then, am I too bold in offering to be your partner for the next quadrille?”

Artless confusion colored her cheeks.

“But, monsieur, I have already refused one partner—a military man—”

“Was it that tall cavalry colonel over there?”

“Precisely.”

“Oh! He’s a friend of mine; don’t worry. Will you grant me the favor I hope for?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

Her tone revealed a new and deep emotion that touched Martial’s world-weary heart. Overcome with shyness, he lost his confidence, and his southern charm ignited. He tried to speak, but his words felt clumsy compared to Madame de Soulanges’ bright replies. Fortunately, the quadrille was forming, and standing beside her eased his nerves. For many men, dancing is a way to express themselves, believing they can win a woman’s heart through their physical grace. Martial likely intended to showcase all his charms, judging by his pretentious movements and gestures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
He led his conquest to the quadrille, where the most brilliant women prioritized dancing above all else. As the orchestra played the introductory bars, the Baron felt immense pride as he surveyed the ladies forming the lines of the square. Madame de Soulanges’ dress rivaled even that of Madame de Vaudremont, who stood opposite him and the lady in blue. All eyes briefly turned to Madame de Soulanges, and a flattering murmur indicated she was the topic of conversation among the men. The admiration and envy directed at her made the young woman blush, enhancing her charm. When she raised her eyes, it was to look at her partner, as if wishing to transfer the glory of admiration to him, showing that she valued his opinion above all others. She embodied a blend of innocence and vanity, displaying a budding affection that only youthful hearts possess. As she danced, onlookers might have believed she was showcasing her grace solely for Martial, and though modest and inexperienced in ballroom tricks, she knew how to glance at him at just the right moments.

When a new figure, invented by a dancer named Trenis, brought Martial face to face with the Colonel, he joked, “I have won your horse.” Montcornet retorted, “Yes, but you have lost eighty thousand francs a year!” Martial dismissed the concern, saying, “What do I care? Madame de Soulanges is worth millions!”

After the quadrille, whispers circulated among the guests. The less attractive women made moral comments about the budding relationship between Martial and the Comtesse de Soulanges, while the more beautiful women marveled at her apparent ease in surrendering to him. The men were baffled by the Baron’s luck, not considering him particularly charming. A few sympathetic women argued against hastily judging the Countess, suggesting that young wives would be in a dire situation if a mere glance or graceful dance could compromise a woman’s reputation.

Only Martial understood the depth of his happiness. During the last figure, as the ladies formed the _moulinet_, he clasped the Countess’s hand, feeling her response to his amorous appeal through the thin fabric of her gloves. “Madame,” he said as the quadrille ended, “do not return to that corner where you’ve been hiding. Is admiration the only benefit you gain from your jewels? Come and enjoy the scene and yourself.”

Madame de Soulanges acquiesced to his charm, and they walked together through the crowded rooms. She hesitated at each door, peering in before entering, her unease only alleviated when he assured her, “He is not here.” They made their way to a grand picture gallery, where a lavish collation awaited three hundred guests. As supper approached, Martial led the Countess to an oval boudoir overlooking the garden, adorned with fragrant flowers and blue hangings. The noise of the festivities faded here. Initially startled, the Countess firmly resisted following him but eventually sat on an ottoman after glancing in a mirror, perhaps reassured they could be seen.

“This room is charming,” she remarked, admiring the decor. “All here is love and delight!” Martial replied, deeply moved. In the dim light, he noticed her face, which displayed a mix of unease, modesty, and eagerness that captivated him. She smiled, and this seemed to quell her internal struggle. In a flirtatious manner, she took his left hand and removed the ring he wore, exclaiming, “What a fine diamond!”

Martial, intoxicated by her touch, looked at her with eyes as bright as the gem. “Wear it,” he said, “in memory of this hour, and for the love of—” He trailed off, captivated by her rapture. She asked, “You give it to me?” with surprise. “I wish I had the whole world to offer you!” he replied. “Will you accept only my diamond?” She pressed, “You will never take it back?” “Never,” he assured her.

She placed the ring on her finger, and as Martial prepared to embrace her, she suddenly stood and said clearly, “I accept the diamond, monsieur, with less scruple because it belongs to me.” The Baron was taken aback. “Monsieur de Soulanges took it from my dressing-table and told me he had lost it.” 

“You are mistaken, madame,” Martial said, irritated. “It was given to me by Madame de Vaudremont.” “Precisely,” she replied with a smile. “My husband borrowed it, gave it to her, and she gifted it to you. This ring has made a little journey, that is all. It may reveal secrets to me.” She touched a spring in the ring, revealing a lock of M. de Soulanges’ hair, then fled into the crowded rooms, leaving Martial confounded. He noticed the Colonel and Madame de Vaudremont laughing nearby, and the Colonel teased, “Will you have my horse to chase after your prize?” Martial took their banter in stride, grateful for their silence regarding the evening’s events, as he exchanged his horse for a young wife.

As the Comtesse de Soulanges drove across Paris to her home in Faubourg Saint-Germain, she was consumed by anxiety. Before leaving the Hotel Gondreville, she searched for her aunt and husband, who had departed without her. Disturbing suspicions plagued her mind. A silent witness to her husband’s torment since Madame de Vaudremont had ensnared him, she had hoped for his repentance. Now, she feared she had made a grave mistake by agreeing to her aunt’s scheme.

The evening’s events had darkened her innocent spirit. Initially alarmed by her husband’s suffering, she grew more distressed upon seeing her rival’s beauty, feeling the weight of societal corruption. As she crossed the Pont Royal, she discarded the hair from the diamond, a token of pure affection. Tears flowed as she recalled her long-standing grief, and she shuddered at the thought that a woman seeking peace must bury her pain without complaint.

“Alas!” she pondered, “What can women do when they do not love? What sustains their devotion? I cannot believe, as my aunt claims, that reason alone is enough.” 

Still sighing, she arrived home and rushed upstairs, startled to find her husband by the fire. “How long has it been since you attended balls without telling me?” he asked, his voice strained. “You know a woman is out of place without her husband. You compromised yourself by hiding in the dark corner.”

“Oh, my dear Leon,” she coaxed, “I couldn’t resist the joy of seeing you without your knowing. My aunt took me to the ball, and I was very happy there!” 

Her words softened the Count’s stern demeanor, as he had been dreading her return, fearing she would learn of his infidelity. In a bid to deflect her anger, he had tried to be the first to criticize her. Delighted to see him smile and grateful for his presence in a room he had frequented less often, the Countess looked at him tenderly, blushing and lowering her eyes. Her kindness further enchanted Soulanges, especially after the misery he had endured at the ball. He took her hand and kissed it gratefully, for gratitude often accompanies love.

“Hortense, what is that on your finger that hurt my lip?” he asked, laughing. “It’s my diamond, which you said you lost, and which I have found.”

General Montcornet did not marry Madame de Vaudremont, despite their brief understanding, as she became one of the victims of the tragic fire that marred the ball hosted by the Austrian ambassador for Napoleon’s marriage to the daughter of Emperor Joseph II.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In September 1835, Mademoiselle du Rouvre, the only daughter of the Marquis du Rouvre, married Comte Adam Mitgislas Laginski, a Polish exile. The Marquis had squandered much of his fortune, leaving Clementine a great heiress through her mother’s family connections. Despite her father's financial mismanagement, Clementine benefited from settlements made by her relatives, ensuring her a yearly income upon marriage.

Comte Adam, though an exile, was not a burden on the French government. He belonged to an illustrious Polish family but faced prejudice in Paris, where Polish nobility was not highly regarded. Initially, he was seen as a student enjoying Parisian life after losing his political prospects. The Polish community in France was divided between republican and noble exiles, leading to a complex social dynamic. Adam became a target of Parisian humor, with some questioning his wealth and status.

In 1834, Adam purchased a house in the rue de la Pepiniere, and by 1836, he was well-established in Parisian society, having fallen in love with Clementine. Their marriage elevated his status, and he was soon recognized as a fashionable figure. The couple's home was a luxurious creation, blending various architectural styles and filled with exquisite decor, reflecting the wealth and tastes of the new bourgeoisie.

Clementine, now a countess, began to notice the mysterious presence of Captain Paz, a factotum who managed their affairs but remained largely invisible. She questioned Adam about Paz, who was a constant figure in their lives, handling everything from finances to horse care. Adam explained that Paz was a nobleman from a poor branch of the Paz family, who had fought valiantly for Poland and had become a close friend through shared hardships.

Paz's loyalty and dedication to Adam were evident; he managed the household with remarkable efficiency and discretion. Adam admired Paz's character, noting that their friendship was built on mutual respect and understanding. Despite the social hierarchy, Adam regarded Paz as an equal, and their bond was strengthened by their shared experiences and challenges.

Clementine expressed her admiration for Paz, recognizing his noble spirit and simplicity. Adam shared how he had once offered Paz a financial gift, which Paz had declined, emphasizing their deep friendship and commitment to one another. Their relationship transcended typical social boundaries, with both men valuing their connection over societal expectations.

As the couple prepared for a day out, Clementine's affection for both Adam and Paz grew, highlighting the unique dynamics of their relationships. The narrative illustrates themes of loyalty, friendship, and the complexities of social status in 19th-century Paris, showcasing how personal connections can defy societal norms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Paz lived a reclusive life, largely unnoticed by the fashionable circles of Paris, until the Comtesse Laginska was seen riding with him in the Bois de Boulogne. During their outing, Clementine insisted that Thaddeus dine with them, prompting him to dress for the occasion. At dinner, he behaved like a subordinate, listening to Clementine and responding minimally, which frustrated Adam, who urged him to be more lively. Despite his dull demeanor, Thaddeus was aware of his feelings for Clementine, which he struggled to conceal.

After dinner, Thaddeus agreed to accompany them to the Opera, where he remained detached and seemingly uninterested. Clementine, feeling slighted, remarked on his lack of engagement, while Adam defended him, suggesting that Thaddeus was simply trying to be disagreeable. Thaddeus, however, was grappling with his unrequited love for Clementine, which intensified upon her return from a trip. He felt a bittersweet joy in being essential to their household, which would falter without his management.

Clementine, now more beautiful and confident in her marriage, was preoccupied with her social standing in Paris, leaving little room for thoughts of Thaddeus. Despite this, she noticed him one day while driving home and invited him to join them, which flustered him. Their interactions were charged with unspoken emotions, and Thaddeus felt a mix of gratitude and longing.

As the days passed, Thaddeus returned to his routine, feeling overlooked by Clementine, who was consumed by her social obligations. He found solace in his role within their household, but his feelings for her remained a source of inner turmoil. He reflected on the nature of his love, recognizing its depth and the pain of his unreciprocated feelings.

One day, Clementine hosted a reception, and Thaddeus's absence went unnoticed. He resumed his solitary habits, content in his silent devotion. However, he was deeply affected by the realization that his love for Clementine was both a source of joy and suffering. He admired her from afar, cherishing the moments he could witness her happiness.

Clementine's life in Paris was a constant battle for attention and admiration, and she found herself increasingly distracted by her rivals. Despite her efforts, she felt the strain of maintaining her status and often neglected her relationship with Adam. Thaddeus, meanwhile, remained a steadfast presence, quietly managing their affairs while grappling with his feelings.

In May, as Clementine prepared to leave for her uncle's estate, she spotted Thaddeus again, and their brief exchange reignited her curiosity about him. She insisted he join them for dinner, and although he initially hesitated, he complied. During the meal, Thaddeus attempted to engage more, but his shyness and sense of duty kept him from fully expressing himself.

As the evening progressed, Clementine's interest in Thaddeus grew, and she began to see him in a new light. Their conversation revealed a deeper connection, and Thaddeus found himself momentarily emboldened. However, the weight of his loyalty to Adam held him back from pursuing his feelings openly.

After dinner, Clementine expressed her dissatisfaction with Adam's behavior, revealing her desire for a more passionate connection. Thaddeus, caught between his love for her and his friendship with Adam, struggled to navigate the complexities of their relationships. He recognized the tension between them and felt the urge to confess his feelings but hesitated.

Clementine's thoughts turned to her own insecurities, and she questioned the nature of love and loyalty. Thaddeus, sensing her vulnerability, felt a surge of hope but remained cautious. Their conversation deepened, and the silence between them was filled with unspoken emotions.

Eventually, Thaddeus fabricated a story about being in love with a circus performer named Malaga, hoping to divert attention from his true feelings for Clementine. He described her as a captivating and agile woman, contrasting her with the societal expectations of Parisian women. This ruse allowed him to express his longing for a simpler, more genuine connection.

Thaddeus's visits to Malaga became a way for him to escape his unrequited love for Clementine, but he remained emotionally distant. He provided for Malaga, ensuring she lived comfortably, yet he never crossed the line into a romantic relationship. His feelings for Clementine continued to haunt him, and he found himself torn between his loyalty to Adam and his desire for Clementine.

As Malaga's fortunes changed, she began to question Thaddeus's intentions, leading to misunderstandings and tension. Despite the challenges, Thaddeus remained committed to his role as her benefactor, even as he grappled with the complexities of his own heart.

In the end, Thaddeus received a heartfelt letter from Malaga, expressing her despair over their separation and her longing for his companionship. This letter, filled with emotion, struck a chord within him, reminding him of the depth of his own feelings for Clementine. He realized that his devotion to her was unwavering, even as he navigated the intricacies of love, loyalty, and sacrifice.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Clementine returned home, and Paz saw her again, more beautiful than ever. After dinner, the count and countess had a conversation about Thaddeus, who had left a letter from Malaga with Adam. Adam lamented Thaddeus's misfortune, believing he would lose everything due to his relationship with a circus performer. Clementine expressed her disdain for such women, while Adam defended Thaddeus's choices, noting his financial sacrifices for Malaga.

Paz, who had asked Adam for money to help Malaga, was seen by the countess as a potential ruin for Adam. Despite her concerns, Adam believed Thaddeus would eventually leave Malaga if it was in his best interest. The countess, however, remained skeptical. 

Days later, Paz noticed a change in Clementine's demeanor towards him, feeling her contempt. This realization plunged him into despair, as he believed he had caused her to despise him. Despite his heartache, he found joy in silently admiring her at social events, dedicating himself to her well-being and the household.

Paz's love for Clementine was profound, yet he felt unworthy of her respect. He fell ill from his emotional turmoil but eventually recovered. Meanwhile, Adam lost a significant amount of money gambling and asked Thaddeus to cover it up by attributing it to Malaga. Thaddeus reluctantly agreed, feeling the weight of his obligations.

Clementine discovered Thaddeus's financial generosity towards Paz and expressed her frustration, questioning his loyalty. Thaddeus, in turn, felt the burden of her expectations and the need to manage her affairs effectively. He took it upon himself to teach her about household management, believing she could handle it independently.

As spring approached, Adam fell seriously ill, and Thaddeus devoted himself to nursing him back to health. Clementine, aware of Adam's precarious condition, sought the best medical care. Thaddeus's dedication to Adam was unwavering, and he managed to keep the count alive through his attentive care.

After weeks of nursing, Adam's condition improved, and Thaddeus received praise for his efforts. However, he was tormented by his feelings for Clementine and the realization that he could never have her. He decided to leave for the Caucasus, believing it was the only way to escape his unrequited love.

Before departing, Thaddeus wrote a heartfelt letter to Clementine, expressing his deep feelings for her and the sacrifices he made for her happiness. He acknowledged that he could not remain in Paris, where his love for her would only bring him pain. 

Clementine, upon receiving Thaddeus's letter, reflected on her own feelings and the loss of a devoted friend. Meanwhile, Adam, unaware of the full extent of Thaddeus's sacrifices, lamented the loss of his friend, believing their bond was strong enough to withstand any trials.

In the years that followed, Clementine often thought of Thaddeus, wondering about his fate. She remained unaware of his true feelings and the depth of his devotion, while he continued to live in the shadows, forever changed by his love for her. 

As time passed, Clementine found herself drawn into a new romantic entanglement, but the memory of Thaddeus lingered, a bittersweet reminder of a love that could never be.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Marquise de Listomere embodies the spirit of the Restoration, balancing religious devotion with worldly elegance. Married for seven years to the unremarkable Marquis de Listomere, she maintains a virtuous facade, possibly to support her husband's political ambitions. While some women reserve judgment until he becomes a peer, the marquise's conduct is strictly virtuous, aligning with her principles.

The marquis is a bland figure, known for his negative qualities rather than any virtuous reputation. He is a silent deputy who votes correctly but lacks charisma, earning him the nickname "dull weather." His unremarkable nature is seen as a safe choice for a virtuous woman, as he is incapable of folly.

Despite her reserved demeanor, the marquise commands respect and disdain from men who attempt to flirt with her, receiving only contempt in return. She engages in conversations with agreeable men without attracting gossip, unlike other women who may have ulterior motives. Her character is not one to play coy; she is genuinely principled.

The narrator, who admires the marquise, describes her physical attributes: she is neither plain nor conventionally pretty but possesses a captivating presence. Her eyes, when animated, reveal a deeper soul, and she exudes charm without actively seeking it. This quality is evident in her recent encounter with Eugène de Rastignac, a clever young man who embodies both modesty and liveliness.

Rastignac, who is adept at navigating social circles, converses with the marquise at a ball, discussing topics ranging from opera to women's duties. Their interaction is marked by a mutual interest, though he abruptly leaves her side afterward. The next morning, Rastignac reflects on their conversation, finding himself preoccupied with thoughts of her.

As he writes letters in his comfortable setting, he becomes increasingly absorbed in his feelings for the marquise. His thoughts drift to the pleasures of romance, and he is interrupted by the arrival of his friend Horace, with whom he discusses various topics, including a potential expedition to Morea.

Meanwhile, the marquise receives a letter from Rastignac while getting ready for the day. The letter begins with affectionate terms, prompting her curiosity about its content. As she reads on, her initial impulse to discard it transforms into a desire to understand the sentiments expressed. The letter's conclusion leaves her fatigued, and she reflects on the impertinence of Rastignac, pondering the implications of his words.

This moment encapsulates the tension between her virtuous exterior and the stirrings of emotion within, setting the stage for the complexities of her character and the dynamics of her relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame de Listomere decided to forbid her porter from admitting Monsieur de Rastignac and resolved to show him more than disdain in social settings, as his insolence exceeded that of others she had overlooked. Initially, she considered keeping a letter he sent but ultimately burned it.

"Madame received a fine love-letter," Caroline remarked to the housemaid, who was surprised.

That evening, Madame de Listomere attended a party at the Marquis de Beauseant's, anticipating Rastignac's arrival. By two in the morning, she realized he was not coming, despite her intention to confront him with coldness. Stendhal described this mental process as "crystallization."

Four days later, Rastignac scolded his valet, Joseph, for misplacing letters he had given him. Joseph, lost in thought, eventually recalled that one was for Madame de Listomere and the other for Rastignac's lawyer. When Rastignac learned that the letter was not for Madame de Nucingen, he laughed, realizing his heart had strayed.

Eugene's mistakes began with his intention to amuse Madame de Listomere by discussing the blunder of the love-letter. He compounded this by not visiting her for several days, allowing her thoughts to crystallize further.

When he finally called, the porter informed him that Madame de Listomere was out. However, the Marquis de Listomere invited him in, unaware of the tension. As they ascended the stairs, Rastignac noted several social blunders.

Upon seeing Eugene, Madame de Listomere blushed, which did not go unnoticed by him. He thought, "What! that fortress, too?" and adjusted his cravat. Monsieur de Listomere, distracted by a newspaper, was oblivious to the undercurrents of the conversation.

Madame de Listomere, despite her initial embarrassment, quickly regained her composure. When Eugene inquired about her health, she assured him she was well. He continued, suggesting that if his visit was unwelcome, it was due to the marquis's introduction. She replied that her husband was not privy to all her secrets, hinting at the complexity of their relationship.

Eugene, attempting to lighten the mood, suggested that silence would be the best excuse for his visit. Madame de Listomere responded with a promise of forgetfulness, though she maintained a dignified demeanor. When he mentioned the letter, she feigned offense but could not hide her amusement.

Eugene insisted that no offense was intended, and the letter was not meant for her. Madame de Listomere, while pretending to be offended, expressed her willingness to laugh at the situation, but warned against further mistakes.

Monsieur de Listomere, still engrossed in his paper, interrupted, mentioning a mutual acquaintance's death. This led to a discussion that revealed the marquise's irritation with Eugene's earlier comments. He naively suggested that she might be trying to extract a secret from him, which only piqued her curiosity further.

When Eugene mentioned Madame de Nucingen, Madame de Listomere's demeanor shifted. She rang for wood, signaling that it was time for him to leave. She coldly confronted him about the letter, questioning how his pen could mistakenly write her name. Discomfited, Eugene stumbled over his words before departing.

Days later, Madame de Listomere confirmed Eugene's claim about the letter's true recipient. She withdrew from society, and the marquis explained her absence as due to a stomach ailment, while her physician recognized it as a nervous condition she was using to justify her seclusion. 

The narrative highlights the complexities of social interactions and misunderstandings, particularly in romantic contexts, while illustrating the characters' emotional responses and societal roles.
