############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

At Paris, balls often feature two distinct gatherings: the official party, where guests exchange superficial pleasantries, and a more intimate gathering that follows, where genuine conversation and laughter flourish. The latter, often hosted by women like Madame d’Espard and Mademoiselle des Touches, serves as a refuge for the old spirit of French wit, allowing for free-flowing ideas and camaraderie.

One memorable evening at Mademoiselle des Touches' salon, a group of notable individuals, including the Minister de Marsay, engaged in lively discourse. The atmosphere was charged with wit and camaraderie, showcasing the unique conversational style that Paris is known for. The evening's charm lay in the ease of exchange, where anecdotes and confessions flowed freely, creating a delightful tapestry of social interaction.

As the night progressed, the conversation turned to de Marsay's reflections on his early experiences with love, particularly a passionate affair with a woman six years his senior. He recounted his youthful infatuation, marked by intense devotion and secrecy. Despite the fervor of his feelings, he maintained a facade of indifference to protect their relationship from the prying eyes of society.

De Marsay described the complexities of their love, characterized by a careful dance of appearances and emotions. He shared how he would ride past her carriage, exchanging silent signals through the flowers she carried. Their relationship thrived on mutual admiration and the thrill of secrecy, yet he remained blissfully unaware of the potential for betrayal.

However, a turning point came when he fell ill and sent her a note to cancel their meeting. Overcome with jealousy and suspicion, he decided to visit her despite his condition. Upon arriving, he witnessed her with another man, igniting a storm of emotions within him. Yet, when they met, her calm demeanor and tender concern momentarily soothed his doubts.

De Marsay's narrative took a darker turn as he grappled with the realization of betrayal. He felt a profound shift within himself, marking the end of his innocence and the beginning of a more cynical view of love. He reflected on the nature of revenge and the complexities of passion, ultimately concluding that true love should transcend the desire for vengeance.

His story resonated deeply with his audience, prompting reflections on the nature of love, jealousy, and the societal expectations that shape relationships. De Marsay's experience served as a poignant reminder of the fragility of trust and the transformative power of love, leaving a lasting impression on those present.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Cured of my cold and my pure love, I embarked on an adventure with a charming heroine, whose beauty contrasted sharply with that of my deceptive angel. I was careful not to quarrel with this clever woman, as I doubted whether true love could provide the same delights as her skilled deceit. Such refined hypocrisy can be as good as virtue. 

I sought a skilled artist in the Rue Boucher to create a keepsake from my hair for my new angel. He had a monopoly on hair mementos and showed me his work, which surpassed even fairy tales. He informed me of the latest trends, including marking linen with hair. A suspicion arose when I recognized a handkerchief he had worked on, confirming my doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections. I left the shop still believing in pleasure but became skeptical about love.

Two months later, I found myself in the boudoir of the ethereal being, holding her beautiful hand as we explored the depths of sentiment. In a moment of tenderness, I seized the opportunity to elicit her most delightful lies. Charlotte professed her love, claiming I was the only man in her world and that she had been seeking ways to bind me to her eternally. 

The women listening to de Marsay seemed offended by his portrayal of their own actions, as he mimicked their airs and attitudes. Just as I might have believed her sweet falsehoods, I asked, “When are you to marry the Duke?” The directness of my question startled her, and her reaction revealed her guilt. She feigned astonishment, but I pressed on, suggesting she should marry him, as he was rich and devout. 

Charlotte fell to her knees, insisting she loved no one but me. I urged her to be truthful, and she swore before God. I confessed my doubts about her fidelity, which she quickly turned against me, claiming I no longer loved her. She sought to escape the confrontation, but curiosity compelled her to ask what I had seen. I revealed that I had noticed her attending mass with the Duke multiple times, which she dismissed as jealousy. 

I explained that I considered her marriage to the Duke an excellent arrangement, one that would elevate her status in society. I advised her to embrace her new role, as the Duke was virtuous and would expect her to be prude. Charlotte, in tears, lamented that if I had only spoken sooner, we could have been happy together. I maintained that it was too late, and she had gone too far with the Duke. 

She expressed concern about the Duke’s suspicions, and I reassured her that he likely had none, but she needed to prove her devotion to him. As she paced the boudoir, she shifted her demeanor, holding out her hand and declaring I was noble and charming, vowing never to forget me. 

Her tactics were admirable, and I played the role of a distressed lover. She then asked what would become of me, and I revealed I had made advances to another woman. This revelation startled Charlotte, who accused me of being unfaithful. I countered that she had far too much wit for her heart to suffer. 

Charlotte expressed her disillusionment with love, claiming women could never be truly loved. I acknowledged her anger but pointed out that taking love seriously in society was akin to romanticizing an actress. She accused me of betrayal, and I feigned submission, asking if she would remember her grievances once she became a duchess. 

Her bitterness confirmed that she still felt wronged, and I realized I had successfully distanced myself from her. I had studied the lives of successful men with women and believed I had mastered the art of retreat. 

The women in the room reacted with sympathy for Charlotte, while de Marsay maintained a slight smile. The conversation shifted to the decline of social distinctions, with Lady Dudley noting that women often did not see their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay lamented the erosion of the aristocracy, stating that duchesses and marquises were disappearing, and the Civil Code had leveled social hierarchies.

Blondet remarked that aristocracy now began with viscountesses, while de Marsay noted that countesses would survive in some form. He reflected on how the last great ladies had vanished with the splendor of the previous century, and the Code had inadvertently created a new class of women. 

The Comte de Vandenesse commented on how the current societal structure allowed anyone to claim nobility, while Lord Dudley pointed out that property had not been organized, leading to the decline of the aristocracy. 

Joseph Bridau observed that the beauty of womanhood had diminished, and the great lady’s fan was broken. Daniel d’Arthez noted that the aristocracy had retreated, leaving behind women who could have shaped society. 

The conversation turned to the modern “perfect lady,” a product of the current era, embodying good taste and grace but lacking the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questioned the notion of progress, while Madame de Nucingen explained that a woman’s worth was no longer tied to her lineage but to her character and presentation.

Count Adam Laginski sought clarification on what constituted a “perfect lady.” Émile Blondet described the ideal woman as one who exudes elegance and refinement, effortlessly commanding respect and admiration. He painted a vivid picture of her appearance and demeanor, emphasizing her grace and poise in various social settings.

The discussion highlighted the complexities of modern femininity, where the “perfect lady” navigates societal expectations with skill and charm, embodying a blend of beauty and intellect that captivates those around her.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Now, you will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet remarked gravely. “In contrast, even if you know a lady is married, she will skillfully conceal her husband, making it a challenge to discover him. Often, you might only notice her gazing at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and leaves. She has ordered her carriage and departs.

“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose, and you go to bed under a golden canopy of a delightful dream, which may linger even after Sleep has opened the ivory gates of dreams.

“When at home, the lady sees no one before four; she is clever enough to keep you waiting. Her home is tastefully arranged; her luxury is for daily use and regularly refreshed. You will find no dusty glass shades or remnants of wrappings. The staircase is warm, and flowers abound—her only accepted gift, and only from select individuals, as they symbolize a promise. Fashionable trinkets are scattered about, but not in a way that resembles a museum. She will be seated by the fire in a low chair, not rising to greet you. Her conversation at home differs from that at the ball; there, she was our creditor; here, she owes you the pleasure of her wit. These are the nuances of which she is a master. What she seeks in you is a man to expand her circle, an object for her attentions. To draw you into her drawing-room, she will be enchantingly charming. This highlights how isolated women are today and why they desire a little world of their own, where they can shine like a constellation. Conversation often relies on generalities.

“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have pinpointed the flaw of our age. The epigram—a volume in a word—no longer strikes at people or things but at sordid events, fading quickly.”

“Hence,” Blondet continued, “a lady’s intelligence, if she possesses any, lies in casting doubt on everything. Herein lies the difference between two women; the townswoman is virtuous, while the lady hesitates and struggles with her virtue. This uncertainty is one of the last graces left to her by our dreadful times. She rarely attends church but will discuss religion; if you express free thought, she will attempt to convert you, leading to the familiar phrases and gestures understood by these women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society is tottering, and you deprive it of its support. Religion means you and me; it is property and the future of our children! Let us not be selfish! Individualism is the disease of the age, and religion is the only remedy; it unites families which your laws put asunder,’ and so forth. She then delves into a neo-Christian discourse laced with political notions that is neither Catholic nor Protestant—but moral? Oh, very moral!—reflecting a blend of modern doctrines at odds with one another.”

The women laughed at Blondet’s satirical portrayal.

“This explanation, dear Count Adam,” Blondet said, addressing the Pole, “will show you that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies both intellectual and political muddle, surrounded by the flashy yet fleeting products of an industry that aims to destroy its work to replace it with something new. When you leave her, you think: She certainly has superior ideas! You believe it more because she has delicately probed your heart and asked for your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows, even when she does. You alone will feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart’s state. The great ladies of old flaunted their affairs, while today’s lady has her passions neatly arranged like a musical score, with its notes and rests, its pauses and sharps. A mere weak woman, she is anxious not to compromise her love, her husband, or her children’s future. Name, position, and fortune no longer shield her from scrutiny. The aristocracy no longer stands united to protect her. Unlike the great lady of the past, she lacks the demeanor of lofty antagonism; she can crush nothing underfoot; it is she who would be crushed. Thus, she is adept at Jesuitical compromises, navigating between propriety and anonymous passions. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. This woman—so free at a ball, so charming while walking—is a slave at home; she is never independent except in perfect privacy or theoretically. Her task is to maintain her position as a lady.

“For in our day, a woman repudiated by her husband, reduced to a meager allowance, without a carriage, luxury, or opera box, is no longer a wife, maid, or townswoman; she is adrift, becoming a chattel. The Carmelites will not accept a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover still want her? That is the question. Thus, your perfect lady may give rise to calumny, but never to slander.”

“It is all so horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.

“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ exists between English hypocrisy and the delightful frankness of the eighteenth century—a bastard system, symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished, where transition leads nowhere, and everything is a matter of degree; all great figures fade into the background, and distinction becomes purely personal. I am convinced that it is impossible for a woman, even if born close to a throne, to acquire before the age of twenty-five the encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the important small things, the musical tones and harmony of coloring, the angelic mischief and innocent cunning, the speech and silence, the seriousness and banter, the wit and obtuseness, the diplomacy and ignorance that make up the perfect lady.”

“And where, according to your sketch,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked Blondet, “would you classify the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman _comme il faut?_”

“When she has no genius, she is a woman _comme il n’en faut pas_,” Blondet replied, emphasizing the words with a glance that could be interpreted as praise for Camille Maupin. “This epigram is not mine, but Napoleon’s,” he added.

“You need not hold a grudge against Napoleon for that,” Canalis interjected emphatically. “He was weak in being jealous of literary genius—he had his flaws. Who can ever explain, depict, or understand Napoleon? A man with arms folded, who did everything, the greatest force known, the most concentrated, the most caustic; a singular genius who spread armed civilization without fixing it anywhere; a man who could do everything because he willed everything; a prodigious phenomenon of will, conquering illness through battle, yet destined to die of disease in bed after living amid balls and bullets; a man with a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear-sighted spirit that foresaw everything but his own fall; a capricious politician who risked men by handfuls for economy, yet spared three heads—those of Talleyrand, Pozzo de Borgo, and Metternich, diplomats whose deaths would have saved the French Empire, and who seemed to him of greater weight than thousands of soldiers; a man to whom nature, as a rare privilege, gave a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind at midnight among women, and the next morning manipulating Europe as a young girl might amuse herself by splashing water in her bath! Hypocritical and generous; loving tawdriness and simplicity; devoid of taste, yet protecting the arts; and despite these contradictions, truly great in everything by instinct or temperament; Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty; and then, like my grocer buried in Père Lachaise, a good husband and father. In short, he improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—and all with more range than precision. Did he not aim to make all Europe France? And after making us weigh on the earth in such a way as to change the laws of gravitation, he left us poorer than on the day he first laid hands on us; while he, who had taken an empire by his name, lost his name on the frontier of his empire in a sea of blood and soldiers. A man of thought and action, who understood Desaix and Fouché.”

“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.

“Ah! what a pleasure it is to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.

“But do you think the treat we are giving you is a common one?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you had to pay for the charms of conversation as you do for those of dancing or music, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”

“And are we really so much diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked, smiling skeptically at the women. “Because, in these days, under a regime that makes everything small, you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books, does that prove that women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? In all ages, passions remain the same. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity—the glory, if you will—that once gave luster to the errors of some women. But even if one has not saved a King of France, one is not less an Agnès Sorel. Do you believe our dear Marquise d’Espard is not the equal of Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffant, in whose rooms so much evil was spoken and done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Or Malibran the equal of Saint-Huberti? Are not our poets superior to those of the eighteenth century? If at this moment, due to the Grocers who govern us, we lack a style of our own, did not the Empire have its distinguishing stamp as the age of Louis XV. did, and was not its splendor fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”

“I completely agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” the Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear in proportions as fine as those of the most beautiful women of the past? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV. had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand now in Paris who certainly write better than she did and do not publish their letters. Whether the Frenchwoman is called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.

“Émile Blondet has painted a picture of the fascinations of a woman of the day; but, when needed, this creature who bristles or shows off, who echoes the ideas of Mr. This and Mr. That, could be heroic. And it must be said, your faults, mesdames, are all the more poetic because they are always surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much of the world, perhaps too late; but in cases where the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I have always observed the effects of what you may call Providence, inevitably overwhelming those we consider light women.”

“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways——”

“Oh, let the Comte de Vandenesse preach to us!” Madame de Serizy exclaimed.

“With all the more reason because he has preached a great deal by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.

“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you are very fond of,” he said, looking at Blondet—“in which the finger of God has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was very near being by my act——”

“Well, tell us all about it!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”

“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay replied, glancing at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.

“During the campaign of 1812,” General de Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may be of use to you, Doctor Bianchon,” turning to me, “since, while you devote yourself to the human body, you concern yourself a good deal with the mind; it may help solve some problems of the will.

“I was going through my second campaign; I enjoyed danger and laughed at everything, like the young and foolish lieutenant of artillery that I was. When we reached the Beresina, the army had lost all discipline and forgotten military obedience. It was a medley of men of all nations, instinctively making their way from north to south. Soldiers would drive a general in rags and barefoot away from their fire if he brought neither wood nor food. After crossing this famous river, disorder did not diminish. I had come quietly and alone, without food, out of the marshes of Zembin, wandering in search of a house where I might be taken in. Finding none or being driven away from those I encountered, I finally spotted a wretched little Polish farm, which you can only imagine if you have seen the wooden houses of Lower Normandy or the poorest farm buildings of la Beauce. These dwellings consist of a single room, with one end divided off by a wooden partition, the smaller division serving as a store-room for forage.

“In the twilight, I could see faint smoke rising above this house. Hoping to find comrades more compassionate than those I had previously encountered, I boldly approached the farm. Inside, I found the table laid. Several officers, along with a woman—a common sight—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh broiled over charcoal, and frozen beetroots. I recognized a few artillery captains from my first regiment. I was welcomed with a shout of acclamation, which would have amazed me on the other side of the Beresina; but at this moment, the cold was less intense; my fellow-officers were warm, they had food, and the room, strewn with straw, promised a delightful night. We did not ask for much in those days. My comrades could be philanthropists for free—one of the commonest ways of being philanthropic. I sat down to eat on one of the straw bundles.

“At the end of the table, by the door leading to the smaller room full of straw, sat my old colonel, one of the most extraordinary men I ever met among the mixed collection of men I encountered. He was Italian. Whenever human nature is truly fine in the South, it is sublime. I do not know if you have noticed the extreme fairness of Italians when they are fair. It is exquisite, especially under artificial light. When I read the fantastical portrait of Colonel Oudet sketched by Charles Nodier, I found my own sensations in every one of his elegant phrases. Italian, as were most of the officers of his regiment, which had been borrowed by the Emperor from Eugene’s army, my colonel was tall, at least eight or nine inches above average, and admirably proportioned—perhaps a little stout, but prodigiously powerful, active, and clean-limbed like a greyhound. His abundant black curls contrasted with his complexion, as white as a woman’s; he had small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and a delicately formed aquiline nose, which would pinch and turn white when he was angry, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; I will not elaborate. No one could remain calm in his presence. I alone, perhaps, was not afraid of him; he had taken such a singular fancy to me that he thought everything I did was right. When he was angry, his brow would knit, and the muscles of his forehead would set in a delta, or, to be more explicit, in Redgauntlet’s horseshoe. This mark was perhaps even more terrifying than the magnetic flashes of his blue eyes. His whole frame quivered, and his strength, great as it was in his normal state, became almost unbounded.

“He spoke with a strong guttural roll. His voice, at least as powerful as that of Charles Nordier’s Oudet, conveyed an incredible fullness of tone into the syllable or consonant in which this burr was sounded. Though this faulty pronunciation could sometimes be a grace, when commanding his men or when excited, you cannot imagine, unless you had heard it, the force expressed by this accent, which is so common in Paris. When the Colonel was calm, his blue eyes were angelically sweet, and his smooth brow had a charming expression. On parade, or with the army of Italy, no man could compare with him. Indeed, d’Orsay himself, the handsome d’Orsay, was eclipsed by our colonel during the last review held by Napoleon before the invasion of Russia.

“Everything about this exceptional man was in contrast. Passion thrives on contrast. Thus, you need not ask whether he exerted over women the irresistible influences to which our nature yields”—and the general looked at the Princesse de Cadignan—“as vitreous matter is molded under the pipe of the glass-blower; still, by a singular fatality—an observer might explain the phenomenon—the Colonel was not a lady-killer or indifferent to such successes.

“To illustrate his violence, I will recount a brief incident. We were dragging our guns up a narrow road bordered by a high slope on one side and thickets on the other. Halfway up, we encountered another regiment of artillery, its colonel marching at the head. This colonel wanted our captain to back down, but the captain refused. The other colonel ordered his foremost battery to advance, and despite the driver’s care to keep among the scrub, the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him over on the near side of his horse. All this happened in an instant. Our Colonel, who was nearby, sensed a quarrel; he galloped up, riding among the guns at the risk of falling with his horse’s four feet in the air, and reached the spot just as the captain fell, calling out ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam like champagne rose to his lips; he roared inarticulately like a lion. Incapable of uttering a word or even a cry, he made a terrifying signal to his opponent, pointing to the wood and drawing his sword. The two colonels stepped aside. In two seconds, we saw our Colonel’s opponent stretched on the ground, his skull split in two. The soldiers of his regiment backed away—yes, by heaven, and quickly too.

“The captain, who had nearly been crushed and lay yelping in the puddle where the gun carriage had thrown him, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This circumstance aggravated his rage. He was pledged to protect the husband, bound to defend him as he would have defended the woman herself.

“Now, in the hovel beyond Zembin, where I was so well received, this captain was sitting opposite me, and his wife was at the other end of the table, facing the Colonel. This Sicilian was a little woman named Rosina, very dark, but with all the fire of the Southern sun in her black almond-shaped eyes. At this moment, she was terribly thin; her face was covered with dust, like fruit exposed to the drought of a highroad. Scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair disheveled and clinging together under a piece of shawl tied over her head, she still possessed the graces of a woman; her movements were engaging, her small rose mouth and white teeth, the outline of her features and figure, charms which misery, cold, and neglect had not entirely defaced, still suggested love to any man who could think of a woman. Rosina had one of those frames that appear fragile but are wiry and full of spring. Her husband, a gentleman from Piedmont, had a face expressive of ironical simplicity, if it is permissible to combine those two words. Brave and well-informed, he seemed unaware of the connections that had existed between his wife and the Colonel for three years. I attributed this unconcern to Italian manners or some domestic secret; yet there was one feature in the man’s countenance that filled me with involuntary distrust. His thin, restless under lip turned down at the corners instead of up, which I thought betrayed a streak of cruelty in a character that seemed phlegmatic and indolent.

“As you can imagine, the conversation was not very lively when I arrived. My weary comrades ate in silence; they asked me some questions, and we shared our misadventures, mingled with reflections on the campaign, the generals, their mistakes, the Russians, and the cold. A minute after my arrival, the colonel, having finished his meager meal, wiped his mustache, bid us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman, saying, ‘Rosina?’ and then, without waiting for a reply, went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of the Colonel’s utterance was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing all the annoyance she could not feel at seeing her thralldom flaunted without human decency, and the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband. But there was also, in the rigid setting of her features and the tight knitting of her brows, a sort of presentiment; perhaps she foresaw her fate. Rosina remained quietly in her place.

“A minute later, and apparently when the Colonel was snug in his straw or hay bed, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’

“The tone of this second call was even more brutally demanding than the first. The Colonel’s strong burr, combined with the length Italian allows for vowels and final syllables, concentrated all the man’s despotism, impatience, and strength of will. Rosina turned pale, but she rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel.

“All the party sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, after looking at them all, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too. ‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband.

“‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied, becoming serious again, ‘I confess that I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’

“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly.

“Thereupon we all lay down in the room, and before long, all were sound asleep.

“Next morning, each one, without waking his neighbor or seeking companionship, set out again on his way, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror ever enacted under heaven. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, we most of us met again and walked on together, like geese led in flocks by a child’s wilful tyranny. The same necessity urged us all.

“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse where we had spent the night, we heard sounds resembling the roar of lions in the desert, the bellowing of bulls—no, it was a noise that can be compared to no known cry. Yet, mingling with this horrible and ominous roar, we could hear a woman’s feeble scream. We all looked around, seized by an impulse of terror; we no longer saw the house, but a huge bonfire. The farmhouse had been barricaded and was in flames. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and an indescribable pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain was quietly approaching to join our caravan; we gazed at him in silence, for no one dared question him; but he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with the forefinger of his right hand, and, waving his left in the direction of the fire, said, ‘_Son’io_.’

“We all walked on without saying a word to him.”

“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.

“It would be frightful to let us leave with this horrible picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”

“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.

“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.

“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.

“Yes,” I replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption, in its last stage, left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking around four in the morning, signed to me in the most touching way, with a friendly smile, to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes and color contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her countenance was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling stemmed from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.

“At this moment, the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. I did not see the gesture of impatience he made, expressing regret for having lost sight of his wife for a few of the last moments granted to him; but it is certain that anyone but the dying woman might have misunderstood it. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, the Duke had a thousand odd ways on the surface, which often lead a genius to be mistaken for a madman, and whose explanation lies in the exacting needs of their intellect. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out her hand, took her husband’s, and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice, she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”

“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”

“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.

PARIS, June 1839-42.

################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have some appalling stories in my collection. But each one has its proper hour in a conversation—you know the jest recorded by Chamfort, said to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”

“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.

“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” was the cry on every side.

The obliging doctor bowed, and silence reigned.

“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house, crowned with very high roofs, so isolated that there is nothing near it, not even a fetid tannery or a squalid tavern. In front of this house is a garden down to the river, where the box shrubs, once clipped to edge the walks, now straggle at will. A few willows rooted in the stream have grown up quickly like an enclosing fence, half hiding the house. The wild plants we call weeds have clothed the bank with their beauty. The fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear fruit, and their suckers have formed a thicket. The espaliers resemble a copse. The paths, once graveled, are overgrown with purslane; to be accurate, there is no trace of a path.

“Looking down from the hilltop, where the ruins of the old castle of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, one can see that this spot must have once been the joy of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture, especially to choice fruit. An arbor is visible, or rather the wreck of one, with a table still standing, not entirely destroyed by time. The aspect of this garden evokes the peaceful life of the provinces, much like reading the epitaph of a worthy tradesman. To complete the mournful impressions, one wall bears a sundial graced with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’

“The roof of this house is dreadfully dilapidated; the outside shutters are always closed; the balconies are hung with swallows’ nests; the doors are forever shut. Straggling grasses outline the flagstones of the steps with green; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter, summer, and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, and peeled off the paint. The dreary silence is broken only by birds and cats, polecats, rats, and mice, free to scamper around. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’

“If, prompted by curiosity, you look at this house from the street, you will see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of holes made by children. I later learned that this door had been blocked for ten years. Through these irregular breaches, you will see that the side towards the courtyard is in perfect harmony with the side towards the garden. The same ruin prevails. Weeds outline the paving-stones; the walls are scored by enormous cracks, and the blackened coping is laced with festoons of pellitory. The stone steps are disjointed; the bell-cord is rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven could have fallen there? By what decree has salt been sown on this dwelling? Has God been mocked here? Or was France betrayed? These are the questions we ask ourselves. Reptiles crawl over it, but give no reply. This empty and deserted house is a vast enigma known to none.

“It was formerly a little domain, held in fief, known as La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me in charge of a rich patient, the sight of this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. Was it not far better than a ruin? Certain memories of authenticity attach themselves to a ruin; but this house, still standing, though slowly destroyed by an avenging hand, contained a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once in the evening, I boarded the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure. I braved scratches to enter this ownerless garden, lingering for hours in its disorder. I would not have asked a single question of any gossiping native for the price of the story to which this strange scene was due. There, I wove delightful romances and indulged in melancholy that enchanted me. If I had known the reason for this neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that intoxicated me. To me, this refuge represented various phases of human life shadowed by misfortune; sometimes the peace of a graveyard without the dead, who speak in the language of epitaphs; one day I saw it as the home of lepers; another, the house of the Atridae; but above all, I found there provincial life, with its contemplative ideas, its hour-glass existence. I often wept there; I never laughed.

“More than once, I felt involuntary terrors as I heard overhead the dull hum of some hurrying wood-pigeon. The earth is dank; you must be on the watch for lizards, vipers, and frogs wandering about with the wild freedom of nature; above all, you must have no fear of cold, for in a few moments, you feel an icy cloak settle on your shoulders, like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.

“One evening, I felt a shudder; the wind had turned an old rusty weathercock, and the creaking sounded like a cry from the house, just as I was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this monumental embodiment of woe. I returned to my inn, lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper, the hostess came into my room with an air of mystery, saying, ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’

“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’

“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said, leaving the room.

“Suddenly, a tall, slim man dressed in black appeared, hat in hand, entering like a ram ready to butt his opponent, with a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. The stranger wore an old coat, much worn at the seams, but had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘whom have I the honor of addressing?’ He took a chair, placed himself in front of my fire, put his hat on my table, and answered while rubbing his hands: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’

“I was encouraging myself by saying to myself, ‘Seek!’

“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’

“‘I am delighted to hear it, monsieur,’ I exclaimed. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’

“‘One moment!’ he said, holding up his hand as if to gain silence. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed that you sometimes walk in the garden of la Grande Bretèche.’

“‘Yes, monsieur.’

“‘One moment!’ he repeated. ‘That constitutes a misdemeanor. Monsieur, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I come in her name to beg you to discontinue the practice. One moment! I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you are free to be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. Nevertheless, you must be a man of education, and you should know that the laws forbid, under heavy penalties, any trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be quite content to make you free to come and go in the house; but being bound to respect the will of the testatrix, I have the honor, monsieur, to beg that you will go into the garden no more. I myself, monsieur, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which, as I had the honor of informing you, is part of the estate of the late Madame de Merret. We have done nothing there but verify the number of doors and windows to assess the taxes I have to pay annually out of the funds left for that purpose by the late Madame de Merret. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in the town.’

“The good man paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility, understanding that the administration of Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life, his reputation, his glory, his Restoration. As I was forced to bid farewell to my beautiful reveries and romances, I was to reject learning the truth on official authority.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘would it be indiscreet if I were to ask you the reasons for such eccentricity?’

“At these words, an expression of pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He pulled up the collar of his shirt, took out his snuffbox, opened it, and offered me a pinch; on my refusing, he took a large one. He was happy! A man without a hobby does not know all the good to be had in life. At this moment, I understood the whole bearing of Sterne’s charming passion and had a perfect idea of the delight with which my uncle Toby, encouraged by Trim, bestrode his hobby-horse.

“‘Monsieur,’ said Monsieur Regnault, ‘I was head-clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first-rate house, which you may have heard mentioned? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having enough money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; among others, a wealthy aunt who allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, as I was going to bed—it was before my marriage—I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Chateau of Merret. Her maid, a good girl, who is now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you that Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, flinging himself into every kind of dissipation. You understand?

“‘On the day when he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted la Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some people even say she burnt all the furniture, the hangings—in short, everything used in furnishing the premises now let by the said M.—(Dear, what am I saying? I beg your pardon, I thought I was dictating a lease.)—In short, that she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’

“‘For about three months previously,’ he continued, with a jerk of his head, ‘the Count and Countess had lived in a very eccentric way; they admitted no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor, and Monsieur on the first floor. When the Countess was left alone, she was never seen except at church. Subsequently, at home, at the chateau, she refused to see friends, whether gentlemen or ladies, who went to call on her. She was already very much altered when she left la Grande Bretèche to go to Merret. That dear lady—I say dear lady, for it was she who gave me this diamond, but indeed I saw her but once—that kind lady was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up all hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; indeed, many of our ladies fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was strangely excited by hearing that Madame de Merret had need of my services. Nor was I the only person who took an interest in the affair. That very night, though it was already late, all the town knew that I was going to Merret.

“‘The waiting-woman replied vaguely to my questions on the way; nevertheless, she told me that her mistress had received the Sacrament in the course of the day at the hands of the Curé of Merret and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the chateau. I went up the great staircase. After crossing some large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors that were current concerning this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I were to repeat all the tales that were told about her), I had imagined her a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty in seeing her in the great bed where she was lying. To light this enormous room, with old-fashioned heavy cornices, and so thick with dust that merely to see it was enough to make you sneeze, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! but you have not been to Merret. Well, the bed is one of those old world beds, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table stood by the bed, on which I saw an “Imitation of Christ,” which, by the way, I bought for my wife, as well as the lamp. There were also a deep armchair for her confidential maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, not enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.

“‘My dear sir, if you had seen, as I then saw, that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt yourself transported into a scene of a romance. It was icy, nay more, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand with a theatrical gesture and paused.

“‘By dint of seeking, as I approached the bed, at last I saw Madame de Merret, under the glimmer of the lamp, which fell on the pillows. Her face was as yellow as wax, and as narrow as two folded hands. The Countess had a lace cap showing her abundant hair, but as white as linen thread. She was sitting up in bed, and seemed to keep upright with great difficulty. Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever, no doubt, and half-dead already, hardly moved under the bony arch of her eyebrows.—There,’ he added, pointing to his own brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; the veins and muscles were perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; but at this moment I was startled into an indescribable emotion at the sight. Never, said those who wrapped her in her shroud, had any living creature been so emaciated and lived. In short, it was awful to behold! Sickness so consumed that woman, that she was no more than a phantom. Her lips, which were pale violet, seemed to me not to move when she spoke to me.

“‘Though my profession has familiarized me with such spectacles, by calling me not infrequently to the bedside of the dying to record their last wishes, I confess that families in tears and the agonies I have seen were as nothing in comparison with this lonely and silent woman in her vast chateau. I heard not the least sound; I did not perceive the movement which the sufferer’s breathing ought to have given to the sheets that covered her, and I stood motionless, absorbed in looking at her in a sort of stupor. In fancy, I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back on the bed, and she uttered these words, which came like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to her to speak.

“‘“Madame,” I began. She signed to me to be silent. At that moment, the old housekeeper rose and said in my ear, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise, and what you say might agitate her.”

“‘I sat down. A few instants later, Madame de Merret collected all her remaining strength to move her right hand and slipped it, not without infinite difficulty, under the bolster; she then paused a moment. With a last effort, she withdrew her hand; and when she brought out a sealed paper, drops of perspiration rolled from her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix that lay on the bed, lifted it hastily to her lips, and died.

“‘The expression of her eyes still makes me shudder as I think of it. She must have suffered much! There was joy in her last glance, and it remained stamped on her dead eyes.

“‘I brought away the will, and when it was opened, I found that Madame de Merret had appointed me her executor. She left the whole of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except for a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding la Grande Bretèche: She ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from the day of her death, in the state it was at the time of her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if it were necessary to secure the absolute fulfillment of her intentions. At the expiration of that term, if the will of the testatrix has been duly carried out, the house is to become the property of my heirs, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise, la Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs-at-law, but on condition of fulfilling certain conditions set forth in a codicil to the will, which is not to be opened until the expiration of the said term of fifty years. The will has not been disputed, so——’ And without finishing his sentence, the lanky notary looked at me with an air of triumph; I made him quite happy by offering him my congratulations.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said in conclusion, ‘you have so vividly impressed me that I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’

“‘Monsieur,’ said he, with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.’

“However, I soon loosened the tongue of the discreet notary of Vendôme, who communicated to me, not without long digressions, the opinions of the deep politicians of both sexes whose judgments are law in Vendôme. But these opinions were so contradictory and diffuse that I was near falling asleep despite my interest in this authentic history. The notary’s ponderous voice and monotonous accent, accustomed no doubt to listen to himself and to make himself listened to by his clients or fellow-townsmen, were too much for my curiosity. Happily, he soon went away.

“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ said he on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five-and-forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid the first finger of his right hand to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—as long as that,’ said he, ‘you must not be past sixty now.’

“I closed my door, having been roused from my apathy by this last speech, which the notary thought very funny; then I sat down in my armchair, with my feet on the fire-dogs. I had lost myself in a romance à la Radcliffe, constructed on the juridical base given me by Monsieur Regnault, when the door, opened by a woman’s cautious hand, turned on the hinges. I saw my landlady come in, a buxom, florid dame, always good-humored, who had missed her calling in life. She was a Fleming, who ought to have seen the light in a picture by Teniers.

“‘Well, monsieur,’ said she, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of la Grande Bretèche?’

“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’

“‘And what did he tell you?’

“I repeated in a few words the creepy and sinister story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence, my hostess put her head forward, looking at me with an innkeeper’s keen scrutiny, a happy compromise between the instinct of a police constable, the astuteness of a spy, and the cunning of a dealer.

“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ said I as I ended, ‘you seem to know more about it. Heh? If not, why have you come up to me?’

“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’

“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’

“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid for everything down, so as never to have difficulties with anyone. He was hot-tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’

“‘Because he was hot-tempered?’ I asked her.

“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose, sir, that a man had to have something to show for a figurehead before he could marry Madame de Merret, who, without any reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet-looking, quite a gem of a woman. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’

“‘And were they happy together?’

“‘Hm, hm! so-so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail-fellow-well-met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’

“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?’

“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’

“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’

“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When I saw Monsieur Regnault go up to see you, it struck me that he would speak to you about Madame de Merret as having to do with la Grande Bretèche. That put it into my head to ask your advice, sir, seeming to me that you are a man of good judgment and incapable of playing a poor woman like me false—for I never did anyone a wrong, and yet I am tormented by my conscience. Up to now, I have never dared to say a word to the people of these parts; they are all chatter-mags, with tongues like knives. And never till now, sir, have I had any traveler here who stayed so long in the inn as you have, and to whom I could tell the history of the fifteen thousand francs——’

“‘My dear Madame Lepas, if there is anything in your story of a nature to compromise me,’ I said, interrupting her flow of words, ‘I would not hear it for all the world.’

“‘You need have no fears,’ said she; ‘you will see.’

“Her eagerness made me suspect that I was not the only person to whom my worthy landlady had communicated the secret of which I was to be the sole possessor, but I listened.

“‘Monsieur,’ said she, ‘when the Emperor sent the Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the charge of the Government a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—neither more nor less. He had a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name down in my books, and you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, but which I admired all the same. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, and, among others, General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to owe him a grudge for that. Oh! I was very fond of him, though he did not say four words to me in a day, and it was impossible to have the least bit of talk with him; if he was spoken to, he did not answer; it is a way, a mania they all have, it would seem.

“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass and all the services quite regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the very first time he entered the church, no one imagined that there was any purpose in it. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“‘One evening, shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys mentioned he thought he saw the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.

“‘Eventually, one morning, we found he hadn’t returned. Searching his belongings, I discovered a note in his drawer, along with fifty doubloons worth about five thousand francs and a sealed box containing diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note stated that if he didn’t return, he left us this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.

“‘At that time, my husband went searching for him. Interestingly, he returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He went early, so no one saw him. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced he had escaped.

“‘The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas believed the Spaniard drowned himself. I, however, think he was involved in the matter concerning Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me that the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was made of ebony and silver; early in his stay, Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, do you think I should feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?’

“‘Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?’ I asked.

“‘Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.’

“After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague, sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread, akin to the emotion felt when entering a dark church at night, where a faint light glimmers under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, suddenly appeared vividly in my mind. I felt compelled to explore the mysterious dwelling to uncover the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.

“Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Observing her, I sensed an inner thought beneath her blooming health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, reminiscent of devout souls who pray excessively or a girl who has killed her child and forever hears its last cry. Yet, she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality, and one would deem her innocent just by seeing the large checked kerchief covering her sturdy figure, tucked into her lilac-and-white-striped gown. ‘No,’ I resolved, ‘I will not leave Vendôme without knowing the full story of la Grande Bretèche. To achieve this, I will make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.’

“‘Rosalie!’ I called one evening.

“‘Your servant, sir?’

“‘You’re not married?’ She flinched slightly.

“‘Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!’ she laughed, quickly regaining her composure; every woman, from the highest lady to the inn-servant, possesses a natural presence of mind.

“‘Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?’

“‘Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.’

“This response seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared to hold a central role in this romantic affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t a matter for ordinary courtship; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance, and from that moment, all my attention was directed toward Rosalie. By studying her, I noticed various admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm desire can bestow upon a woman, regardless of her station. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning, I said to Rosalie:

“‘Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.’

“‘Oh!’ she replied, ‘I will tell you, but keep it a secret.’

“‘Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.’

“‘If it’s all the same to you,’ she said, ‘I’d prefer it be with your own.’

“She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. The best tales are told at a certain hour—just as we are all here at table. No one ever tells a story well standing or fasting.

“If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarcely suffice. Now, as the event she recounted stands midway between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it concisely.

“The room at la Grande Bretèche where Madame de Merret slept was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been seriously ill, so much so that her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. By chance, he returned home two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. His wife assumed he was in bed and asleep. However, the evening’s animated discussions and his losses at billiards had delayed him.

“For some time, Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to inform him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative responses led him to his room, trusting her completely. But that evening, he decided to check on Madame de Merret to share his misfortunes and perhaps seek comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner and thought her convalescence had enhanced her beauty, a realization that often comes too late for husbands.

“Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. His instincts were piqued by a nagging doubt, and he noticed an anxious expression in her eyes.

“‘You’re very late,’ she said, her voice slightly husky.

“Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing the room, arms crossed.

“‘Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?’ his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t respond.

“‘You can go, Rosalie,’ Madame de Merret said; ‘I can manage my curl-papers myself.’ She sensed disaster in her husband’s demeanor and wanted to be alone with him. Once Rosalie left, or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage, Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, stating coldly, ‘Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!’ She calmly replied, ‘No, monsieur.’

“This ‘No’ pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand, stopping him, and said with strange emotion, ‘Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.’

“The extraordinary dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could become legendary on a grander stage.

“‘No, Josephine,’ he said, ‘I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.’—At these words, Madame de Merret looked at him with a haggard expression. ‘Here is your crucifix,’ he continued. ‘Swear to me before God that there’s no one in there; I will believe you—I will never open that door.’

“Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, ‘I swear it.’

“‘Louder,’ he insisted; ‘repeat: “I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.”’ She repeated the words without flinching.

“‘That will do,’ said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, ‘You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,’ examining the ebony and silver crucifix.

“‘I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.’

“‘Indeed,’ said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.

“He waited for her to arrive. ‘Go to bed, all of you,’ he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, ‘When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.’

“Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss the billiards game and the club’s discussions. When Rosalie returned, she found them conversing amiably.

“Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the reception rooms on the ground floor. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a considerable quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.

“‘Gorenflot is here, sir,’ Rosalie whispered.

“‘Tell him to come in,’ he replied aloud.

“Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.

“‘Gorenflot,’ said her husband, ‘go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.’ Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, ‘Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning, I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, which won’t be paid until your wedding day, contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!’

“‘Rosalie,’ Madame de Merret said, ‘come and brush my hair.’

“Her husband quietly paced the room, keeping an eye on the door, the mason, and his wife, without displaying any suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: ‘My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.’ Then she added casually, ‘You’d better help him.’

“Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. This silence was intentional on the husband’s part; he didn’t want to give his wife the chance to say anything ambiguous. On Madame de Merret’s side, it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason took advantage of his master’s back being turned to break one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to Gorenflot. They all three then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.

“Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling ‘Hope.’

“At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.

“The next morning, he casually mentioned, ‘Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.’ He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy.

“‘He will go to Duvivier’s,’ she thought.

“As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, ‘The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.’

“In an instant, Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with an intensity beyond words, she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.

“‘Lay madame on her bed,’ he said coldly.

“Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.

“‘Duvivier,’ Monsieur de Merret asked, ‘didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?’

“‘No, monsieur.’

“‘Very well; thank you,’ he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. ‘Jean,’ he added, turning to his valet, ‘you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.’

“The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet, and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said, without allowing her to speak, ‘You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.’”

After this story, all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some among them had shivered at the last words.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

One of the few drawing-rooms where the Archbishop of Besançon was seen during the Restoration was that of Baronne de Watteville, who was closely associated with him due to her devout nature.

Monsieur de Watteville, a descendant of the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming compared to his ancestor. After living quietly in La Franche-Comté, he married the heiress of the Rupt family, Mademoiselle de Rupt, who brought a substantial income to the marriage. Their union, arranged in 1802, was formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following the death of her grandparents, Madame de Watteville became more devout, leading a pious life that influenced the upper circles of Besançon.

Monsieur de Watteville, a thin and unremarkable man, spent his days as a turner and collector of local geological specimens. Some remarked that he had a noble soul, recognizing early on that he would never dominate his wife, so he immersed himself in his work and hobbies.

The Rupt residence was grand, reflecting the nobility of both families. It featured elegant furnishings and exquisite food, with Monsieur de Watteville serving as his own butler. Madame de Watteville’s wealth was significant, while her husband’s estate was modest. Their close relationship with the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés to their home.

During a ceremonial dinner in September 1834, guests eagerly welcomed Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit. The Vicar-General explained that their legal victory was unexpected, especially given the political climate. He credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron, for his pivotal role in the case.

Curious about Savaron, Madame de Watteville inquired about his background. The Vicar-General noted that Savaron lived nearby, but his origins were unknown, prompting further intrigue among the guests. Amedee de Soulas, present at the gathering, would later prove significant to the unfolding story.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In all ages, France and England have exchanged cultural influences, particularly in language and fashion, often bypassing customs regulations. The term "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting a mutual influence. For instance, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, while the hoop skirts, initially ridiculed, became a dominant fashion in Europe for decades. By 1815, English long waists were mocked in Paris, but soon French styles evolved.

In recent years, England has contributed new terms to the French language, such as "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset's poetry, led to a trend of stylish young men in Paris, which spread to provincial towns. In 1834, Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt out of place in the dull, military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained a lavish appearance, employing a young servant named Babylas as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas in fashionable attire, spending extravagantly on his own wardrobe while managing to live on a limited income.

Despite being perceived as a spendthrift, Amedee was skilled at budgeting, often dining out to save on meals. His lifestyle, including expensive grooming and clothing, was a façade to impress the local aristocracy and win the favor of Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. Amedee's travels and knowledge of Parisian culture helped him gain a reputation as a well-informed young man, despite his financial struggles.

Besançon was a conservative town resistant to change, where the local aristocracy, referred to as "the Colony," maintained strict social hierarchies. Amedee's unique fashion choices, such as wearing trouser-straps, set him apart and contributed to his status as a "lion." The town's aristocrats were keen to present a modern image to visiting Parisians, and Amedee's presence allowed them to do so.

Amedee's ambitions included marrying Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict, religious household with little exposure to the outside world. At eighteen, she was a modest, unassuming girl, educated under her mother's watchful eye, with little knowledge of contemporary society. Her upbringing emphasized domestic skills over social graces, and she was largely sheltered from the influences of modern culture.

Despite her seemingly passive demeanor, Rosalie possessed a strong spirit inherited from her family lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, believing her daughter's honor was at stake. This dynamic created tension between them, especially as Amedee courted Rosalie. The Baroness's strictness led Rosalie to suppress any feelings she might have for Amedee, resulting in a complex relationship marked by her mother's jealousy and Rosalie's own uncertainty.

As Amedee pursued Rosalie, he navigated the social intricacies of Besançon, aiming to secure a favorable marriage while maintaining his reputation. The town's elite viewed him as a charming young man, and discussions about his potential engagement to Rosalie circulated among the local nobility. However, Rosalie's upbringing and her mother's influence complicated Amedee's efforts, as she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations surrounding her.

In summary, Amedee de Soulas's life in Besançon exemplifies the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and the constraints of provincial life, as he strives to win the heart of Rosalie de Watteville amidst the rigid social structures of his time.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness, as he waited for his soup to cool, “one fine morning, a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and chose to stay on the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered as a resident at the Mairie and had his name, ‘ALBERT SAVARON,’ entered on the list of barristers at the Court, leaving his card with local officials.”

“The name Savaron is well-known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville interjected, “as one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”

“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he wishes to claim the Savaron arms, he must add a bar-sinister. The only remaining member of the Brabant family is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”

“The bar-sinister indicates a bastard, but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble,” Rosalie added.

“Enough, that will do, mademoiselle!” the Baroness interjected.

“You insisted on her learning heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville remarked, “and she knows it well.”

“Please continue, Monsieur de Soulas.”

“In a town like Besancon, where everything is classified and known, Albert Savaron was received without hesitation by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he hoped to achieve, especially since he sent his card to the judges instead of calling in person. Three days later, he was forgotten. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. Savaron was completely overlooked because no one had seen him.”

“Does he not go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, has breakfast, then continues working. He walks in his garden, circling fifty or sixty times, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”
