“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt inquired.

“I live on Rue Neuve, overlooking his house, and my servant communicates with Jerome.”

“And you gossip with Babylas?”

“What else is there to do while riding?”

“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked, directing the conversation to the Vicar-General.

“The President of the Court assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man’s innocence and exposed the real culprits, leading to their arrest. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant subsequently entrusted him with a difficult case, which he won. Given our situation, Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron, predicting our success. Upon meeting him, I felt confident in him, and I was right.”

“Is he truly extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.

“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.

“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in his outer room, which he had decorated like old oak and lined with law books. The room was simply furnished with an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, and a green carpet. As I waited, I did not expect my advocate to be a young man. The setting matched his appearance; he wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”

“The devil’s colors!” exclaimed Madame de Watteville.

“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. His black hair is streaked with gray, thick and curly, with a round white throat, a splendid forehead marked by deep lines of thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and fiery eyes. Despite his passionate nature, he has a calm demeanor and a sweet voice that can be thunderous when needed.”

“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”

“The second time I visited, he received me in his bedroom, which was quite shabby. The third time, he was having a frugal breakfast in his library, and I observed him closely. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”

“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”

“After studying his fine head,” the Abbe replied, “and hearing him respond to a prominent Parisian lawyer, I believe he will eventually make a significant impact.”

“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.

The conversation shifted, and Albert Savaron was no longer mentioned. The Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie, who found herself drawn to the romance of his character. For the first time, she encountered someone exceptional, contrasting sharply with the mundane Count who had previously courted her. Albert Savaron presented many enigmas to unravel.

“Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him. She replayed the Abbe’s words in her mind, fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, scrutinizing the men at the table, finding none that compared.

“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.

“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she replied, lost in thought about the prelate’s hands.

As she pondered, she recalled a lighted window she had seen from her bed, realizing it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.

“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull in conversation.

“What does that matter to you?” her mother replied, sensing her daughter’s unusual interest.

“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” the Abbe answered.

“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit during dinner,” Madame de Watteville remarked.

“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting laughter. “But Monsieur de Grancey was so engrossed that I became interested.”

After dinner, Rosalie listened for any mention of Albert Savaron, but aside from congratulations to the Abbe for winning his suit, he was not discussed. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse Albert’s windows. When the hour arrived, she felt a thrill watching the light from his candles through the trees. She saw him writing and imagined the color of his furniture.

“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.

The education of girls poses serious challenges, as the future of a nation rests on mothers. The University of France has long avoided this issue: Should girls be informed about everything? Should their minds be restrained? The religious system often imposes such restraint. Enlightening them too much can lead to premature maturity, while suppressing their thoughts can result in explosive outbursts, as seen in Moliere’s character Agnes. This crisis was triggered in Rosalie by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.

The next morning, while dressing, Rosalie looked out to see Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered.

Having seen this extraordinary man, Rosalie was determined to enter his house, uncover the mystery surrounding him, hear his eloquent voice, and catch a glimpse of his eyes. She resolved to achieve this.

Throughout the day, she embroidered with the concentration of a girl who appears to think of nothing but is deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.

She visited her father in his workshop, suggesting he turn larger pieces and construct a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. As he delighted in the idea, she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding.

“Don’t worry!” he replied, sharing her frustration with his wife.

Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.

The eagerly awaited Sunday arrived, and Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled.

“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.

“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.

As she crossed the courtyard, her heart raced with anticipation. She had never walked the streets like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and would forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church before Mass, praying briefly before checking if the Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.

No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression on her. His solemn demeanor suggested he bore a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. Rosalie understood the Vicar-General’s words fully now. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a moment of boldness, she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.

After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a spot to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced the arrival of Monsieur Giroud, it felt as if her time in the confessional had been fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended, and Albert had left.

“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”

With this new desire, Rosalie focused intently on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.

From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie eagerly supported the plan for the Belvedere.

“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere he wants to build in the garden. Do you approve?”

“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”

“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us. Perhaps remarks will be made—”

“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”

“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”

“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.

She fully approved of the design, choosing a location in the garden that could not be seen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but would allow them to see into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor, while Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.

“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumors spread in Besancon.

“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”

“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.

“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”

“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” said an Abbe.

“No,” young Monsieur de Soulas corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”

“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt remarked sourly, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.

Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the success of her Belvedere, sensing her superiority over others. No one suspected that a girl thought to be a witless goose had simply devised a plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.

Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten, as envy among other lawyers grew. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings, which increased the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger in Besancon. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal acumen.

On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was also establishing a new venture. Through connections he had quietly formed among local merchants, he was launching a fortnightly paper called the _Eastern Review_, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, who were convinced of the need to promote Besancon’s interests as a trade hub.

To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon, bringing light to Eastern France. His ideas resonated with the merchants, who believed they were their own.

Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, allowing his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, to handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The _Review_ appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price, it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.

To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side of the _Review_. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while allowing him to believe he was gaining from their association. The commercial community, pleased with the _Review’s_ success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.

After the third issue, the _Review_ gained recognition among all French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, which sparked discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, despite the higher circles of Besancon dismissing it for its liberal views.

“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a _Review_ published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”

Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie, who had shown him much affection, went to subscribe to the _Eastern Review_ and lent her the four issues already published. That night, Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact of this work on her cannot be judged by ordinary standards. She had hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages, she became convinced that it reflected his experiences, as critics noted that Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In 1823, two young men set out from Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland, planning to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered stunning views of mountains, rivers, and meadows. As they passed the hamlet of Gersau, one of the friends, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman who appeared at the window. He decided to stay in Gersau while his friend Leopold continued the journey.

Rodolphe and Leopold were close friends, having studied law together. Leopold was destined for a career as a notary in Paris, while Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more tumultuous background. His mother had sacrificed everything to provide for his education, hoping to secure a better future for him. Rodolphe was passionate and sensitive, often driven by his desires, which contrasted sharply with Leopold's calm demeanor.

While in Gersau, Rodolphe found lodging with a couple willing to take him in. He learned about the woman he had seen, Fanny Lovelace, who had moved to the area with her elderly father for his health. They were of modest means, and Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was intrigued by her beauty and musical talent.

The next day, Rodolphe sought permission to visit the gardens of the Bergmanns, who rented to the Lovelaces. After some initial hesitation, he was allowed to explore. He noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who seemed to have a unique background. Rodolphe was eager to meet Fanny but was met with resistance from the Bergmanns, who were protective of their tenants.

That evening, Rodolphe heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later encountered her and Gina, but their conversation was interrupted when Rodolphe was attacked and wounded by an unknown assailant. Fanny and Gina helped him, and he was taken back to safety.

As Rodolphe recovered, he received devastating news: his mother had died. This loss plunged him into a deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny's company. They began to bond over shared interests, and Rodolphe expressed his feelings for her, only to learn that she was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny's affection for Rodolphe grew, and they shared intimate moments.

Fanny revealed that her husband was not as old as he appeared, and she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his feelings for her and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing that he had great potential.

Their relationship deepened, but Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Fanny received news from Tito, a young man from her past. Tito's arrival stirred feelings of rivalry in Rodolphe, who feared losing Fanny's affection.

Despite the challenges, Rodolphe and Fanny's connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances loomed over them. Rodolphe's ambition and Fanny's loyalty to her husband created a tension that neither could ignore.

As they navigated their feelings, Rodolphe grappled with the notion of love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions, but the reality of their lives threatened to overshadow their dreams.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For twenty days, Rodolphe spent his time at the Bergmanns’, observing Francesca without intending to. He recognized in her the youthful imprudence and the unbroken nature of a woman, sometimes resisting her love and at other times succumbing to it. The old man treated her like a father, and Francesca expressed deep gratitude, which stirred her innate nobility. Rodolphe found the situation and Francesca an intriguing enigma.

These days were filled with secret joys and melancholic moods, with delightful quarrels that were as enjoyable as their harmonious moments. Rodolphe became increasingly captivated by Francesca's unwavering tenderness, which was often jealous of trivial matters.

One evening, as Francesca expressed her desire to leave Gersau due to its lack of luxuries, Rodolphe asked if she cared for luxury. She replied passionately, equating her love for luxury with her love for art and beauty, asserting that she had never complained during their hardships. She mischievously questioned whether it was not natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, showcasing her charming personality.

Francesca then insisted that Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing the town's gossip and her desire to protect her husband’s reputation. She expressed her love for Rodolphe but emphasized that their affection must remain hidden from the world. She kissed him and left him astonished.

The next day, Rodolphe learned that the Bergmanns’ lodgers had departed early. Feeling restless, he left for Vevay, taking a longer route to reach Geneva by the end of October. To avoid the town's discomforts, he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiry was about Italian refugees, particularly a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord informed him that the only recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.

Rodolphe's first walk led him to the Villa Diodati, where he was struck by the beauty of the surroundings. As he approached, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady, which left him dizzy. Upon learning that they were at the Colonna residence, he felt a veil lift from his eyes, realizing the implications of their past interactions.

He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca, grappling with the reality of her noble lineage. He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was welcomed by the false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Despite his curiosity, Rodolphe refrained from asking to see the Princess, returning to Eaux-Vives with a mix of anticipation and anxiety.

As the evening approached, Rodolphe's passion intensified, and he recognized the need to elevate his social standing to match Francesca's. When he finally saw her at the concert, she was stunning, and their eyes met in a moment that felt electric. Francesca's beauty and grace captivated him, and he felt a profound connection, realizing she remained true to their past despite her royal status.

After the concert, Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who playfully referred to herself as "Princess Rodolphini." This light-hearted jest rekindled memories of their time together in Gersau, and Rodolphe was overwhelmed by the beauty of her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.

As days passed, Francesca recognized Rodolphe's deep love, which felt natural to her. She expressed that their love should be faithful and persistent, regardless of distance. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition, wanting to achieve greatness so that Francesca could be proud of him.

Francesca, with her enchanting blend of gaiety and tenderness, was a remarkable woman. Initially destined for a cloistered life, her family's tragedies led her to marry Prince Gandolphini, preserving their family's status. Despite her noble background, she had developed a taste for independence and liberal ideas, which aligned her with revolutionary sentiments.

Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva, where time flew by in the company of the intelligent and beautiful Francesca. However, as spring approached, he experienced inner turmoil, feeling that Francesca's calm demeanor contrasted with his own emotional struggles. He longed for her to show more feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.

One day, while they were on the terrace, Rodolphe expressed bitterness over the happiness of others, particularly a woman of noble lineage. Francesca, feeling wounded by his words, distanced herself, prompting Rodolphe to follow her. He found her in tears, and in a moment of passion, he kissed her, but she rebuffed him, asserting her dignity.

Francesca then ordered Rodolphe to leave, stating they would not meet again until Naples. Though her command was firm, Rodolphe complied, recognizing the strength of her will.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On his return to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini painted by Schinner, who had previously refused to paint several women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade the artist, but Francesca managed to charm him into creating an original portrait for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She conveyed this in a delightful letter, sparking a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca, their only indulgence.

Driven by ambition fueled by love, Rodolphe sought to make his fortune, investing all his resources in a venture that ultimately failed due to deceit, costing him three years of struggle and perseverance. The Villele ministry fell just as Rodolphe faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but first, he traveled to Naples to heal his wounds and regain his courage at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their positions. This respite brought him joy as he spent three months there, filled with hope.

Rodolphe then attempted to rebuild his fortune. His talents were recognized, and a high position was promised as a reward for his dedication and past efforts. However, the storm of July 1830 struck, thwarting his ambitions once again. 

Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts and daring attempts of this young man, endowed with fine qualities but lacking the favor of fortune. Despite the setbacks, Rodolphe, sustained by love, returned to face new challenges, guided by an ever-supportive presence and a faithful heart.

Lovers! Pray for him!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As Mademoiselle de Watteville concluded her narrative, her cheeks burned with rage, and tears streamed down her face. This novel, reflecting the popular literary style of the time, was Rosalie's first encounter with such a story. It depicted love through the eyes of a man sharing his own experiences, stirring deep emotions within her. Rosalie's agitation stemmed from jealousy towards Francesca Colonna, the woman she believed was Albert's love interest.

Rosalie was consumed by curiosity and felt a fierce desire to fight for Albert, whom she loved, despite her insecurities about her lack of beauty and musical talent. She feared he would never love her back, which intensified her need to uncover the truth about his affections. That night, her imagination ran wild with schemes typical of young girls confined by strict upbringing. She fantasized about sneaking into Albert's garden or writing to him, even considering a visit to her father's land dispute to lure him into her drawing-room.

As she gazed out at Albert's window, she witnessed a scene that could provide insight into his secrets: Jerome, Albert's servant, was being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie recognized Mariette's ugliness but also her financial stability, which made her attractive to Jerome. This revelation sparked a dangerous lesson for Rosalie, as the strict upbringing her mother enforced was easily undermined by a servant's actions.

The next morning, Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the opportunity to question her about Jerome. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, in a surprising turn, offered her support in exchange for access to the letters Albert sent through Jerome. Mariette, relieved by Rosalie's discretion, agreed to the arrangement.

Rosalie's obsession with Albert deepened as she read a letter from him to his friend Leopold, revealing his noble lineage and past struggles. She became enthralled by his ambitions and the depth of his character, which only intensified her feelings for him. The letter painted a picture of a man striving for success while remaining devoted to the woman he loved, Francesca.

As Rosalie continued to read Albert's correspondence, she became increasingly aware of her own feelings of inadequacy and the moral implications of her actions. She wrestled with guilt over invading Albert's privacy but justified it as a testament to her love. Her passion morphed into a dangerous infatuation, leading her to penances and self-imposed suffering in hopes of proving her devotion.

One evening, Mariette delivered another letter from Albert to Francesca, which sent Rosalie into a spiral of jealousy and self-doubt. She grappled with the decision to read the letter, ultimately succumbing to her desire for knowledge about Albert's feelings. The letter revealed Albert's ongoing love for Francesca, detailing his ambitions and the hope of a future together.

Rosalie's heart ached as she absorbed the contents of the letter, realizing the depth of Albert's love for another woman. Despite her initial resolve to respect their relationship, she found herself torn between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing for love. In a moment of clarity, she resolved to step back, recognizing the nobility of their bond and her own shortcomings.

As she prepared to send the letter back with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her feelings and the nature of love. She felt a mix of sorrow and resignation, understanding that true love often requires sacrifice. In her heart, she yearned for the kind of love Albert and Francesca shared, while grappling with the reality of her own unrequited feelings.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Rosalie spent the winter of 1834-35 in turmoil, but by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. In her solitude, this struggle fueled her passion and dark thoughts, leading her to devise numerous plans. Unfortunately, many young women like Rosalie exist, and this story serves as a cautionary tale for them.

During that winter, Albert de Savarus made significant strides in Besancon, eagerly anticipating the dissolution of the Chamber. He had garnered support from a wealthy contractor influential in the moderate party. The Romans had historically invested heavily in ensuring a good water supply for their towns, and Besancon sourced its water from Arcier, a distant hill. The idea of restoring an aqueduct to drink the same water as the Romans seemed absurd, especially in a town already served by the Doubs River. However, if the citizens could be convinced, it would lead to substantial expenses benefiting the contractor.

Savarus believed the river water was unfit for drinking, advocating instead for the Arcier water. Articles in the local _Review_ echoed this sentiment, and the townspeople, regardless of their political affiliations, agreed on the need to drink the same water as the Romans. This issue became a focal point in Besancon, driven by hidden interests. Those opposing the scheme were dismissed as old-fashioned, while Savarus’s projects dominated discussions. After eighteen months of effort, he had stirred the stagnant town, gaining influence without being popular.

Throughout the winter, he won several lawsuits for local priests, and his ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in financial gain was, in reality, a form of moral usury, as he anticipated a much greater reward.

In October 1834, he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Leopold Hannequin, which also provided him with a qualification for election. The Abbe de Grancey, who understood Savarus’s ambitions, remarked on his remarkable nature, suggesting he had taken a wrong turn in life.

Meanwhile, Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hotel de Rupt, initially content to observe him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, was worth ten thousand francs a year, but its management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant named Modinier. The Baron's indifference stemmed from his wife's substantial income, and they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque location created by the famous Watteville.

Watteville constructed a dam and lake, enhancing the beauty of the area, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, which had long enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.

Rosalie’s efforts to achieve her goals were complex and subtle. In May 1835, she left Besancon with her father for les Rouxey, where she was captivated by the lake’s beauty, associating it with love. She suggested a boat trip to explore the lake, during which her father confided about legal troubles with the village of Riceys over land rights. Rosalie encouraged him to hire Savarus as a lawyer, emphasizing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate.

She argued that a lawsuit would add excitement to their lives and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey. Rosalie’s determination to secure her future at les Rouxey was evident, as she envisioned creating beautiful gardens there.

Four days later, the Abbe de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. However, Savarus was hesitant to align himself with them, fearing it would compromise his neutrality. The Abbe urged him to consider the political advantages of supporting the Wattevilles, especially given the potential for votes.

Rosalie, meanwhile, was determined to keep Savarus in Besancon and prevent his election. She wrote a letter to the Prefet, revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the Prefet disregarded it. Rosalie understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it to her advantage.

As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besancon decided to nominate Savarus as a candidate, much to his surprise. He was initially unaware of the political machinations at play. The Abbe de Grancey, who had been observing the situation, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.

Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, captivating his audience. However, he faced challenges as the Prefet worked to undermine his support, leading to a decline in his voter base. Despite his efforts, the political landscape shifted against him, and he struggled to maintain his position.

On the day of the first voting, the results were disappointing for Savarus, who felt the weight of his ambitions and the pressure of the election. He returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.

Just as he was about to succumb to hopelessness, the Abbe de Grancey arrived with news of hope. The priest had been working behind the scenes to secure support for Savarus, urging him to visit the Wattevilles and promising that their case would be resolved in time for the election.

The Abbe revealed that by winning the Rouxey lawsuit, Savarus could secure crucial votes, and he encouraged him to pursue a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to further solidify his political standing. Savarus was initially resistant but realized the potential benefits of such a union.

The Abbe’s guidance proved invaluable, and Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, understanding that his future depended on the choices he made in the coming days.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By nine o’clock the following evening, Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were filled with the aristocracy of Besançon, gathered to discuss the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. It was known that the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, would be presented that evening. Madame de Chavoncourt attended with her younger daughter Sidonie, who was elegantly dressed, while her elder sister, confident in her relationship, had not made an effort with her appearance. In small towns, such details are noted. The Abbe de Grancey moved among the groups, listening intently and offering incisive comments that shaped the conversation.

“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” The statesman replied that Berryer, alone on his bench, was uncertain and could disrupt the Government if he had enough support. They discussed the nomination of the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse and how voting for Monsieur Savarus could align Republicans with them instead of the Moderates.

By nine-thirty, Albert had still not arrived, and Madame de Watteville began to view his tardiness as disrespectful. Madame de Chavoncourt advised her not to let such trivial matters affect serious issues, suggesting that Albert might be delayed by a consultation. Rosalie, observing her mother’s exchange with Madame de Chavoncourt, noted her leniency towards Albert, hinting at a potential marriage between Sidonie and him.

At ten o’clock, Albert was still absent, and frustration grew among the guests. Some gentlemen began playing cards, while the Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to the window where Rosalie was hidden, exclaiming in disbelief, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, along with Monsieur de Watteville and Rosalie, ventured into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.

“Jerome!” Rosalie called to the servant below. When he confirmed that Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe de Grancey exclaimed, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s expression betrayed her joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended to ignore. He wondered about her involvement in the situation.

Returning to the drawing-room, Monsieur de Watteville shared the shocking news of Albert’s departure without explanation. By half-past eleven, only fifteen guests remained, including Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas, and a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election. The Abbe de Grancey positioned himself near the Baroness to observe Rosalie, whose usually pale face now showed signs of agitation.

“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt wondered aloud. At that moment, a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did, he noticed Rosalie turn pale, recognizing the handwriting. He folded the letter and discreetly pocketed it, having exchanged glances with Rosalie that hinted at her feelings.

“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie intercepted him, saying, “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” When he questioned how she recognized the writing, her passionate response revealed her feelings: “I love him! What is the matter?” He informed her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Rosalie, taken aback, asked him to keep this information confidential, realizing that if the election ended, so too would the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In the morning, on her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette about the circumstances surrounding Albert’s sudden disappearance. An old gentleman from Paris had arrived at the Hotel National, prompting a visit to Monsieur Savaron. The lawyer turned pale upon seeing the gentleman's card, and they met for over an hour. Afterward, the gentleman left with a large package, while Savaron appeared distressed. He then went to the Prefecture, purchased an old carriage, and ordered post horses, leaving behind his keys and telling his servant to find another job.

The next day, news of Savaron's disappearance caused a stir in Besançon, with the Prefect quickly granting him a passport. This event fueled local prejudices against outsiders, and soon Albert was forgotten by most, except for Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo's death.

By April 1836, there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville suggested Rosalie should marry, as she had changed significantly. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness was concerned about the financial implications. They decided on a marriage with Monsieur de Soulas, but Rosalie firmly rejected the idea, insisting she was happy as she was.

The conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, leading to a summer spent at les Rouxey, where Rosalie's refusal of the marriage became known. After Mariette and Jerome married, the Baron made improvements to the estate, which the Baroness viewed with resentment. As tensions grew, the Vicar-General attempted to mediate between mother and daughter.

One day, Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had interfered in Albert's relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo. She had intercepted letters, leading to their separation. The Vicar-General was horrified by her actions, emphasizing the moral weight of her crimes. He urged her to seek forgiveness and to understand the consequences of her actions.

Rosalie's guilt deepened when she learned of Albert's fate. A clerk from Paris, representing Albert, sought to sell his belongings, revealing that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery, receiving a letter from Albert expressing his complete forgiveness for Rosalie and his desire to remain in solitude.

As the Vicar-General shared this news with Rosalie, he urged her to reconcile with her mother and marry Monsieur de Soulas. However, Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to destroy her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General's death left a void, and soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie was supportive.

Rosalie, now legally of age, encouraged her mother to marry, securing her own financial independence. With a substantial fortune, Rosalie entered Parisian society, where she sought to confront the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball, she publicly revealed her role in Albert's downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.

Rosalie returned to Besançon, having achieved her goal of exposing the truth, but the emotional toll of her actions weighed heavily on her. The story of love, betrayal, and the consequences of ambition unfolded, leaving a lasting impact on all involved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her estate of les Rouxey, engaging in riding, hunting, and land improvement, which made her a notable figure in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, who has two children, appears younger, while her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has aged significantly. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”

Mademoiselle de Watteville is known for her eccentric behavior, with people noting her “vagaries.” Each year, she visits the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, possibly inspired by her grand-uncle, who broke through monastery walls to find freedom. In 1841, she left Besancon, reportedly to marry, but the true reason remains unclear. She returned home after a tragic accident on a steamboat on the Loire, where a boiler explosion severely injured her, resulting in the loss of her right arm and left leg, along with disfiguring scars on her face. Her health deteriorated, leaving her in constant pain, and she now lives a life devoted to religious practices at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.

PARIS, May 1842.

ADDENDUM

- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########

Produced by John Bickers and Dagny

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

By Honore de Balzac

Translated by R. S. Scott

DEDICATION

To George Sand

Your name, dear George, adds a reflected radiance to my book, not for self-interest or diffidence, but to witness our enduring friendship, which has triumphed over the world's malice. This bond is unlikely to change. The names attached to my works bring me pleasure amidst the vexation of their increasing number. Each new book brings fresh annoyance, as if my pen could rival the world from which I draw my models! Would it not be wonderful if future antiquarians found only great names and generous hearts among my friends? I take pride in this possession rather than in unstable popularity. For those who know you well, it is happiness to sign myself, as I do here,

Your friend,  
DE BALZAC.  
PARIS, June 1840.

LETTERS OF TWO BRIDES

FIRST PART

I. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.

Sweetheart, I too am free! I am the first to write, unless you have already sent a letter to Blois.

Raise those great black eyes of yours, fixed on my opening sentence, and save your excitement for the letter that will tell you of my first love. By the way, why always "first?" Is there a second love?

Don't run on like this, you will say, but tell me how you escaped from the convent where you were to take your vows. My own deliverance was quite ordinary. The cries of an alarmed conscience triumphed over stern policy. The melancholy that seized me after you left hastened my aunt's decision; she did not want to see me die of decline, and my mother, whose cure for my malady was a novitiate, relented.

So I am in Paris, thanks to you, my love! If you had seen me the day I parted from you, you would have gloried in the deep impression you made on my youthful heart. We had lived so closely together, sharing dreams and letting our fancies roam, that I believe our souls became welded together, like those two Hungarian girls whose tragic story we heard from M. Beauvisage—poor man! Never was anyone better suited to the role of convent physician!

Tell me, did you not droop and sicken without your darling?

In my gloomy depression, I could only count the ties that bind us. It seemed distance had loosened them; I wearied of life, like a turtle-dove widowed of her mate. Death smiled sweetly on me, and I was quietly proceeding to die. To be at Blois, at the Carmelites, consumed by dread of taking my vows there, a Mlle. de la Valliere without her prelude, and without you! How could I not be sick—sick unto death?

How different it used to be! That monotonous existence, where every hour brings its duty, prayer, and task, with such desperate regularity that you could tell what a Carmelite sister was doing at any hour, had become for us a world of life and movement. Imagination opened her fairy realms, and our spirits roamed freely, each serving as a magic steed to the other. The world from which our bodies were shut out became our playground, where we reveled in frolicsome adventure. Even the _Lives of the Saints_ helped us understand what was left unsaid! But the day I lost your sweet company, I became a true Carmelite, like a modern Danaid, drawing an empty pitcher from Heaven knows where, thinking to find it full.

My aunt knew nothing of this inner life. How could she, who has made a paradise for herself within the two acres of her convent, understand my revolt against life? A religious life, if embraced by girls of our age, demands either extreme simplicity of soul, which we do not possess, or an ardor for self-sacrifice like my aunt's noble character. But she sacrificed herself for a devoted brother; to do the same for an unknown person or an idea is surely more than can be asked of mortals.

For the last fortnight, I have been swallowing reckless words, burying reflections in my bosom, and accumulating a store of things to tell you, fit for your ear alone. I would have suffocated without the resource of letter-writing as a poor substitute for our beloved talks. How hungry one's heart gets! I am beginning my journal this morning, picturing that yours is already started, and that soon I shall be at home in your beautiful Gemenos valley, which I know only through your descriptions, just as you will live that Paris life revealed to you only in our dreams.

Well, sweet child, know that on a certain morning—a red-letter day in my life—a lady companion and Philippe, the last of my grandmother's valets, arrived from Paris to carry me off. When my aunt summoned me to her room and told me the news, I could not speak for joy, only gazed at her stupidly.

"My child," she said, "I can see you leave me without regret, but this farewell is not the last; we shall meet again. God has placed on your forehead the sign of the elect. You have the pride that leads to heaven or hell, but your nature is too noble to choose the downward path. I know you better than you know yourself; with you, passion will be very different from what it is with most women."

She drew me gently to her and kissed my forehead. The kiss burned with the consuming fire that has turned her life to black, softened the lines around her eyes, and cast a sallow tinge over her beautiful face.

Before replying, I kissed her hands.

"Dear aunt," I said, "I shall never forget your kindness; and if it has not made your nunnery all that it ought to be for my health of body and soul, you may be sure nothing short of a broken heart will bring me back again—and that you would not wish for me. You will not see me here again till my royal lover has deserted me, and I warn you that if I catch him, death alone shall tear him from me. I fear no Montespan."

She smiled and said:

"Go, madcap, and take your idle fancies with you. There is certainly more of the bold Montespan in you than of the gentle la Valliere."

I threw my arms around her. The poor lady could not refrain from escorting me to the carriage. There her tender gaze was divided between me and the armorial bearings.

At Beaugency, night overtook me, still sunk in a stupor of mind produced by these strange parting words. What awaits me in this world for which I have so hungered?

To begin with, I found no one to receive me; my heart had been schooled in vain. My mother was at the Bois de Boulogne, my father at the Council; my brother, the Duc de Rhetore, never comes in until it is time to dress for dinner. Miss Griffith (she resembles a griffin) and Philippe took me to my rooms.

The suite belonged to my beloved grandmother, the Princess de Vauremont, to whom I owe some fortune no one has ever mentioned. As you read this, you will understand the sadness that came over me as I entered a place sacred to so many memories, finding the rooms just as she had left them! I was to sleep in the bed where she died.

Sitting on the edge of the sofa, I burst into tears, forgetting I was not alone, remembering how often I had stood there by her knees to hear her words. I had gazed upon her face, buried in brown lace, worn by age and the pangs of approaching death. The room seemed still warm with her presence. How is it that Armande-Louise-Marie de Chaulieu must be like a peasant girl, who sleeps in her mother's bed the very morrow of her death? For me, it was as though the Princess, who died in 1817, had passed away but yesterday.

I saw many things in the room that ought to have been removed. Their presence showed the carelessness with which busy people treat their own affairs, and the little thought given since her death to this grand old lady, a striking figure of the eighteenth century. Philippe seemed to sense my tears. He told me that the Princess's furniture had been left to me in her will, and that my father had allowed the larger suites to remain dismantled, as the Revolution had left them. Upon hearing this, I rose, and Philippe opened the door to the small drawing-room leading into the reception-rooms.

In these, I found all the well-remembered wreckage; the panels above the doors, which had contained valuable pictures, bare of all but empty frames; broken marbles, mirrors carried off. In old days, I was afraid to go up the state staircase and cross these vast, deserted rooms; I used to get to the Princess's rooms by a small staircase that runs under the arch of the larger one, leading to the secret door of her dressing-room.

My suite, consisting of a drawing-room, bedroom, and pretty morning-room in scarlet and gold, lies in the wing on the side of the Invalides. The house is separated from the boulevard by a wall covered with creepers and a splendid avenue of trees, mingling their foliage with that of the young elms on the sidewalk. But for the blue-and-gold dome of the Invalides and its gray stone mass, you might be in a wood.

The style of decoration in these rooms, along with their situation, indicates they were the old show suite of the duchesses, while the dukes must have had theirs in the opposite wing. The two suites are decorously separated by the two main blocks, as well as by the central one, which contained those vast, gloomy, resounding halls shown to me by Philippe, all despoiled of their splendor, as in my childhood.

Philippe grew quite confidential when he saw my surprise. In this home of diplomacy, even the servants have a reserved and mysterious air. He told me that a law would soon be passed restoring the value of property to the fugitives of the Revolution, and that my father is waiting to renovate the house until this restitution is made, the king's architect having estimated the damage at three hundred thousand livres.

This news flung me back despairing on my drawing-room sofa. Could it be that my father, instead of spending this money arranging a marriage for me, would have left me to die in the convent? This was the first thought to greet me on the threshold of my home.

Ah! Renee, what would I have given then to rest my head upon your shoulder, or to return to the days when my grandmother filled these rooms with life? You two in all the world have loved me—one away at Maucombe, and the other who survives only in my heart, the dear old lady, whose still youthful eyes opened from sleep at my call. How well we understood each other!

These memories suddenly changed my mood. What had seemed profanation now breathed of holy association. It was sweet to inhale the faint odor of the powder she loved still lingering in the room; sweet to sleep beneath the shelter of those yellow damask curtains, which must have retained something of her spirit. I told Philippe to polish the old furniture and make the rooms look lived in; I explained how I wanted everything arranged, and where to place each piece. In this way, I entered into possession, showing how an air of youth might be given to the dear old things.

The bedroom is white, a little dulled with time, harmonizing well with the faded colors of the Savonnerie tapestry, presented to my grandmother by Louis XV. The timepiece was a gift from the Marechal de Saxe, and the china ornaments on the mantelpiece came from the Marechal de Richelieu. My grandmother's portrait, painted at twenty-five, hangs in an oval frame opposite that of the King. The Prince, her husband, is conspicuous by his absence. I like this frank negligence, untinged by hypocrisy—a characteristic touch summing up her charming personality. Once, when my grandmother was seriously ill, her confessor urged that the Prince, waiting in the drawing-room, should be admitted.

"He can come in with the doctor and his drugs," was her reply.

The bed has a canopy and well-stuffed back, with curtains looped up with fine wide bands. The furniture is gilded wood, upholstered in the same yellow damask with white flowers that drapes the windows, lined with white silk that looks watered. The panels over the doors have been painted, representing a sunrise and a moonlight scene.

The fireplace is a very interesting feature. Life in the last century centered around the hearth, where great events were enacted. The copper gilt grate is a marvel of workmanship, and the mantelpiece is delicately finished; the fire-irons are beautifully chased; the bellows are a perfect gem. The tapestry of the screen comes from the Gobelins and is exquisitely mounted; charming figures run all over the frame, on the feet, the supporting bar, and the wings; the whole thing is wrought like a fan.

Dearly would I like to know who was the giver of this dainty work of art, which was such a favorite with her. How often have I seen the old lady, her feet upon the bar, reclining in the easy-chair, toying with the snuff-box, which lay upon the ledge between her box of pastilles and her silk mitts. What a coquette she was! To the day of her death, she took as much care with her appearance as though the beautiful portrait had been painted only yesterday, and she were waiting to receive the throng of exquisites from the Court! How the armchair recalls the inimitable sweep of her skirts as she sank back in it!

These women of a past generation carried off secrets typical of their age. The Princess had a certain turn of the head, a way of dropping her glance and remarks, a choice of words, which I look for in vain, even in my mother. There was subtlety and good nature; the points were made without affectation. Her talk was lengthy yet concise; she told a good story and could express her meaning in three words. Above all, she was extremely free-thinking, which has undoubtedly influenced my perspective.

From seven to ten, I never left her side; it pleased her to attract me as much as it pleased me to go. This preference caused more than one passage at arms between her and my mother, and nothing intensifies feeling like the icy breath of persecution. How charming was her greeting, "Here you are, little rogue!" when curiosity taught me to glide stealthily to her room. She felt that I loved her, and this childish affection was welcome as a ray of sunshine in the winter of her life.

I don't know what went on in her rooms at night, but she had many visitors; when I tiptoed in the morning to see if she were awake, I would find the drawing-room furniture disarranged, card-tables set out, and patches of snuff scattered about.

This drawing-room is furnished in the same style as the bedroom. The chairs and tables are oddly shaped, with claw feet and hollow moldings. Rich garlands of flowers, beautifully designed and carved, wind over the mirrors and hang down in festoons. On the consoles are fine china vases. The ground colors are scarlet and white. My grandmother was a high-spirited, striking brunette, as might be inferred from her choice of colors. I have found in the drawing-room a writing-table I remember well; the figures on it used to fascinate me; it is plaited in graven silver, a present from one of the Genoese Lomellini. Each side of the table represents the occupations of a different season, with hundreds of figures in relief.

I remained alone for two hours, while old memories rose before me, one after another, in this spot hallowed by the death of a remarkable woman among the witty and beautiful Court ladies of Louis XV.'s day.

You know how abruptly I was parted from her, at a day's notice, in 1816.

"Go and bid good-bye to your grandmother," said my mother.

The Princess received me as usual, without display of feeling, expressing no surprise at my departure.

"You are going to the convent, dear," she said, "and will see your aunt there, who is an excellent woman. I shall take care, though, that they don't make a victim of you; you shall be independent and able to marry whom you please."

Six months later, she died. Her will was given into the keeping of the Prince de Talleyrand, her most devoted friend. He contrived, while visiting Mlle. de Chargeboeuf, to intimate through her that my grandmother forbade me to take the vows. I hope to meet the Prince soon, and then I shall learn more from him.

Thus, sweetheart, if I have found no one in flesh and blood to meet me, I have comforted myself with the shade of the dear Princess and prepared to carry out one of our pledges—to keep each other informed of the smallest details in our homes and occupations. It makes such a difference to know where and how the life of one we love is passed. Send me a faithful picture of the veriest trifles around you, omitting nothing, not even the sunset lights among the tall trees.

October 19th.

It was three in the afternoon when I arrived. About half-past five, Rose came and told me that my mother had returned, so I went downstairs to pay my respects to her.

My mother lives in a suite on the ground floor, exactly corresponding to mine, in the same block. I am just over her head, and the same secret staircase serves for both. My father's rooms are in the opposite block, larger by the space occupied by the grand staircase on our side. These ancestral mansions are so spacious that my parents continue to occupy the ground-floor rooms, despite the social duties that have returned with the Bourbons, and can even receive guests there.

I found my mother, dressed for the evening, in her drawing-room, where nothing had changed. I came slowly down the stairs, speculating how I would be met by this mother who had shown so little maternal affection, from whom I had heard nothing beyond the two letters you know. Judging it unworthy to simulate an affection I could not feel, I put on the air of a pious imbecile and entered the room with many inward qualms, which soon disappeared. My mother’s tact was equal to the occasion. She made no pretense of emotion; she neither held me at arm's length nor hugged me to her bosom like a beloved daughter, but greeted me as though we had parted the evening before. Her manner was that of the kindliest and most sincere friend, addressing me like a grown person, first kissing me on the forehead.

"My dear little one," she said, "if you were to die at the convent, it is much better to live with your family. You frustrate your father's plans and mine; but the age of blind obedience to parents is past. M. de Chaulieu's intention, and in this I am quite at one with him, is to lose no opportunity of making your life pleasant and letting you see the world. At your age, I would have thought as you do, therefore I am not vexed with you; it is impossible you should understand what we expected from you. You will not find any absurd severity in me; and if you have ever thought me heartless, you will soon find out your mistake. Still, though I wish you to feel perfectly free, I think that, to begin with, you would do well to follow the counsels of a mother who wishes to be a sister to you."

I was charmed by the Duchess, who spoke gently, straightening my convent tippet as she did. At thirty-eight, she is still exquisitely beautiful. She has dark-blue eyes, silken lashes, a smooth forehead, and a complexion so pink and white that you might think she paints. Her bust and shoulders are marvelous, and her waist is as slender as yours. Her hand is milk-white and extraordinarily beautiful; the nails catch the light in their perfect polish, the thumb is like ivory, the little finger stands just a little apart from the rest, and the foot matches the hand; it is the Spanish foot of Mlle. de Vandenesse. If she is like this at forty, she will still be beautiful at sixty.

I replied, sweetheart, like a good little girl. I was as nice to her as she was to me, nay, nicer. Her beauty completely vanquished me; it seemed only natural that such a woman should be absorbed in her regal part. I told her this as simply as if I were talking to you. I daresay it surprised her to hear words of affection from her daughter's mouth, and the unfeigned homage of my admiration evidently touched her deeply. Her manner changed and became even more engaging; she dropped all formality as she said:

"I am much pleased with you, and I hope we shall remain good friends."

The words struck me as charmingly naive, but I did not let this appear, for I saw at once that the prudent course was to allow her to believe herself much deeper and cleverer than her daughter. So I only stared vacantly, and she was delighted. I kissed her hands repeatedly, telling her how happy it made me to be treated so and to feel at ease with her. I even confided my previous tremors. She smiled, put her arm around my neck, and drawing me towards her, kissed me on the forehead most affectionately.

"Dear child," she said, "we have people coming to dinner today. Perhaps you will agree with me that it is better for you not to make your first appearance in society until you have been in the dressmaker's hands; so, after you have seen your father and brother, you can go upstairs again."

I assented most heartily. My mother's exquisite dress was the first revelation to me of the world our dreams had pictured; but I did not feel the slightest desire to rival her.

My father now entered, and the Duchess presented me to him.

He became all at once most affectionate, playing the father's part so well that I could not but believe his heart was in it. Taking my two hands in his and kissing them, with more of the lover than the father in his manner, he said:

"So this is my rebel daughter!"

He drew me towards him, with his arm passed tenderly around my waist, kissing me on the cheeks and forehead.

"The pleasure with which we shall watch your success in society will atone for the disappointment we felt at your change of vocation," he said. Then, turning to my mother, "Do you know that she is going to turn out very pretty, and you will be proud of her some day?—Here is your brother, Rhetore." He introduced a fine young man who came in, "Alphonse, here is your convent-bred sister, who threatens to send her nun's frock to the deuce."

My brother came up leisurely and took my hand, which he pressed.

"Come, come, you may kiss her," said my father.

And he kissed me on both cheeks.

"I am delighted to see you," he said, "and I take your side against my father."

I thanked him, but could not help thinking he might have come to Blois when he was at Orleans visiting our Marquis brother in his quarters.

Fearing the arrival of strangers, I now withdrew. I tidied up my rooms and laid out on the scarlet velvet of my lovely table all the materials necessary for writing to you, meditating all the while on my new situation.

This, my fair sweetheart, is a true and veracious account of the return of a girl of eighteen, after an absence of nine years, to the bosom of one of the noblest families in the kingdom. I was tired from the journey and all the emotions I had been through, so I went to bed in convent fashion, at eight o'clock after supper. They have preserved even a little Saxe service which the dear Princess used when she had a fancy for taking her meals alone.

II. THE SAME TO THE SAME November 25th.

The next day, I found my rooms done out and dusted, and even flowers put in the vases by old Philippe. I began to feel at home. Only it didn't occur to anyone that a Carmelite schoolgirl has an early appetite, and Rose had no end of trouble getting breakfast for me.

"Mlle. goes to bed at dinner-time," she said, "and gets up when the Duke is just returning home."

I began to write. About one o'clock, my father knocked at the door of the small drawing-room and asked if he might come in. I opened the door; he came in and found me writing to you.

"My dear," he began, "you will have to get yourself clothes and make these rooms comfortable. In this purse, you will find twelve thousand francs, which is the yearly income I purpose allowing you for your expenses. You will make arrangements with your mother for a governess you may like, in case Miss Griffith doesn't please you, for Mme. de Chaulieu will not have time to go out with you in the mornings. A carriage and man-servant shall be at your disposal."

"Let me keep Philippe," I said.

"So be it," he replied. "But don't be uneasy; you have money enough of your own to be no burden either to your mother or me."

"May I ask how much I have?"

"Certainly, my child," he said. "Your grandmother left you five hundred thousand francs; this was the amount of her savings, for she would not alienate a foot of land from the family. This sum has been placed in Government stock, and, with the accumulated interest, now brings in about forty thousand francs a year. With this, I had purposed making an independence for your second brother, and it is here that you have upset my plans. Later, however, it is possible that you may fall in with them. It shall rest with yourself, for I have confidence in your good sense far more than I had expected.

"I do not need to tell you how a daughter of the Chaulieus ought to behave. The pride so plainly written in your features is my best guarantee. Safeguards, such as common folk surround their daughters with, would be an insult in our family. A slander reflecting on your name might cost the life of the man bold enough to utter it, or the life of one of your brothers, if by chance the right should not prevail. No more on this subject. Good-bye, little one."

He kissed me on the forehead and went out. I cannot understand the relinquishment of this plan after nine years' persistence in it. My father's frankness is what I like. There is no ambiguity about his words. My money ought to belong to his Marquis son. Who, then, has had bowels of mercy? My mother? My father? Or could it be my brother?

I remained sitting on my grandmother's sofa, staring at the purse my father had left on the mantelpiece, pleased and vexed that I could not withdraw my mind from the money. Further speculation was useless. My doubts had been cleared up, and there was something fine in the way my pride was spared.

Philippe has spent the morning rushing about among the various shops and workpeople who are to undertake the task of my metamorphosis. A famous dressmaker, by name Victorine, has come, as well as a woman for underclothing, and a shoemaker. I am as impatient as a child to know what I shall be like when I emerge from the sack that constituted the conventual uniform; but all these tradespeople take a long time; the corset-maker requires a whole week if my figure is not to be spoiled. You see, I have a figure, dear; this becomes serious. Janssen, the Operatic shoemaker, solemnly assures me that I have my mother's foot. The whole morning has gone in these weighty occupations. Even a glovemaker has come to take the measure of my hand. The underclothing woman has got my orders.

At the meal I call dinner, and the others lunch, my mother told me that we were going together to the milliner's to see some hats, so that my taste should be formed, and I might be in a position to order my own.

This burst of independence dazzles me. I am like a blind man who has just recovered his sight. Now I begin to understand the vast interval separating a Carmelite sister from a girl in society. Of ourselves, we could never have conceived it.

During this lunch, my father seemed absent-minded, and we left him to his thoughts; he is deep in the King's confidence. I was entirely forgotten; but from what I have seen, I have no doubt he will remember me when he has need of me. He is a very attractive man in spite of his fifty years. His figure is youthful; he is well made, fair, and extremely graceful in his movements. He has a diplomatic face, at once dumb and expressive; his nose is long and slender, and he has brown eyes.

What a handsome pair! Strange thoughts assail me as it becomes plain to me that these two, so perfectly matched in birth, wealth, and mental superiority, live entirely apart, having nothing in common but their name. The show of unity is only for the world.

The cream of the Court and diplomatic circles were here last night. Very soon, I am going to a ball given by the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and I shall be presented to the society I am so eager to know. A dancing-master is coming every morning to give me lessons, for I must be able to dance in a month, or I can't go to the ball.

Before dinner, my mother came to talk about the governess with me. I have decided to keep Miss Griffith, who was recommended by the English ambassador. Miss Griffith is the daughter of a clergyman; her mother was of good family, and she is perfectly well bred. She is thirty-six and will teach me English. The good soul is quite handsome enough to have ambitions; she is Scotch—poor and proud—and will act as my chaperon. She is to sleep in Rose's room. Rose will be under her orders. I saw at a glance that my governess would be governed by me. In the six days we have been together, she has made sure that I am the only person likely to take an interest in her; while, for my part, I have ascertained that, for all her statuesque features, she will prove accommodating. She seems to me a kindly soul, but cautious. I have not been able to extract a word of what passed between her and my mother.

Another trifling piece of news! My father has this morning refused the appointment as Minister of State which was offered him. This accounts for his preoccupied manner last night. He says he would prefer an embassy to the worries of public debate.

This news was told to me at lunch, the one moment of the day when my father, mother, and brother see each other in an easy way. The servants then only come when they are rung for. The rest of the day, my brother, as well as my father, spends out of the house. My mother has her toilet to make; between two and four, she is never visible; at four o'clock, she goes out for an hour's drive; when she is not dining out, she receives from six to seven, and the evening is given to entertainments of various kinds—theatres, balls, concerts, at homes. In short, her life is so full that I don't believe she ever has a quarter of an hour to herself. She must spend considerable time dressing in the morning; for at lunch, which takes place between eleven and twelve, she is exquisite. The meaning of the things said about her is dawning on me. She begins the day with a bath barely warmed and a cup of cold coffee with cream; then she dresses. She is never, except on some great emergency, called before nine o'clock. In summer, there are morning rides, and at two o'clock, she receives a young man whom I have never yet contrived to see.

Behold our family life! We meet at lunch and dinner, though often I am alone with my mother at this latter meal, and I foresee that still oftener I shall take it in my own rooms (following the example of my grandmother) with only Miss Griffith for company, for my mother frequently dines out. I have ceased to wonder at the indifference my family has shown to me. In Paris, my dear, it is a miracle of virtue to love the people who live with you, for you see little enough of them; as for the absent—they do not exist!

Knowing as this may sound, I have not yet set foot in the streets, and am deplorably ignorant. I must wait until I am less of the country cousin and have brought my dress and deportment into keeping with the society I am about to enter, the whirl of which amazes me even here, where only distant murmurs reach my ear. So far, I have not gone beyond the garden; but the Italian opera opens in a few days, and my mother has a box there. I am crazy with delight at the thought of hearing Italian music and seeing French acting.

Already I begin to drop convent habits for those of society. I spend the evening writing to you until the moment for going to bed arrives. This has been postponed to ten o'clock, the hour at which my mother goes out, if she is not at the theatre. There are twelve theatres in Paris.

I am grossly ignorant and I read a lot, but quite indiscriminately, one book leading to another. I find the names of fresh books on the cover of the one I am reading; but as I have no one to direct me, I light on some that are fearfully dull. What modern literature I have read all turns upon love, the subject that used to bulk so largely in our thoughts, because it seemed our fate was determined by man and for man. But how inferior are these authors to two little girls, known as Sweetheart and Darling—otherwise Renee and Louise. Ah! my love, what wretched plots, what ridiculous situations, and what poverty of sentiment! Two books, however, have given me wonderful pleasure—_Corinne_ and _Adolphe_. Apropos of this, I asked my father one day whether it would be possible for me to see Mme. de Stael. My father, mother, and Alphonse all burst out laughing, and Alphonse said:

"Where in the world has she sprung from?"

To which my father replied:

"What fools we are! She springs from the Carmelites."

"My child, Mme. de Stael is dead," said my mother gently.

When I finished _Adolphe_, I asked Miss Griffith how a woman could be betrayed.

"Why, of course, when she loves," was her reply.

Renee, tell me, do you think we could be betrayed by a man?

Miss Griffith has at last discerned that I am not an utter ignoramus, that I have somewhere a hidden vein of knowledge, the knowledge we learned from each other in our random arguments. She sees that it is only superficial facts of which I am ignorant. The poor thing has opened her heart to me. Her curt reply to my question, when I compare it with all the sorrows I can imagine, makes me feel quite creepy. Once more, she urged me not to be dazzled by the glitter of society, to be always on my guard, especially against what most attracts me. This is the sum-total of her wisdom, and I can get nothing more out of her. Her lectures, therefore, become a trifle monotonous, and she might be compared in this respect to the bird which has only one cry.

III. THE SAME TO THE SAME December.

My Darling,—Here I am ready to make my bow to the world. By way of preparation, I have been trying to commit all the follies I could think of before sobering down for my entry. This morning, I have seen myself, after many rehearsals, well and duly equipped—stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments—all in order. Following the example of duelists before a meeting, I tried my arms in the privacy of my chamber. I wanted to see how I would look and had no difficulty discovering a certain air of victory and triumph, bound to carry all before it. I mustered all my forces, in accordance with that splendid maxim of antiquity, "Know thyself!" and boundless was my delight in thus making my own acquaintance. Griffith was the sole spectator of this doll's play, in which I was at once doll and child. You think you know me? You are hugely mistaken.

Here is a portrait, then, Renee, of your sister, formerly disguised as a Carmelite, now brought to life again as a frivolous society girl. She is one of the greatest beauties in France—Provence, of course, excepted. I don't see how I can give a more accurate summary of this interesting topic.

True, I have my weak points; but were I a man, I should adore them. They arise from what is most promising in me. When you have spent a fortnight admiring the exquisite curves of your mother's arms, and that mother the Duchesse de Chaulieu, it is impossible, my dear, not to deplore your own angular elbows. Yet there is consolation in observing the fineness of the wrist and a certain grace of line in those hollows, which will yet fill out and show plump, round, and well-modeled under the satiny skin. The somewhat crude outline of the arms is seen again in the shoulders. Strictly speaking, indeed, I have no shoulders, but only two bony blades, standing out in harsh relief. My figure also lacks pliancy; there is a stiffness about the side lines.

Poof! There's the worst out. But then the contours are bold and delicate, the bright, pure flame of health bites into the vigorous lines, a flood of life and blue blood pulses under the transparent skin, and the fairest daughter of Eve would seem a Negress beside me! I have the foot of a gazelle! My joints are finely turned, my features of Greek correctness. It is true, madame, that the flesh tints do not melt into each other; but at least, they stand out clear and bright. In short, I am a very pretty green fruit, with all the charm of unripeness. I see a great likeness to the face in my aunt's old missal, which rises out of a violet lily.

There is no silly weakness in the blue of my insolent eyes; the white is pure mother-of-pearl, prettily marked with tiny veins, and the thick, long lashes fall like a silken fringe. My forehead sparkles, and the hair grows deliciously; it ripples into waves of pale gold, growing browner towards the center, whence escape little rebel locks, which alone would tell that my fairness is not of the insipid and hysterical type. I am a tropical blonde, with plenty of blood in my veins, a blonde more apt to strike than to turn the cheek. What do you think the hairdresser proposed? He wanted, if you please, to smooth my hair into two bands and place over my forehead a pearl, kept in place by a gold chain! He said it would recall the Middle Ages.

I told him I was not aged enough to have reached the middle or to need an ornament to freshen me up!

The nose is slender, and the well-cut nostrils are separated by a sweet little pink partition—an imperious, mocking nose, with a tip too sensitive ever to grow fat or red. Sweetheart, if this won't find a husband for a dowerless maiden, I'm a donkey. The ears are daintily curled; a pearl hanging from either lobe would show yellow. The neck is long, with an undulating motion full of dignity. In the shade, the white ripens to a golden tinge. Perhaps the mouth is a little large. But how expressive! what a color on the lips! how prettily the teeth laugh!

Then, dear, there is a harmony running through all. What a gait! what a voice! We have not forgotten how our grandmother's skirts fell into place without a touch. In a word, I am lovely and charming. When the mood comes, I can laugh one of our good old laughs, and no one will think the less of me; the dimples, impressed by Comedy's light fingers on my fair cheeks, will command respect. Or I can let my eyes fall and my heart freeze under my snowy brows.
