My peaceful park gives me all the company I care for; everything there is full of glorious images of my vanished joy, invisible to others but eloquent to me.

"I cannot away with you Spaniards!" I exclaimed one morning as my sister-in-law embraced me. "You have some nobility that we lack."

Ah! Renee, if I still live, it is because Heaven tempers the sense of affliction to the strength of those who bear it. Only a woman can know what it is to lose a love that was genuine, a passion that satisfied both spirit and flesh. How rare it is to find a man so gifted that to worship him brings no sense of degradation! If such fortune befalls us once, we cannot hope for it again. Men of true greatness, veiled by poetic grace, charm by high spiritual power—beware of love! Love will ruin you and the woman of your heart. This is my cry as I pace my woodland walks.

And he has left me no child! That love, rich in smiles and joy, has left no fruit. I am accursed. Can it be that, like the extremes of polar ice and torrid sand, the purity and vehemence of a single-hearted passion render it barren as hate? Is it only a marriage of reason, like yours, that is blessed with a family? Can Heaven be jealous of our passions? Such wild thoughts.

You are the one person whose company I could endure. Come to me; none but Renee should be with Louise in her sorrow. What a day when I first donned my widow's bonnet! When I saw myself in black, I fell back and wept until night. I weep again recalling that moment of anguish.

Goodbye. Writing tires me; thoughts crowd fast, but I have no heart to express them. Bring your children; you can nurse the baby here without making me jealous; all that is gone, he is not here, and I shall be glad to see my godson. Felipe wished for a child like little Armand. Come, then, come and help me bear my woe.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
My darling,--By the time you read this letter, I will be near, as I am leaving shortly after sending it. We will be alone together, as Louis must stay in Provence for the upcoming elections, where he seeks re-election amidst Liberal opposition.

I do not come to comfort you; I bring my heart to resonate with yours and help you endure life. I encourage you to weep, for only through tears can you find the joy of reuniting with him. Remember, he is journeying towards Heaven, and each step you take brings you closer to him. Every duty fulfilled breaks a link in the chain that separates you.

In my arms, you will lift your head and continue your path to him, pure and noble, free from errors that do not define you, and carrying the good deeds you will accomplish in his name.

I write these hurried lines amid preparations, interrupted by the children and Armand, who keeps calling, "Godmother, godmother! I want to see her," making me almost jealous. He could be your child!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Yes, Renee, it is true; I have sold my house, Chantepleurs, and the farms in Seine-et-Marne, but I assure you, I am neither mad nor ruined.

To explain, after settling everything, I have about twelve hundred thousand francs left from my late husband’s fortune. I invested a million in the Three per Cents when they were at fifty, giving me an income of sixty thousand francs instead of the thirty thousand from the property. My previous life was tedious—six months in the country managing leases, dealing with farmers who paid at their convenience, and selling produce at a loss. In Paris, my house brought in ten thousand francs in rent, but I faced delays and legal issues with my investments. It was a burdensome and frustrating existence for a widow of twenty-seven.

Now, my fortune is secured with the state. Instead of paying taxes, I receive thirty thousand francs every six months from the Treasury, handed to me by a pleasant clerk. If the nation were to go bankrupt, I would still be as well off as before, and I have time to save a little capital. Besides, the Comte de l'Estorade is a peer of this semi-republic and a friend at Court, so I have no reason to worry.

What drives a woman to such financial calculations? Love, my dear!

Now, I must reveal the reasons behind my actions, which may have puzzled you. I am to be married in a village near Paris. I love and am loved deeply. Forgive me for keeping this secret; I wanted to avoid the inevitable concerns and doubts from you and de l'Estorade. You know how jealous I can be, and I wanted to spare myself the anxiety.

The man I love has thirty thousand francs in debts, which I have paid. You might think him an adventurer, but I chose to investigate him myself. He has been courting me for nearly two years. I am twenty-seven, and he is twenty-three, which is a significant age difference. He is a poet, living on very little, and has spent more time dreaming than working. Practical people often view artists as unreliable, and I understand the skepticism.

Despite his debts and our age difference, I am ready to end a long period of restraint. This is not a naive surrender; my love for him is profound, and he has waited patiently for me. He is noble and tender, and his love is genuine. My beloved is Marie Gaston, the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, who died tragically. He was raised by his brother Louis, who has not been heard from in years. Louis, a sailor, wrote encouraging letters, but his absence has left Gaston in despair.

Gaston believed that genius would lead to fortune, but his life has been filled with hardship. He has struggled to make a name for himself in literature, and his debts have only increased. I met him at Mme. d'Espard's and felt an immediate connection. He possesses both talent and pride, which can intimidate women.

Gaston has no idea of the depth of my love for him. I am confident in our happiness together, and I feel a passion I have never known before. I adore him as Felipe once adored me, and I am filled with fears of losing him or becoming unattractive as he remains youthful. Yet, I believe I can nurture our love and keep it alive.

If I find his love waning, I would blame myself, not him. I know my nature leans more towards being a lover than a mother. Should I face such a disaster, I ask you, Renee, to care for my children as my legacy. Your qualities would make my death less bitter.

I wish to keep my marriage private, performed in secret, to avoid any anxiety from you. I will retain control of my property, and Gaston will not know its full extent. I plan to assign him a stock that brings in twelve thousand francs a year, which he will find on the eve of our wedding. If he refuses it, I would end our engagement.

This long confession has exhausted me. I will continue it later; I must spend tomorrow in the country.

October 20th.

I have taken steps to ensure our secrecy. I purchased a small property near Ville d'Avray, consisting of twenty acres, a lakelet, and a charming garden. The area is secluded, surrounded by woods, and beautifully landscaped. My chalet, inspired by Swiss designs, is well-equipped and hidden from view. 

The property includes a separate house for staff and a hidden entrance, ensuring our privacy. I have carefully selected my servants, who will be loyal and discreet. All my cherished belongings from Paris have been moved to the chalet, creating a simple yet elegant home.

The tranquility of this valley is unparalleled, filled with the sounds of nature. I worry that such perfect happiness may attract misfortune, as you have often warned. 

When I visited the property recently, I was moved to tears and paid my architect without hesitation, insisting on confidentiality regarding my ownership. 

Now you understand my mysterious actions and the true purpose behind my investments. Love is a serious endeavor, and I have removed all financial burdens from my life to focus solely on him. 

Gaston remains unaware of my plans. We will begin our life at the chalet soon, and while our living expenses will be modest, I intend to invest significantly in my appearance for him. I want to maintain my beauty throughout our marriage, ensuring that I am loved as much at the end as at the beginning.

In two days, I will be Mme. Gaston. Is it fitting for a Christian to love so deeply?

My man of business remarked on my decision, saying he was losing a client. And you, my dear, may feel you are losing a sister.

Please address future letters to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We will keep our lives secret from the locals and procure provisions from Paris. Our retreat has been prepared in silence, and only the architect knows of our existence.

Farewell. As I write, I feel a mix of grief and joy, proving that my love for Gaston is as profound as my sorrow in leaving you.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
My Dear Daniel, 

I need two witnesses for my marriage and ask you to come tomorrow evening with our friend, Joseph Bridau. My future wife has expressed a desire to live in complete retirement, which is why we have seen so little of each other this past year. After our wedding, we will be apart for a long time, but I know you will understand. Friendship can endure absence, and while I may miss you, I will not see you in my own home. She has sacrificed her closeness with a childhood friend for me, and I must do the same.

This shows that our love is profound and genuine, based on a deep understanding of each other. It brings me immense joy, yet I harbor a painful secret. You know how desperate my financial situation has been, and it is only through your support that I have managed to cope. My wife is wealthy, while I am not. I once joked about wanting a rich heiress, but now that it is a reality, I feel a bitter humiliation that I cannot shake off. This shame proves my love for her, as I do not recoil from it, even if it overwhelms me.

Aside from this, my dreams have come true. She is the most beautiful and noble woman, with a charming personality and a sharp mind. At twenty-seven, she is a widow without children, full of life yet occasionally melancholic. Despite her aristocratic background, she loves me despite my humble origins. Our love has blossomed over time, and we share a deep emotional connection, avoiding the physical aspect of love out of mutual respect. 

One day, she confessed that her marriage was her regret, revealing the depth of her feelings. You, Daniel, are a giant among men, and you love a remarkable woman, just as I love my Armande.

---

Louise, can it be that you are planning a life of wedded solitude after sacrificing your first husband? You seem to be setting yourself up for future misery. I see that you are committed to this path, and the man who has won your heart must possess a unique charm. But have you forgotten your previous criticisms of young men? You once claimed they all have tarnished their purity. 

You are fortunate to believe in happiness, and while I cannot blame you for it, I feel compelled to warn you. Nature and society conspire against absolute happiness, and I foresee some disaster arising from your boundless joy. I have no objections to your fiancé, but I hope you will share a description of him with me someday.

I have not yet told Louis about your marriage; it would only invite his ridicule. I will simply inform him that you wish to keep it secret. You are brave to face winter first, and I will be your discreet friend. Your journey into this emotional wilderness is akin to the perilous expeditions of explorers, but thankfully, it is only a short distance from Paris. I wish you a safe journey and a swift return.

---

What has happened to you, my dear? After two years of silence, Renee has a right to be concerned about Louise. Is this what love does? It scatters friendships like ours! You must admit that, while I am devoted to my children, a mother's love is expansive and does not diminish other affections. I miss your letters and long to see your face.

Regarding our situation, I will be brief. Your last letter contained sharp comments about our political situation. You mocked us for keeping our position in the Audit Department, which Louis owes to Charles X's favor. But how could we provide for Athenais and Rene on an income of forty thousand livres, of which thirty thousand is entailed? We must live on our salary and save the estate's income for their future. In twenty years, we will have saved enough to provide for both children.

Louis has been raised to the Peerage and is now a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. He has made himself useful in the Chamber and has been offered an embassy, which I declined for the sake of our children's education. I will not leave Paris until Rene has completed his schooling.

We could not remain loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without jeopardizing our children's prospects. A mother is not called to be a Decius, especially in these times. In fifteen years, Louis will retire on a good pension, and I hope to secure a position for Armand in the Audit Department.

I am a happy mother, and my children bring me endless joy. Armand is now a day scholar at Henry IV's school. I wanted him to have a public-school education but could not bear to part with him, so I arranged for him to be taken to school and brought home each day. He is the same charming boy you loved, and his tutor is pleased with his progress.

Home education has its drawbacks, as children raised at home may be exposed too early to the world's passions. They may lack the social skills that come from interaction with peers. A mother must keep her children away from society's distractions, as I have done. 

Now that I am thirty, I feel a sense of duty fulfilled. My children seem to sense my thoughts and respond accordingly. Armand, who was once dull, has recently excelled in school, winning prizes for translation and essays. I felt immense pride when his name was called, and I longed to shout, "I am his mother!" 

Rene, inspired by Armand's success, wants to attend school too. The three children can be quite noisy, but I cherish every moment with them. I am the only one allowed to care for them, as the joys of motherhood outweigh the challenges. 

I must study their characters closely, as a mother alone can discern her children's natural aptitudes. Home-bred children often possess ease and tact, which can compensate for a lack of natural ability. I believe Armand will become an admirable public servant, while Rene will be a bold sailor. 

Nais is so dear to me that I can hardly see her as separate from myself. I want her to be happy and will only give her to a man who loves her. Yet, I worry about her future and the possibility of her marrying poorly. Your silence has reminded me of the anguish that marriage can bring to a mother. Farewell for now, dear Louise, and please write back.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
So, after two years of silence, you are curious about my lack of letters. My dear Renee, words cannot express my happiness. We have the strength to bear it, and it costs us no effort, as we are in perfect harmony. There has been no discord in our feelings, and each day has brought its own joy. We fear not monotony, but rather that our time together may not be long enough to contain all the poetic expressions of our love. We find more pleasure in each other's company than we did on the first day, and every hour reveals new reasons to love one another. 

Every evening, we talk about visiting Paris, discussing the new boulevards and the finished Madeleine, but when tomorrow comes, we linger over breakfast and take a siesta instead. Gaston gazes at me as if I were a painting, and we both feel tears of joy as we reflect on our love. I still play the role of the mistress, pretending to give less than I receive, and I delight in this dynamic. There is something sweet about seeing passion tempered by tenderness, with Gaston, my master, remaining a timid suitor at my command.

You asked me to describe him, but it is impossible to portray the man we love without infusing it with emotion. Society has altered men so much that the man in public can seem entirely different from the man of strong feelings. The charming suitor may lack the deeper qualities a woman values, while an unrefined man may possess a profound capacity for love. Our ideal is a man whose outward appearance aligns with his true nature, embodying the grace that cannot be acquired. This ideal haunts every woman's imagination, and in Gaston, I have found it.

Ah, dear, I never knew love could be so intertwined with youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston is unpretentious, moving with an effortless grace. When we walk together, our bodies fit perfectly, and we seem like a single figure gliding through the woods. Our thoughts, desires, and words are in harmony. Sometimes, we walk in silence, listening to the rain and admiring the sunset, feeling as if we are in silent prayer for our happiness. A timid kiss exchanged in nature feels like a divine act of worship.

We return home, each more in love than before. Such passionate love between older married people would be scandalous in Paris; only in the woods can we express it freely.

Gaston is of average height, well-proportioned, and agile. He has a fair complexion, dark hair, and a striking resemblance to Louis XIII. His honorable poverty has preserved him from the vices that ruin many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he possesses a robust constitution. His blue eyes, filled with tenderness for me, can flash with intensity at inspiring thoughts.

He has a remarkable temperament, surprising in its evenness. I have heard many tales of women's troubles with men who are dissatisfied with themselves, but Gaston is free from such burdens. I have learned that marrying an older man can lead to unequal unions, and I urge you not to let Athenais marry one.

His smile is a treasure, always fresh and full of unspoken love and gratitude. Our shared experiences have become part of our joy, and even the smallest details of nature remind us of our time together. We share a deep connection, as if we are two editions of the same book. Our ability to view subjects broadly brings us constant pleasure, and this unity of thought feels like a pledge of love.

Despite the pleasures of my life, it is laborious. I do my own room, refusing to let a hired hand intrude on my personal space. My room is meticulously arranged, and I ensure that Gaston finds it pleasing. I rise early, bathing in cold water to refresh myself before he wakes, emerging like a newly opened flower. He appreciates this freshness without knowing the effort behind it.

My daily grooming is handled by my maid, and I dress multiple times a day for Gaston's delight. We take great interest in our flowers and horticulture, surrounding our chalet with beauty. Our garden is a source of pride, and we eagerly tend to our fruits and vegetables.

To keep Gaston's mind engaged, I encourage him to finish some plays he started during his earlier struggles. This literary work requires reflection and inspiration, and I assist him, ensuring our winter evenings are never dull.

Our servants have an easy time, never needing reprimand. They have cleverly presented us as a traveling couple, and they are content with their roles. The gardeners sell surplus produce, and we enjoy the abundance that is hard to find in Paris.

I strive to fill the role of society for Gaston, ensuring his solitude does not become tedious. I have learned true jealousy, the kind that comes from love. The thought of him ceasing to love me sends shivers through me, and I tremble before him.

Alas, I am still without a child. I long for the day when our home will need the laughter of children, running through our garden. It pains me to see your lovely children, as my life feels narrower without them. Motherhood expands a woman's heart, while love can be selfish. I envy your happiness and the joy you find in your children.

As I approach thirty, I feel the limits of my beauty closing in. Though I am still attractive, I worry about aging while Gaston remains young. I often ask him to promise he will tell me if his love ever fades, and he swears it will not, believing it to be absurd.

Goodbye, dear friend. Shall we let years pass without writing again? Happiness can be monotonous, and perhaps that is why Dante seems greater in the _Paradiso_ than in the _Inferno_. I am not Dante, just your friend, and I do not wish to bore you. You can write, as your children provide endless joy, while I... No more of this. A thousand loves.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
My dear Louise, I have read your letter multiple times, and it increasingly seems to me that it reflects the thoughts of a child rather than a woman. You remain the same old Louise, forgetting that love, in its purest form, belongs to nature and has been distorted by civilization. Society has created the family, the foundation of organized life, at the cost of individual desires. A married man dedicates his life to his wife, who benefits from his efforts. 

You have taken a misguided path; the dream of being adored is for the young, not for a mature woman. A woman's vanity may find satisfaction in knowing she can inspire adoration, but true fulfillment lies in the life of a wife and mother. I urge you to return to Paris, for it is not misfortune you should fear, but happiness.

Life's simple pleasures—bread, air, silence—are what sustain us. Passionate love can be exhausting, while the feelings of motherhood are enduring and rewarding. I remember my own choice to embrace a life of self-sacrifice, and I would choose it again. Your life, despite its romantic veneer, is rooted in selfishness, and I cannot help but plead with you once more as I see your happiness withstand trials.

Life consists of balance; excess leads to pain or fleeting pleasure. You are unwell because you maintain a passion that marriage should transform into a steady influence. The true glory of home lies in calmness and shared experiences, which you seem to overlook. You, who are more passionate than tender, would be both wife and mistress, undermining the sanctity of marriage. 

If only you could understand the sweetness of a mother's self-discipline. My proud nature has softened into a gentle melancholy, rewarded by the joys of motherhood. I fear your life may take a darker turn. I wish you could witness the genuine joys of family life, but I fear my arguments are futile against your delusions. I had hoped that time would restore your reason, but it seems your appetite for love is insatiable. Farewell, dear friend; I despair that my letter, meant to reconcile you with society, has only highlighted your selfishness.

---

Renee, calamity has struck—no, it is beyond calamity; it is a thunderbolt for poor Louise. Doubt is my calamity; certainty would be death. The day before yesterday, after finishing my first toilet, I searched for Gaston to take a walk, but he was nowhere to be found. In the stable, I saw his mare, Fedelta, in a lather, indicating he had been to Paris without telling me. Suspicion gripped me, and I struggled to regain my composure.

When Gaston found me, I was pale and shaken. He carried me to the parlor, where I refused to speak, retreating to my room to weep. He waited outside, concerned, but I told him I would see him when I was ready. After I composed myself, he returned, and I confronted him about the mud on Fedelta's legs. He playfully threatened to withhold explanations, but I saw no signs of disloyalty in his eyes, even though I remained unconvinced.

We spent the day together, and I felt my heartstrings bind tighter to him. I hated that mare for causing my distress. I asked if I bored him, and he reassured me of his love. I suggested selling Fedelta, but his response crushed me, making me feel inadequate. Alone with my thoughts, I was consumed by despair, feeling as if I were surrounded by darkness.

The next morning, I told him to take Fedelta to Paris, but my tone betrayed my inner turmoil. He urged me to trust him, and I tried to suppress my suspicions. He left, and I was left to grapple with my fears. I felt a strange, tragic solemnity in my love for him, and I watched him leave with a sense of impending doom.

When he returned late that night, he brought me a whip as a gift, but I couldn't shake the feeling that it was a distraction from something deeper. I later discovered that he had bought it from Verdier, confirming my suspicions. I resolved to keep my eyes open while remaining silent about my doubts.

Days passed, and I noticed a change in Gaston; he was working harder than ever, and I suspected he needed money. I discovered that his drawer was empty, and he was receiving letters he quickly tore up. My jealousy consumed me, and I followed him to the post office, where I confronted him about a letter from Nathan, revealing that a play of his had been accepted.

Despite the joy of his success, I couldn't shake the feeling that he was hiding something. I resolved to keep my suspicions to myself until I could confirm my fears. I felt a growing certainty that he had a rival, and I was determined to uncover the truth.

After further investigation, I learned that Gaston was visiting a woman in Paris, and I followed him to discover that she was his brother's widow, legally married. This revelation shattered my heart, as I realized he had been providing for her and her children. I returned home, tormented by thoughts of betrayal and jealousy.

Despite my pain, my love for Gaston remained strong, and I struggled with the knowledge of his past. I wanted to confront him about his feelings for this woman, but I hesitated, fearing the truth would destroy me. I made plans for my own demise, feeling that I could not bear the weight of my suffering any longer.

---

My dear Louise, I send this letter with urgency, as your last correspondence alarmed me. I confided in Louis, believing it necessary to save you from yourself. Though our methods may seem extreme, the outcome is satisfactory. I even involved the police, but it remains a secret among us.

Gaston is not what you think. His brother, Louis, died in Calcutta, leaving behind a widow and two children. Gaston has been secretly supporting them, using his earnings to provide for his sister-in-law and her children. The woman you mistook for a rival is not a threat; she is a mother in need, and Gaston has acted out of kindness and duty.

You should find reason to love him more, as he has shown great sensitivity and care. I believe you should welcome the widow and her children into your life, perhaps even adopt the boys. This revelation should bring you peace, not despair.

---

Ah, my dear friend, what can I say? It is too late for me. My own actions have sealed my fate. I am tormented by my love for Gaston, and I cannot bear the thought of losing him. I am filled with despair, and I long for relief from this suffering. Come to me, dear friend, and witness my decline.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
My love,--Take the children to Provence without me; I must stay with Louise, who has only a few days left. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whom I worry.

You know the letter that sent me rushing to Ville d'Avray, gathering doctors along the way. Since then, I have not left my dear friend, sitting up every night for two weeks.

When I arrived, Louise was with Gaston, looking beautiful and happy, but it was a facade. She whispered to me, "He must not know; I am dying." A chill ran through me as I felt her burning hand and saw the red spots on her cheeks. I was glad I had left the doctors in the woods until needed.

"Leave us for a little," she told Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have secrets to share."

Once alone, she collapsed into my arms, unable to hold back her tears. "Tell me about it," I urged. "I have brought the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, along with Bianchon."

"Ah! Have them in at once if they can save me! The passion that drove me to death now cries for life!" 

"But what have you done to yourself?"

"I have brought myself to the last stage of consumption in just a few days."

"But how?"

"I got myself into a profuse perspiration at night, then lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, but I am dying!"

"Send him to Paris; I will fetch the doctors myself," I said, rushing out to find them.

Alas! After the consultation, none of the doctors gave me hope; they all believe Louise will die with the fall of the leaves. Her constitution has aided her plan, but a few days of folly have made her case desperate.

I cannot express my feelings upon hearing this. I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. I was crushed and could not escort the doctors out. I was lost in bitter thoughts when I heard Louise's clear voice: "So there is no hope for me!"

She made me get up and took me to her drawing-room. "Stay with me to the end; I won't have sad faces around me. I must keep the truth from him. I know I have the strength to do it. I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood."

"For him, I can see I would have made his life miserable. My jealousy has already wounded him. When my suspicions met indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. There are people who have lived sixty years without two years of real life, while my thirty years are doubled by the intensity of my love."

"For him and me, the end is happy. But for you, dear Renee, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss that cannot be repaired. You alone have the right to mourn my death."

After a long pause, she continued: "The moral of my death is cruel. My dear doctor was right; marriage cannot rest on passion or even love. Your life is noble, giving your husband growing affection, while my passionate eagerness diminished. Twice I have gone astray, and twice Death has reached for my happiness. The first time, he took the noblest of men; now he takes me from my poet husband, with all his beauty and grace."

"I would not complain. I have known two men of nobility—one in mind, the other in form. I die adored—what more could I wish? Perhaps time to draw near to God, whom I may have thought too little of. My spirit will fly towards Him, full of love, praying to be reunited with the two who made my life a heaven. Without them, paradise would be a desert."

"My example would be fatal to others; to meet a Felipe or a Gaston is more than mortals can expect. The doctrine of society regarding marriage aligns with natural law. Women are weak and should sacrifice their will to their husbands, who should lay their selfishness at their feet. The stir women have created by their whining is ridiculous, showing how well we deserve the epithet of children."

She continued speaking in her gentle voice, expressing grave truths prettily, until Gaston entered with his sister-in-law, the children, and the English nurse, whom he had fetched from Paris.

"Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!"

She welcomed Mme. Gaston and insisted she consider the chalet her home, playing the hostess in her best manner.

I wrote to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, as well as to Madeleine. It was time. The next day, Louise, exhausted from the exertion, could not go out; she only got up for dinner. That evening, Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her brothers, and her mother arrived. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage faded. Every day since, her father and brothers have visited, and the duchesses spend their evenings at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences petty feelings.

Louise remains charming, graceful, sensible, witty, and tender. She has retained her perfect tact and lavishes her brilliant mind on us, which made her one of Paris's queens.

"I want to look well even in my coffin," she said with her matchless smile as she lay down on the bed where she would linger for a fortnight.

Her room lacks the sick-chamber atmosphere; medicines and nursing supplies are concealed.

"Is not my deathbed pretty?" she asked the priest who came to confess her.

We watched her like misers. The anxiety and harsh truths have prepared Gaston for the worst. He is brave, but the blow has struck deep. I would not be surprised if he followed her soon. Yesterday, while walking by the lake, he said, "I must be a father to those two children," pointing to his sister-in-law with the boys. "But I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them and persuade your husband to accept the role of guardian."

He spoke simply, like a man who knows his time is short. He wears smiles for Louise, but I see through his facade. He matches her courage.

Louise wishes to see her godson, but I prefer he remains in Provence; she might want to remember him generously, putting me in a difficult position.

Goodbye, my love.

August 25th (her birthday).

Yesterday evening, Louise was delirious for a short time, but her babbling was beautiful, showing that even the madness of gifted people is not that of fools. In a faint voice, she sang Italian airs from _I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moise_, while we stood around her bed in silence. Not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes, realizing her soul was departing. She could no longer see us, yet her charm lingered in the melody, sweet and otherworldly.

During the night, the death agony began. It is now seven in the morning, and I have just raised her from bed. A flicker of strength returned; she wished to sit by the window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving us with an empty shell.

The last sacrament was administered the evening before, unbeknownst to Gaston, who was sleeping during the ceremony. After she was moved to the window, she asked me to read the _De Profundis_ in French, facing the lovely scene she created. She repeated the words after me and pressed her husband's hands.

August 26th.

My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. Oh! I want my children! Bring me my children!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

A DAUGHTER OF EVE

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

To Madame la Comtesse Bolognini, nee Vimercati.

Madame, recalling the joy your conversation brought to a traveler in Milan, I lay this work at your feet, hoping you will protect it with your name, as it once shielded the tales of a beloved writer. You have a daughter, Eugenie, whose beauty and intelligence promise her a joyful childhood, unlike the rigid upbringing of the Eugenie in these pages. While Frenchmen are often seen as fickle, I remain Italian in my loyalty and memory. Writing “Eugenie” brings me back to the laughter and warmth of our time together in Milan, though I now think of you as a distant figure, like a masterpiece from a great artist.

Your devoted servant,
De Balzac.

A DAUGHTER OF EVE

CHAPTER I. THE TWO MARIES

In a luxurious boudoir in the rue Neuve-des-Mathurins, two young women sat weeping before a fireplace. The room, adorned with blue velvet and fine furnishings, belonged to Ferdinand du Tillet, a wealthy banker who had married the daughter of a prominent magistrate. Despite his wealth, du Tillet lacked social standing, having married into the aristocracy through a dowry agreement.

Madame du Tillet, holding her sister Madame Felix de Vandenesse’s hand, listened to her sister’s heartfelt confessions. The two sisters, raised under a strict and pious mother, had married without ever experiencing the joys of youth. Their upbringing had been so severe that they entered marriage utterly naive about life and love.

The sisters shared a deep bond, having grown up in a repressive household where their mother’s rigid control stifled their spirits. They had never been allowed to experience the world outside their home, and their education was limited to religious instruction and needlework. Their innocence was preserved, but at the cost of their understanding of life’s complexities.

As they confided in each other, the sisters reflected on their childhood and the oppressive atmosphere created by their mother. They had longed for freedom and love, and marriage was seen as their escape. However, they were unaware of the challenges that awaited them in their new lives.

CHAPTER II. A CONFIDENCE BETWEEN SISTERS

Madame de Vandenesse, overwhelmed by her troubles, sought solace in her sister’s company. She expressed her despair over her marriage to a man who seemed to care little for her happiness. Madame du Tillet, feeling helpless, listened as her sister lamented her situation. The sisters’ love for each other was evident, and they shared a bond that transcended their husbands’ indifference.

Madame du Tillet revealed her own struggles, feeling trapped in a marriage devoid of affection. She described her husband’s coldness and the emptiness of her life, contrasting it with the warmth of their sisterly bond. The sisters’ shared experiences of repression and longing for love deepened their connection.

As they spoke, Madame de Vandenesse confided her desire for a life filled with passion and excitement, while Madame du Tillet encouraged her to seek happiness. They both recognized the limitations imposed by their marriages and the societal expectations that constrained them.

CHAPTER III. THE HISTORY OF A FORTUNATE WOMAN

Felix de Vandenesse, a man of noble lineage, had married Madame de Vandenesse, hoping to find happiness in a union with a pure and innocent woman. He had experienced love in its most ideal form and sought to recreate that happiness in his marriage. However, as time passed, he realized that the constraints of society and the expectations placed upon them were stifling their relationship.

Madame de Vandenesse, initially content in her marriage, began to feel the weight of monotony and the absence of passion. She longed for excitement and adventure, feeling trapped in a life that had become predictable. Her husband, while kind and attentive, could not fulfill her desire for deeper emotional connection.

As the couple navigated their lives, Madame de Vandenesse found herself drawn to the idea of love outside her marriage. She became aware of the allure of romantic passion and the excitement it promised, leading her to question her own happiness.

CHAPTER IV. A CELEBRATED MAN

Raoul Nathan, a rising literary figure, entered the social scene with a unique blend of talent and ambition. He was known for his sharp wit and caustic humor, which endeared him to some while alienating others. His unconventional appearance and demeanor set him apart, making him a captivating figure in the eyes of women.

At a gathering hosted by Madame de Montcornet, Nathan caught the attention of Madame de Vandenesse, who was intrigued by his charisma. Their initial interactions were charged with unspoken attraction, and both felt a magnetic pull toward one another.

As Nathan navigated the complexities of his career and personal life, he found himself increasingly drawn to Madame de Vandenesse. Their connection deepened, and he began to see her as a muse, inspiring his creative endeavors. However, the societal constraints surrounding them posed challenges to their budding romance.

CHAPTER V. FLORINE

Florine, a talented actress and Nathan’s long-time companion, represented a different side of his life. Their relationship was marked by a blend of affection and practicality, as Florine provided Nathan with emotional support while also relying on him for her own success. Their dynamic was complex, with both individuals navigating the demands of their respective careers.

As Nathan’s ambitions grew, he found himself torn between his loyalty to Florine and his burgeoning feelings for Madame de Vandenesse. The actress, aware of Nathan’s infatuation with the countess, felt threatened by the potential shift in their relationship.

CHAPTER VI. ROMANTIC LOVE

Madame de Vandenesse and Nathan’s relationship blossomed, fueled by their shared passion for art and literature. Their encounters became increasingly intimate, and both felt a sense of exhilaration in their connection. However, the societal expectations and the realities of their lives loomed over them, creating tension in their romance.

As their love deepened, Madame de Vandenesse grappled with her feelings of guilt and desire. She found herself caught between her loyalty to her husband and her growing affection for Nathan. The emotional turmoil she experienced only intensified her longing for a life filled with passion and excitement.

CHAPTER VII. SUICIDE

As Nathan faced mounting pressures in his career and personal life, he found himself overwhelmed by despair. The weight of his ambitions and the fear of failure led him to contemplate suicide. However, a chance encounter with Madame de Vandenesse reignited his hope and desire to live.

The countess, determined to save Nathan from his self-destructive tendencies, took it upon herself to support him. She recognized the depth of his struggles and sought to provide him with the encouragement he needed to overcome his challenges.

CHAPTER VIII. A LOVER SAVED AND LOST

In the aftermath of Nathan’s near-suicide, Madame de Vandenesse became increasingly involved in his life. She sought to protect him from the dangers of his ambitions while also navigating her own feelings for him. Their relationship became a source of both strength and turmoil, as they grappled with the complexities of love and societal expectations.

As Nathan’s career began to flourish, he found himself torn between his loyalty to Florine and his growing affection for Madame de Vandenesse. The actress, feeling threatened by the countess, became increasingly possessive of Nathan, leading to tension in their relationship.

CHAPTER IX. THE HUSBAND’S TRIUMPH

Felix de Vandenesse, aware of the growing bond between his wife and Nathan, took action to protect his marriage. He sought to uncover the truth behind their relationship while also ensuring that Madame de Vandenesse remained safe from scandal. His determination to shield his wife from potential harm demonstrated his love and commitment to her.

As the story unfolds, the characters navigate the complexities of love, ambition, and societal expectations, ultimately leading to a dramatic conclusion that highlights the challenges faced by those caught in the web of desire and duty.

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

A WOMAN OF THIRTY

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Ellen Marriage

DEDICATION

To Louis Boulanger, Painter.

A WOMAN OF THIRTY

I. EARLY MISTAKES

On a Sunday morning in April 1813, a luxurious cabriolet pulled up in front of the Tuileries, carrying a father and his beautiful daughter, Julie. The father, looking anxious and aged, was proud of the attention his daughter attracted. As they walked through the gardens, Julie was eager to see the military review commanded by Napoleon, which was to be the last of its kind before a disastrous campaign. The father noted her impatience and felt a mix of joy and sorrow for her fleeting happiness.

As they approached the gates, they were met with disappointment; the review had not yet begun, and the father suggested they leave. Julie, however, was determined to stay, fearing she might miss seeing the Emperor. An officer recognized her and allowed them entry, leading them to a prime viewing spot. The spectacle was grand, with the Old Guard preparing for the review, and the atmosphere was charged with anticipation as the crowd awaited Napoleon's arrival.

When Napoleon finally appeared, the crowd erupted in cheers. Julie was captivated by the scene, but her attention soon shifted to a young officer, Colonel Victor d’Aiglemont, who was part of the Emperor's staff. She felt a pang of jealousy as she watched him interact with others, but her father’s anxious demeanor reminded her of the weight of their situation.

As the review concluded, Julie's father expressed his concerns about her future happiness, fearing that her youthful dreams might lead to disappointment. He warned her against falling in love with a man like Victor, whom he deemed unworthy. Despite her father's warnings, Julie felt drawn to Victor, and their connection deepened.

Nearly a year later, in March 1814, Julie and Victor were traveling together. The once vibrant and carefree Julie had become a shadow of her former self, her health deteriorating. Victor, now a colonel, was preoccupied with his military duties, leaving Julie feeling isolated. Their relationship was strained, and Julie's longing for love and connection grew.

As they traveled, they encountered an Englishman, Arthur, who had been detained in France. Julie felt a strange connection to him, and their paths crossed frequently. Arthur's quiet admiration for her contrasted sharply with Victor's indifference, and Julie found herself drawn to his gentleness.

Despite her growing feelings for Arthur, Julie remained committed to her marriage. She struggled with her emotions, torn between her duty as a wife and her desire for love. The tension between her responsibilities and her heart's yearnings created a deep inner conflict.

As the political landscape shifted with the fall of Napoleon, Julie's life took another turn. Victor's ambitions grew, and he became more involved in the new regime. Julie, meanwhile, continued to grapple with her feelings for Arthur, who remained a constant presence in her thoughts.

Their relationship reached a breaking point when Victor's infidelities became apparent. Julie's heartache deepened as she realized the extent of her husband's selfishness. In a moment of desperation, she sought solace in Arthur, but the weight of her choices loomed over her.

As time passed, Julie's health continued to decline, and she found herself increasingly isolated. The once vibrant woman was now a mere shadow, trapped in a loveless marriage. Her longing for connection and love remained unfulfilled, and she faced the harsh reality of her situation.

In a moment of clarity, Julie resolved to take control of her life. She would no longer be a passive participant in her own story. With newfound determination, she sought to reclaim her happiness, even if it meant defying societal expectations.

As the narrative unfolds, Julie's journey becomes a poignant exploration of love, sacrifice, and the complexities of womanhood. Her struggles resonate with the timeless themes of desire, duty, and the quest for personal fulfillment, ultimately leading her to confront the choices that define her existence.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Between the Seine and the Loing lies a flat, dreary country bordered by the Forest of Fontainebleau, with towns like Moret, Montereau, and Nemours marking its southern limits. The landscape is characterized by its monotonous horizon, where melancholy seems to thrive. In the center of this plain stands the old chateau of Saint-Lange, surrounded by stately avenues and gardens, a remnant of wealth now diminished.

In late 1820, a young woman, well-known in Paris for her beauty and wit, arrived at Saint-Lange, shocking the local villagers who had not seen gentry in years. The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s presence stirred curiosity, especially as she appeared frail and withdrawn, leading villagers to speculate about her financial ruin due to her husband’s gambling in Spain. They anticipated the estate might be sold off, prompting them to consider their own financial interests.

The Marquise, however, remained isolated, rarely leaving her room and only dining briefly with her daughter, whom she seemed unable to connect with. Her profound sorrow and silence led to speculation among the locals, but as time passed, their interest waned, leaving her to her solitude. At twenty-six, she was grappling with a deep, unacknowledged grief that had extinguished her maternal instincts and left her feeling like a ghost in her own life.

Her anguish stemmed from a lost love, a man who had died to preserve her honor. Bound by societal expectations, she felt she could not express her pain, leading to a profound internal conflict. The Marquise’s beauty and youth felt meaningless to her, as she was haunted by the absence of true love and the joy it could have brought. She reflected on her past, recognizing the emptiness of her marriage, which, while socially acceptable, was devoid of the emotional fulfillment she craved.

As she sat in her room, the flat horizon mirrored her desolation. The seasons changed, but her heart remained heavy with unfulfilled longing. She felt trapped in a life that offered no hope or joy, her thoughts consumed by the memory of what could have been. The Marquise’s internal struggle was compounded by her sense of guilt and the societal pressures that demanded she maintain a façade of happiness.

One day, the village priest, M. le Curé, visited her, sensing her suffering. He spoke of the universal nature of grief and the importance of faith, but she dismissed his attempts at comfort, feeling that her pain was unique and insurmountable. The priest shared a story of a father who lost all his sons in battle, emphasizing resilience in the face of despair. This resonated with the Marquise, who found solace in the idea that others had endured similar suffering.

Despite her initial resistance, she began to open up to the priest, revealing her feelings of isolation and the emptiness of her motherhood. She felt disconnected from her daughter, Helene, whom she viewed as a reminder of her lost love. The Marquise expressed her belief that true motherhood required a deep emotional bond, which she felt she lacked. Her relationship with Helene was marked by a sense of duty rather than genuine affection, leading to further despair.

The priest encouraged her to find strength in her role as a mother, but she struggled to reconcile her feelings of inadequacy. She lamented the societal constraints that dictated her life, feeling trapped in a marriage that felt more like a prison than a partnership. The Marquise’s bitterness towards societal norms grew, as she recognized the hypocrisy of a system that forced women into roles that stifled their true selves.

As spring arrived, the Marquise found some distraction in managing her estate, which provided a temporary reprieve from her grief. However, her emotional turmoil remained unresolved. When she left Saint-Lange in October, she had regained some vitality, but the scars of her sorrow lingered. She encountered the curé one last time, but her avoidance of him signified her desire to move on from the painful memories associated with her time at the chateau.

In summary, the Marquise d’Aiglemont’s journey through grief and isolation reflects the struggles of a woman caught between societal expectations and her own desires. Her story is one of profound loss, the search for identity, and the complexities of motherhood, ultimately highlighting the challenges faced by women in a rigidly structured society.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame Firmiani was hosting a ball, and M. Charles de Vandenesse, a promising young diplomat, attended to bid farewell to Paris before his appointment at the Congress of Laybach. Although he had enjoyed the city's pleasures, he felt little regret, having grown accustomed to the transient nature of his life. At nearly thirty, he viewed life philosophically, prioritizing ambition over romantic entanglements, and found the social scene filled with superficiality and empty intrigues.

As he surveyed the ball, he reflected on the emptiness of the Parisian elite, noting that the women and men present seemed devoid of genuine emotion, caught in a cycle of trivial pursuits. His thoughts were interrupted when Madame Firmiani introduced him to the enigmatic Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman shrouded in mystery and known for her past romantic entanglements. Intrigued, Vandenesse was struck by her beauty and the depth of her character, which contrasted sharply with the frivolity around him.

The Marquise, at thirty, possessed a delicate beauty and an air of melancholy that drew Vandenesse in. Her demeanor suggested a life filled with unfulfilled dreams and a resignation to her fate. He recognized the disparity between her and her husband, a dull man who had risen to prominence through her influence. Despite her outward calm, Vandenesse sensed a profound sadness within her, which only deepened his interest.

Their conversation flowed from light banter to deeper themes of love and sentiment, revealing a connection that both excited and troubled Vandenesse. He found himself captivated by her, feeling a mix of admiration and longing. As he prepared to leave, he requested to visit her again, feeling a strong pull towards her despite the complexities of her past.

In the days that followed, Vandenesse's thoughts were consumed by the Marquise. He oscillated between hope and doubt regarding her feelings for him, ultimately deciding to visit her. Their meetings became a blend of friendship and unspoken desire, with Vandenesse grappling with the implications of his growing affection for a married woman.

The Marquise, too, found herself drawn to Vandenesse, yet she struggled with her loyalty to the memory of her deceased lover. She was aware of the societal constraints that bound them, and her heart was torn between the past and the possibility of new love. Despite her reservations, she could not deny the connection they shared.

As their relationship deepened, Vandenesse became increasingly aware of the challenges they faced. He admired the Marquise's strength and resilience, yet he also felt the weight of her past. Their love blossomed amidst the tension of societal expectations and personal histories, leading to moments of both joy and anguish.

One evening, as they shared a quiet moment, their emotions culminated in a tender kiss, marking a turning point in their relationship. However, the intrusion of the Marquise's husband shattered the moment, forcing them to confront the reality of their situation. Vandenesse's ambitions and the Marquise's loyalty to her memories created a complex web of desire and duty, leaving them both to navigate the treacherous waters of love and societal norms.

In the end, their connection was a testament to the power of love, capable of transcending the boundaries set by society, yet fraught with the dangers of unfulfilled desires and the haunting presence of the past.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Between the Barrière d’Italie and the Barrière de la Santé, the boulevard leading to the Jardin des Plantes offers a breathtaking view of Paris that can inspire even the most jaded observer. From a higher vantage point, the boulevard, lined with tall trees, reveals a deep valley filled with factories nestled among greenery and the brown streams of the Bièvre.

On the opposite slope, the Faubourg Saint-Marceau's squalor contrasts sharply with the majestic cupola of the Panthéon and the somber dome of the Val-du-Grace, which rise above a densely packed town. The two monuments appear as giants overshadowing the small houses and tall poplars below. To the left, the observatory stands out, its windows casting strange shadows, while in the distance, the elegant lantern tower of the Invalides rises between the Luxembourg and Saint-Sulpice. The architecture blends with the surrounding foliage, shifting with the changing light and sky. 

To the right, the canal Saint-Martin stretches out, bordered by reddish stone quays and lime trees, with public granaries in a Roman style lining its banks. Beyond, the smoke-laden slopes of Belleville are dotted with houses and windmills, merging with the clouds. Between this horizon and the valley's rooftops lies a vast city, hidden yet alive, echoing with the sounds of life, as if to declare its presence.

When sunlight floods this part of Paris, it transforms the landscape, illuminating windows, brightening tiles, and creating a magical atmosphere. The scene is idyllic, filled with the sounds of humanity and nature, a harmonious blend that captivates the soul. Beneath the cypress trees of Pere-Lachaise, the entire capital lies in peaceful splendor.

One morning, as I admired this beautiful view, I reflected bitterly on the disdain some express for our homeland, lamenting the wealthy who travel abroad only to mock France. I gazed lovingly at modern Paris, lost in thought until the sound of a kiss interrupted my reverie. I spotted a young woman, dressed simply yet elegantly, with a handsome young man and a charming little boy. Their joy was palpable, and their connection was evident in their movements and expressions. 

A second child, a little girl, stood apart, her demeanor suggesting a deeper, more troubled nature. As I observed the family, I noted the stark contrast between the siblings. The girl, perhaps seven or eight, had dark hair and a serious expression, while her younger brother was fair and carefree. Their clothing was similar, but the girl's plain collar hinted at a hidden preference from their mother. 

The boy approached his sister with a small toy, but she scowled, revealing a complexity in her character that belied her age. Her gaze was intense, suggesting a burden of thought too heavy for a child. I was drawn to her, intrigued by the shadows of maturity in her young face. 

As the young couple continued their playful interaction, the little girl’s expression shifted from indifference to something darker. When her brother called out to her, she reacted with hostility, pushing him away. In a tragic moment, he stumbled and fell into the river, disappearing beneath the murky water. The scene unfolded with terrifying speed, and I rushed to help, but it was too late. The mother arrived, frantic, but there was no hope of saving the child. 

The incident left a haunting mark on the mother, who would have to face the consequences of this tragedy upon returning home. The weight of her grief and the implications of her daughter’s actions loomed large, a silent witness to the unfolding drama.

Years later, the Marquis de Vandenesse, now in mourning for his father, hosted a dinner with a notary present. The notary, oblivious to the tension in the room, monopolized the conversation, unaware of the Marquise’s impatience to leave and join her children at the theater. Despite the couple’s subtle hints to end the discussion, the notary continued, oblivious to their discomfort.

As the clock struck nine, the Marquis finally interrupted, insisting the notary leave so he could escort his wife. The notary, still unaware of the gravity of the situation, continued to speak, leading to a frustrating exchange. The Marquis, exasperated, attempted to correct the notary’s misunderstandings, but the atmosphere grew increasingly tense.

Just then, the Marquise’s husband returned unexpectedly with their children, who appeared upset. The little girl’s eyes were red from crying, and the boy was fretful. The Marquise’s concern deepened as she realized the children’s distress was linked to the play they had attended. The boy recounted a scene from the theater, revealing the source of their sudden return.

The Marquise’s face hardened as she processed the implications of her daughter’s emotional state. The notary, attempting to lighten the mood, inadvertently exacerbated the situation with his comments about family dynamics and preferences, which only heightened the tension in the room.

The Marquise, overwhelmed, sent her daughter away, while the notary continued to speak, oblivious to the gravity of his words. The Marquis, unable to contain his anger, confronted the notary, demanding he leave. The notary, confused and flustered, exited, leaving the couple to grapple with the emotional fallout of the evening.

As the notary reflected on the events, he failed to grasp the underlying issues at play, attributing the tension to the whims of the aristocracy. His wife, however, recognized the truth of the situation, advising him to stick to business in future interactions. The notary’s inability to understand the nuances of social dynamics would likely lead to further misunderstandings in his professional life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
V. TWO MEETINGS

One of Napoleon’s orderly staff-officers, referred to as the General or the Marquis, spent the spring at Versailles. He had amassed a fortune during the Restoration and, due to his court duties, took a country house near Montreuil, allowing him solitude close to Paris. The house, originally a retreat for a nobleman’s fleeting romances, had expansive grounds, yet its entrance faced the street, suggesting a once-quiet road.

One winter evening, the family was alone in the house, as the servants had gone to Versailles for a wedding. It was Christmas time, and the revelers lingered, causing the General to feel some concern as the clock struck eleven without their return. Outside, a deep silence enveloped the countryside, broken only by the wind and the sounds of distant footsteps.

Inside, the family enjoyed a cozy evening. The General, relaxed in a high-back armchair by the fire, watched his youngest son, who resisted his mother’s attempts to undress him, preferring to play with his sister. The children’s laughter filled the room, and their mother, beautiful and content, observed them with affection. The General, a rugged soldier, found joy in their innocence, embodying the warmth of family life.

At a nearby table, their thirteen-year-old son read the _Arabian Nights_, absorbed in the story. His sister, Helene, worked on a tapestry, her beauty striking and her demeanor thoughtful. The family’s dynamic was rich, with the children’s play contrasting with the older siblings’ more serious reflections.

As the evening progressed, the General expressed concern over the absence of the servants, while the Marquise, sensing tension, urged their son to go to bed. Just then, a series of hurried knocks echoed from the street, signaling an urgent situation. The General, armed and cautious, answered the door to find a breathless man seeking refuge, claiming he was being pursued.

The stranger, desperate and disheveled, insisted on his need for secrecy and water. The General, despite his initial hesitation, agreed to shelter him, feeling an inexplicable pull towards the man. The stranger, revealing bloodied hands, explained he had been involved in a duel. As the General led him to a hidden room, he felt a mix of unease and obligation.

Meanwhile, the Marquise sensed something was amiss and questioned her husband about the stranger. The General, maintaining his promise of secrecy, urged his family to remain unaware of the situation. However, Helene, curious and concerned, overheard their conversation and felt compelled to investigate.

When Helene approached the locked room, she was struck by the presence of the stranger, who, despite his grim circumstances, exuded a magnetic power. Their eyes met, and an unspoken connection formed, leaving Helene both intrigued and unsettled. She returned to her family, her mind racing with thoughts of the man she had just encountered.

As the night wore on, the General’s thoughts were consumed by the implications of harboring a fugitive. The tension in the household grew palpable, and the Marquise, sensing her daughter’s distraction, pressed her to explain her feelings. Helene, torn between loyalty to her family and her fascination with the stranger, struggled to articulate her emotions.

The General, now fully aware of the danger posed by the stranger’s presence, resolved to protect his family at all costs. He called for the servants to return, his anger boiling over as he demanded answers about their delay. The atmosphere in the house shifted, filled with a sense of impending crisis.

As the General prepared to confront the gendarmes who had arrived seeking the fugitive, he felt a surge of protectiveness for his family. The stranger, aware of the growing tension, remained silent, contemplating his next move. The General’s resolve hardened as he faced the reality of the situation, determined to shield his loved ones from the consequences of his decision to offer sanctuary.

In the ensuing chaos, Helene’s feelings for the stranger deepened, leading her to question her own desires and the life she had known. The night culminated in a dramatic confrontation, forcing the General to confront the moral complexities of his actions and the impact they would have on his family.

Ultimately, the General’s hospitality would lead to unforeseen consequences, as Helene’s connection to the stranger would alter the course of their lives forever. The family’s idyllic evening had transformed into a night of turmoil, revealing the fragility of their happiness and the weight of their choices.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
It was a June day in 1844 when the Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman of about fifty, was walking in the garden of her daughter’s mansion in Paris. She was focused on a row of windows, occasionally sitting on a bench to gaze at the dome of the Invalides and her own grounds. The neighborhood was quiet, as the aristocracy was just waking up.

The Marquise had given her fortune to her daughter, the Comtesse Moïna de Saint-Héreen, after losing her two sons, Gustave and Abel. Her affection for Moïna was profound, a stark contrast to her feelings for her deceased children, whom she remembered only faintly. The Marquise’s devotion to Moïna was seen as a kind of superstition by society, which largely overlooked her past and her coldness towards her daughter-in-law.

Despite her sacrifices, the Marquise was often alone, as Moïna was busy with her social life. The Marquise’s love for her daughter was evident, but it was tinged with sadness and a sense of loss. Gossip suggested that she might regret giving up her fortune, but most praised her for her devotion. The Marquise rarely attended social events, and when she did, she felt overshadowed by her daughter’s youthful charm.

As she sat in the garden, the Marquise reflected on her life, her face revealing the weight of her experiences. Her beauty had faded, marked by sorrow and the passage of time. She was aware of her daughter’s flirtation with Alfred de Vandenesse, a man she viewed with disdain. The Marquise feared for Moïna, knowing that this relationship could lead to heartbreak.

The Comte de Saint-Héreen had been away for months, and Moïna was indulging in a flirtation that the Marquise believed was dangerous. She felt powerless to intervene, knowing that her warnings would likely fall on deaf ears. The Marquise’s heart ached as she realized that her daughter was on the brink of a precipice, and she could do nothing to save her.

In a moment of despair, the Marquise recalled the small, painful moments that had accumulated over the years, each one a reminder of her daughter’s growing distance. She approached Moïna’s room, hoping to speak to her, but was met with indifference. The Countess’s dismissive attitude stung, and the Marquise felt the weight of her daughter’s contempt.

When she finally confronted Moïna, the conversation turned tense. The Marquise attempted to warn her about the risks of her relationship with Alfred, but Moïna’s flippant responses only deepened the Marquise’s sorrow. As she left the room, the Marquise felt a physical pain in her heart, realizing that her daughter had lost respect for her.

Outside, she collapsed on a bench, overwhelmed by grief and the realization that her daughter was lost to her. Moïna, unaware of the gravity of the situation, thought she could easily mend things with a few kind gestures. However, the Marquise’s condition worsened, and she was carried inside, lifeless.

Moïna, witnessing her mother’s decline, was struck by remorse. She had taken her mother’s love for granted, and now it was too late for reconciliation. As she wept, the Marquise, in her final moments, managed to smile at her daughter, a testament to a mother’s enduring love and forgiveness.

Servants rushed to fetch a physician, but the Marquise’s fate was sealed. Moïna, now fully aware of her loss, stood before her family, a hollow figure of regret, realizing the depth of her mother’s sacrifice and love.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In the spring of 1822, Parisian doctors sent a young man, M. le Baron Gaston de Nueil, to Lower Normandy for recovery from an inflammatory illness, likely caused by overwork or excess. Seeking rest, a light diet, and fresh air, he settled in Bayeux, where he was warmly welcomed by his relatives, who viewed his arrival as a blessing.

Small towns share common traits, and after a few evenings with his cousin, Mme. de Sainte-Severe, Gaston quickly met the local elite, who embodied the typical characters found in provincial France. Among them was a noble family with a long but obscure lineage, whose patriarch was a determined sportsman, dismissive of modernity and the new political order. His wife, a loud and domineering figure, believed their daughters would secure wealth through their name alone. They clung to outdated customs, reflecting a bygone era of nobility without the accompanying wealth or influence.

In contrast, another family, wealthier but of more recent origin, embraced contemporary Parisian trends. The wife, though somewhat behind the fashion, was a woman of society, while the husband, a member of the Departmental Council, was savvy in financial matters and well-versed in political gossip. Their Royalism was less fervent than that of the noble family, as they engaged with modernity while still maintaining a connection to their roots.

The local bishop, a former Vicar-General, navigated the tensions between these two families, respected for his religious standing but often reminded of his plebeian origins. Other notable figures included retired military officers and their families, who, while maintaining a semblance of aristocracy, were more concerned with local affairs than national politics. The women in these families often dressed in a manner reminiscent of courtly life, yet their lives were marked by routine and tradition.

This exclusive social circle also included a few wealthy townspeople who had managed to infiltrate the aristocracy, often viewed with skepticism by the older generation. The presence of ecclesiastics added a touch of diversity, as they brought wit and a bourgeois element to the otherwise staid gatherings.

The collective mindset of this society was characterized by antiquated beliefs and a resistance to change. Conversations echoed the same themes, with members offering immutable judgments on various matters, creating a stagnant intellectual environment. New ideas were rarely welcomed, and any outsider who dared to critique their way of life was quickly labeled as a misfit.

Before Gaston arrived, he had already been evaluated by the local elite, with his cousin sharing details about his fortune and family background. At just twenty-three, he was welcomed into their fold, recognized as a promising young man with an income of eighteen thousand livres from his land in the Auge valley. His father was negotiating to acquire additional woodland, further solidifying Gaston’s status.

However, Gaston’s family dynamics were complicated by the existence of an elder brother, who was consumptive and likely to die soon, a fact that was conveniently omitted by Mme. de Sainte-Severe. Initially, Gaston found amusement in observing the quirks of his new acquaintances, sketching mental portraits of their eccentricities and enjoying their quaintness. Yet, as time passed, he grew weary of their monotonous lives, feeling trapped in a cloistered existence devoid of stimulation.

This sense of confinement led to a crisis of spirit, a blend of ennui and discontent. As he adapted to the unchanging environment, he began to lose touch with the vibrant life he once knew in Paris. The once vivid memories of intellectual engagement faded, and he risked becoming just another fossil among the fossils, content to exist in a dull routine, much like the companions of Ulysses who settled for a life of complacency.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One evening, Gaston de Nueil sat between a dowager and a vicar-general in a gray-paneled drawing-room, where family portraits overlooked four card tables and sixteen people engaged in whist. As he digested a delightful dinner, he began to appreciate the customs of the provincial life around him, understanding why these people clung to their old cards and threadbare tablecloths. He found a certain philosophy in their monotonous routine and ignorance of luxury, leading him to question the value of opulence. The vibrant life of Paris felt like a distant memory.

His attention was caught by a young lady with red hands and a shy demeanor, initially appearing awkward and unattractive. However, a chance remark about Mme. de Beauseant stirred emotions within him, reminiscent of the stirring of good music amidst a tedious opera. An elderly lady mentioned that the Vicomtesse was unwell and had declined an invitation to dinner, which piqued Gaston's interest. The conversation revealed that Mme. de Beauseant had a scandalous past involving M. d’Ajuda-Pinto, leading to her isolation in Courcelles. Despite her noble lineage, society shunned her due to her separation from her husband, a situation that Gaston found intriguing.

Gaston was captivated by the idea of Mme. de Beauseant, viewing her as a figure of hope and desire amidst the dullness of provincial life. He felt a growing obsession with her, imagining her as a woman worth pursuing in a world that felt stagnant. His thoughts were consumed with how to gain access to her, recognizing the challenges posed by her intelligence and social standing. The next morning, he found himself wandering near Courcelles, hoping for a chance encounter, his imagination fueled by romantic notions.

He considered writing to her but hesitated, fearing rejection. Instead, he devised a plan to enlist M. de Champignelles to facilitate an introduction. He approached the Marquis, claiming he had a delicate matter to discuss with the Vicomtesse and requested that the Marquis inquire if she would receive him. Gaston’s charm and sincerity convinced the Marquis, who was eager to assist a pretty woman. M. de Champignelles hurried to Courcelles, where he relayed Gaston’s request, piquing Mme. de Beauseant’s curiosity despite her initial reluctance.

After some deliberation, she agreed to meet Gaston, intrigued by the mystery surrounding him. Meanwhile, Gaston was filled with excitement and anxiety about the upcoming meeting, hoping to impress her. He meticulously prepared for the encounter, believing that his appearance would influence her perception of him. Unbeknownst to him, true attraction stemmed from wit and character rather than mere looks.

As Gaston approached Courcelles, he felt a mix of confidence and nervousness. He was aware that entering a room with just one woman was more daunting than facing a crowd. When he was announced, he entered with a blend of ease and trepidation, ready to engage with the enigmatic Vicomtesse.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A great fire burned on the hearth despite the mild weather, casting a soft light over a young woman seated in a high-backed bergère by the fire. The Vicomtesse, with her graceful movements, stirred the flames and reached for a fallen glove, her delicate hand pointing to a chair for M. de Nueil to sit. Her charm and poise were striking, a blend of fastidious carelessness and high-bred elegance that made her stand out from the automatons he had encountered during his two-month exile in Normandy. In her presence, he felt transported back to Paris, surrounded by the beauty and sophistication he had missed.

Mme. de Beauseant had fair hair, dark eyes, and a pale complexion, exuding a regal air reminiscent of a fallen angel. Her hair, gathered into a crown of plaits, enhanced her noble appearance. Despite her beauty, there was an underlying sadness in her expression, hinting at past unhappiness and lost love. Alone in her silent salon, she was a woman cut off from the world, haunted by memories of a vibrant youth now replaced by emptiness. M. de Nueil felt humbled by her presence, captivated by the combination of beauty, nobility, and misfortune that surrounded her.

When she acknowledged him, he felt the weight of his own deception. He had come to deliver a message from M. de Champignelles, but the realization of his dishonesty made him blush. Summoning his courage, he confessed to her, “Madame, I do not deserve the happiness of seeing you. I have deceived you basely.” However, his apology was met with disdain; she rang for her servant to escort him out, her demeanor shifting from gracious to frigid.

As he left, M. de Nueil realized that if he departed, he would never return, and the Vicomtesse would think of him as a fool. He hesitated, contemplating the possibility that she might regret dismissing him. Driven by a sudden impulse, he turned back, claiming he had forgotten something. The servant, unaware of the true nature of his return, followed him back into the salon.
