“Jacques lighted me to the door,” he said with a half-sad smile, which softened Mme. de Beauseant’s demeanor. She invited him to sit, and he did so eagerly, his eyes shining with happiness. She felt a thrill at his delight, grateful for his understanding of her complex emotions.

“Madame,” he began, “my blunder you know, but you do not know how much I am to blame.” She interrupted him with a playful gesture, warning him to be careful. Her expression darkened as she reflected on her changed life, stating, “This is all very ridiculous! I cannot do as I like; I am obliged to think. What brings you here? Is it curiosity? If so, I am paying dearly for a fleeting pleasure.”

She accused him of disrespect and questioned whether he had come to pity her, asserting that she would rather be scorned than pitied. The tension in the room was palpable as she expressed her pain at his intrusion, urging him to understand that his behavior was improper. She commanded him not to return, fearing that their connection would lead to gossip about their relationship.

M. de Nueil acknowledged his wrongdoing, attributing it to youthful enthusiasm and a desire for happiness. He explained that his longing to see her was natural, describing the weariness of his exile and the fires of life burning within him. He painted a picture of a young man yearning for love, emphasizing that she had become the ideal woman in his dreams. He spoke of his feelings and the wild thoughts that had driven him to seek her out, hoping to evoke a sense of indulgence in her heart for the folly committed in her name.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
An impassioned voice resonated in the chill solitude, bringing warmth and youthful charm. Mme. de Beauseant, stirred by the genuine expression of feeling, found herself captivated by M. de Nueil’s noble countenance, untouched by the world's harsh realities. Gaston, in the prime of his youth, seemed to embody the ideal man, untainted by ambition or vanity. Both were drawn to each other, reflecting on their feelings while trying to conceal their thoughts. M. de Nueil saw in the Vicomtesse a rare woman, whose beauty was eclipsed by her depth of feeling and goodness. Mme. de Beauseant, listening to Gaston’s youthful troubles, recognized the struggles of those who, through hard work, remain untainted by the corrupting influences of society. 

As they delved into their sentiments, Gaston’s explorations were instinctive, while Mme. de Beauseant had a purpose, seeking to understand his views by presenting opposing opinions. Her wit and grace made her feel at ease with him, leading Gaston to exclaim, “Oh! madame, how could any man have left you?” This remark struck her deeply, reviving her sense of innocence and condemning the world for her past. It provided her with a sense of absolution and validation, allowing her to rise above her fall. 

She looked at the clock and, with a smile, acknowledged the fleeting nature of their encounter. Gaston, sensing the gravity of their situation, expressed his desire to prolong their time together. Mme. de Beauseant, however, explained that she must maintain her solitary life, fearing that any further connection would tarnish her reputation. She spoke candidly about her past, revealing the pain of her forsaken love and the lessons learned from her solitude. 

Her eloquence and beauty captivated Gaston, who felt a longing to express his feelings but restrained himself, fearing rejection. He struggled with his emotions, torn between admiration and the fear of making a fool of himself. Eventually, he confessed his feelings, expressing a desire to make her forget her past wounds. Mme. de Beauseant, however, reminded him that they must remain strangers, emphasizing the pain of being unable to follow the man she loves through life’s phases. 

After their conversation, Gaston left feeling more distant from her, questioning whether her charm was merely a display of coquetry. He returned home, consumed by thoughts of her, oscillating between despair and hope. At twenty-three, he grappled with the diffidence of youth, feeling unworthy of her love and fearing the difficulties of expressing his feelings. 

That night, he was restless, unable to sleep, and began writing letters to her, none of which satisfied him. The next day, he returned to Courcelles under the cover of night, hesitant to be seen. After much deliberation, he penned a heartfelt letter, expressing his deep feelings and the impact she had on his life. He implored her not to dismiss his words, revealing his vulnerability and desperation. 

However, as he awaited her response, he was plagued by doubts, fearing she would laugh at his letter and his love. He wished he could take back his words, feeling they were inadequate and pretentious. Overwhelmed by his emotions, he struggled to cope, longing for the comfort of friends who could distract him from his turmoil. 

In this state of despair, Gaston faced the harsh reality of unrequited love, feeling the weight of his youthful passion and the uncertainty of his future without Mme. de Beauseant.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At length, M. de Nueil received a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, marked with the arms of Burgundy. He locked himself away to read and re-read her words:

“You are punishing me for my attempt to spare you a rebuff and for my attraction to intellect. I trusted in your youth's generosity, but you have disappointed me. I spoke frankly about my position to show you that I am not to be swayed by youthful passion. My distress is heightened by my interest in you. I am kind-hearted, but circumstances force me to act otherwise. Another woman might have discarded your letter, but I read it and am responding. My answer will clarify that while I acknowledge your feelings, I do not share them. I wish to use the authority you give me over your life to help you see clearly.

“I am nearly thirty; you are just over twenty. You cannot know what your thoughts will be at my age. The vows you make lightly now may become burdensome later. I believe you would give your life for a moment of happiness, but as you age, you will find it hard to make sacrifices for me, and I would feel humiliated to accept them. A day will come when everything will urge you to leave me, and I have said that death is preferable to desertion. I must tell you that I do not love you; I cannot, and I will not. It is too late for me to yield to a blind impulse of the heart. My solace comes from God, not from earthly attachments. I read hearts too clearly to accept your friendship. I forgive your youthful folly, but for your sake and mine, I urge you to stay in your own country. Do not ruin a fair life for an illusion that cannot last. In the future, when you have achieved your destiny, you will appreciate my answer, even if you find it harsh now. You will cherish the friendship of an older woman, untainted by passion, and it will be sweet and precious to you. Farewell; think of me as you would an absent friend.”

Gaston de Nueil read her letter and wrote back:

“MADAME,--If I could cease to love you and accept the ordinary life you propose, I would deserve my fate. I will not do as you ask; my oath of fidelity will only be absolved by death. Take my life, unless you fear to carry remorse.”

When the messenger returned, M. de Nueil asked where he had left the note. 

“I gave it to Mme. la Vicomtesse; she was in her carriage, about to leave.”

“For the town?”

“I don’t think so; she had post-horses.”

“Ah! then she is going away,” said the Baron.

“Yes, sir.”

Gaston de Nueil immediately prepared to follow Mme. de Beauseant. She traveled to Geneva, unaware of his pursuit. His mind was consumed with one question: “Why did she leave?” He speculated that if she cared for him, she would choose Switzerland, where they could be free from judgment.

The Vicomtesse rented a small house by the lake. One summer evening, Gaston visited her. Jacques, the servant, announced him without surprise. Mme. de Beauseant dropped her book in shock, giving him a moment to approach her and express his joy at following her.

For three years, they lived in the villa by Lake Geneva, alone and without visitors, enjoying a happiness that seemed like a dream. Their home was simple yet perfect for lovers, with beautiful views of the lake and mountains. They cherished their time together, but weighty matters soon called M. de Nueil back to France after the deaths of his father and brother. They bought the house, wishing they could take it with them.

Mme. de Beauseant followed him, selling her property to buy an estate near Manerville, adjacent to Gaston’s lands. They lived together, with Gaston graciously allowing his mother to occupy Manerville, enjoying their secluded happiness once more.

For nine years, they experienced bliss that transcended description. However, Mme. de Beauseant’s husband, the Marquis, remained in good health, complicating their situation. He was a methodical man, aware of the benefits of his life, which made it impossible for the Marquise to marry M. de Nueil.

After nine years of happiness, they found themselves in a precarious situation. Mme. la Comtesse de Nueil, Gaston’s mother, would never accept Mme. de Beauseant. The Marquise understood that the dowager would try to pull Gaston away from their relationship. She considered selling her estate and returning to Geneva but could not bring herself to do so, fearing it would show distrust in M. de Nueil.

A young heiress, Mlle. de la Rodiere, moved nearby, and Gaston met her at Manerville. This prompted a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, revealing her inner turmoil:

“My beloved angel, it seems absurd to write when we are together, but there are things I cannot say face to face. I feel anguish. You have erased my past pain, and I did not know what love was until I met you. I have never felt jealousy in our nine years together, and I have known infinite happiness. But now, I fear that you may feel burdened by our relationship. You are thirty; I am forty. This age difference weighs heavily on me. You may have thought of your future, of a marriage that would bring you wealth and children. To owe your love to pity is a painful thought. 

“Have you considered leaving me? I sensed your hesitation, and it made me cry. I am too proud to let you see my tears, but I did not reproach you. I should not bind your future to my fading life. But what if I am mistaken? If you still love me, come to me after reading this letter. I will love you more than ever. Speak openly; do not deceive me. If you wish for your freedom, I will not let you see my suffering. I love you enough to prioritize your happiness over mine.”

When Mme. de Beauseant learned that her letter was in M. de Nueil’s hands, she felt overwhelmed, her thoughts numbing her senses. Meanwhile, M. de Nueil, reading her letter, found himself in a difficult position, a crisis that many young men face.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By this time, he had nearly succumbed to his mother’s pleas and the allure of Mlle. de la Rodiere, a rather unremarkable young woman with a substantial dowry of forty thousand livres. Mme. de Nueil, with maternal affection, sought to guide her son toward virtuous choices, emphasizing the honor of being chosen by Mlle. de la Rodiere, who had turned down many prestigious suitors. She urged him to consider his future, noting that he could one day have an income of eighty thousand livres from land, which would make life easier. If Mme. de Beauseant truly loved him, she should encourage him to marry. Thus, the well-meaning mother employed every argument to sway her son, leaving him in a state of indecision.

Just as Gaston’s feelings for Mme. de Beauseant were resisting the pull of a conventional life, a letter from her changed everything. He resolved to end his relationship with the Marquise and marry. “One must live a man’s life,” he told himself, but he was also acutely aware of the pain this decision would inflict on Mme. de Beauseant. His vanity and conscience amplified his sorrow, and he felt a genuine pity for her. He decided to soften the blow by gradually preparing her for their separation, initially sacrificing Mlle. de la Rodiere to ease the transition. He planned to marry later, respecting Mme. de Beauseant’s wishes, and intended to write her a letter to quell her suspicions. However, he realized that for a woman with such keen perception, a letter could be devastating.

When Jacques delivered the letter to Mme. de Beauseant, she trembled, sensing the impending loss. If he did not come to her in person, she feared all was lost. Yet, the hopes of a woman in love can endure much pain. As she read his words, a voice within her cried, “He lies!” She quickly discerned that this was not a heartfelt declaration but a calculated message. In despair, she tore the letter and cast it into the fire, feeling betrayed and abandoned.

M. de Nueil left to find Jacques, who soon returned with a note. “Madame la Marquise has left the chateau,” he informed him. M. de Nueil opened the note to find it was his own letter from nine years prior, in which he had sworn eternal fidelity to Mme. de Beauseant. At the bottom, Claire de Bourgogne had written, “Monsieur, you are free.” Shocked, M. de Nueil went to his mother and, within three weeks, married Mlle. Stephanie de la Rodiere.

If this ordinary tale ended here, it would seem trivial. However, the notoriety of the tragedy, being a true story, resonates with those who have experienced the profound joys of love and the pain of its loss. After parting from M. de Nueil, Mme. de Beauseant never left Valleroy. She lived in seclusion, rarely seen by anyone except her servants, and attended mass daily.

M. de Nueil, shortly after his marriage, fell into a state of emotional indifference, which could be interpreted as either happiness or unhappiness. His mother claimed he was perfectly happy. Mme. Gaston de Nueil, like many young women, was sweet but lacked depth. A month after their marriage, she expected a child, which aligned with societal norms. However, two months later, M. de Nueil became increasingly pensive. 

After seven months of this lukewarm happiness, a seemingly trivial incident occurred that hinted at deeper turmoil. One day, after hunting, M. de Nueil crossed Mme. de Beauseant’s park and asked Jacques if she still enjoyed game. Jacques confirmed, and M. de Nueil offered him a sum of money to set aside the game he shot for her. Jacques saw no harm in this, and for days, M. de Nueil returned home with an empty bag, while Jacques discreetly delivered the game to the Marquise. 

Eventually, M. de Nueil grew bold enough to write a letter to Mme. de Beauseant, but it was returned unopened. One evening, as his wife played the piano, he suddenly rushed to the Marquise’s room. There, he found her in a state of despair, her posture reflecting hopelessness. When she saw him, she threatened to throw herself out the window if he approached. Terrified, M. de Nueil fled.

Back at home, he wrote another letter, insisting it was a matter of life and death, and sent it with his servant. An hour later, the servant returned with the letter unopened. In despair, M. de Nueil retreated to another room and shot himself.

The tragic conclusion of this story, though contrary to the norms of young France, was perhaps inevitable. Those who have experienced the profound connection between two souls will understand Gaston de Nueil’s suicide. Love requires time and nurturing, and when a man forsakes a deep love for a loveless marriage, he faces a choice between despair and a cold, materialistic existence.

Mme. de Beauseant likely did not foresee that her friend’s anguish could lead to such a drastic end. She may have believed she alone would suffer. Nevertheless, she rightly refused to accept a humiliating position; a wife may compromise for social reasons, but a mistress cannot accept such a fate, as the purity of her passion demands respect.

ANGOULEME, September 1832.

ADDENDUM

The following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:

- Beauseant, Marquise de: Letters of Two Brides
- Beauseant, Vicomte de: Father Goriot
- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot, Albert Savarus
- Champignelles, De: The Seamy Side of History
- Jacques (M. de Beauseant’s butler): Father Goriot
- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman, Albert Savarus
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

La Grenadiere is a quaint house located on the right bank of the Loire, about a mile downstream from the bridge of Tours. The river, broad and dotted with green islands, flows between cliffs adorned with white stone country houses nestled among gardens and vineyards. This region, with its southern exposure, produces some of the finest fruit, thanks to generations of labor that have created terraces in the cliffs, allowing for the cultivation of hot-climate produce outdoors.

A church spire marks the nearby village of Saint-Cyr, and further along, the Choisille river flows into the Loire through a fertile valley. La Grenadiere, situated halfway up the hillside and about a hundred paces from the church, is an old-fashioned house dating back two to three hundred years, typical of the picturesque spots in Touraine. A fissure in the rock provides steps leading down to the “dike,” the local embankment that keeps the Loire in its bed and serves as a causeway from Paris to Nantes. At the top of the steps, a gate opens onto a narrow footpath flanked by terraces, where walls prevent landslips. The path is almost concealed by trees, and the view of the river expands as one ascends.

At the end of the path, a crumbling Gothic archway leads into a small garden filled with flowers and rose bushes, won from the rock on the highest terrace. A wooden summer-house stands nearby, adorned with jessamine and honeysuckle. The house itself is centrally located in this garden, above a vine-covered flight of steps, with an arched doorway leading to extensive cellars carved into the rock. Surrounding the house are trellised vines and pomegranate trees, which give the property its name. The front features two large windows flanking a rustic door and three dormer windows in a high-pitched slate roof. The walls are washed in yellow, with green-painted doors and shutters.

Inside, a crooked staircase leads to a lobby. To the right is a large dining room with white tile flooring, and to the left is a similarly sized sitting room with saffron-colored walls and visible walnut rafters. The first floor contains two whitewashed bedrooms with simpler stone chimney-pieces. Most doors and windows face south, except for one leading to the vineyard. A small timber-framed kitchen, a modern addition, connects to the house and features a rustic pump and well, indicating that La Grenadiere was originally a vintage house for townsfolk in Tours, who would visit for picnics or during grape harvests.

The property includes two acres of vineyard, with a steep slope at the back. A vinedresser’s cottage leans against the western gable, and stone walls or espaliers surround the area, maximizing the use of the land. Nature fills any overlooked spaces with fig trees, wildflowers, or strawberries.

La Grenadiere is a humble yet imposing dwelling, rich in fruit and fragrant scents, embodying the beauty of Touraine. It features a variety of grapes, figs, peaches, and melons, alongside exotic plants like Spanish broom and Italian oleanders. The Loire flows below, and the evening breeze carries scents of the sea and distant flowers. The landscape transforms with the clouds, offering views of the south bank of the Loire, Tours, and the surrounding hills dotted with chateaux and parks. 

This charming vintage house, owned by a prosperous burgess of Tours, appeals to all imaginations, providing a peaceful retreat for lovers and dreamers alike. The atmosphere is imbued with happiness, and the sounds of the river inspire contemplation. The owner can sit among the perennial flowers and fruit, feeling the world’s vibrancy around him.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
If an Englishman rents La Grenadiere for the summer, he pays a thousand francs for six months, excluding vineyard produce. If he wants orchard fruit, the rent doubles; for the vintage, it doubles again. What could La Grenadiere be worth? With its stone staircase, triple terrace, two acres of vineyard, flowering roses, and well-head, it seems priceless. It has not been on the market since it was sold in 1690 for forty thousand francs, remaining a cherished family possession since then. The view from La Grenadiere encompasses three valleys of Touraine and cathedral towers, making it impossible to assign a monetary value to the health and beauty it offers.

In the spring of a bright year during the Restoration, a lady named Mme. Willemsens arrived in Tours with her housekeeper and two children, a thirteen-year-old boy and an eight-year-old boy. She chose La Grenadiere, perhaps drawn by its distance from the town. She converted the drawing-room into a bedroom, gave the children the two rooms above, and the housekeeper slept in a closet. The dining-room served as a combined sitting and drawing room, furnished simply yet tastefully, with no luxury items. The walnut furniture was plain, and the house's charm lay in its neatness and harmony with the surroundings.

It was hard to classify Mme. Willemsens socially; her plain dress led to various assumptions, but her manners suggested a higher status. Her reserve piqued the curiosity of the local populace, who were eager for any distraction in their lives. Mme. Willemsens was tall and slender, with delicate features and a melancholy expression. Her dark eyes, sometimes revealing secret anguish, were framed by premature wrinkles. She wore a long black gown, a silk ribbon at the waist, and a bonnet in the English style, suggesting a commitment to mourning. Her health appeared fragile, and she took walks with her children to the bridge of Tours to enjoy the fresh air and sunset views.

During her time at La Grenadiere, she visited Tours only twice: once to inquire about the best teachers for her children and again to arrange their lessons. Her occasional appearances on the bridge attracted the interest of the townspeople, but little was known about her true status. The owner of La Grenadiere mentioned to friends that she signed the lease as Augusta Willemsens, Countess of Brandon, hinting at her husband's name. This revelation remained within the business community.

Mme. Willemsens was a mystery to the local elite. Her natural manners and sweet voice charmed those around her, but her sadness and beauty made her seem almost untouchable. Several young men fell in love with her, but their timidity prevented them from approaching her. She burned all letters she received, indicating her desire for solitude and a life free from outside concerns.

The three tutors allowed at La Grenadiere admired the close bond between Mme. Willemsens and her children. The boys, resembling their mother, drew envy from local mothers. Louis-Gaston, the elder, had dark hair and a robust demeanor, while Marie-Gaston, the younger, had almost golden hair and a delicate appearance. Their contrasting looks highlighted their different personalities, with Louis displaying a protective nature towards his brother.

The boys were well-behaved and attentive, reflecting their mother's careful upbringing. They rose early, prayed, and maintained a scrupulous morning routine. Their interactions with their mother were filled with affection, laughter, and a deep understanding of her needs. Mme. Willemsens fostered a nurturing environment, ensuring her children felt loved and valued.

Their mornings included playful moments, with Louis often taking charge to protect Marie from fatigue. He sensed his mother's struggles, noting the signs of her declining health. Despite her reassurances, he remained vigilant, aware of the unspoken worries that lingered in their home. The bond between them was profound, characterized by a shared life of love and mutual understanding, untouched by external influences.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mme. Willemsens dressed during her children's early breakfast and play, wanting to be a pleasing vision for them. She was always ready to hear their lessons from ten to three, with a lunch break in the summer-house where they all ate together. After lunch, the children played while she rested on a sofa, enjoying the beautiful landscape of Touraine.

The children explored the orchard and garden, chasing lizards and investigating nature, often running to their mother with questions. Mme. Willemsens embroidered during their lessons, attentively following along without interrupting. She focused on her eldest son, Louis, treating him with respect and nurturing his ambition, while she asked little about Marie. After lessons, she would walk the tutor to the gate, inquiring about Louis’ progress, and the tutors would honestly share his strengths and weaknesses.

Their days were filled with a balance of work and play, leaving no room for discouragement or quarrels. Mme. Willemsens’ love smoothed over any difficulties, teaching her sons moderation and submission through her nurturing. She often felt tears of joy watching them play, reflecting on their happiness and her own.

Those hours spent in the garden-house were precious, filled with laughter and the children’s voices. They spoke both English and French, thanks to their English nurse, and their mother skillfully guided their learning, ensuring they understood the world around them. She provided them with interesting books about great figures in history, using these stories to teach them about ambition and success.

As the children listened to stories, little Marie would often fall asleep on her mother’s knee. However, after these readings, Mme. Willemsens would sometimes become pensive and tearful. One evening, Louis noticed her tears and asked why she was crying. She explained that she feared for their future, knowing she would leave them alone and without resources. She expressed her deep love for them, which made her sadness even more profound.

Louis, confused, promised her he would take care of Marie. She reassured him that her love kept her alive, and perhaps the sunny air of Touraine would help her recover. From that day, as she spoke of her impending death, Louis became more focused on his studies, adapting their lives to her melancholy. The once vibrant household grew quieter, and the old servant, Annette, watched with concern as Mme. Willemsens’ health declined.

By August, the family’s life had changed significantly. Mme. Willemsens, still beautiful but increasingly frail, made an effort to maintain her appearance. The house, once lively, felt somber, and they rarely ventured out. Louis, feeling a deep connection to his mother, noticed her exhaustion and often suggested shorter walks.

As autumn arrived, bringing sunny days and ripe fruits, Mme. Willemsens spent her time solely with her children, cherishing each moment. Meanwhile, Louis worked diligently at night, making significant progress in his studies, preparing for the entrance examination to the Ecole polytechnique.

He often visited the bridge of Tours, where he met a retired naval officer who shared tales of adventure. Louis, eager for knowledge about life in the military, asked many questions and learned about the hardships of such a life. Encouraged by his mother, he began taking long walks to build his endurance, climbing trees and learning to swim. He transformed from a boy into a young man, his sunburned face reflecting a newfound purpose.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When October arrived, Mme. Willemsens could only rise at noon. The warmth of the Loire made the air feel almost like the Bay of Naples, prompting the faculty to recommend the area. At midday, she sat under the shade with her two boys, who never left her side. With lessons over, they sought a life of closeness, free from outside worries. The elder boy lay beside her, basking in her affection, while Marie, the more restless child, gathered flowers for her. Mme. Willemsens, with her tired eyes and languid movements, never complained, smiling at her vibrant children—a poignant scene enhanced by the autumn's melancholy beauty.

Eventually, the doctor ordered Mme. Willemsens to stay in her room. It was brightened daily by her favorite flowers, and her children were always near. One early November day, she sat at the piano for the last time, gazing at a Swiss landscape above it and her children outside. Her fingers danced over the keys, filled with passionate emotion. When the doctor arrived, he insisted she stay in bed, leaving her in stunned silence.

After he left, she asked her older son, Louis, to take her out to the terrace for one last view of her homeland. As they stepped outside, her gaze turned skyward, captivated by the clouds. A look of anguish crossed her face as she clutched her children's hands, lamenting their uncertain future. She feared for them, wondering what they would think of her life when they grew older. After a moment of solitude, she saw Louis and Marie kneeling beside her, their love evident. Wishing to take their smiles with her, she returned to bed, never to rise again.

Days passed in a blur, with Annette and Louis keeping vigil over her. The atmosphere was heavy with the dread of impending loss. On the fifth day, the doctor banned flowers from her room, signaling the decline of life. As her condition worsened, Louis refused to leave her side. One quiet Sunday night, he noticed her hand move the curtain, and she spoke solemnly, telling him that tomorrow would be her last day. She instructed him to retrieve important papers from her table, including their birth certificates and a letter she wished to send to Lord Brandon, her estranged husband.

As she dictated the letter, she hesitated at how to sign it, finally deciding on "Louis-Gaston." She then revealed that she had twelve thousand francs saved, all that remained for them. She urged him to keep the money close, as everything would be sealed after her death. Louis vowed to care for Marie, planning to send her to school while he apprenticed at sea, determined to return successful.

Her eyes sparkled with joy at his resolve, and she felt a surge of hope. However, her strength waned, and she requested a priest. Louis fetched Annette, who hurried to find one. The next morning, surrounded by loved ones, Mme. Willemsens received the sacrament, her children kneeling beside her. As her breathing slowed, the room fell silent, and she eventually passed away, leaving her children in shock.

After her death, Annette and the vinedresser’s wife prepared her body for burial, following local customs. Only a few mourners attended her funeral, a stark contrast to the grand send-off she would have received had she lived a different life. Marie, still too young to grasp the finality of death, cried as they buried her.

A simple cross marked her grave, inscribed with a plea for prayers. After the burial, the children returned to La Grenadiere for a final look at their home before leaving with Annette, who took them to her cousin’s house in Tours. Louis shared his plans with Annette, entrusting her with Marie’s birth certificate and the money for her care. The next day, he took Marie to school, offering her heartfelt advice before parting ways.

A month later, Louis-Gaston, now an apprentice on a warship, watched the French coast fade into the horizon. Alone on the vast ocean, he felt lost but was comforted by an old sailor's words. With newfound resolve, he embraced his life at sea, determined to honor his mother’s memory.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

I have always wanted to tell a simple and true story that would terrify two young lovers, driving them to seek refuge in each other's hearts, much like children clinging together at the sight of a snake. I acknowledge that my intention may spoil the story and make me seem vain.

I played a part in this almost ordinary tragedy, so if it fails to engage you, the fault lies partly with me and partly with the mundane nature of reality. Life often lacks interest, and it is the artist's task to select from reality those elements that hold poetic potential.

In 1819, I was traveling from Paris to Moulins and, due to my finances, took an outside seat on the coach. An Englishman, seemingly better off, joined me, and we quickly bonded over our shared love for fresh air and the beautiful landscape. Our conversation soon turned to women and love, and we found ourselves admiring "the woman of a certain age," specifically those between thirty-five and forty. A poet listening to our discussion would have found a wealth of passionate expressions and tender confidences. Our bashful fears and silent interjections conveyed a youthful charm that I no longer possess.

We agreed that a certificate of birth was meaningless and that many women were younger at forty than some girls at twenty. This belief opened the door to a boundless exploration of love. As we described our mistresses—young, charming, and devoted—my companion revealed that his lady was thirty-eight, while I confessed to adoring a woman of forty. This admission freed us to share our romantic follies, each trying to outdo the other in sentiment.

One had traveled six hundred miles for a brief meeting with his mistress, while the other had risked danger to meet his lady in secret. We recounted our joys and perils, reliving those moments of delight. My friend's countess had lit a cigar for him; mine made chocolate and wrote daily when we were apart. Our countesses were adored by their husbands, who, in their simplicity, provided just enough danger to heighten our pleasure. But soon, our laughter was swept away by the wind.

Upon reaching Pouilly, I observed my new friend closely. He was a well-proportioned young man with an expressive face, dark hair, blue eyes, and a certain pallor that suggested he was recovering from an illness. He had shapely hands and appeared well-informed and clever, making him worthy of a countess. He was a Vicomte with a respectable income, and many a girl would have desired him.

However, disaster struck about a league out of Pouilly when the coach overturned. My unfortunate companion jumped to escape but miscalculated and was crushed beneath the vehicle. We carried him to a peasant's cottage, where, in agony, he entrusted me with a sacred task—delivering the news of his death to his mistress. He worried about the shock she would feel upon reading of his demise in a newspaper. He asked me to retrieve a key he wore around his neck, which I found embedded in his flesh. After giving me directions to his home in La Charite-sur-Loire for his love letters, he grew speechless and died, leaving me with the burden of his last wish.

The coachman remarked that it was partly my friend's fault, but I was consumed by the task ahead. At La Charite, I fulfilled his wishes, but his mother was away, leaving me to comfort an old servant who fainted at the news of her master's death. I took the sealed letters, burdened by the sorrow of a woman who had lost her last love.

The Countess' chateau was eight leagues beyond Moulins, and I had little money for the journey. Nevertheless, my youthful enthusiasm propelled me to walk as quickly as possible, eager to deliver the bad news before anyone else. As I approached the Chateau de Montpersan, I felt increasingly uneasy about my role as the bearer of such tragic news.

Upon reaching the chateau, I found the doors open, which unsettled me. I entered and was met by barking dogs and a servant-maid who directed me to the park where Madame was. I felt lost, unsure of how to find her. A little girl overheard my inquiry and, with a playful demeanor, led me through the winding paths of the park, calling for her mother.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
I must confess that I paused behind the last shrub on the avenue, adjusting my shabby attire in hopes that the lady would not mistake me for a local rate collector. Reflecting on that youthful episode now brings me amusement.

Just as I composed myself, I spotted Juliette and her husband among the flowers, the lady's pace quickening at her child's ambiguous remark. Taken aback by my presence, she regarded me with a cool expression that revealed her disappointment. I struggled to recall the elegant phrases I had prepared.

This hesitation allowed her husband to approach. Thoughts raced through my mind as I awkwardly inquired if they were indeed M. le Comte and Mme. la Comtesse de Montpersan. My clumsy questions gave me time to assess the couple. The husband, a typical provincial nobleman, wore large shoes that left a deeper impression on me than his worn coat and trousers. He exuded a mix of self-importance and a lack of manners, a man led by his wife yet believing himself the master.

In stark contrast, the Countess was petite and graceful, her delicate figure and enchanting shape evoking a desire for possession. Dressed in white muslin with rose-colored accents, she appeared youthful and vibrant, reminiscent of the Comtesse de Lignolles and the Marquise de B----, two types familiar to any young man who has read Louvet's romance.

Recognizing the situation, I took a diplomatic approach, addressing the Count with an air of mystery, requesting a private word. He obliged, leaving Juliette to wander off, confident she could learn her husband's secrets at will.

I informed the Count of my traveling companion's death, noting his genuine affection for the man. He expressed concern for his wife, indicating the need for caution in delivering the news. I explained that I could not approach the Countess without first informing him, emphasizing the sacred nature of the message entrusted to me. The Count, flattered by my words, allowed me to proceed.

As we approached the house, Juliette cast a glance at us, her expression a mix of curiosity and disdain, likely due to my appearance and the unexpected guest. I recalled her beauty, standing in the sunlit alley, and sighed.

"Alas, madame, I have made a difficult journey solely for you," I began. Her face paled at my words.

"Is he ill?" she asked, her voice dropping.

"Yes, but please control yourself. He entrusted me with secrets concerning you, and I assure you, I am a discreet and devoted messenger."

"What is wrong with him?" she pressed.

"What if he no longer loves you?" I replied, and her reaction was immediate.

"That is impossible!" she exclaimed, a faint smile breaking through. But then, panic set in, and she asked, "Is he alive?"

The weight of her question struck me, and I could only nod in response. Her distress was palpable.

"Give me an answer!" she cried.

"Yes, madame."

"Is it true? Tell me the truth! Any pain would be better than this suspense."

I could only respond with tears, and she leaned against a tree, crying out in despair.

"Your husband is coming!" I warned.

"Do I have a husband?" she fled from sight.

The Count called me to dinner, and I followed him into the dining room, where a lavish meal awaited. The Countess was notably absent, and the canon of Saint-Denis inquired about her whereabouts. The Count dismissed the concern, hastily serving dinner.

After a while, a maid entered, reporting that the Countess was missing. Alarmed, I rushed out with the canon and the maid, searching the garden and park for her. As we called out, I recounted the tragic accident, and the maid's concern for her mistress became evident.

Eventually, we heard a muffled cry from a granary. Inside, we found Juliette, buried in hay, sobbing in despair. The maid and I helped her to her room, where I instructed that she not be disturbed, claiming she had a headache.

When we returned to the dining room, I was surprised to find the Count still eating, seemingly unfazed by his wife's absence. His indifference was explained by a serious medical condition that had left him ravenous, overshadowing his concern for Juliette.

The evening was somber. I was exhausted, and the canon pondered the reason for Juliette's tears. The Count remained preoccupied with his meal, content with a vague explanation relayed through the maid. We all retired early.

As I passed the Countess's room, I asked the servant for news. Juliette called me in, attempting to speak but unable to. I left, feeling the weight of her agitation.

Later that night, I was awakened by the sound of curtain rings. The Countess sat at the foot of my bed, her face pale and drawn.

"Is it true?" she asked, her voice trembling. "I don't know how I can live after this blow, but I want to know everything."

Her appearance shocked me; she had lost her youthful glow, her eyes red and swollen. I recounted the accident briefly, avoiding distressing details. She listened intently, leaning forward as if to catch every word.

When I shared the dying man's fears and the message he had entrusted to me, her tears dried, replaced by a deep pallor. I presented her with letters from him, and she took them mechanically, lamenting that she had burned all his letters, leaving her with nothing.

"I cut this lock of his hair," she said, revealing a precious token. I handed it to her, and her tears fell on my hands, a moment of profound gratitude and connection.

"Ah! You love! May you always be happy," she said, clutching the lock and fleeing with her treasure.

The next morning felt surreal, and I searched for the letters under my pillow, finding nothing. I spent the day with Juliette, witnessing her noble spirit and selflessness.

In the evening, the Comte de Montpersan accompanied me to Moulins, asking if I could deliver a sum of money to a contact in Paris. I agreed, taking a rouleau of twenty-five louis d'or, which covered my journey back. Upon arrival, I realized the delicate way Juliette had arranged the loan, revealing the depth of her love and thoughtfulness. What a joy it was to share this adventure with a woman who cherished me, saying, "Oh, my dear, not you! You must not die!"
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

GOBSECK

By Honore De Balzac

Translated By Ellen Marriage

DEDICATION

To M. le Baron Barchou de Penhoen.

Among all the pupils of the Oratorian school at Vendome, we are, I think, the only two who have afterwards met in mid-career of a life of letters. When we met, you were engaged upon your noble works on German philosophy, and I on this study. So neither of us has missed his vocation; and you, when you see your name here, will feel as much pleasure as I do in inscribing my work to you. Your old schoolfellow, 1840 De Balzac.

GOBSECK

It was one o’clock in the morning during the winter of 1829-30, but in the Vicomtesse de Grandlieu’s salon, two guests remained who were not part of her family. A young man took his leave, and as the sound of a departing carriage faded, the Vicomtesse turned to her daughter, Camille, who was by the chimney-piece, seemingly examining a fire-screen but listening intently to the courtyard sounds, which raised maternal concerns.

“Camille,” the Vicomtesse said, “if you continue to treat young Comte de Restaud as you have this evening, I will have to prevent him from visiting. Trust my love and let me guide you. At seventeen, one cannot judge the past or future or social standings. M. de Restaud has a mother, Mlle. Goriot, who squandered her father’s fortune and is of no birth. Despite his admirable behavior towards her and his siblings, any family would hesitate to entrust their daughter’s future to him while she lives.”

“I overheard part of your conversation with Mlle. de Grandlieu,” interjected Derville, a family friend. “I have won, M. le Comte,” he added, turning to his opponent. “I shall assist your niece.”

“See what it is to have an attorney’s ears!” exclaimed the Vicomtesse. “How could you know what I was saying to Camille?”

“I knew it from your looks,” Derville replied, settling into a chair by the fire.

Camille’s uncle approached her, and the Vicomtesse took a seat on a hearth stool between them.

“It’s time for a story that may change your view of Ernest de Restaud’s prospects,” Derville said.

“A story?” Camille exclaimed. “Please begin!”

Derville’s glance at the Vicomtesse indicated the tale was for her. The Vicomtesse de Grandlieu was a prominent figure in Faubourg Saint-Germain due to her wealth and lineage. Although it may seem unusual for a Paris attorney to be so familiar with her, it was easily explained.

Upon returning to France with the Royal family, the Vicomtesse lived on a pension from Louis XVIII, a situation she found intolerable. The Hotel de Grandlieu had been sold during the Republic. Derville discovered flaws in the title and successfully initiated proceedings to reclaim it for her. He also won lawsuits that restored significant property, increasing her income to around sixty thousand francs, along with substantial indemnities. Derville, a man of high character and modesty, became a close family friend.

His management of the Vicomtesse’s affairs earned him respect in Faubourg Saint-Germain, and he counted many elite families among his clients. However, he did not exploit his popularity for personal gain. The Vicomtesse encouraged him to pursue a magistracy, but he consistently declined, preferring to maintain his practice and connections. His dedication to the Vicomtesse had brought his talents to light, and he had been visiting the Hotel de Grandlieu more frequently since Ernest de Restaud’s arrival, especially as Camille showed interest in him.

At a recent ball, Derville had remarked to Camille, “It’s a pity that young man doesn’t have two or three million francs, isn’t it?”

“Is it a pity? I don’t think so,” she replied. “M. de Restaud is capable and well-educated. He will have as much money as he wishes when he comes into power.”

“But what if he were already rich?” Derville countered.

“Rich already?” Camille blushed. “All the girls would be vying for him,” she said, glancing at the dancers.

“Then Mlle. de Grandlieu might not be the one he favors,” Derville teased. “That blush says it all! You like him, don’t you? Speak up.”

Suddenly, Camille stood to leave.

“She loves him,” Derville thought.

Since that evening, Camille had been unusually attentive to Derville, who approved of her feelings for Ernest. Although she recognized her family’s debt to him, she had previously felt more respect than warmth towards him, maintaining a polite distance. Gratitude often weighs heavily on the next generation, who may wish to distance themselves from it.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“This adventure,” Derville began, “brings to mind the one romantic event in my life. You may find it ridiculous that an attorney speaks of romance, but once I was twenty-five and had seen some strange things. I should start by telling you about someone you could not possibly know—a usurer.

“Can you picture his sallow, wan face? I wish the _Academie_ would allow me to call such faces the _lunar_ type. His hair was iron-gray, sleek, and carefully combed; his features were as impassive as Talleyrand’s. His little yellow eyes, devoid of eyelashes, peered from under a shabby cap, as if afraid of the light. He had thin lips reminiscent of Rembrandt’s portraits of alchemists and a sharp nose that resembled a gimlet. His voice was low and suave; he never lost his temper. It was hard to tell his age; he seemed to have either aged prematurely or conserved his youth.

“His room was as clean and threadbare as that of an elderly spinster. In winter, the fire smoldered in a bank of ashes, never producing a flame. He moved through his day with the regularity of a pendulum, stopping mid-sentence when a cart passed by to save his voice. He was thrifty with his pulse, concentrating all human sensibility within himself.

“His life flowed soundlessly, like the sands of an hourglass. His victims sometimes erupted in rage, only to fall silent afterward, much like a kitchen after a fowl’s neck has been wrung.

“Toward evening, this bill of exchange incarnate would take on a human shape, and his metals would transform into a human heart. When satisfied with his day’s business, he would rub his hands, and his inward glee would escape through the wrinkles of his face, akin to the soundless laughter of _Leather Stocking_. Even in joy, his conversation was limited to monosyllables, and he maintained a non-committal expression.

“This was the neighbor Chance found for me in the Rue de Gres, where I lived as a second clerk finishing my studies. The house was damp and dark, with all windows facing the street, resembling an old convent. It was so gloomy that the gaiety of eldest sons vanished before they reached my neighbor’s door. He and his house were alike; even so does the oyster resemble its rock.

“I was the only person he communicated with socially; he would come to borrow a light or a book, and in the evenings, he would allow me to visit him. These gestures of confidence resulted from four years of neighborly interaction and my sober conduct. I lived much like he did due to my lack of funds. Did he have relatives or friends? Was he rich or poor? No one could answer these questions. I never saw money in his room; his capital was likely secured in the Bank. He collected his debts himself, running all over Paris on skinny legs. Once, when he had gold in his pocket, a double napoleon fell out, and another lodger returned it to him.

“That isn’t mine!” he exclaimed, surprised. “If I were rich, would I live as I do?”

“He made his coffee every morning on a cast-iron chafing dish in the corner of the grate; his dinner came from a cookshop, and our old porter’s wife tidied his room at the prescribed hour. A whimsical chance, which Sterne would have seen as predestination, named him Gobseck. When I later did business for him, I learned he was about seventy-six when we met. Born around 1740 in an outlying suburb of Antwerp to a Dutch father and a Jewish mother, his name was Jean-Esther Van Gobseck. You may recall the murder case of _La belle Hollandaise_? I mentioned it to him, and he replied without interest, ‘She is my grandniece.’

“That was the only remark he made about the death of his sole surviving relative. Reports indicated that _La belle Hollandaise_ was named Sara Van Gobseck. When I asked how his grandniece bore his surname, he smiled, saying, ‘The women never marry in our family.’

“He had never cared to find out about any relatives over four generations on the female side. The thought of heirs was abhorrent to him; the idea of his wealth passing to others after his death was inconceivable.

“He was ten when his mother sent him as a cabin boy to the Dutch Straits Settlements, where he spent twenty years. The inscrutable lines on his sallow forehead concealed secrets of horrible adventures, sudden panic, unanticipated luck, and lost fortunes. He had known notable figures like Admiral Simeuse, M. de Lally, and Tippoo Sahib. His long stay in St. Thomas brought him into contact with notorious pirates. He had left no stone unturned in his quest for fortune, even attempting to discover treasure from a famous tribe in Buenos Ayres. He had personal knowledge of the American War of Independence. However, when he spoke of the Indies or America, he seemed to regard it as indiscretion and would repent afterward. If humanity and sociability are a religion, Gobseck might be considered an infidel; despite my efforts to understand him, his true nature remained impenetrable.

“Did he adhere to his mother’s religion? Did he view Gentiles as legitimate prey? Had he converted to Roman Catholicism, Lutheranism, or something else? I never learned his religious opinions; he appeared indifferent rather than incredulous.

“One evening, I visited this man who had turned himself to gold, the usurer whom his clients called Daddy Gobseck, perhaps ironically. He sat motionless in his armchair, staring at the mantel-shelf, seemingly reading his statements. A lamp burned in the room, but his face appeared paler against the background. He gestured to a chair for me but said nothing.

“What thoughts could this being have?” I wondered. “Does he know God exists? Does he know about feeling, women, happiness?” I felt pity for him, akin to pitying a diseased creature. Yet, I knew that while he had millions at his command, he possessed the world in idea—having explored, ransacked, weighed, appraised, and exploited it.

“‘Good day, Daddy Gobseck,’ I began.

“He turned his face toward me, slightly contracting his bushy black eyebrows, a characteristic expression that conveyed as much as a jubilant smile on a Southern face.

“‘You look as gloomy as you did when you heard about the failure of that bookseller you admired, though you were one of his victims.’

“‘One of his victims?’ he repeated, astonished.

“‘Yes. You refused to accept composition at the creditors’ meeting until he promised to pay you in full, and he gave you bills accepted by the bankrupt firm. When he resumed business, he paid you the dividend on those bills.’

“‘He was sharp, but I got it out of him.’

“‘Do you have bills to protest? Today is the 30th, I believe.’

“It was the first time I mentioned money to him. He looked at me ironically, then replied in his bland voice, ‘I am amusing myself.’

“‘So you amuse yourself now and again?’

“‘Do you think the only poets are those who print their verses?’ he asked, pityingly.

“‘Poetry in that head!’ I thought, knowing little of his life.

“‘What life could be as glorious as mine?’ he continued, his eyes lighting up. ‘You are young; your visions are colored by youthful blood. You see women’s faces in the fire, while I see only coals. You have beliefs; I have none. Keep your illusions if you can. I will show you life with the discount taken off. You can go wherever you like or stay at home with your wife, but eventually, you settle into a groove. Happiness consists in applying your faculties to realities. Anything more is false. My principles have varied among different men; I had to change them with every latitude. What we admire in Europe is punishable in Asia, and a vice in Paris becomes a necessity elsewhere. There are no hard-and-fast rules, only conventions adapted to the climate. Throw a man into various social melting pots, and convictions and moral systems become meaningless. The only constant is self-interest. If you lived as long as I have, you would know that the only concrete reality worth caring about is GOLD. Gold represents every form of human power. I have traveled and found either hills or plains everywhere; the plains are monotonous, the hills wearisome; thus, place is irrelevant. Manners are universal; the battle between the poor and the rich is inevitable everywhere. It is better to exploit than to be exploited. Everywhere, you find the laboring man and the idle man; pleasures are the same, and when all sensations are exhausted, only Vanity remains. Vanity is satisfied only by gold. Our dreams require time and means to realize. Gold contains everything in embryo; it realizes all things for us.

“‘Only fools and invalids find pleasure in shuffling cards for a few pence. Only dupes think they are useful by laying down political rules amid unpredictable events. Only simpletons delight in discussing stage players and repeating their sayings. What is all this but Parisian life summed up? Let us seek a higher outlook on life. Happiness consists in strong emotions that drain our vitality or in methodical occupation that makes existence like clockwork. A higher happiness consists in noble curiosity, a wish to learn Nature’s secrets, or to imitate Nature. In two words, that is Science and Art, or passion and calm. Every human passion, heightened in the struggle for existence, parades before me while I live in calm. As for scientific curiosity, I replace it with insight into the springs of action in man and woman. In summary, the world is mine without effort, and it has no hold on me. Listen,’ he continued, ‘I will tell you about my morning, and you will understand my pleasures.’

“He got up, bolted the door, drew a tapestry curtain across it, and sat down again.

“‘This morning,’ he said, ‘I had only two amounts to collect; the rest of the bills due I gave away instead of cash to my customers yesterday. So much saved, you see, for when I discount a bill, I always deduct two francs for a hired brougham—collection expenses. It would be absurd for my clients to make me trudge all over Paris for a few francs of discount when no man is my master, and I only pay seven francs in taxes.

“‘The first bill for a thousand francs was presented by a young fellow, a dandy with a spangled waistcoat, eyeglass, tilbury, and English horse. The bill bore the signature of one of the prettiest women in Paris, married to a Count, a great landowner. How did that Countess come to sign a bill of exchange, legally worthless but practically good business? These women, poor things, fear the scandal of a protested bill and would rather pay than fail. I wanted to find out what that bill truly represented—stupidity, imprudence, love, or charity?

“‘The second bill, signed “Fanny Malvaut,” came from a linen-draper on the brink of bankruptcy. No one with bank credit comes to _me_. The first step to my door means desperation; the news of his failure will soon emerge, and he has been everywhere else first. The stag is always at bay when I see him, and a pack of creditors is on his tail. The Countess lived in the Rue du Helder, and my Fanny in the Rue Montmartre. I made many conjectures as I set out this morning. If these two women could not pay, they would show me more respect than their fathers. What tricks would the Countess try for a thousand francs? She would be so nice to me, speaking in that ingratiating tone of endorsers, pouring out coaxing words, while I...’ (here the old man turned his pale eyes on me)—‘and I would remain unmoved, inexorable! So much for hypotheses. I reached the house.

“‘“Madame la Comtesse is asleep,” said the maid.

“‘“When can I see her?”

“‘“At twelve o’clock.”

“‘“Is Madame la Comtesse ill?”

“‘“No, sir, but she returned home at three o’clock this morning from a ball.”

“‘“My name is Gobseck; tell her I will call again at twelve,” and I left, leaving muddy footprints on the carpet. I enjoy leaving mud on a rich man’s carpet; it’s not spite, but a reminder of Necessity. In the Rue Montmartre, I entered a poor-looking house and looked into a dark courtyard where sunlight never shone. The porter’s lodge was grimy, the window like a shabby gown—greasy, dirty, and full of holes.

“‘“Mlle. Fanny Malvaut?”

“‘“She has gone out; but if you’ve come about a bill, the money is waiting for you.”

“‘“I will look in again,” I said.

“‘As soon as I knew the porter had the money, I wanted to know what the girl was like; I pictured her as pretty. The rest of the morning, I spent looking at prints in shop windows along the boulevard; then, just as it struck twelve, I entered the Countess’ antechamber.

“‘“Madame has just rung for me,” said the maid; “I don’t think she can see you yet.”

“‘“I will wait,” I said, sitting in an easy chair.

“‘Venetian shutters opened, and the maid hurried back.

“‘“Come in, sir.”

“‘From the sweet tone of the girl’s voice, I knew the mistress was not ready to pay. What a handsome woman I saw! She had thrown an Indian shawl over her bare shoulders, covering herself while revealing the outlines beneath. She wore a loose gown trimmed with ruffles, indicating her laundress’ bills amounted to about two thousand francs a year. Her dark curls escaped from a bright handkerchief knotted carelessly around her head. The bed lay in disorder, suggesting broken slumber. A painter would have paid to see the scene. Under luxurious draperies, the pillow, crushed into the depths of an eider-down quilt, bore a vague impress that sparked the imagination. Satin slippers gleamed from the bear-skin rug at the bed-foot, where she had flung them off in weariness after the ball. A crumpled gown hung over a chair, stockings twisted around an easy chair leg, and ribbon garters sprawled over a settee. A half-open fan glittered on the chimney-piece. Drawers stood open; flowers, diamonds, gloves, a bouquet, a girdle were scattered about. The room was filled with vague sweet perfume. And beneath all the luxury and disorder, I saw Misery lurking, ready for her or her lover, rearing its head, for the Countess felt the edge of those fangs. Her tired face epitomized the room strewn with relics of past revelry. The scattered gewgaws, pitiable this morning, had turned heads the night before.

“‘What efforts to drink from the Tantalus cup of bliss I could read in these traces of love struck by remorse—in this visible representation of a life of luxury, extravagance, and riot. There were faint red marks on her young face, signs of fine skin; her features were coarsened, and dark circles surrounded her eyes. Yet Nature was vigorous in her; these traces of past folly did not mar her beauty. Her eyes glittered. She resembled a da Vinci _Herodias_, magnificently full of life and energy; nothing was starved or stinted in feature or outline; she awakened desire; it seemed there was a passion in her stronger than love. I was taken with her. It had been long since my heart throbbed; I was paid then and there—for I would give a thousand francs for a sensation that brought back memories of youth.

“‘“Monsieur,” she said, finding a chair for me, “will you be so good as to wait?”

“‘“Until this time tomorrow, madame,” I replied, folding the bill again. “I cannot legally protest this bill any sooner.” And within myself, I thought—“Pay the price of your luxury, your name, your ease, the monopoly you enjoy! The rich have invented judges and courts to secure their goods, and the guillotine—where many lie in silk, under silken coverlets, there is remorse and grinding of teeth beneath a smile, and those fantastical lions’ jaws are gaping to sink their fangs into your heart.”

“‘“Protest the bill! Can you mean it?” she cried, her eyes on me; “could you have so little consideration for me?”

“‘“If the King himself owed me money and did not pay, I would summon him even sooner than any other debtor.”

“‘While we spoke, someone tapped gently at the door.

“‘“I cannot see anyone,” she cried imperiously.

“‘“But, Anastasie, I particularly wish to speak to you.”

“‘“Not just now, dear,” she answered in a milder tone, but with no sign of relenting.

“‘“What nonsense! You are talking to someone,” said the voice, and in came a man who could only be the Count.

“‘The Countess glanced at me. I saw how it was; she was thoroughly in my power. There was a time when I was young and might have been foolish enough not to protest the bill. At Pondicherry in 1763, I let a woman off, and she paid me back nicely. I deserved it; what reason had I to trust her?

“‘“What does this gentleman want?” asked the Count.

“‘I could see the Countess trembling; the white satin skin of her throat was rough, “turned to goose flesh.” I laughed inwardly without moving a muscle.

“‘“This gentleman is one of my tradesmen,” she said.

“‘The Count turned his back on me; I drew the bill halfway out of my pocket. After that inexorable movement, she came over and placed a diamond in my hands. “Take it,” she said, “and be gone.”

“‘We exchanged values, and I bowed and left. The diamond was worth twelve hundred francs to me. Outside, I saw a swarm of flunkeys brushing their liveries, waxing their boots, and cleaning sumptuous equipages.

“‘“This is what brings these people to me!” I thought. “They steal millions with all due formalities to maintain this lifestyle. The great lord and the little man who apes him bathe in mud to save themselves a splash or two when they walk through the streets.”

“‘Just then, the great gates opened to admit a cabriolet. It was the same young fellow who had brought the bill to me.

“‘“Sir,” I said as he alighted, “here are two hundred francs, which I beg you to return to Mme. la Comtesse, and please tell her I hold the pledge she deposited with me this morning for a week.”

“‘He took the two hundred francs, and an ironical smile crossed his face, as if to say, “Aha! So she has paid, has she? ... Faith, so much the better!” I read the Countess’ future in his face. That good-looking, fair-haired young man is a heartless gambler; he will ruin himself, her, her husband, and the children, devouring their portions and wreaking more havoc in Parisian salons than a battery of howitzers.

“‘I returned to see Mlle. Fanny in the Rue Montmartre, climbed a steep, narrow staircase, and reached a two-room dwelling on the fifth floor. Everything was as neat as a new ducat. I saw no dust on the furniture in the first room, where Mlle. Fanny sat. She was a young Parisian girl, quietly dressed, with a delicate fresh face and a winning look. The arrangement of her neatly brushed chestnut hair in a double curve on her forehead lent a refined expression to her blue eyes, clear as crystal. The daylight streaming through the short curtains softened the light on her girlish face. A pile of shaped linen indicated she was a sempstress. She looked like a spirit of solitude. When I held out the bill, I noted she had not been home when I called in the morning.

“‘“But the money was left with the porter’s wife,” she said.

“‘I pretended not to understand.

“‘“You go out early, mademoiselle, it seems.”

“‘“I very seldom leave my room; but when you work all night, you must take a bath sometimes.”

“‘I looked at her. A glance revealed her life. Here was a girl condemned by misfortune to toil, from honest farmer folk, with a freckle or two indicating her country birth. There was an indefinable atmosphere of goodness about her; I felt as if I were breathing sincerity and frank innocence. It was refreshing. Poor innocent child, she had faith in something; a crucifix and a sprig of green box adorned her little painted wooden bedstead. I felt touched, inclined to offer to charge no more than twelve percent, helping her establish a good business.

“‘But maybe she has a cousin who would raise money on her signature and sponge on her,’ I thought.

“‘So I left, keeping my generous impulses in check; I have often observed that when benevolence harms no one who gives it, it ruins those who take it. When you came in, I was thinking that Fanny Malvaut would make a nice little wife; I was considering the contrast between her pure, lonely life and the Countess’s life—she has already sunk as low as a bill of exchange; she will sink to the lowest depths of degradation before she is done!’ I scrutinized him during the deep silence that followed, but he spoke again. ‘Well,’ he said, ‘do you think it is nothing to have this power of insight into the deepest recesses of the human heart, to embrace so many lives, to see the naked truth underlying it all? There are no two dramas alike: hideous sores, deadly chagrins, love scenes, misery that will soon lie under the Seine, young men’s joys leading to the scaffold, laughter of despair, and sumptuous banquets. Yesterday was a tragedy. A father drowned himself because he could not support his family. Tomorrow will be a comedy; some young man will try to rehearse the scene of M. Dimanche, brought up to date. You have heard people extol the eloquence of our latter-day preachers; I have wasted time listening to them; they changed my opinions, but never my conduct. Well, well; these good priests and your Mirabeaus and Vergniauds are mere stammering beginners compared to my orators.

“‘Often it is a girl in love, a gray-headed merchant on the verge of bankruptcy, a mother concealing her son’s wrongdoing, a starving artist, or a great man whose influence wanes, who pleads for me. Their power of pleading makes me shudder. Sublime actors play for me, for an audience of one, and they cannot deceive me. I can see their inmost thoughts, read them as God reads them. Nothing is hidden from me. The holder of the purse-strings can loose and bind. I am rich enough to buy the consciences of those who control ministers, from their office boys to their mistresses. Is that not power? I can possess the fairest women, receive their softest caresses; is that not Pleasure? And is not your whole social economy summed up in terms of Power and Pleasure?

“‘There are ten of us in Paris, silent, unknown kings, arbiters of your destinies. What is life but a machine set in motion by money? Know this for certain—methods are always confounded with results; you will never separate the soul from the senses, spirit from matter. Gold is the spiritual basis of existing society. The ten of us are bound by common interest; we meet certain days at the Cafe Themis near the Pont Neuf, revealing the mysteries of finance. No fortune can deceive us; we possess family secrets in all directions. We keep a kind of Black Book, noting the most important bills issued, drafts on public credit, or on banks, or given and taken in business. We are the Casuists of the Paris Bourse, a kind of Inquisition weighing and analyzing every man of fortune’s actions, and our forecasts are infallible. One of us oversees the judicial world, another the financial, another the administrative, and yet another the business world. I keep an eye on eldest sons, artists, people in high society, and gamblers—on the most sensational side of Paris. Everyone who comes to us reveals their neighbor’s secrets. Thwarted passion and mortified vanity are great babblers. Vice, disappointment, and vindictiveness are the best detectives. My colleagues and I have enjoyed all things, are sated with all things, and have reached the point where power and money are loved for their own sake.

“‘Here,’ he said, indicating his bare, chilly room, ‘the most high-mettled gallant, who chafes at a word and draws swords for a syllable elsewhere, will entreat with clasped hands. No city merchant is so proud, no woman so vain of her beauty, no soldier so bold, but they all entreat me here, with tears of rage or anguish in their eyes. Here they kneel—the famous artist, the man of letters whose name will go down to posterity. Here, in short, all inheritances and concerns of all Paris are weighed in the balance. Are you still of the opinion that there are no delights behind the blank mask that has often amazed you with its impassiveness?’ he asked, stretching out that livid face that reeked of money.

“I returned to my room, feeling stupefied. The little, wizened old man had grown great. He had transformed into a strange visionary symbol; he had become the power of gold personified. I shrank from life and my kind.

“‘Is it really so?’ I thought; ‘must everything be resolved into gold?’

“I remember it was long before I slept that night. I envisioned heaps of gold surrounding me. My thoughts were filled with the lovely Countess; I confess, to my shame, that the vision eclipsed another quiet, innocent figure—the woman who had entered a life of toil and obscurity. But the next morning, through the clouds of slumber, Fanny’s sweet face rose before me in all its beauty, and I thought of nothing else.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Will you take a glass of _eau sucree_?” asked the Vicomtesse, interrupting Derville.

“I should be glad of it.”

“But I see nothing here that concerns us,” said Mme. de Grandlieu, ringing the bell.

“Sardanapalus!” cried Derville, invoking his favorite phrase. “Mademoiselle Camille’s happiness once depended on Daddy Gobseck; but with his death at ninety, M. de Restaud will soon inherit a fortune. This needs explanation. As for poor Fanny Malvaut, you know her; she is my wife.”

“Poor fellow, he would admit that, with his usual frankness, in front of a crowd!” said the Vicomtesse.

“I would proclaim it to the universe,” said the attorney.

“Go on, drink your glass, my poor Derville. You will always be the happiest and best of men.”

“I left you in the Rue du Helder,” remarked the uncle, waking from a gentle doze. “You had gone to see a Countess; what became of her?”

“A few days after my conversation with the old Dutchman,” Derville continued, “I submitted my thesis, became a licentiate in law, and then an advocate. The old miser’s opinion of me improved. He consulted me (for free) on tricky business, which would have seemed risky to any ordinary practitioner. This man, who appeared immune to influence, listened to my advice with respect, and it often turned out well.

“Eventually, I became head-clerk in the office where I had worked for three years and then moved into my employer’s house. I had board and lodging and a salary of one hundred and fifty francs per month. It was a great day for me!

“When I went to bid the usurer goodbye, he showed no emotion, neither cordial nor regretful, and did not ask me to visit him again, only giving me a glance that seemed to reveal a second sight.

“A week later, my old neighbor came to see me with a thorny business matter, an expropriation, and continued to seek my advice as if I were still his clerk.

“My principal was a man of pleasure and expensive tastes; by the end of the second year (1818-1819), he found himself in difficulties and had to sell his practice. Back then, a professional connection didn’t fetch the exorbitant prices of today, and my principal asked for one hundred and fifty thousand francs. An active, competent man might hope to pay off the capital in ten years, living respectably in the meantime—if he could command confidence. But as the seventh child of a small tradesman from Noyon, I had no money and no connections except Daddy Gobseck. An ambitious idea and a glimmer of hope encouraged me. I went to Gobseck, my heart pounding as I knocked on his door in the gloomy house, recalling his warnings about the anguish awaiting those who crossed his threshold. Now, I was about to beg like so many others.

“‘Well, no, not _that_,’ I told myself; ‘an honest man must keep his self-respect. Success is not worth cringing for; let’s show him a front as determined as his own.’

“Daddy Gobseck had taken my room since I left, so as to have no neighbor; he had even added a grated window to his door and didn’t open until he had looked at me.

“‘So your principal is selling his practice?’ he said.

“How did you know that?” I asked, surprised.

“‘Nothing else would have brought you here,’ he replied dryly.

“‘Listen to me, M. Gobseck,’ I began, trying to maintain my composure. ‘I am a penniless clerk, and only you can understand my future. Let’s leave feelings aside. My principal’s practice is worth about twenty thousand francs per annum; in my hands, I believe it could bring in forty thousand. He is willing to sell it for one hundred and fifty thousand francs. If you would lend me the purchase money, I could pay it off in ten years.’

“‘Come, that is plain speaking,’ said Daddy Gobseck, shaking my hand. ‘Nobody has stated their motives more clearly. Guarantees?’ he asked, scanning me. ‘None to give,’ he added after a pause. ‘How old are you?’

“‘Twenty-five in ten days,’ I replied.

“‘Precisely. Well?’

“‘It is possible.’

“‘We must be quick about it, or I shall have someone buying over my head.’

“‘Bring your birth certificate tomorrow morning, and we will talk. I will think it over.’

“Next morning, at eight o’clock, I stood in the old man’s room. He took the document, put on his spectacles, coughed, and read it through. Then he said, ‘We will try to arrange this business.’

“I trembled.

“‘I make fifty percent on my capital,’ he continued, ‘sometimes I make a hundred, two hundred, five hundred percent.’

“I turned pale.

“‘But as we are acquaintances, I shall be satisfied to take twelve and a half percent per—well, yes, from you I would be content to take thirteen percent per annum. Will that suit you?’

“‘Yes,’ I answered.

“‘But if it is too much, stand up for yourself, Grotius!’ (a name he jokingly gave me). ‘When I ask for thirteen percent, it is all in the way of business; look into it, see if you can pay it; I don’t like a man to agree too easily. Is it too much?’

“‘No,’ I said, ‘I will make up for it by working a little harder.’

“‘Gad! your clients will pay for it!’ he said, looking at me wickedly.

“‘No, by all the devils in hell!’ I cried, ‘it shall be I who pays. I would sooner cut my hand off than flay people.’

“‘Good-night,’ said Daddy Gobseck.

“‘Fees are all according to scale,’ I added.

“‘Not for compromises and settlements out of court, and cases where litigants come to terms,’ he replied. ‘You can send in a bill for thousands of francs, six thousand even at a swoop, depending on the case’s importance. A man must learn to look out for business of this kind. I will recommend you as a competent attorney. I will send you so much work that your colleagues will be fit to burst with envy. Werbrust, Palma, and Gigonnet, my cronies, shall hand over their expropriations to you; they have plenty of them! So you will have two practices—the one you are buying and the other I will build up for you. You ought almost to pay me fifteen percent on my loan.’

“‘So be it, but no more,’ I said firmly.

“Daddy Gobseck’s face relaxed; he looked pleased.

“‘I shall pay the money over to your principal myself, to establish a lien on the purchase and caution-money.’

“‘Oh, anything you like in the way of guarantees.’

“‘And you will give me bills for the amount made payable to a third party (name left blank), fifteen bills of ten thousand francs each.’

“‘Well, so long as it is acknowledged in writing that this is a double----’

“‘No!’ Gobseck interrupted. ‘Why should I trust you any more than you trust me?’

“I kept silent.

“‘Furthermore,’ he continued, ‘you will give me your advice without charging fees as long as I live, will you not?’
