Rochefide's indifference to domestic life was evident; he rarely dined or slept at home. After several romantic escapades, he married Madame Schontz, a woman of dubious reputation known in the circles of Fanny Beaupre and others. This world, filled with women who often ended in poverty or early death, required a nuanced understanding of their social dynamics. Madame Schontz, originally known as La Petite-Aurelie, was a product of a difficult upbringing, having lost her parents and been educated at Saint-Denis, a school that prepared young women but offered little in terms of future opportunities.

Aurelie, the daughter of Colonel Schiltz, had a tumultuous life filled with various relationships, leading her to a chance encounter with Rochefide at Valentino’s. Captivated by her charm, Rochefide married her two years after Beatrix's departure, and society did not criticize him for this choice.

Rochefide's relationship with Madame Schontz can be divided into four phases. The first phase involved their initial courtship, where he provided her with a modest allowance and a furnished apartment. As he studied her character, he found her noble and charming, and she began to display her talents, which he admired. However, as time passed, her ambition became evident, and she sought a more comfortable life.

In the second phase, Madame Schontz moved to a better apartment, and Rochefide lavished her with gifts and financial support. She managed their household efficiently, impressing him with her ability to maintain a budget and host superior dinners. This newfound domesticity led Rochefide to believe he was truly loved for himself, as she rejected advances from wealthier suitors.

The third phase saw Madame Schontz become a mother figure to Rochefide's son, managing his education and finances. She advised Rochefide on investments, leading to significant financial gains, which she did not claim for herself. As she took on more responsibilities, she became an integral part of his life, managing his affairs and ensuring his comfort.

In the fourth phase, Madame Schontz established herself as a prominent figure in society, carefully curating her guest list and maintaining her reputation. She had amassed a small fortune through her savings and investments, while also wearing and selling jewelry to increase her wealth. Despite her financial success, she downplayed her riches, emphasizing the challenges of maintaining her lifestyle.

Through these phases, Madame Schontz demonstrated her tactical superiority, transforming her relationship with Rochefide into one of mutual benefit, while also navigating the complexities of their social world.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous of Madame du Bruel for two years, she was determined to marry in both church and civil ceremonies. This ambition required a second Arthur, but no one had yet uncovered Rochefide’s secret rival. Bixiou suspected Leon de Lora, while the painter thought Bixiou himself was the favored one. Others, like Victor de Vernisset, a poet, and Stidmann, a young sculptor, were also considered potential rivals. However, none had truly understood Madame Schontz, who was focused solely on securing a good marriage.

The only man of questionable reputation in her salon was Couture, a stock-gambler who had fallen from grace. Despite his failures, Couture remained loyal to Madame Schontz, who was contemplating her options when he introduced her to a provincial man, Fabien du Ronceret. Fabien, the son of a chief-justice, had left his judgeship in Alencon to seek fame in Paris, armed with a modest income and a desire to capitalize on the social changes of the time.

The modern era had fostered a culture of pride, vanity, and ambition, where individuals sought recognition at any cost. Fabien, though not particularly clever, understood this societal shift and aimed to make a name for himself. He believed that to be successful, one must be talked about, and he endeavored to gain attention through extravagant living and social connections.

Fabien’s debut in Parisian society was marked by his extravagant lifestyle, which included a lavish apartment and a greenhouse, not out of love for horticulture but as a means to attract public attention. He sought to associate with influential figures and gain entry into Madame Schontz’s circle. After several failed attempts to connect with her through established acquaintances, he turned to Couture, who recognized Fabien’s potential and encouraged Madame Schontz to consider him.

Madame Schontz quickly assessed Fabien and saw in him a suitable candidate for her ambitions. She flattered him with attention, leading him to believe he was the object of her affection. Fabien, eager to impress, presented himself in his finest attire, fully enamored by her charm. A month before a significant meeting, Madame Schontz revealed her true identity and aspirations to Fabien, who failed to grasp her intentions.

Realizing his misunderstanding, Madame Schontz took Fabien for a carriage ride, where she expressed her desire to marry a man of ambition who could understand her character. Despite her wealth, Fabien hesitated at the prospect of marrying her, leading Madame Schontz to turn her attention to Couture, who quickly proposed after learning of her fortune.

Meanwhile, the Duchesse de Grandlieu sought the assistance of the Comte Maxime de Trailles, a seasoned political player, to navigate a delicate situation involving her family. Maxime, aware of the underlying motives behind the duchess’s invitation, agreed to help but insisted on certain conditions, including the acceptance of his future wife into the duchess’s social circle.

Maxime’s proposal included a plan to remove a rival from the picture, requiring the duchess to follow his instructions closely. He acknowledged the challenges ahead, particularly regarding the emotional entanglements of those involved, especially Calyste, who was deeply infatuated with Beatrix. Maxime’s understanding of love and manipulation revealed the complexities of the relationships at play, as he prepared to execute his plan.

As the duchess agreed to support Maxime’s wife, he assured her that he would handle the intricacies of the situation, albeit with a hint of moral ambiguity. The conversation highlighted the lengths to which individuals would go to secure their desires, often at the expense of others’ feelings and reputations.

The next day, Rochefide informed Madame Schontz of Maxime’s impending visit, prompting her to prepare her home and appearance to impress him. Maxime’s reputation as a powerful figure in Parisian society preceded him, and his presence was both a privilege and a challenge for Madame Schontz, who sought to navigate the complexities of her ambitions and relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next day, Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot to arrange a breakfast at the Café Anglais with Couture and Lousteau, where they discussed Madame Schontz. Maxime, while appearing to engage in business with du Tillet on Tortoni’s portico, awaited the Comte de la Palferine. The boulevard des Italiens was a hub for the famous, and soon, he approached La Palferine, inquiring about his debts. La Palferine humorously acknowledged his financial struggles, and Maxime, impressed by his spirit, offered to help him win over a woman, promising to lend him twenty thousand francs for the endeavor.

Maxime’s plan involved a well-bred marquise, and he encouraged La Palferine to pursue her genuinely. They agreed to meet later at Madame Schontz’s. Meanwhile, Maxime visited the duchess, who was distressed over her daughter Sabine’s worries about Calyste’s absence. He requested her to keep his plans secret, needing twenty thousand francs for his scheme, which he assured would not require her to spend excessively.

Madame Schontz prepared for a dinner gathering, inviting notable guests, including Claude Vignon and La Palferine. She dressed elegantly, aiming to impress. Maxime complimented her on her success with Rochefide, noting how she had saved him money compared to other women. Madame Schontz asserted her identity as an artist, not a courtesan, and expressed her desire to marry well.

During the dinner, Maxime discussed his plans with La Palferine, providing him with the promised funds and instructions for winning over the marquise. He emphasized the importance of making a strong impression. As the evening progressed, Maxime and Madame Schontz plotted to manipulate Rochefide’s affections, with Maxime promising to secure a position for Couture in exchange for his support.

The next day, Calyste, feeling neglected, confronted Beatrix, who was torn between her feelings for him and her attraction to La Palferine. Their relationship became strained as Beatrix struggled with her emotions, fearing the consequences of her actions. Meanwhile, La Palferine’s charm and wit captivated Beatrix, leading her to question her loyalty to Calyste.

Maxime’s machinations continued, as he orchestrated encounters between Beatrix and La Palferine, further complicating the dynamics. Calyste, feeling increasingly desperate, sought to reclaim Beatrix’s affection, but she remained ensnared by La Palferine’s allure. The tension escalated, culminating in a public humiliation for Beatrix at the opera, where she was left alone and exposed.

In the aftermath, Beatrix’s feelings for Calyste began to wane as she became more entangled with La Palferine. Maxime, observing the unfolding drama, recognized the shifting allegiances and the emotional turmoil affecting all parties involved. He advised Calyste to reconsider his approach, suggesting that Beatrix was not worth the pain.

As the story progressed, the characters navigated their desires and ambitions, leading to a series of confrontations and revelations. Beatrix’s internal conflict deepened, and her relationship with La Palferine became more complicated as she grappled with her identity and societal expectations.

Ultimately, the narrative explored themes of love, betrayal, and the complexities of human relationships, highlighting the characters’ struggles to reconcile their desires with the realities of their lives. The interplay between ambition and affection drove the plot, culminating in a series of disillusionments that left the characters questioning their choices and futures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

If the French have a strong aversion to traveling, the English have a propensity for it, both nations having their reasons. While other countries may offer admirable scenery and comfort, the unique charms of France are hard to replicate elsewhere. The essence of French life—its wit, conversation, and unspoken understanding—cannot be found abroad. Thus, many Frenchmen feel a deep pleasure upon encountering their homeland, even in foreign settings, reminiscent of the comforts of Paris.

In 1836, during the Sardinian Court's stay in Genoa, two notable Parisians found themselves in a palazzo, a remnant of Genoese nobility. The beauty of Genoa's evenings, especially after rain, creates a serene atmosphere, perfect for enjoying the company of friends and the delights of Italian cuisine. Among the guests at a dinner hosted by the French Consul-General were the celebrated Mademoiselle des Touches, known as Camille Maupin, and her companions, the painter Léon de Lora and the critic Claude Vignon.

Mademoiselle des Touches had traveled to Florence for business and brought Léon along to experience Italy. Their stop in Genoa was a courtesy to the Consul-General, who wished to honor her presence. Despite her fame, Camille was cautious about engaging with the ambassador, preferring to enjoy the evening without the pressures of social expectations. The dinner was lively, with conversation flowing freely, though literature was scarcely mentioned until the Consul-General decided to share a personal story.

The Consul-General, a man of thirty-four, bore a striking resemblance to Lord Byron, with a dreamy expression that captivated many. He had married Onorina Pedrotti, the only child of a wealthy banker, after a period of reluctance. Their union was influenced by various factors, including the ambassador's intervention and the Consul's own hidden struggles. Onorina, a beautiful Genoese woman, embodied the elegance of her heritage, and their family life appeared harmonious, though Camille sensed an underlying melancholy in the Consul.

As the evening progressed, the conversation turned to the topic of women's morality, leading to a debate on accountability in cases of infidelity. The three women present held a firm stance against the woman, while the men argued for a more nuanced view. The Consul-General, seeking to shift the mood, began recounting a pivotal moment from his youth.

He described how, at twenty-two, his uncle, the Abbe Loraux, arranged for him to become the secretary to Comte Octave, a prominent statesman. The uncle's influence secured him a position that promised a bright future, despite the challenges he faced. The Consul's narrative revealed his initial innocence and naivety, having been sheltered from the complexities of society.

As he settled into his role, he observed the Count's character, noting the depth of his intellect and the burdens he carried. The Count, though outwardly composed, harbored a profound sadness, which the Consul began to understand over time. Their relationship deepened, with the Count treating him with kindness and respect, often blurring the lines between master and mentor.

The Consul's reflections on the Count's life revealed a man dedicated to his work yet haunted by unspoken sorrows. Despite his success, the Count's personal struggles remained hidden, and the Consul felt a growing curiosity about the source of his patron's melancholy. The Count's devotion to his duties and his pious nature contrasted sharply with the emotional turmoil he concealed.

As the Consul continued his tale, he described the Count's meticulous nature and his love for flowers, which mirrored his own faded beauty. The Count's life was marked by a relentless pursuit of duty, yet he seemed to be searching for something more—a happiness that eluded him. The Consul's observations painted a picture of a man caught between public expectations and private despair, leading to a complex relationship that would shape both their lives.

The Consul-General's story, rich with detail and emotion, captivated his audience, revealing the intricate dynamics of love, duty, and the human condition. As he concluded, the guests were left to ponder the depths of the Count's character and the nature of the struggles that lay beneath the surface of their seemingly perfect lives.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le President de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice-President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three, with me as secretary. The Count had secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All necessary documents for their inquiry were laid out in the library. MM. de Grandville and de Serizy relied on the Count for the preliminary examination of the papers. To avoid transporting all the documents to M. de Serizy, it was decided to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Cabinet at the Tuileries valued this work, which ultimately led to my appointment as Master of Appeals that year.

“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, who rarely dined out, were still discussing the matter late into the evening, we were interrupted by a servant informing me that ‘MM. the Cures of Saint-Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing-room for two hours.’

“It was nine o’clock.

“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown----’

“‘It depends on the priest.’

“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbe Gaudron,’ I said. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbe Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint-Paul----’

“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the President de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’

“We entered the drawing-room. The dinner was delightful. Politicians with real knowledge are excellent storytellers. They are either heavy or sublime. In this delightful sport, Prince Metternich is as good as Charles Nodier. The wit of my uncle was refined and elegant, typical of those who conceal their thoughts under a black robe. There was nothing vulgar in this light talk, which I would compare to Rossini’s music.

“The Abbe Gaudron, as M. de Grandville noted, was more like a Saint Peter than a Saint Paul, a peasant full of faith, whose ignorance of worldly matters enlivened the conversation with guileless amazement. They began discussing a social issue—adultery. My uncle pointed out the contradiction between civil and religious law established by the legislators, rooted in the revolutionary aftermath.

“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ he said, ‘adultery is a crime; in your tribunals, it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar. Napoleon’s Council of State, touched with tenderness towards erring women, was quite inefficient. Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’

“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents; monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbe—give to God what society would have none of?’

“‘Oh!’ said Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They had to leave the husband free to take proceedings; well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’

“‘M. l’Abbe preaches for his own saint, for it was Jesus Christ who invented adultery,’ said Comte Octave. ‘In the East, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, the modern family in Europe—daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage and made it a sacrament.’

“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.

“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count continued with a smile. ‘But the practices of that world will never match a climate where women are marriageable at seven and considered old at twenty-five. The Catholic Church overlooked the needs of half the globe. So let us discuss Europe only.

“‘Is woman our superior or inferior? That is the real question. If woman is our inferior, by placing her on such a high level as the Church does, severe punishments for adultery were necessary. Formerly, that was the case. The cloister or death sums up early legislation. But since then, practice has modified the law. The throne served as a hotbed for adultery, and the increase of this inviting crime marks the decline of Catholic dogmas. Nowadays, where the Church demands only sincere repentance from the erring wife, society is satisfied with a brand-mark instead of execution. The law still condemns the guilty, but it no longer terrifies them. In short, there are two moral standards: that of the world and that of the Code. Where the Code is weak, the world is audacious and satirical. Few judges would not gladly have committed the faults they condemn. The world punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The entire marriage law seems to require reconstruction. The French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’

“‘We three know the question thoroughly,’ said Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and if divorce is ever brought in again, we shall form the committee.’

“Octave’s fork dropped, breaking his glass and plate. He turned pale and shot a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence.

“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the President continued. ‘Serizy and I, after witnessing your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in front of these venerable priests.’

“M. de Serizy changed the subject, recounting his efforts to please his wife without success. The old man concluded that human sympathies and antipathies cannot be regulated; social law is most perfect when closest to natural law. Nature does not consider the affinities of souls; her aim is the propagation of the species. Thus, the Code, in its current form, wisely leaves latitude to chance. The incapacity of daughters to inherit while male heirs exist is an excellent provision, whether to prevent degeneration or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions.

“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand in disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country that insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators! After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’

“‘Setting aside the religious question,’ my uncle remarked, ‘Nature only owes us life; society owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ he asked.

“‘And I—do I have any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, making such an impression that the topic of wives and marriage was dropped.

“When coffee was served, the two Counts and the two priests quietly left, noticing that poor Octave had fallen into a melancholy fit, oblivious to their departure. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, crushed.

“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said to me when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, I came home one evening to find a letter from the Countess announcing her flight. The letter was dignified; women often retain some virtues even when committing such a sin. The story now is that my wife went abroad on a ship that was wrecked; she is presumed dead. I have lived alone for seven years! Enough for tonight, Maurice. We will discuss my situation when I am ready to speak of it. When suffering from a chronic disease, it takes time to adjust to the idea of improvement. That improvement often seems merely another aspect of the complaint.’

“I went to bed greatly agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure than ever. I anticipated a strange drama, realizing that the events leading the Countess to leave such a noble and loving man must have been extraordinary. M. de Grandville’s remark had illuminated the caverns I had been navigating, and though the light was dim, I could perceive their vastness. I could imagine the Count’s suffering without grasping its depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, and moments of absentmindedness stood out vividly during the twilight of mental questioning before sleep.

“Oh! how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a poem of melancholy, seeing perpetual activity in the heart I had accused of being torpid. Must not supreme grief eventually lead to stagnation? Had this judge, with so much power, ever sought revenge? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable to harbor anger for ten years in Paris? What had Octave done since this great misfortune—when the separation of husband and wife has become a social question?

“We allowed a few days to pass, for great sorrows have their own diffidence; but eventually, one evening, the Count said gravely:

“‘Stay.’
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“This, as nearly as may be, is his story.

“My father had a ward, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I returned from college to live in this old house. Honorine, raised by my mother, was just awakening to life. Full of grace and childish ways, she dreamed of happiness as one dreams of jewels; perhaps happiness seemed to her the jewel of the soul. Her piety was tinged with childish pleasures; everything, even religion, was poetry to her innocent heart. She viewed the future as a perpetual fête, untouched by shame or grief. She awaited marriage without desiring it, her imagination untainted by the corruption literature often imparts. She was so innocent that she could have walked among serpents, like the ideal figure of Innocence. We lived together like siblings.

“After a year, I asked her one day in the garden, by the basin where we fed the fish, if she would like to marry me. She told my mother, who was surprised and insisted they wait eighteen months to see if their feelings remained. When I was twenty-six and Honorine nineteen, we married. Our respect for my parents prevented us from modernizing the house; we lived as we always had. I introduced my wife to society, seeing it as my duty to guide her.

“I later realized that marriages like ours often harbor hidden dangers. The husband becomes a teacher, and love can wither under such authority. Perhaps I was wrong to assume Honorine would never rebel; I trusted her innocence too much. Who can say if empires and happiness are destroyed by too much confidence or too much severity? I remember the broad outlines of the self-reproaches I made, akin to an anatomist seeking the cause of a disease. His merciful indulgence reminded me of Christ’s compassion for the woman taken in adultery.

“It was eighteen months after my father’s death, shortly after my mother passed, that I received Honorine’s farewell letter. What delusion had led her to leave? I was stunned for a month, then chose ignorance, learning too much about such matters through her misfortunes. So far, Maurice, the story is commonplace, but one word changes everything: I love Honorine, and I have never ceased to worship her. Since her departure, I have lived on memories, recalling pleasures she may not have appreciated.

“Do not make a hero of me; I sought no diversion. I was either too young or too in love; I have not met another woman like her. After struggling to forget, I stood on the brink of infidelity, but Honorine’s memory stopped me. I recalled her delicate skin, her innocent face, and the fragrance of her virtue. I fled from temptation, haunted by thoughts of her. My life is a constant paroxysm of fears, joy, and despair.

“I felt no anger towards her, like a father seeing a beloved child in danger. I understood I had idealized my wife, believing she shared my intoxication. An uncritical passion in a husband can lead to a wife’s infidelity. I may have exhausted her with my love before she was ready. Too young to understand that a wife’s constancy is the seed of maternal devotion, she mistook our early marriage for life itself. In her cruel position, she was defenseless against any man who stirred her deeply. I, who was supposed to be wise, failed to grasp the unwritten laws of a woman’s heart until it was too late. I constituted my heart a tribunal, acquitting my wife and condemning myself. Yet, love took hold of me as a desperate passion, and I love Honorine as a man of sixty loves a woman he must possess, even as I feel the strength of youth.

“Society laughs at such a predicament. Where it pities a lover, it mocks a husband who cannot keep his wife. I had to remain silent. Serizy is happy; he can see and protect his wife, enjoying the joys of a benefactor. But I have nothing; I live solely on a love that is starving. I loathe prostitution and remain faithful under a spell. Without my religious faith, I might have killed myself. I have thrown myself into work, emerging fevered and sleepless.

“I cannot recall all the words of this eloquent man, whose passion gave him a voice far beyond that of a pleader. You can imagine my feelings when he concluded with this revelation: “This is the drama of my soul, but it is not the actual living drama being acted in Paris! The interior drama interests nobody. I know it; and you will one day admit it, you who shed tears with me; no one can bear another’s pain. The measure of our sufferings is in ourselves. You understand my sorrows only by vague analogy. Can you see me calming despair by contemplating a miniature of her, kissing her brow, breathing in her fragrance? I have fits of collapse, moods of wild hilarity, and terrors like a murderer meeting a sergeant of police. My life is a continual paroxysm of fears, joy, and dejection.

“The real drama is this: you think I am occupied with the Council of State, the Chamber, the Courts, Politics. Seven hours at night suffice for that, so much are my faculties overworked by my life! Honorine is my true concern. To recover my wife is my only study; to guard her without her suspecting my presence, to satisfy her needs, to provide her with the little pleasures she allows herself, to be always near her like a sylph without her knowing it—that is my life. For seven years, I have never gone to bed without first checking on her night-lamp or her shadow on the curtains.

“She left my house, taking only the dress she wore that day. Eighteen months later, she was deserted by her lover, who was appalled by the cold, cruel aspect of poverty. The coward had counted on a luxurious life in Switzerland or Italy. Honorine has sixty thousand francs a year of her own. In November 1820, I persuaded the best accoucheur in Paris to act as a humble suburban apothecary. I induced the parish priest to assist her as if performing an act of charity. Then, to hide my wife, secure her against discovery, and find a devoted housekeeper who would be my confidante—it was a task worthy of Figaro! You can imagine that to discover where my wife had taken refuge was not difficult for me.

“After three months of desperation, I decided to devote myself to Honorine, with God as my only witness. Love must have its daily food. Should I not protect this child, whose guilt stemmed from my imprudence, against further disaster? At seven months, her infant died, happily for her and for me. For nine months, my wife lay between life and death, deserted when she needed a man’s support. But this arm was extended over her head. Honorine was cared for as she would have been in her own home. When she recovered and asked who had assisted her, she was told it was the Sisters of Charity, the Maternity Society, and the parish priest.

“This woman, whose pride is almost a vice, has shown a power of resistance in misfortune that I sometimes call obstinacy. Honorine was determined to earn her living. For five years, I have lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur, in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers. She believes she sells her elegant creations to a shop, earning twenty francs a day, living as if she had fifteen thousand. She is devoted to flowers, paying a gardener a hundred crowns, which costs me twelve hundred in wages. I promised the gardener a market-garden with a house close to the porter’s lodge in the Rue Saint-Maur, held in the name of a clerk of the law courts. The smallest indiscretion would ruin the gardener’s prospects. Honorine has her little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for a rent of five hundred francs a year. She lives under the name of her housekeeper, Madame Gobain, an old woman of impeccable discretion whom I was fortunate to find, and whose affection Honorine has won. For three years, Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort to her own toil.

“Oh! I know what you are about to say,” cried the Count, seeing a question in my eyes. “Yes, I have made the attempt. My wife was living in the Faubourg Saint-Antoine. One day, believing in a chance for reconciliation, I wrote her a letter, which I rewrote twenty times. I will not describe my agonies. I went from the Rue Payenne to the Rue de Reuilly like a condemned wretch going to execution. It was dark and foggy; I went to meet Madame Gobain, who was to tell me what my wife had done. Honorine, recognizing my writing, threw the letter into the fire without reading it, exclaiming, ‘I leave this tomorrow.’

“What a dagger-stroke to a man who finds pleasure in trickery! I had just succeeded in deceiving my wife—sending her an Indian shawl, presented as the property of an actress, which I had wrapped myself in for a night! My life may be summed up in two words: I love, and I wait! I have in Madame Gobain a faithful spy on the heart I worship. I visit the old woman every evening to hear about Honorine’s day, her lightest word, for a single exclamation might reveal the secrets of that soul which is willfully deaf and dumb. Honorine is pious; she attends church and prays, but she has never confessed or taken Communion, foreseeing what a priest would say. She will not listen to the advice to return to me. This horror of me overwhelms me, for I have never harmed her. I have always been kind. Even if I was a bit hasty in teaching her, is that a reason for her determination, which only the most implacable hatred could inspire? Honorine has never told Madame Gobain who she is; she keeps absolute silence about her marriage, so the housekeeper cannot speak in my favor. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and respect the power of a Minister. Hence, it is impossible for me to penetrate her heart; the citadel is mine, but I cannot enter it. I have no means of action. An act of violence would ruin me forever.

“How can I argue against reasons of which I know nothing? Should I write a letter, have it copied by a public writer, and laid before Honorine? But that risks a third removal, which cost me fifty thousand francs. The last purchase was made in the name of the secretary who succeeded you. The unfortunate man, unaware of my vigilance, was caught opening a box containing a private agreement. I coughed, and he panicked; the next day, I forced him to sell the house to the man in whose name it now stands, and I turned him out.

“If I did not feel my noblest faculties satisfied, happy, and expansive; if I were not playing the role of divine fatherhood; if I did not drink in delight by every pore, I might believe I was a monomaniac. Sometimes at night, I hear the jingling bells of madness. I dread the violent transitions from feeble hope to complete despair. Recently, I considered the tragic end of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe, thinking that if Honorine were the mother of my child, she must return under her husband’s roof.

“I have such complete faith in a happy future that ten months ago, I bought one of the finest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win back Honorine, I will not allow her to see this house again, nor the room from which she fled. I intend to place my idol in a new temple, where she may feel that life is altogether new. That house is being transformed into a marvel of elegance and taste. I was inspired by a poet who, mad with love for an actress, bought the finest bed in Paris without knowing how she would reward his passion. Well, one of the coldest lawyers, thought to be the gravest adviser of the Crown, was stirred to the depths of his heart by that anecdote. The orator of the Legislative Chamber can understand the poet who fed his ideal on material possibilities. Three days before Maria Louisa’s arrival, Napoleon threw himself on his wedding bed at Compiègne. All great passions share the same impulses. I love as a poet—like an emperor!”

As I heard the last words, I believed Count Octave’s fears were realized; he had risen, walking and gesticulating, but stopped, shocked by the vehemence of his own words.

“I am very ridiculous,” he added after a long pause, looking at me, as if craving pity.

“No, monsieur, you are very unhappy.”

“Ah yes!” he said, resuming his confidences. “From the violence of my speech, you must believe in the intensity of a physical passion that has absorbed me for nine years; but that is nothing compared to the worship I feel for her soul, mind, and heart—all the enchanting divinities of Love, with whom we pass our lives, forming the daily poem of fleeting delight. By a phenomenon of retrospection, I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart, which I overlooked during my happiness—like all who are happy. Day by day, I appreciate my loss, discovering the exquisite gifts of that capricious and proud young creature who has grown strong under the heavy hand of poverty and the cowardly shock of desertion. And that heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding! The law we discussed is a squad of gendarmes—my wife seized and dragged away by force! Would that not be to triumph over a corpse? Religion has no hold on her; she craves its poetry, prays, but does not heed the Church’s commandments. I have exhausted all mercy, kindness, and love; I am at my wits’ end. Only one chance of victory remains: the cunning and patience of bird-catchers who finally trap the wariest birds. Hence, Maurice, when M. de Grandville’s indiscretion revealed my life’s secret to you, I regarded it as fate’s decree, one of the utterances gamblers listen for in their most impassioned play. Have you enough affection for me to show me romantic devotion?”

“I see what you are coming to, Monsieur le Comte,” I interrupted. “Your first secretary tried to open your deed box. I know your second’s heart—he might fall in love with your wife. Can you send him into the fire? Can anyone put their hand into a brazier without burning it?”

“You are a foolish boy,” replied the Count. “I will send you well-gloved. It is not my secretary who will be lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur in the little garden-house I have at his disposal. It is my distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office…”

After a moment of silent surprise, I heard the gate bell ring, and a carriage entered the courtyard. The footman announced Madame de Courteville and her daughter. The Count had a large family connection on his mother’s side. Madame de Courteville, his cousin, was the widow of a judge, leaving her a daughter and no fortune. What could a woman of nine-and-twenty be compared to a lovely young girl of twenty, ideal for a mistress?

“Baron, and Master of Appeals, until you get something better, and this old house settled on her—would you not have enough reasons not to fall in love with the Countess?” he whispered to me as he took my hand and introduced me to Madame de Courteville and her daughter.

I was dazzled, not so much by these advantages I had never dreamed of, but by Amelie de Courteville, whose beauty was enhanced by one of those well-chosen outfits a mother can achieve for her daughter when she wants to see her married.

“But I will not talk of myself,” said the Consul after a pause.

Three weeks later, I moved into the gardener’s cottage, which had been cleaned, repaired, and furnished with the celerity explained by three words: Paris; French workmen; money! I was as much in love as the Count could desire as security. Would the prudence of a young man of five-and-twenty be equal to the part I was undertaking, involving a friend’s happiness? To settle that matter, I confess I counted on my uncle’s advice; for I had been authorized by the Count to confide in him if necessary. I engaged a garden, devoted myself to horticulture, and worked frantically, like a man who cannot be diverted, turning up the soil of the market-garden and appropriating it to flower cultivation. Like the maniacs of England or Holland, I claimed to be devoted to one kind of flower, especially dahlias, collecting every variety. You will understand that my conduct, even in the smallest details, was dictated by the Count, whose intellect was focused on the trifling incidents of the tragi-comedy enacted in the Rue Saint-Maur. As soon as the Countess went to bed, around eleven, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I held council. I heard the old woman’s report on Honorine’s daily activities, meals, frame of mind, and plans. I understood what love in despair can be when it is a threefold passion of heart, mind, and senses. Octave lived only for that hour.

For two months, while I worked in the garden, I never saw the little house where my fair neighbor lived. I had not even inquired whether I had a neighbor, though the Countess’ garden was divided from mine by a paling, along which she had planted cypress trees already four feet high. One fine morning, Madame Gobain announced to her mistress the disastrous news that an eccentric creature, her neighbor, intended to build a wall between the two gardens at the end of the year. I will say nothing of the curiosity that consumed me to see the Countess! The wish almost extinguished my budding love for Amelie de Courteville. My scheme for building a wall was indeed a dangerous threat. There would be no fresh air for Honorine, whose garden would then be a narrow alley between my wall and her house. This dwelling, once a summer villa, was like a house of cards; it was not more than thirty feet deep and about a hundred feet long. The garden front, painted in the German style, imitated a trellis with flowers up to the second floor, and was a charming example of the Pompadour style. A long avenue of limes led up to it. The gardens of the pavilion and my plot were shaped like a hatchet, with this avenue as the handle. My wall would cut away three-quarters of the hatchet.

“The Countess was in despair.

“‘My good Gobain,’ she said, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’

“‘On my word,’ said the housekeeper, ‘I do not know if it will be possible to tame him. He seems to have a horror of women. He is the nephew of a Paris cure. I have seen the uncle but once; a fine old man of sixty, very ugly, but very amiable. It is quite possible that this priest encourages his nephew, as they say in the neighborhood, in his love of flowers, to prevent anything worse from happening…’

“‘What do you mean?’

“‘Well, your neighbor is a little cracked!’ said Gobain, tapping her head!

“Now a harmless lunatic is the only man no woman ever distrusts in matters of sentiment. You will see how wise the Count was in choosing this disguise for me.

“‘What ails him then?’ asked the Countess.

“‘He has studied too hard,’ replied Gobain; ‘he has turned misanthropic. And he has his reasons for disliking women—well, if you want to know all that is said about him…’

“‘Well,’ said Honorine, ‘madmen frighten me less than sane folks; I will speak to him myself! Tell him that I beg him to come here. If I do not succeed, I will send for the cure.’

“The day after this conversation, as I walked along my graveled path, I caught sight of the half-opened curtains on the first floor of the little house and a woman’s face peering out. Madame Gobain called me. I hastily glanced at the Countess’ house, shrugging rudely to express my indifference.

“‘Madame,’ said Gobain, called upon to give an account of her errand, ‘the madman bid me leave him in peace, saying that even a charcoal seller is master in his own premises, especially when he has no wife.’

“‘He is perfectly right,’ said the Countess.

“‘Yes, but he ended by saying, “I will go,” when I told him that he would greatly distress a lady living in retirement, who found her greatest solace in growing flowers.’

“Next day, a signal from Gobain informed me that I was expected. After the Countess’ breakfast, when she was walking in front of her house, I broke some palings and approached her. I had dressed like a countryman, in old gray flannel trousers, heavy wooden shoes, a shabby shooting coat, a peaked cap, a ragged bandana, and hands soiled with mold.

“‘Madame,’ said the housekeeper, ‘this good man is your neighbor.’

“The Countess was not alarmed. At last, I saw the woman whose conduct and my husband’s confidences had made me so curious to meet. It was early May. The air was pure, the weather serene; the fragrance of spring formed a setting for this creature of sorrow. As I saw Honorine, I understood Octave’s passion and the truthfulness of his description: ‘A heavenly flower!’

“Her pallor struck me with its unique tone of white—there are as many tones of white as of red or blue. Looking at the Countess, the eye seemed to feel that tender skin, where blood flowed in blue veins. At the slightest emotion, blood mounted under the surface in rosy flushes. When we met, the sunshine filtering through the acacia’s light foliage cast a pale gold glow around Honorine, reminiscent of the ambient glory in which Raphael and Titian enwrapped the Virgin. Her brown eyes expressed tenderness and vivacity, their brightness reflected in her face through long downcast lashes. Merely lifting her delicate eyelids, Honorine could cast a spell; there was so much feeling, dignity, terror, or contempt in her way of raising or dropping those veils of the soul. She could freeze or give life with a look. Her light-brown hair, carelessly knotted, framed a high, powerful, dreamy brow. The mouth was wholly voluptuous. To crown it all, her head bore a stamp of nobility that would defy the outrages of time.

“Though slight, Honorine was not thin, and her figure seemed capable of reviving love when it believed itself exhausted. She perfectly represented the idea conveyed by the word _mignonne_, being one of those pliant women who allow themselves to be taken up, petted, and set down again like a kitten. Her small feet made a light sound on the gravel, harmonizing with the rustle of her dress, producing a feminine music that remained distinct from the footfalls of a thousand other women. Her gait bore all the quarterings of her race with such pride that even the least respectful working man would make way for her. Gay and tender, haughty and imposing, she was impossible to understand except as gifted with these seemingly incompatible qualities, which nevertheless left her still a child. But it was a child who might be as strong as an angel; and, like the angel, once hurt in her nature, she would be implacable.

“Coldness on that face must be death to those on whom her eyes had smiled, for whom her lips had parted, for those whose souls had drunk in the melody of her voice, lending her words the poetry of song by its peculiar intonation. Inhaling the perfume of violets that accompanied her, I understood how the memory of this wife had arrested the Count on the threshold of debauchery, and how impossible it would be to forget a creature who truly was a flower to the touch, a flower to the eye, a flower of fragrance, a heavenly flower to the soul. Honorine inspired devotion, chivalrous devotion, regardless of reward. A man seeing her must think:

“‘Think, and I will divine your thought; speak, and I will obey. If my life, sacrificed in torments, can procure you one day’s happiness, take my life; I will smile like a martyr at the stake, for I shall offer that day to God, as a token to which a father responds on recognizing a gift to his child.’ Many women study their expressions, producing effects similar to those which would strike you at first sight of the Countess; only, in her, it was all the outcome of a delightful nature, that inimitable nature that goes straight to the heart. If I tell you all this, it is because her soul, her thoughts, the exquisiteness of her heart, are all we are concerned with, and you would have blamed me if I had not sketched them for you.

“I was very near forgetting my part as a half-crazy lout, clumsy and by no means chivalrous.

“‘I am told, madame, that you are fond of flowers?’

“‘I am an artificial flower-maker,’ she said. ‘After growing flowers, I imitate them, like a mother who is artist enough to have the pleasure of painting her children… That is enough to tell you that I am poor and unable to pay for the concession I am anxious to obtain from you?’

“‘But how,’ said I, as grave as a judge, ‘can a lady of such rank as yours would seem to be, ply so humble a calling? Have you, like me, good reasons for employing your fingers to keep your brains from working?’

“‘Let us stick to the question of the wall,’ she said with a smile.

“‘Why, we have begun at the foundations,’ said I. ‘Must I not know which of us ought to yield to the other in behalf of our suffering, or, if you choose, of our mania?—Oh! what a charming clump of narcissus! They are as fresh as this spring morning!’

“I assure you, she had created a perfect museum of flowers and shrubs, arranged in a way that only the sun might see, and the arrangement was prompted by artistic genius; even the most heartless landlord must have treated it with respect. The masses of plants, arranged according to their height or in single clumps, were truly a joy to the soul. This retired and solitary garden breathed comforting scents, suggesting only sweet thoughts and graceful, even voluptuous pictures. It bore that inscrutable sign-manual, which our true character stamps on everything, as soon as nothing compels us to obey the various hypocrisies, necessary as they are, which Society insists on. I looked alternately at the mass of narcissus and at the Countess, pretending to be far more in love with the flowers than with her, to maintain my part.

“‘So you are very fond of flowers?’ said she.

“‘They are,’ I replied, ‘the only beings that never disappoint our cares and affection.’ I went on to deliver such a diatribe comparing botany and the world that we ended miles away from the dividing wall, and the Countess must have thought me a wretched and wounded sufferer worthy of her pity. However, after half an hour, my neighbor naturally brought me back to the point; for women, when they are not in love, have all the cold blood of an experienced attorney.

“‘If you insist on my leaving the paling,’ said I, ‘you will learn all the secrets of gardening that I want to hide; I am seeking to grow a blue dahlia, a blue rose; I am crazy for blue flowers. Is not blue the favorite color of superior souls? We are neither of us really at home; we might as well make a little door of open railings to unite our gardens… You, too, are fond of flowers; you will see mine, I shall see yours. If you receive no visitors at all, I, for my part, have none but my uncle, the Cure of the White Friars.’

“‘No,’ said she, ‘I will give you the right to come into my garden, my premises at any hour. Come and welcome; you will always be admitted as a neighbor with whom I hope to keep on good terms. But I like my solitude too well to burden it with any loss of independence.’

“‘As you please,’ said I, and with one leap, I was over the paling.

“‘Now, of what use would a door be?’ said I from my own domain, turning to the Countess and mocking her with a madman’s gesture and grimace.

“For a fortnight, I seemed to take no heed of my neighbor. Towards the end of May, one lovely evening, we happened to be out on opposite sides of the paling, both walking slowly. Having reached the end, we could not help exchanging a few civil words; she found me in such deep dejection, lost in painful meditations, that she spoke to me of hopefulness in brief sentences that sounded like lullabies. I then leaped the fence and found myself at her side for the second time. The Countess led me into the house, wishing to soothe my sadness. At last, I had penetrated the sanctuary where everything harmonized with the woman I have tried to describe.

“Exquisite simplicity reigned there. The interior of the little house was just such a dainty box as the art of the eighteenth century devised for the pretty profligacy of a fine gentleman. The dining-room, on the ground floor, was painted in fresco, with garlands of flowers, marvelously executed. The staircase was charmingly decorated in monochrome. The little drawing-room, opposite the dining-room, was faded; but the Countess had hung it with panels of tapestry from old screens. A bathroom came next. Upstairs, there was only one bedroom, with a dressing-room and a library she used as her workroom. The kitchen was beneath in the basement, raised by several steps outside. The balustrade of a balcony in garlands a la Pompadour concealed the roof; only the lead cornices were visible. In this retreat, one was a hundred leagues from Paris.

“But for the bitter smile that occasionally played on the beautiful red lips of this pale woman, it would have been possible to believe that this violet buried in her thicket of flowers was happy. In a few days, we reached a certain degree of intimacy, the result of our close proximity and the Countess’ conviction that I was indifferent to women. A look would have spoiled everything, and I never allowed a thought of her to be seen in my eyes. Honorine regarded me as an old friend. Her manner towards me stemmed from a kind of pity. Her looks, voice, and words showed that she was a hundred miles away from the coquetry that the strictest virtue might allow under such circumstances. She soon granted me the right to enter her pretty workshop, a retreat full of books and curiosities, as smart as a boudoir where elegance emphasized the vulgarity of her tools. The Countess had, over time, poetized a thing that is at the antipodes of poetry—a manufacture.

“Perhaps of all the work a woman can do, making artificial flowers allows her to display the most grace. For coloring prints, she must sit bent over a table, devoting herself to this half-painting. Embroidering tapestry, as diligently as a woman must who earns her living by it, entails consumption or curvature of the spine. Engraving music is laborious, requiring care, minute exactitude, and intelligence. Sewing and white embroidery do not earn thirty sous a day. But making flowers and light articles of wear necessitates a variety of movements, gestures, and ideas that do not take a pretty woman out of her sphere; she remains herself; she may chat, laugh, sing, or think.

“There was certainly an artistic feeling in how the Countess arranged myriad-colored petals on a long table for composing flowers. The saucers of color were white china, always clean, arranged so the eye could see the required shade in the scale of tints. Thus, the aristocratic artist saved time. A pretty little cabinet with a hundred tiny drawers, ebony inlaid with ivory, contained the little steel molds for shaping leaves and petals. A fine Japanese bowl held the paste, which was never allowed to turn sour, with a fitted cover that she could lift with a fingertip. The wire, of iron and brass, lurked in a little drawer of the table before her.

“Under her eyes, in a Venetian glass shaped like a flower-cup on its stem, was the living model she strove to imitate. She had a passion for achievement; she attempted the most difficult things, close racemes, the tiniest corollas, heaths, and nectaries of the most varied hues. Her hands, as swift as her thoughts, moved from the table to the flower she was making, as those of an accomplished pianist fly over the keys. Her fingers seemed to be fairies, to use Perrault’s expression, so infinite were the different actions of twisting, fitting, and pressure needed for the work, all hidden under graceful movement, while she adapted each motion to the result with instinctive lucidity.

“I could not tire of admiring her as she shaped a flower from the materials sorted before her, padding the wire stem and adjusting the leaves. She displayed the genius of a painter in her bold attempts; she copied faded flowers and yellowing leaves; she even struggled with wildflowers, the most artless and the most elaborate in their simplicity.

“‘This art,’ she would say, ‘is in its infancy. If the women of Paris had a little of the genius that the slavery of the harem brings out in Oriental women, they would lend a complete language of flowers to the wreaths they wear on their heads. To please my own taste as an artist, I have made drooping flowers with leaves the hue of Florentine bronze, such as are found before or after winter. Would not such a crown on the head of a young woman whose life is a failure have a certain poetical fitness? How many things a woman might express by her head-dress! Are there not flowers for drunken Bacchantes, flowers for gloomy and stern bigots, pensive flowers for bored women? Botany, I believe, can express every sensation and thought of the soul, even the most subtle.’

“She employed me to stamp out leaves, cut up material, and prepare wires for the stems. My affected desire for occupation made me soon skilled. We talked as we worked. When I had nothing to do, I read new books to her, for I had to maintain my part as a man weary of life, worn out with grief, gloomy, skeptical, and soured. My person led to adorable banter about my purely physical resemblance—except for his club foot—to Lord Byron. It was tacitly acknowledged that her own troubles, about which she kept profound silence, far outweighed mine, though the causes I assigned for my misanthropy might have satisfied Young or Job.

“I will say nothing of the feelings of shame that tormented me as I inflicted on my heart, like beggars in the street, false wounds to excite the compassion of that enchanting woman. I soon appreciated the extent of my devotion by learning to estimate the baseness of a spy. The sympathy bestowed on me would have comforted the greatest grief. This charming creature, weaned from the world and alone for so many years, having, besides love, treasures of kindness to bestow, offered these to me with childlike effusiveness and such compassion that it would inevitably have filled with bitterness any profligate who fell in love with her; for, alas, it was all charity, all sheer pity. Her renunciation of love, her dread of what is called happiness for women, she proclaimed with equal vehemence and candor. These happy days proved to me that a woman’s friendship is far superior to her love.

“I suffered the revelations of my sorrows to be dragged from me with as many grimaces as a young lady allows herself before sitting down to the piano, so conscious are they of the annoyance that will follow. As you may imagine, the necessity for overcoming my dislike to speak had induced the Countess to strengthen the bonds of our intimacy; but she found in me such an exact counterpart of her own antipathy to love that I fancied she was content with the chance that had brought to her desert island a sort of Man Friday. Solitude was perhaps beginning to weigh on her. At the same time, there was nothing of the coquette in her; nothing remained of the woman; she did not feel she had a heart, except in the ideal world where she found refuge. I involuntarily compared these two lives—hers and the Count’s: his, all activity, agitation, and emotion; hers, all inaction, quiescence, and stagnation. The woman and the man were admirably obedient to their nature. My misanthropy allowed me to utter cynical sallies against both men and women, hoping to bring Honorine to the confidential point; but she was not to be caught in any trap, and I began to understand that mulish obstinacy is more common among women than is generally supposed.

“‘The Orientals are right,’ I said to her one evening, ‘when they shut you up and regard you merely as the playthings of their pleasure. Europe has been well punished for having admitted you to form an element of society and for accepting you on an equal footing. In my opinion, woman is the most dishonorable and cowardly being to be found. Nay, and that is where her charm lies. Where would be the pleasure of hunting a tame thing? When once a woman has inspired a man’s passion, she is sacred to him forever; in his eyes, she is hedged round by an imprescriptible prerogative. Men’s gratitude for past delights is eternal. Even if he finds his mistress grown old or unworthy, the woman still has rights over his heart; but to you women, the man you have loved is as nothing; nay, more, he is unpardonable in one thing—he lives on! You dare not own it, but you all harbor the feeling that the popular calumny called tradition ascribes to the Lady of the Tour de Nesle: “What a pity it is that we cannot live on love as we live on fruit, and that when we have had our fill, nothing should survive but the remembrance of pleasure!”’

“‘God has, no doubt, reserved such perfect bliss for Paradise,’ she said. ‘But if your argument seems witty to you, it has the disadvantage of being false. What can those women be who give themselves up to a succession of loves?’ she asked, looking at me as the Virgin in Ingres’ picture looks at Louis XIII. offering her his kingdom.

“‘You are an actress in good faith,’ said I, ‘for you gave me a look just now that would make an actress famous. Still, lovely as you are, you have loved; _ergo_, you forget.’

“‘I!’ she exclaimed, evading my question, ‘I am not a woman. I am a nun, and seventy-two years old!’

“‘Then, how can you so positively assert that you feel more keenly than I? Sorrow has but one form for women. The only misfortunes they regard are disappointments of the heart.’

“She looked at me sweetly, and, like all women caught between the issues of a dilemma or held in the clutches of truth, she persisted in her willfulness.

“‘I am a nun,’ she said, ‘and you talk to me of the world where I shall never again set foot.’

“‘Not even in thought?’ said I.

“‘Is the world so much to be desired?’ she replied. ‘Oh! when my mind wanders, it goes higher. The angel of perfection, the beautiful angel Gabriel, often sings in my heart. If I were rich, I should work, all the same, to keep me from soaring too often on the many-tinted wings of the angel, and wandering in the world of fancy. There are meditations that ruin us women! I owe much peace of mind to my flowers, though sometimes they fail to occupy me. On some days, I find my soul invaded by purposeless expectancy; I cannot banish an idea that takes possession of me, making my fingers clumsy. I feel that some great event is impending, that my life is about to change; I listen vaguely, I stare into the darkness, I have no liking for my work, and after a thousand fatigues, I find life once more—everyday life. Is this a warning from heaven? I ask myself…’

“After three months of this struggle between two diplomats, concealed under the semblance of youthful melancholy, and a woman whose disgust for life made her invulnerable, I told the Count that it was impossible to drag this tortoise out of her shell; it must be broken. The evening before, in our last friendly discussion, the Countess had exclaimed:

“‘Lucretia’s dagger wrote in letters of blood the watchword of woman’s charter: _Liberty!_’

“From that moment, the Count left me free to act.

“‘I have been paid a hundred francs for the flowers and caps I made this week!’ Honorine exclaimed gleefully one Saturday evening when I visited her in the little sitting-room on the ground floor, which the unavowed proprietor had had regilt.

“It was ten o’clock. The twilight of July and a glorious moon lent us their misty light. Gusts of mingled perfumes soothed the soul; the Countess was jingling the five gold pieces given to her by a supposititious dealer in fashionable frippery, another of Octave’s accomplices found for him by a judge, M. Popinot.

“‘I earn my living by amusing myself,’ she said; ‘I am free, when men, armed with their laws, have tried to make us slaves. Oh, I have transports of pride every Saturday! In short, I like M. Gaudissart’s gold pieces as much as Lord Byron, your double, liked Mr. Murray’s.’

“‘This is not becoming in a woman,’ said I.

“‘Pooh! Am I a woman? I am a boy gifted with a soft soul, that is all; a boy whom no woman can torture…’

“‘Your life is the negation of your whole being,’ I replied. ‘What? You, on whom God has lavished His choicest treasures of love and beauty, do you never wish…’

“‘For what?’ said she, somewhat disturbed by a speech that, for the first time, contradicted my assumed role.

“‘For a pretty little child, with curling hair, running and playing among the flowers, like a flower itself of life and love, calling you mother!’

“I waited for an answer. A prolonged silence led me to perceive the terrible effect of my words, though the darkness initially concealed it. Leaning on her sofa, the Countess had not fainted, but frozen under a nervous attack, the first chill, as gentle as everything that was part of her, feeling, as she later said, like the influence of a most insidious poison. I called Madame Gobain, who came and led her mistress away, laid her on her bed, unlaced her, undressed her, and restored her, not to life, it is true, but to the consciousness of some dreadful suffering. I meanwhile walked up and down the path behind the house, weeping and doubting my success. I wished to abandon the part of the bird-catcher I had so rashly assumed. Madame Gobain, who found me with my face wet with tears, hastily went up again to say to the Countess:

“‘What has happened, madame? Monsieur Maurice is crying like a child.’

“Roused to action by the evil interpretation that might be put on our mutual behavior, she summoned superhuman strength to put on a wrapper and come down to me.

“‘You are not the cause of this attack,’ said she. ‘I am subject to these spasms, a sort of cramp of the heart…’

“‘And will you not tell me of your troubles?’ said I, in a voice that could not be affected, as I wiped away my tears. ‘Have you not just now told me that you have been a mother, and have been so unhappy as to lose your child?’

“‘Marie!’ she called as she rang the bell. Gobain came in.

“‘Bring lights and some tea,’ said she, with the calm decision of a Mylady clothed in the armor of pride by the dreadful English training you know too well.

“When the housekeeper had lit the tapers and closed the shutters, the Countess showed me a mute countenance; her indomitable pride and gravity, worthy of a savage, had already reasserted their mastery. She said:

“‘Do you know why I like Lord Byron so much? It is because he suffered as animals do. Of what use are complaints when they are not an elegy like Manfred’s, nor bitter mockery like Don Juan’s, nor a reverie like Childe Harold’s? Nothing shall be known of me. My heart is a poem that I lay before God.’

“‘If I chose—’ said I.

“‘If?’ she repeated.

“‘I have no interest in anything,’ I replied, ‘so I cannot be inquisitive; but, if I chose, I could know all your secrets by tomorrow.’

“‘I defy you!’ she exclaimed, with ill-disguised unease.

“‘Seriously?’

“‘Certainly,’ said she, tossing her head. ‘If such a crime is possible, I ought to know it.’

“‘In the first place, madame,’ I went on, pointing to her hands, ‘those pretty fingers, which show you are not a mere girl—were they made for toil? Then you call yourself Madame Gobain, you, who, in my presence the other day on receiving a letter, said to Marie: “Here, this is for you?” Marie is the real Madame Gobain; so you conceal your name behind that of your housekeeper. Fear nothing, madame, from me. You have in me the most devoted friend you will ever have: Friend, do you understand me? I give this word its sacred and pathetic meaning, so profaned in France, where we apply it to our enemies. And your friend, who will defend you against everything, only wishes you to be as happy as such a woman ought to be. Who can tell whether the pain I have involuntarily caused you was not a voluntary act?’

“‘Yes,’ she replied with threatening audacity, ‘I insist on it. Be curious, and tell me all that you can find out about me; but,’ and she held up her finger, ‘you must also tell me by what means you obtain your information. The preservation of the small happiness I enjoy here depends on the steps you take.’

“‘That means you will fly—’

“‘On wings!’ she cried, ‘to the New World—’

“‘Where you will be at the mercy of the brutal passions you will inspire,’ I interrupted. ‘Is it not the very essence of genius and beauty to shine, to attract men’s gaze, to excite desires and evil thoughts? Paris is a desert with Bedouins; it is the only place in the world where those who must work for their livelihood can hide their lives. What have you to complain of? Who am I? An additional servant—M. Gobain, that is all. If you have to fight a duel, you may need a second.’

“‘Never mind; find out who I am. I have already said that I insist. Now, I beg that you will,’ she went on, with the grace that you ladies have at command,” said the Consul, looking at the ladies.

“‘Well, then, tomorrow, at the same hour, I will tell you what I may have discovered,’ I replied. ‘But do not therefore hate me! Will you behave like other women?’

“‘What do other women do?’

“‘They lay upon us immense sacrifices, and when we have made them, they reproach us for it later as if it were an injury.’

“‘They are right if the thing required appears to be a sacrifice!’ replied she pointedly.

“‘Instead of sacrifices, say efforts and—’

“‘It would be an impertinence,’ said she.

“‘Forgive me,’ said I. ‘I forget that woman and the Pope are infallible.’

“‘Good heavens!’ said she after a long pause, ‘only two words would be enough to destroy the peace so dearly bought, and which I enjoy like a fraud—’

“She rose and paid no further heed to me.

“‘Where can I go?’ she said. ‘What is to become of me? Must I leave this quiet retreat, which I arranged with such care to end my days in?’

“‘To end your days!’ exclaimed I with visible alarm. ‘Has it never struck you that a time would come when you could no longer work, when competition will lower the price of flowers and articles of fashion—?’

“‘I have already saved a thousand crowns,’ she said.

“‘Heavens! what privations such a sum must represent!’ I exclaimed.

“‘Leave me,’ said she, ‘till tomorrow. This evening I am not myself; I must be alone. Must I not save my strength in case of disaster? For if you should learn anything, others besides you would be informed, and then—Good-night,’ she added shortly, dismissing me with an imperious gesture.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“The battle is to-morrow, then,” I said with a smile, trying to appear indifferent. As I walked down the avenue, the words echoed in my mind: “The battle is to-morrow.”

Octave shared Honorine’s anxiety. The Count and I walked together until two in the morning, pacing by the trenches of the Bastille like generals preparing for battle, aware that victory hinged on seizing the right opportunity. Each of us lay awake, one hopeful, the other in dread of reunion. The true dramas of life unfold not in circumstances but in feelings, played out in the heart, in what we might call the Spiritual World. Octave and Honorine existed in a realm of lofty spirits.

I arrived punctually at ten the next evening, entering a charming bedroom furnished in white and blue—the sanctuary of this wounded dove. The Countess looked at me, about to speak, but fell silent at my respectful demeanor.

“Madame la Comtesse,” I said with a grave smile.

She sank back into her chair, enveloped in grief, a pose I wished a great painter could capture.

“You are,” I continued, “the wife of the noblest and most respected of men, a man acknowledged as great, yet far greater in his conduct towards you than in the eyes of the world. You and he are two lofty natures. Where do you suppose you are living?” 

“In my own house,” she replied, astonished.

“In Count Octave’s,” I corrected. “You have been deceived. M. Lenormand, the usher of the Court, is merely a front for your husband. The delightful seclusion you enjoy is his doing; the money you earn is from him, and his protection extends to every detail of your life. Your husband has saved you in the eyes of the world, providing plausible reasons for your disappearance. He professes hope that you were not lost in the wreck of the _Cecile_, the ship you took to Havana to secure your inheritance. He has sent agents to various places and received letters that give him great hope. He takes as many precautions to hide you as you do yourself. In short, he obeys you...”

“That is enough,” she interrupted. “I want to know one thing more. From whom have you obtained all these details?”

“Well, madame, my uncle got a position for a penniless youth as secretary to the local police commissary. That young man told me everything. If you leave this house tonight, your husband will know where you’ve gone, and his care will follow you everywhere. How could a clever woman like you believe that shopkeepers buy flowers and caps at the prices they sell them? Ask a thousand crowns for a bouquet, and you will get it. No mother’s tenderness is more ingenious than your husband’s! I learned from the porter that the Count often comes behind the fence at night to see the glimmer of your nightlight! Your large cashmere shawl cost six thousand francs—your old-clothes seller brings you second-hand items fresh from the best makers. In short, you are living here like Venus in Vulcan’s toils, but you are alone in your prison due to a sublime magnanimity that has persisted for seven years.”

The Countess trembled like a trapped swallow, studying me with defiance. Her dry eyes sparkled with a fierce light; still, she was a woman! Tears eventually forced their way out, not from being touched but from feeling helpless; they were tears of desperation. She had believed herself independent and free; marriage weighed on her like a prison cell.

“I will go!” she cried through her tears. “He forces me to it; I will go where no one will come after me.”

“What,” I said, “you would kill yourself? You must have very powerful reasons for not wishing to return to Comte Octave.”

“Certainly I have!”

“Then tell me; tell my uncle. You will find two devoted advisers in us. Though my uncle is a priest in the confessional, he is not one in a drawing-room. We will listen to you; we will try to find a solution to your problems. If you are the dupe or victim of some misunderstanding, perhaps we can clear it up. Your soul is pure, but if you have done wrong, your fault is fully expiated... At any rate, remember that in me you have a sincere friend. If you wish to evade the Count’s tyranny, I will find you the means; he shall never find you.”

“Oh! there is always a convent!” she said.

“Yes, but the Count, as Minister of State, can procure your rejection from every convent in the world. Even though he is powerful, I will save you from him—but only when you demonstrate that you cannot and ought not to return to him. Do not fear that you would escape his power only to fall into mine,” I added, noticing her glance of suspicion. “You shall have peace, solitude, and independence; in short, you shall be as free and as little annoyed as if you were an ugly, cross old maid. I would never see you without your consent.”

“And how? By what means?”

“That is my secret. I am not deceiving you, of that you may be sure. Prove to me that this is the only life you can lead, that it is preferable to that of the Comtesse Octave, rich, admired, in one of the finest houses in Paris, beloved by her husband, a happy mother... and I will decide in your favor.”

“But,” she said, “will there never be a man who understands me?”

“No. And that is why I appeal to religion to decide between us. The Cure of the White Friars is a saint, seventy-five years old. My uncle is not a Grand Inquisitor; he is Saint John. But for you, he will be Fenelon—the Fenelon who said to the Duc de Bourgogne: ‘Eat a calf on a Friday by all means, monseigneur. But be a Christian.’”

“Nay, nay, monsieur, the convent is my last hope and my only refuge. There is none but God who can understand me. No man, not even Saint Augustine, could enter into the scruples of my conscience, which are to me as the circles of Dante’s hell, whence there is no escape. Another man, a different man, however unworthy, has had all my love. No, he has not had it, for he did not take it; I gave it to him as a mother gives her child a wonderful toy, which it breaks. For me, there can never be two loves. In some natures, love can never be on trial; it is, or it is not. When it comes, when it rises up, it is complete. That life of eighteen months was to me a life of eighteen years; I poured all my being into it, which was not impoverished by its effusiveness; it was exhausted by that delusive intimacy in which I alone was genuine. For me, the cup of happiness is not drained, nor empty; nothing can refill it, for it is broken. I am out of the fray; I have no weapons left. Having utterly abandoned myself, what am I?—the leavings of a feast. I had but one name bestowed on me, Honorine, as I had but one heart. My husband had the young girl; a worthless lover had the woman—there is nothing left! Then let myself be loved! That is the great idea you mean to utter to me. Oh! but I still am something, and I rebel at the idea of being a prostitute! Yes, by the light of the conflagration, I saw clearly; and I tell you—well, I could imagine surrendering to another man’s love, but to Octave’s?—No, never.”

“Ah! you love him,” I said.

“I esteem him, respect him, venerate him; he has never done me the smallest hurt; he is kind, he is tender; but I can never love him. However,” she continued, “let us talk no more of this. Discussion makes everything small. I will express my notions on this subject in writing to you, for at this moment they are suffocating me; I am feverish, my feet are standing in the ashes of my Paraclete. All that I see, these things I believed I had earned by my labor, now remind me of everything I wish to forget. Ah! I must fly from hence as I fled from my home.”

“Where will you go?” I asked. “Can a woman exist unprotected? At thirty, in all the glory of your beauty, rich in powers of which you have no suspicion, full of tenderness to be bestowed, are you prepared to live in the wilderness where I could hide you?—Be quite easy. The Count, who for nine years has never allowed himself to be seen here, will never go there without your permission. You have his sublime devotion of nine years as a guarantee for your tranquillity. You may therefore discuss the future in perfect confidence with my uncle and me. My uncle has as much influence as a Minister of State. So compose yourself; do not exaggerate your misfortune. A priest whose hair has grown white in the exercise of his functions is not a boy; you will be understood by him, to whom every passion has been confided for nearly fifty years, and who weighs in his hands the ponderous heart of kings and princes. If he is stern under his stole, in the presence of your flowers, he will be as tender as they are, and as indulgent as his Divine Master.”

I left the Countess at midnight; she appeared calm but depressed, harboring some secret purpose that eluded my understanding. I found the Count a few paces away in the Rue Saint-Maur. Drawn by an irresistible attraction, he had left the spot on the Boulevards where we had agreed to meet.

“What a night my poor child will go through!” he exclaimed after I recounted the scene. “Supposing I were to go to her! What if she were to see me suddenly?”
