“At this moment, she is capable of throwing herself out of the window,” I replied. “The Countess is one of those Lucretias who could not survive any violence, even if it were done by a man into whose arms she could throw herself.”

“You are young,” he answered; “you do not know that in a soul tossed by such dreadful alternatives, the will is like waters of a lake lashed by a tempest; the wind changes every instant, and the waves are driven now to one shore, now to the other. During this night, the chances are quite as great that on seeing me, Honorine might rush into my arms as that she would throw herself out of the window.”

“And you would accept the equal chances?” I asked.

“Well, come,” he said, “I have at home a dose of opium which Desplein prepared for me to send me to sleep without any risk!”

The next day at noon, Gobain brought me a letter, informing me that the Countess had gone to bed at six, worn out with fatigue, and that, having taken a soothing draught prepared by the chemist, she had now fallen asleep.

“This is her letter, of which I kept a copy—for you, mademoiselle,” said the Consul, addressing Camille. “You know all the resources of art, the tricks of style, and the efforts made in their compositions by writers who do not lack skill; but you will acknowledge that literature could never find such language in its assumed pathos; there is nothing so terrible as truth. Here is the letter written by this woman, or rather by this anguish:

“‘MONSIEUR MAURICE,—

“I know all your uncle would say to me; he is not better informed than my own conscience. Conscience is the interpreter of God to man. I know that if I am not reconciled to Octave, I shall be damned; that is the sentence of religious law. Civil law condemns me to obey, cost what it may. If my husband does not reject me, the world will regard me as pure, as virtuous, whatever I may have done. Yes, that much is sublime in marriage; society ratifies the husband’s forgiveness; but it forgets that the forgiveness must be accepted. Legally, religiously, and from the world’s point of view, I ought to go back to Octave. Keeping only to the human aspect of the question, is it not cruel to refuse him happiness, to deprive him of children, to wipe his name out of the Golden Book and the list of peers? My sufferings, my repugnance, my feelings, all my egoism—for I know that I am an egoist—ought to be sacrificed to the family. I shall be a mother; the caresses of my child will wipe away many tears! I shall be very happy; I certainly shall be much looked up to. I shall ride, haughty and wealthy, in a handsome carriage! I shall have servants and a fine house, and be the queen of as many parties as there are weeks in the year. The world will receive me handsomely. I shall not have to climb up again to the heaven of aristocracy; I shall never have come down from it. So God, the law, society are all in accord.

“‘“What are you rebelling against?” I am asked from the height of heaven, from the pulpit, from the judge’s bench, and from the throne, whose august intervention may at need be invoked by the Count. Your uncle, indeed, at need, would speak to me of a certain celestial grace which will flood my heart when I know the pleasure of doing my duty.

“‘God, the law, the world, and Octave all wish me to live, no doubt. Well, if there is no other difficulty, my reply cuts the knot: I will not live. I will become white and innocent again; for I will lie in my shroud, white with the blameless pallor of death. This is not in the least “mulish obstinacy.” That mulish obstinacy of which you jestingly accused me is in a woman the result of confidence, of a vision of the future. Though my husband, sublimely generous, may forget all, I shall not forget. Does forgetfulness depend on our will? When a widow re-marries, love makes a girl of her; she marries a man she loves. But I cannot love the Count. It all lies in that, do you see?

“‘Every time my eyes met his, I should see my sin in them, even when his were full of love. The greatness of his generosity would be the measure of the greatness of my crime. My eyes, always uneasy, would be forever reading an invisible condemnation. My heart would be full of confused and struggling memories; marriage can never move me to the cruel rapture, the mortal delirium of passion. I should kill my husband by my coldness, by comparisons which he would guess, though hidden in the depths of my conscience. Oh! on the day when I should read a trace of involuntary, even of suppressed reproach in a furrow on his brow, in a saddened look, in some imperceptible gesture, nothing could hold me: I should be lying with a fractured skull on the pavement, and find that less hard than my husband. It might be my own over-susceptibility that would lead me to this horrible but welcome death; I might die the victim of an impatient mood in Octave caused by some matter of business, or be deceived by some unjust suspicion. Alas! I might even mistake some proof of love for a sign of contempt!

“‘What torture on both sides! Octave would be always doubting me, I doubting him. I, quite involuntarily, should give him a rival wholly unworthy of him, a man whom I despise, but with whom I have known raptures branded on me with fire, which are my shame, but which I cannot forget.

“‘Have I shown you enough of my heart? No one, monsieur, can convince me that love may be renewed, for I neither can nor will accept love from anyone. A young bride is like a plucked flower; but a guilty wife is like a flower that has been walked over. You, who are a florist, know whether it is ever possible to restore the broken stem, to revive the faded colors, to make the sap flow again in the tender vessels of which the whole vegetative function lies in their perfect rigidity. If some botanist should attempt the operation, could his genius smooth out the folds of the bruised corolla? If he could remake a flower, he would be God! God alone can remake me! I am drinking the bitter cup of expiation; but as I drink it, I painfully spell out this sentence: Expiation is not annihilation.

“‘In my little house, alone, I eat my bread soaked in tears; but no one sees me eat nor sees me weep. If I go back to Octave, I must give up my tears—they would offend him. Oh! monsieur, how many virtues must a woman tread underfoot, not to give herself, but to restore herself to a betrayed husband? Who could count them? God alone; for He alone can know and encourage the horrible refinements at which the angels must turn pale. Nay, I will go further. A woman has courage in the presence of her husband if he knows nothing; she shows a sort of fierce strength in her hypocrisy; she deceives him to secure him double happiness. But common knowledge is surely degrading. Supposing I could exchange humiliation for ecstasy? Would not Octave at last feel that my consent was sheer depravity? Marriage is based on esteem, on sacrifices on both sides; but neither Octave nor I could esteem each other the day after our reunion. He would have disgraced me by a love like that of an old man for a courtesan, and I should forever feel the shame of being a chattel instead of a lady. I should represent pleasure, and not virtue, in his house. These are the bitter fruits of such a sin. I have made myself a bed where I can only toss on burning coals, a sleepless pillow.

“‘Here, when I suffer, I bless my sufferings; I say to God, “I thank Thee!” But in my husband’s house, I should be full of terror, tasting joys to which I have no right.

“‘All this, monsieur, is not argument; it is the feeling of a soul made vast and hollow by seven years of suffering. Finally, must I make a horrible confession? I shall always feel at my bosom the lips of a child conceived in rapture and joy, and in the belief in happiness, of a child I nursed for seven months, that I shall bear in my womb all the days of my life. If other children should draw their nourishment from me, they would drink in tears mingling with the milk, turning it sour. I seem a light thing; you regard me as a child—Ah yes! I have a child’s memory, the memory which returns to us on the verge of the tomb. So, you see, there is not a situation in that beautiful life to which the world and my husband’s love want to recall me, which is not a false position, which does not cover a snare or reveal a precipice down which I must fall, torn by pitiless rocks. For five years now I have been wandering in the sandy desert of the future without finding a place convenient to repent in, because my soul is possessed by true repentance.

“‘Religion has its answers ready to all this, and I know them by heart. This suffering, these difficulties, are my punishment, she says, and God will give me strength to endure them. This, monsieur, is an argument to certain pious souls gifted with an energy which I have not. I have made my choice between this hell, where God does not forbid my blessing Him, and the hell that awaits me under Count Octave’s roof.

“‘One word more. If I were still a girl, with the experience I now have, my husband is the man I should choose; but that is the very reason for my refusal. I could not bear to blush before that man. What! I should be always on my knees, he always standing upright; and if we were to exchange positions, I should scorn him! I will not be better treated by him in consequence of my sin. The angel who might venture under such circumstances on certain liberties which are permissible when both are equally blameless is not on earth; he dwells in heaven! Octave is full of delicate feeling, I know; but even in his soul (which, however generous, is a man’s soul after all) there is no guarantee for the new life I should lead with him.

“‘Come then, and tell me where I may find the solitude, the peace, the silence, so kindly to irreparable woes, which you promised me.’

“After making this copy of the letter to preserve it complete, I went to the Rue Payenne. Anxiety had conquered the power of opium. Octave was walking up and down his garden like a madman.

“‘Answer that!’ I said, giving him his wife’s letter. ‘Try to reassure the modesty of experience. It is rather more difficult than conquering the modesty of ignorance, which curiosity helps to betray.’

“She is mine!” cried the Count, whose face expressed joy as he continued reading the letter.

He gestured for me to leave him. I understood that extreme happiness and extreme pain obey the same laws; I went in to receive Madame de Courteville and Amelie, who were to dine with the Count that day. However handsome Mademoiselle de Courteville might be, I felt, on seeing her again, that love has three aspects, and that the women who can inspire us with perfect love are very rare. As I involuntarily compared Amelie with Honorine, I found the erring wife more attractive than the pure girl. To Honorine’s heart, fidelity had not been a duty but the inevitable; while Amelie would serenely pronounce solemn promises without knowing their purport or to what they bound her. The crushed, the dead woman, so to speak, the sinner to be reinstated, seemed to me sublime; she incited the special generosities of a man’s nature; she demanded all the treasures of the heart, all the resources of strength; she filled his life and gave the zest of a conflict to happiness; whereas Amelie, chaste and confiding, would settle down into the sphere of peaceful motherhood, where the commonplace must be its poetry, and where my mind would find no struggle and no victory.

“Of the plains of Champagne and the snowy, storm-beaten but sublime Alps, what young man would choose the chalky, monotonous level? No; such comparisons are fatal and wrong on the threshold of the Mairie. Alas! only the experience of life can teach us that marriage excludes passion, that a family cannot have its foundation on the tempests of love. After having dreamed of impossible love, with its infinite caprices, after having tasted the tormenting delights of the ideal, I saw before me modest reality. Pity me, for what could be expected! At five-and-twenty, I did not trust myself; but I took a manful resolution.

“I went back to the Count to announce the arrival of his relations, and I saw him grow young again in the reflected light of hope.

“What ails you, Maurice?” he asked, struck by my changed expression.

“Monsieur le Comte—”

“No longer Octave? You, to whom I shall owe my life, my happiness—”

“My dear Octave, if you should succeed in bringing the Countess back to her duty, I have studied her well”—(he looked at me as Othello must have looked at Iago when Iago first contrived to insinuate a suspicion into the Moor’s mind)—“she must never see me again; she must never know that Maurice was your secretary. Never mention my name to her, or all will be undone... You have got me an appointment as Maitre des Requetes—well, get me instead some diplomatic post abroad, a consulship, and do not think of my marrying Amelie—Oh! do not be uneasy,” I added, seeing him draw himself up, “I will play my part to the end.”

“Poor boy!” he said, taking my hand, which he pressed while holding back tears.

“You gave me the gloves,” I said, laughing, “but I have not put them on; that is all.”

We then agreed on what I was to do that evening at Honorine’s house, whither I presently returned. It was now August; the day had been hot and stormy, but the storm hung overhead, the sky was like copper; the scent of the flowers was heavy, and I felt as if I were in an oven, wishing that the Countess might have set out for the Indies; but she was sitting on a wooden bench shaped like a sofa, under an arbor, in a loose white muslin dress fastened with blue bows, her hair unadorned in waving bands over her cheeks, her feet on a small wooden stool, showing a little way beyond her skirt. She did not rise; she gestured for me to sit beside her, saying:

“Now, is not life at a deadlock for me?”

“Life as you have made it,” I replied. “But not the life I propose to make for you; for, if you choose, you may be very happy...”

“How?” she asked, her whole person a question.

“Your letter is in the Count’s hands.”

Honorine started like a frightened doe, sprang a few paces away, walked down the garden, turned about, remained standing for some minutes, and finally went in to sit alone in the drawing-room, where I joined her after giving her time to get accustomed to the pain of this poniard thrust.

“You—a friend? Say rather a traitor! A spy, perhaps, sent by my husband.”

Instinct in women is as strong as the perspicacity of great men.

“You wanted an answer to your letter, did you not? And there was but one man in the world who could write it. You must read the reply, my dear Countess; and if after reading it you still find that your life is a deadlock, the spy will prove himself a friend; I will place you in a convent whence the Count’s power cannot drag you. But before going there, let us consider the other side of the question. There is a law, alike divine and human, which even hatred affects to obey, and which commands us not to condemn the accused without hearing his defense. Till now you have passed condemnation, as children do, with your ears stopped. The devotion of seven years has its claims. So you must read the answer your husband will send you. I have forwarded to him, through my uncle, a copy of your letter, and my uncle asked him what his reply would be if his wife wrote him a letter in such terms. Thus you are not compromised. He will himself bring the Count’s answer. In the presence of that saintly man, and in mine, out of respect for your own dignity, you must read it, or you will be no better than a wilful, passionate child. You must make this sacrifice to the world, to the law, and to God.”

As she saw in this concession no attack on her womanly resolve, she consented. All the labor of four or five months had been building up to this moment. But do not the Pyramids end in a point on which a bird may perch? The Count had set all his hopes on this supreme instant, and he had reached it.

In all my life, I remember nothing more formidable than my uncle’s entrance into that little Pompadour drawing-room at ten that evening. The fine head, with its silver hair thrown into relief by the entirely black dress, and the divinely calm face, had a magical effect on the Comtesse Honorine; she felt the cool balm on her wounds and beamed in the reflection of that virtue which gave light without knowing it.

“Monsieur the Cure of the White Friars,” said old Gobain.

“Are you come, uncle, with a message of happiness and peace?” I asked.

“Happiness and peace are always to be found in obedience to the precepts of the Church,” replied my uncle, handing the Countess the following letter:

“‘MY DEAR HONORINE,—

“If you had but done me the favor of trusting me, if you had read the letter I wrote to you five years since, you would have spared yourself five years of useless labor and of privations which have grieved me deeply. In it, I proposed an arrangement of which the stipulations will relieve all your fears and make our domestic life possible. I have much to reproach myself with, and in seven years of sorrow, I have discovered all my errors. I misunderstood marriage. I failed to scent danger when it threatened you. An angel was in the house. The Lord bid me guard it well! The Lord has punished me for my audacious confidence.

“You cannot give yourself a single lash without striking me. Have mercy on me, my dear Honorine. I so fully appreciated your susceptibilities that I would not bring you back to the old house in the Rue Payenne, where I can live without you, but which I could not bear to see again with you. I am decorating, with great pleasure, another house in the Faubourg Saint-Honore, to which, in hope, I conduct not a wife whom I owe to her ignorance of life, and secured to me by law, but a sister who will allow me to press on her brow such a kiss as a father gives the daughter he blesses every day.

“Will you bereave me of the right I have conquered from your despair—that of watching more closely over your needs, your pleasures, your life even? Women have one heart always on their side, always abounding in excuses—their mother’s; you never knew any mother but my mother, who would have brought you back to me. But how is it that you never guessed that I had for you the heart of a mother, both of my mother and of your own? Yes, dear, my affection is neither mean nor grasping; it is one of those which will never let any annoyance last long enough to pucker the brow of the child it worships. What can you think of the companion of your childhood, Honorine, if you believe him capable of accepting kisses given in trembling, of living between delight and anxiety? Do not fear that you will be exposed to the laments of a suppliant passion; I would not want you back until I felt certain of my own strength to leave you in perfect freedom.

“Your solitary pride has exaggerated the difficulties. You may, if you will, look on at the life of a brother, or of a father, without either suffering or joy; but you will find neither mockery nor indifference, nor have any doubt as to his intentions. The warmth of the atmosphere in which you live will be always equable and genial, without tempests, without a possible squall. If, later, when you feel secure that you are as much at home as in your own little house, you desire to try some other elements of happiness, pleasures, or amusements, you can expand their circle at your will. The tenderness of a mother knows neither contempt nor pity. What is it? Love without desire. Well, in me, admiration shall hide every sentiment in which you might see an offense.

“Thus, living side by side, we may both be magnanimous. In you, the kindness of a sister, the affectionate thoughtfulness of a friend, will satisfy the ambition of him who wishes to be your life’s companion; and you may measure his tenderness by the care he will take to conceal it. Neither you nor I will be jealous of the past, for we may each acknowledge that the other has sense enough to look only straight forward.

“Thus you will be at home in your new house exactly as you are in the Rue Saint-Maur; unapproachable, alone, occupied as you please, living by your own law; but having in addition the legitimate protection, of which you are now exacting the most chivalrous labors of love, with the consideration which lends so much luster to a woman, and the fortune which will allow you to do many good works. Honorine, when you long for an unnecessary absolution, you have only to ask for it; it will not be forced upon you by the Church or by the Law; it will wait on your pride, on your own impulsion. My wife might indeed have to fear all the things you dread; but not my friend and sister, towards whom I am bound to show every form and refinement of politeness. To see you happy is enough happiness for me; I have proved this for the seven years past. The guarantee for this, Honorine, is to be seen in all the flowers made by you, carefully preserved, and watered by my tears. Like the _quipos_, the tally cords of the Peruvians, they are the record of our sorrows.

“If this secret compact does not suit you, my child, I have begged the saintly man who takes charge of this letter not to say a word in my behalf. I will not owe your return to the terrors threatened by the Church, nor to the bidding of the Law. I will not accept the simple and quiet happiness that I ask from anyone but yourself. If you persist in condemning me to the lonely life, bereft even of a fraternal smile, which I have led for nine years, if you remain in your solitude and show no sign, my will yields to yours. Understand me perfectly: you shall be no more troubled than you have been until this day. I will get rid of the crazy fellow who has meddled in your concerns, and has perhaps caused you some annoyance...’

“‘Monsieur,’ said Honorine, folding up the letter, which she placed in her bosom, and looking at my uncle, ‘thank you very much. I will avail myself of Monsieur le Comte’s permission to remain here----’

“‘Ah!’ I exclaimed.

This exclamation made my uncle look at me uneasily and won from the Countess a mischievous glance, which enlightened me as to her motives.

Honorine had wanted to ascertain whether I were an actor, a bird snarer; and I had the melancholy satisfaction of deceiving her by my exclamation, which was one of those cries from the heart which women understand so well.

“‘Ah, Maurice,’ she said, ‘you know how to love.’

The light that flashed in my eyes was another reply that would have dissipated the Countess’ uneasiness if she still had any. Thus the Count found me useful to the very last.

Honorine then took out the Count’s letter again to finish reading it. My uncle signed to me, and I rose.

“Let us leave the Countess,” said he.

“You are going already, Maurice?” she asked, without looking at me.

She rose, and still reading, followed us to the door. On the threshold, she took my hand, pressed it very affectionately, and said, “We shall meet again...”

“No,” I replied, wringing her hand, so that she cried out. “You love your husband. I leave tomorrow.”

And I rushed away, leaving my uncle, to whom she said:

“Why, what is the matter with your nephew?”

The good Abbe completed my work by pointing to his head and heart, as much as to say, “He is mad, madame; you must forgive him!” and with all the more truth, because he really thought it.

Six days later, I set out with an appointment as vice-consul in Spain, in a large commercial town, where I could quickly qualify to rise in the career of a consul, to which I now restricted my ambition. After I had established myself there, I received this letter from the Count:

“‘MY DEAR MAURICE,—

“If I were happy, I should not write to you, but I have entered on a new life of suffering. I have grown young again in my desires, with all the impatience of a man of forty, and the prudence of a diplomatist, who has learned to moderate his passion. When you left, I had not yet been admitted to the _pavillon_ in the Rue Saint-Maur, but a letter had promised me that I should have permission—the mild and melancholy letter of a woman who dreaded the agitations of a meeting. After waiting for more than a month, I made bold to call and desired Gobain to inquire whether I could be received. I sat down in a chair in the avenue near the lodge, my head buried in my hands, and there I remained for almost an hour.

“‘“Madame had to dress,” said Gobain, to hide Honorine’s hesitancy under a pride of appearance which was flattering to me.

“‘During a long quarter of an hour, we both were possessed by an involuntary nervous trembling as great as that which seizes a speaker on the platform, and we spoke to each other sacred phrases, like those of persons taken by surprise who “make believe” a conversation.

“‘“You see, Honorine,” said I, my eyes full of tears, “the ice is broken, and I am so tremulous with happiness that you must forgive the incoherency of my language. It will be so for a long time yet.”

“‘“There is no crime in being in love with your wife,” said she with a forced smile.

“‘“Do me the favor,” said I, “no longer to work as you do. I have heard from Madame Gobain that for three weeks you have been living on your savings; you have sixty thousand francs a year of your own, and if you cannot give me back your heart, at least do not abandon your fortune to me.”

“‘“I have long known your kindness,” said she.

“‘“Though you should prefer to remain here,” said I, “and to preserve your independence; though the most ardent love should find no favor in your eyes, still, do not toil.”

“‘I gave her three certificates for twelve thousand francs a year each; she took them, opened them languidly, and after reading them through, she gave me only a look as my reward. She fully understood that I was not offering her money, but freedom.

“‘“I am conquered,” said she, holding out her hand, which I kissed. “Come and see me as often as you like.”

“‘So she had done herself a violence in receiving me. Next day, I found her armed with affected high spirits, and it took two months of habit before I saw her in her true character. But then it was like a delicious May, a springtime of love that gave me ineffable bliss; she was no longer afraid; she was studying me. Alas! when I proposed that she should go to England to return ostensibly to me, to our home, that she should resume her rank and live in our new residence, she was seized with alarm.

“‘“Why not live always as we are?” she said.

“‘I submitted without saying a word.

“‘“Is she making an experiment?” I asked myself as I left her. On my way from my own house to the Rue Saint-Maur, thoughts of love swelled in my heart, and I said to myself, like a young man, “This evening she will yield.”

“‘All my real or affected force was blown to the winds by a smile, by a command from those proud, calm eyes, untouched by passion. I remembered the terrible words you once quoted to me, “Lucretia’s dagger wrote in letters of blood the watchword of woman’s charter—Liberty!” and they froze me. I felt imperatively how necessary to me was Honorine’s consent, and how impossible it was to wring it from her. Could she guess the storms that distracted me when I left as when I came?

“‘At last, I painted my situation in a letter to her, giving up the attempt to speak of it. Honorine made no answer, and she was so sad that I made as though I had not written. I was deeply grieved by the idea that I could have distressed her; she read my heart and forgave me. And this was how. Three days ago, she received me, for the first time, in her own blue-and-white room. It was bright with flowers, dressed, and lighted up. Honorine was in a dress that made her bewitching. Her hair framed that face you know in its light curls; and in it were some sprays of Cape heath; she wore a white muslin gown, a white sash with long floating ends. You know what she is in such simplicity, but that day she was a bride, the Honorine of long past days. My joy was chilled at once, for her face was terribly grave; there were fires beneath the ice.

“‘“Octave,” she said, “I will return as your wife when you will. But understand clearly that this submission has its dangers. I can be resigned----”

“‘I made a movement.

“‘“Yes,” she went on, “I understand: resignation offends you, and you want what I cannot give—Love. Religion and pity led me to renounce my vow of solitude; you are here!” She paused.

“‘“At first,” she continued, “you asked no more. Now you demand your wife. Well, here I give you Honorine, such as she is, without deceiving you as to what she will be.--What shall I be? A mother? I hope it. Believe me, I hope it eagerly. Try to change me; you have my consent; but if I should die, my dear, do not curse my memory, and do not set down to obstinacy what I should call the worship of the Ideal, if it were not more natural to call the indefinable feeling which must kill me the worship of the Divine! The future will be nothing to me; it will be your concern; consult your own mind.”

“‘And she sat down in the calm attitude you used to admire, and watched me turning pale with the pain she had inflicted. My blood ran cold. On seeing the effect of her words, she took both my hands, and, holding them in her own, she said:

“‘“Octave, I do love you, but not in the way you wish to be loved. I love your soul.... Still, understand that I love you enough to die in your service like an Eastern slave, and without a regret. It will be my expiation.”

“‘She did more; she knelt before me on a cushion, and in a spirit of sublime charity, she said:

“‘“And perhaps I shall not die!”

“‘For two months now I have been struggling with myself. What shall I do? My heart is too full; I therefore seek a friend, and send out this cry, “What shall I do?”’

“I did not answer this letter. Two months later, the newspapers announced the return on board an English vessel of the Comtesse Octave, restored to her family after adventures by land and sea, invented with sufficient probability to arouse no contradiction.

“When I moved to Genoa, I received a formal announcement of the happy event of the birth of a son to the Count and Countess. I held that letter in my hand for two hours, sitting on this terrace—on this bench. Two months later, urged by Octave, by M. de Grandville, and Monsieur de Serizy, my kind friends, and broken by the death of my uncle, I agreed to take a wife.

“Six months after the revolution of July, I received this letter, which concludes the story of this couple:

“‘MONSIEUR MAURICE,—I am dying though I am a mother—perhaps because I am a mother. I have played my part as a wife well; I have deceived my husband. I have had happiness not less genuine than the tears shed by actresses on the stage. I am dying for society, for the family, for marriage, as the early Christians died for God! I know not of what I am dying, and I am honestly trying to find out, for I am not perverse; but I am bent on explaining my malady to you—you who brought that heavenly physician, your uncle, at whose word I surrendered. He was my director; I nursed him in his last illness, and he showed me the way to heaven, bidding me persevere in my duty.

“And I have done my duty.

“I do not blame those who forget. I admire them as strong and necessary natures; but I have the malady of memory! I have not been able twice to feel that love of the heart which identifies a woman with the man she loves. To the last moment, as you know, I cried to your heart, in the confessional, and to my husband, “Have mercy!” But there was no mercy. Well, and I am dying, dying with stupendous courage. No courtesan was ever more gay than I. My poor Octave is happy; I let his love feed on the illusions of my heart. I throw all my powers into this terrible masquerade; the actress is applauded, feasted, smothered in flowers; but the invisible rival comes every day to seek its prey—a fragment of my life. I am rent and I smile. I smile on two children, but it is the elder, the dead one, that will triumph! I told you so before. The dead child calls me, and I am going to him.

“The intimacy of marriage without love is a position in which my soul feels degraded every hour. I can never weep or give myself up to dreams but when I am alone. The exigencies of society, the care of my child, and that of Octave’s happiness never leave me a moment to refresh myself, to renew my strength, as I could in my solitude. The incessant need for watchfulness startles my heart with constant alarms. I have not succeeded in implanting in my soul the sharp-eared vigilance that lies with facility, and has the eyes of a lynx. It is not the lip of one I love that drinks my tears and kisses them; my burning eyes are cooled with water, and not with tender lips. It is my soul that acts a part, and that perhaps is why I am dying! I lock up my griefs with so much care that nothing is to be seen of it; it must eat into something, and it has attacked my life.

“I said to the doctors, who discovered my secret, “Make me die of some plausible complaint, or I shall drag my husband with me.”

“So it is quite understood by M. Desplein, Bianchon, and myself that I am dying of the softening of some bone which science has fully described. Octave believes that I adore him, do you understand? So I am afraid lest he should follow me. I now write to beg you in that case to be the little Count’s guardian. You will find with this a codicil in which I have expressed my wish; but do not produce it except in case of need, for perhaps I am fatuously vain. My devotion may perhaps leave Octave inconsolable but willing to live—poor Octave! I wish him a better wife than I am, for he deserves to be well loved.

“Since my spiritual spy is married, I bid him remember what the florist of the Rue Saint-Maur hereby bequeaths to him as a lesson: May your wife soon be a mother! Fling her into the vulgarest materialism of household life; hinder her from cherishing in her heart the mysterious flower of the Ideal—of that heavenly perfection in which I believed, that enchanted blossom with glorious colors, and whose perfume disgusts us with reality. I am a Saint-Theresa who has not been suffered to live on ecstasy in the depths of a convent, with the Holy Infant, and a spotless winged angel to come and go as she wished.

“You saw me happy among my beloved flowers. I did not tell you all: I saw love budding under your affected madness, and I concealed from you my thoughts, my poetry; I did not admit you to my kingdom of beauty. Well, well; you will love my child for love of me if he should one day lose his poor father. Keep my secrets as the grave will keep them. Do not mourn for me; I have been dead this many a day, if Saint Bernard was right in saying that where there is no more love, there is no more life.’”

“And the Countess died,” said the Consul, putting away the letters and locking the pocket-book.

“Is the Count still living?” asked the Ambassador, “for since the revolution of July, he has disappeared from the political stage.”

“Do you remember, Monsieur de Lora,” said the Consul-General, “having seen me going to the steamboat with—”

“A white-haired man! an old man?” said the painter.

“An old man of forty-five, going in search of health and amusement in Southern Italy. That old man was my poor friend, my patron, passing through Genoa to take leave of me and place his will in my hands. He appoints me his son’s guardian. I had no occasion to tell him of Honorine’s wishes.”

“Does he suspect himself of murder?” said Mademoiselle des Touches to the Baron de l’Hostal.

“He suspects the truth,” replied the Consul, “and that is what is killing him. I remained on board the steam packet that was to take him to Naples until it was out of the roadstead; a small boat brought me back. We sat for some little time taking leave of each other—for ever, I fear. God only knows how much we love the confidant of our love when she who inspired it is no more.

“‘That man,’ said Octave, ‘holds a charm and wears an aureole.’ The Count went to the prow and looked down on the Mediterranean. It happened to be fine, and, moved no doubt by the spectacle, he spoke these last words: ‘Ought we not, in the interests of human nature, to inquire what is the irresistible power which leads us to sacrifice an exquisite creature to the most fugitive of all pleasures, and in spite of our reason? In my conscience, I heard cries. Honorine was not alone in her anguish. And yet I would have it!... I am consumed by remorse. In the Rue Payenne, I was dying of the joys I had not; now I shall die in Italy of the joys I have had... Wherein lay the discord between two natures, equally noble, I dare assert?’”

For some minutes, profound silence reigned on the terrace.

Then the Consul, turning to the two women, asked, “Was she virtuous?”

Mademoiselle des Touches rose, took the Consul’s arm, went a few steps away, and said to him:

“Are not men wrong too when they come to us and make a young girl a wife while cherishing at the bottom of their heart some angelic image, and comparing us to those unknown rivals, to perfections often borrowed from a remembrance, and always finding us wanting?”

“Mademoiselle, you would be right if marriage were based on passion; and that was the mistake of those two, who will soon be no more. Marriage with heart-deep love on both sides would be Paradise.”

Mademoiselle des Touches turned from the Consul and was immediately joined by Claude Vignon, who said in her ear:

“A bit of a coxcomb is M. de l’Hostal.”

“No,” replied she, whispering to Claude, “for he has not yet guessed that Honorine would have loved him.—Oh!” she exclaimed, seeing the Consul’s wife approaching, “his wife was listening! Unhappy man!”

Eleven was striking by all the clocks, and the guests went home on foot along the seashore.

“Still, that is not life,” said Mademoiselle des Touches. “That woman was one of the rarest, and perhaps the most extraordinary exceptions in intellect—a pearl! Life is made up of various incidents, of pain and pleasure alternately. The Paradise of Dante, that sublime expression of the ideal, that perpetual blue, is to be found only in the soul; to ask it of the facts of life is a luxury against which nature protests every hour. To such souls as those, the six feet of a cell and the kneeling chair are all they need.”

“You are right,” said Leon de Lora; “but good-for-nothing as I may be, I cannot help admiring a woman who is capable, as that one was, of living by the side of a studio, under a painter’s roof, and never coming down, nor seeing the world, nor dipping her feet in the street mud.”

“Such a thing has been known—for a few months,” said Claude Vignon, with deep irony.

“Comtesse Honorine is not unique of her kind,” replied the Ambassador to Mademoiselle des Touches. “A man, nay, and a politician, a bitter writer, was the object of such a passion; and the pistol shot which killed him hit not him alone; the woman who loved lived like a nun ever after.”

“Then there are yet some great souls in this age!” said Camille Maupin, and she stood for some minutes pensively leaning on the balustrade of the quay.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!” exclaimed a lawyer’s clerk, Simonnin, as he leaned out of a window, munching on dry bread. He playfully shot a crumb at a stranger crossing the courtyard of Maitre Derville’s office in the Rue Vivienne.

“Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks on people, or I will turn you out of doors. However poor a client may be, he is still a man!” admonished the head clerk, who was busy with a bill of costs.

Simonnin, a typical messenger boy of about thirteen or fourteen, was known for his impudence and mischief. He and his fellow clerks, including Godeschal, were engaged in a light-hearted discussion about the old man in the courtyard, who they referred to as “old Box-coat.” Godeschal suggested they play a trick on him, claiming that M. Derville only saw clients at odd hours.

As the clerks continued their banter, the atmosphere in the office was lively, filled with laughter and camaraderie. The office itself was cluttered and dimly lit, with the smell of food mingling with the musty odor of old papers. The clerks were accustomed to the disarray, which reflected the chaotic nature of their work.

Suddenly, the old man entered the office, looking weary and out of place. He politely inquired if M. Derville was available, but the clerks, preoccupied with their own tasks, dismissed him. Boucard, the head clerk, suggested the man return at one in the morning, leaving the old man bewildered.

The clerks resumed their mockery, speculating about the old man’s past. They debated whether he was a nobleman or a former porter, each making humorous claims about his shabby appearance. When the old man returned to confirm his identity as Colonel Chabert, who had supposedly died at Eylau, the clerks erupted in laughter, dismissing his claim as a joke.

As the clerks continued their banter, they discussed their plans for the evening, debating which theater to attend. The conversation was filled with playful arguments and laughter, showcasing their camaraderie amidst the drudgery of their work.

Boucard eventually refocused the clerks on their tasks, emphasizing the importance of completing their work efficiently. The scene encapsulated the youthful exuberance and lightheartedness of the clerks, contrasting sharply with the somber reality of the old man’s situation.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At about one in the morning, Colonel Chabert knocked at the door of Maitre Derville, a prominent attorney in Paris. The porter informed him that Derville had not yet returned, but the Colonel insisted on his appointment and was shown upstairs. He found the head clerk organizing papers for the next day's cases. The clerk explained that Derville preferred to work at night when he could think clearly, and that the Colonel was only the third person to seek him out at such an hour.

When Derville arrived, he was taken aback by the Colonel's strange appearance. Chabert was thin and pale, with a scarred head that suggested a traumatic past. Upon introducing himself, Chabert claimed to be the Colonel who had been killed at Eylau, which led Derville and his clerk to suspect he was mad. Chabert insisted on sharing the details of his situation.

Derville, accustomed to dealing with various clients, urged Chabert to be concise. The Colonel recounted his military service at Eylau, where he was gravely injured and presumed dead. He explained that after being buried among the dead, he miraculously survived, thanks to the body of his horse shielding him. He described his harrowing experience of awakening in a grave, struggling to escape, and eventually being rescued by a woman who took him to a hospital.

After months of recovery, Chabert realized he had lost everything, including his identity. He had no documents to prove who he was, and his attempts to reclaim his life were met with disbelief and ridicule. He had wandered as a beggar, often treated as a madman, and had been confined in a mental institution for two years. Eventually, he was released but remained haunted by his past and the loss of his wife, the Countess Ferraud, who had remarried and had children.

Derville, realizing the gravity of Chabert's claims, expressed his disbelief but also his willingness to help. Chabert revealed that he sought to reclaim his rights and fortune, which he believed were unjustly taken from him. He lamented that no lawyer had taken him seriously enough to assist him in proving his identity and reclaiming his estate.

Moved by Chabert's plight, Derville offered to help him obtain the necessary documents from Germany and provided him with a small sum of money to support him in the meantime. Chabert was overwhelmed with gratitude, feeling a flicker of hope for the first time in years. He shared his longing for vengeance against his wife, who had moved on without him, and expressed his deep emotional turmoil.

Derville acknowledged the complexity of Chabert's case, noting that it would require careful consideration and could face significant legal challenges. Chabert, however, was resolute, stating that if he failed, he would rather die than continue living in obscurity. Derville assured him that he would treat the case with the seriousness it deserved and provided him with a letter to a notary for further assistance.

As Chabert left, he felt a renewed sense of purpose and determination. He had not only received financial help but also the promise of legal support in his quest to reclaim his identity and rights. The encounter marked a turning point for the Colonel, who had spent years in despair, and he left with a glimmer of hope for the future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
About three months after the interview, in Derville’s room, the notary Crottat, tasked with advancing half-pay to Derville’s eccentric client, sought the attorney's advice on a serious matter, beginning with a request to refund the six hundred francs the old soldier had received.

“Are you amusing yourself with pensioning the old army?” Crottat laughed, a young man who had recently taken over the office after his chief fled due to bankruptcy.

“Thank you for reminding me of that affair,” Derville replied. “My philanthropy will not extend beyond twenty-five louis; I fear I have already been duped by my patriotism.”

As Derville spoke, he noticed papers on his desk, including a letter with stamps from various post-offices. 

“Ah ha! Here is the last act of the comedy; now we shall see if I have been taken in!” he exclaimed, opening the letter, but finding it written in German.

“Boucard, go and have this letter translated immediately,” Derville instructed his head clerk.

The notary in Berlin informed Derville that the requested documents would arrive shortly and were all regular and legally stamped. He noted that most witnesses to the facts recorded were still in Eylau, Prussia, and the woman who saved M. le Comte Chabert was alive in Heilsberg.

“This looks like business,” Derville said after Boucard translated the letter. He then asked Crottat for information from his office regarding the settlement of Chabert’s estate, recalling the old rascal Roguin.

“Let’s call him the unfortunate Roguin,” Crottat interjected with a laugh.

“Wasn’t it that ill-used man who absconded with eight hundred thousand francs of his clients’ money?” Derville continued. “I believe he handled Chabert’s estate.”

“Yes,” Crottat confirmed. “I copied the papers when I was third clerk. Rose Chapotel, wife of Hyacinthe, called Chabert, Count of the Empire, married without settlement, so they held all property in common. The personalty was about six hundred thousand francs. Before his marriage, Colonel Chabert made a will leaving one-quarter to the hospitals and the State to take the other quarter. The will was contested, leading to a forced sale, and the widow received the portion bequeathed to the treasury.”

“So Comte Chabert’s personal fortune was no more than three hundred thousand francs?” Derville asked.

“Exactly!” Crottat replied. “You lawyers can be quite clear-headed despite accusations of false practices.”

Colonel Chabert was lodging in the Faubourg Saint-Marceau with an old quartermaster named Vergniaud. Derville had to walk to find his client, as his coachman refused to navigate the unpaved street. He eventually found shabby stone gate-posts with a sign reading “Vergniaud, dairyman.” The yard was a dismal sight, with a dilapidated house that seemed on the verge of collapse.

The house was poorly constructed, with mismatched windows and a floor of trodden earth. Derville noted the squalor but also the calm demeanor of the Colonel, who emerged from the house with a pipe in his mouth, displaying military coolness.

“Why did you not write to me?” Chabert asked, directing Derville to a better path to avoid the manure.

Derville entered the Colonel’s room, which contained only a chair and a bed of straw. The walls were damp, and the atmosphere was grim, yet Chabert seemed content. Derville expressed concern over the Colonel’s living conditions.

“Here, we are not distinguished for luxury,” Chabert replied. “But I have done no one wrong, and I sleep in peace.”

Derville hesitated to ask about the money he had advanced, instead inquiring why Chabert had not moved to Paris, where he could live better.

“The good folks here have taken me in and fed me for a year,” Chabert explained. “I cannot leave them now that I have a little money.”

Derville informed Chabert that he would soon receive papers from Heilsberg, and the woman who saved him was still alive.

“Curse the money! To think I haven’t got any!” Chabert exclaimed, throwing his pipe down in frustration.

Derville explained the complexities of the legal situation, emphasizing that proving Chabert’s identity would involve lengthy and costly legal battles. He warned that the case could be prolonged by the bigamy issue with Comtesse Ferraud, who had remarried.

“Things are not done so in the legal world,” Derville cautioned. “You will face many challenges, and your opponents will demand inquiries that could take years.”

Chabert, overwhelmed by the prospect of a lengthy lawsuit, expressed despair. Derville reassured him that the outcome could be favorable, suggesting a friendly suit to annul both the death certificate and the marriage.

“I will send you a power of attorney to sign,” Derville concluded, offering to help with finances.

Chabert, feeling hopeless, agreed to trust Derville completely. As Derville left, he encountered Louis Vergniaud, who expressed concern for Chabert’s well-being and requested a loan to help improve the Colonel’s living conditions.

Derville agreed to provide the funds but insisted that Chabert should be the one to help his friends. He then planned to visit Comtesse Ferraud, considering her position and the potential leverage he could gain.

Monsieur le Comte Ferraud, a former Councillor’s son, had returned to France after the Revolution and married Madame Chabert, who had managed to turn her share of her husband’s fortune into a substantial income. Their marriage was socially accepted, and Ferraud was politically ambitious, but he had begun to regret his union with Madame Ferraud.

Derville, reflecting on the situation, recognized the Countess’s precarious position. He arrived at the Hotel Ferraud, where he found her in a luxurious setting, contrasting sharply with Chabert’s squalor.

“Good-morning, Monsieur Derville,” she greeted him, but he quickly shifted to serious matters.

“I have come to speak with you on a very serious matter,” he stated, revealing that Colonel Chabert was alive.

The Countess laughed dismissively, but Derville’s serious demeanor silenced her. He warned her of the danger she faced if Chabert’s identity was proven, hinting at the potential legal ramifications.

She insisted that her second marriage was secure due to her children, but Derville countered that the law might not favor her if Chabert proved his identity. He suggested that Ferraud might not support her if he learned of the situation.

Derville’s words struck a nerve, and the Countess’s demeanor shifted as she considered the implications. He proposed a compromise, sensing her vulnerability.

“Does he still love me?” she asked, contemplating how to use Chabert’s affection to her advantage.

Derville left her with the suggestion to consider a compromise, confident that he had gained the upper hand in the situation.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A week after Derville's visits, Colonel Chabert and his wife, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet at their lawyer's office. Thanks to Derville's financial support, Chabert arrived looking dignified in a blue suit and the red ribbon of the Legion of Honor, embodying the soldierly style he had lost. He was transformed from the shabby figure he once was into a man who reflected the nobility of his past.

As Chabert entered Derville's office, Madame Ferraud arrived shortly after, dressed elegantly to appear youthful. The office remained unchanged, with clerks joking about Chabert's transformation. Derville, aware of the tension between the couple, separated them to discuss the terms of their agreement. He presented a memorandum outlining the conditions of their relationship, including Chabert's acknowledgment of his identity and the financial support he would receive from his wife.

The Countess was taken aback by the terms, particularly the financial obligations, and expressed her reluctance to engage in a lawsuit. When Chabert entered the room, he confronted her about her past, revealing the pain of their separation. The Countess, feigning surprise, attempted to leave, but Derville urged her to stay. Chabert, filled with anger and betrayal, expressed his desire for justice, but Derville cautioned him against rash actions.

After a heated exchange, the Countess led Chabert to her carriage, and they traveled to her country house in Groslay. During the ride, the Countess's tone shifted, revealing her emotional turmoil. She acknowledged her love for another man, Comte Ferraud, and expressed her desire to maintain dignity without public scandal. Chabert, though hurt, offered his understanding and support, recognizing the complexities of their situation.

At the country house, the Countess treated Chabert with kindness, attempting to erase the pain of their past. They reminisced about their life together, and Chabert found solace in her presence, though he struggled with feelings of betrayal. The Countess, meanwhile, plotted to secure her position and eliminate Chabert's claim to her.

As days passed, the Countess's unease grew, prompting her to send a letter to Delbecq, seeking to manipulate the situation further. When Chabert discovered her intentions, he felt betrayed and resolved to confront her. Their relationship became a battleground of emotions, with Chabert torn between love and resentment.

Ultimately, Chabert decided to sacrifice his identity for the sake of the Countess and their children, but the Countess's manipulations led him to realize the depth of her betrayal. He confronted her, expressing his disdain and choosing to live without claiming his former identity. The Countess, recognizing the consequences of her actions, understood that she could live peacefully on Chabert's word.

Chabert vanished from society, and months later, Derville learned of his fate when he encountered Chabert in a courtroom, now a vagabond. Despite his circumstances, Chabert maintained a sense of dignity, reflecting on the indifference of the world around him. Derville, moved by Chabert's plight, sought to help him, but Chabert's disdain for societal norms led him to reject material concerns.

In the end, Chabert's story became one of loss and resilience, illustrating the complexities of love, betrayal, and the struggle for identity in a world that often overlooks the individual.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In June 1840, Godeschal, now an attorney, accompanied Derville to Ris. As they approached Bicetre, they noticed an old pauper under an elm tree, engaged in drying his handkerchief in the sun. Dressed in a shabby wrapper-coat, the man had a striking appearance. Godeschal remarked on his resemblance to grotesque German carvings, while Derville recognized him as Comte Chabert, the husband of the pious Comtesse Ferraud, who had likely sent him to the workhouse due to his reminders of their past.

Intrigued, Godeschal encouraged Derville to share Chabert's story. Two days later, they returned to Bicetre and found the old man sitting on a tree trunk, drawing in the sand. When addressed, he insisted he was no longer Chabert but "Hyacinthe," and identified himself as a mere number in the institution. He expressed envy for a condemned man, believing him fortunate for not being married.

Godeschal offered him money for snuff, and Chabert eagerly accepted, thanking them as "brave troopers" and playfully pretending to aim his stick at them, exclaiming "Vive Napoleon!" Another pauper commented on Chabert's character, noting that he could be quite philosophical and imaginative, despite appearing childish at times. He recounted a past encounter where Chabert had impressed a Prussian officer with his military history.

Derville reflected on the tragic fate of Chabert, who had gone from a foundling to a soldier under Napoleon, only to end up in a workhouse. He lamented the disillusionment of three professions: the priest, the doctor, and the lawyer, all of whom witness the darker sides of humanity. Derville described the lawyer's experience as particularly grim, filled with tales of betrayal, greed, and moral decay. He recounted instances of familial strife, including a father abandoned by his daughters and mothers exploiting their children for personal gain. 

Godeschal acknowledged having seen similar horrors in his own legal practice and expressed a desire to escape the corruption of Paris for a quieter life in the countryside with his wife. 

The narrative concludes with an addendum listing characters from Balzac's "Human Comedy," indicating their appearances in other stories, including notable figures like Napoleon Bonaparte and Comtesse Ferraud.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Bianchon, a physician renowned for his theoretical physiology, initially trained in surgery under the illustrious Desplein, a celebrated French surgeon. Desplein's genius, while acknowledged, was ultimately personal and did not extend to a legacy that would influence future generations. His remarkable ability to diagnose and operate was coupled with a strong atheism, shaped by his experiences dissecting the human body without ever encountering a soul, which he believed was absent from the physical form.

Despite his brilliance, Desplein's life was marked by contradictions that his detractors seized upon to undermine his reputation. His eccentricities, such as fluctuating between lavish and indifferent dress, and his complex personality, which oscillated between kindness and harshness, led to mixed judgments about him. He was a man of profound insight, capable of understanding human nature deeply, yet he often displayed a disdain for humanity, having observed its follies closely.

Among his students, Horace Bianchon stood out, having endured poverty while studying medicine. Bianchon was characterized by his integrity, loyalty, and a cheerful disposition despite his financial struggles. His relationship with Desplein blossomed as the surgeon recognized Bianchon's potential, taking him under his wing and introducing him to the intricacies of medical practice and the social dynamics of Paris.

One day, Bianchon informed Desplein about a destitute water-carrier suffering from a debilitating illness. Desplein, moved by compassion, rushed to help the man, demonstrating a softer side to his otherwise stern demeanor. This act of kindness was not an isolated incident; Desplein had a particular fondness for helping the less fortunate, especially those from his native Auvergne.

Bianchon later witnessed Desplein entering a church, which surprised him given the surgeon's outspoken atheism. Curious, he followed Desplein and observed him attending mass, an act that seemed contradictory to his beliefs. During a dinner conversation, Desplein dismissed the mass as a farce, yet Bianchon remained puzzled by his master's actions.

Months later, another physician questioned Desplein about his church visit, to which Desplein provided a seemingly innocuous explanation. Bianchon, however, remained skeptical and decided to investigate further. He returned to the church on the same day the following year and found Desplein attending mass again, confirming a pattern. The sacristan revealed that Desplein had been attending this mass regularly for twenty years and had even founded it.

This revelation deepened the mystery surrounding Desplein's beliefs and actions, suggesting a complexity in his character that defied simple categorization. Bianchon was left to ponder the implications of a man who, while professing atheism, engaged in rituals that seemed to contradict his convictions, highlighting the intricate relationship between belief, practice, and the human experience.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Some time passed before Doctor Bianchon, despite their friendship, found a moment to discuss a significant incident in Desplein's life. Their meetings, whether in consultation or social settings, rarely allowed for private conversation. Finally, seven years later, after the Revolution of 1830, Bianchon spotted Desplein entering Saint-Sulpice and knelt beside him without surprise. They attended a mass together.

"Can you explain your sudden piety?" Bianchon asked as they left the church. "You, who do not believe in God, attending mass? You owe me an explanation."

"I am like many devout people—outwardly religious but inwardly skeptical," Desplein replied, launching into a critique of certain political figures.

"That doesn't answer my question," Bianchon insisted. "Why did you found this mass?"

"Well, my dear boy," Desplein said, "I am nearing the end of my life; I can share the beginnings of it."

They stood in the Rue des Quatre-Vents, a grim street in Paris. Desplein gestured to a building where he had lived for two years. "I lived up there," he said, recalling the hardships of his early life. "I endured hunger, poverty, and isolation. I had no money for books or medical training, and my irritable nature made it hard to connect with others. I was alone, struggling to rise from the depths of poverty."

Desplein recounted his meager meals and the sacrifices he made to pursue his studies. "I worked tirelessly, often without sympathy or support. I had no friends to share my burdens, and I felt the weight of my circumstances deeply."

He expressed frustration at those born into wealth who could not understand his struggles. "They ask why I got into debt, as if it were a choice. They have no idea what it means to be truly destitute."

Desplein continued, explaining how his early experiences shaped his view of humanity. "If I seem hard or bitter, it's because I've witnessed the selfishness and envy of those around me. In Paris, mediocrity often seeks to undermine those who strive for greatness."

He shared a pivotal moment when he faced extreme poverty and considered enlisting in the military. He had hoped for a box of linen from a relative, but when it arrived, he found it had cost too much for the porter to release it. Despairing, he encountered his neighbor, Bourgeat, a water-carrier who offered to help.

Bourgeat, despite his own struggles, paid Desplein's debts and helped him find new lodgings. "He devoted his savings to my future," Desplein said, visibly moved. "He understood my mission and supported me selflessly."

Desplein described Bourgeat as a nurturing figure, who cared for him like a mother. "He took pride in my success and never sought recognition for his sacrifices. When I became a house surgeon, I felt a deep pain leaving him behind, but he was proud of my achievements."

Desplein eventually repaid Bourgeat by buying him a horse and barrel, fulfilling a dream Bourgeat had cherished. "He was furious I had spent my money on him, yet he was delighted. He never claimed credit for my success, even though I owed him everything."

When Bourgeat fell ill, Desplein dedicated himself to his care, but ultimately, Bourgeat succumbed. "He died in my arms, leaving me everything he had. He had a strong faith, and I honored his wishes by having masses said for him after his death."

Desplein concluded, "I can only offer this as a tribute to Bourgeat's faith. I hope that if there is a paradise, he finds peace there. I would give anything for the faith he had."

Bianchon, who witnessed Desplein's last days, could not definitively say whether the great surgeon died an atheist. He pondered whether Bourgeat might have opened the gates of Heaven for Desplein, just as he had opened the doors to the earthly temple they had shared.

PARIS, January 1836.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########

In 1828, at around one o’clock in the morning, two friends, Dr. Horace Bianchon and Baron de Rastignac, left a house in the Rue du Faubourg Saint-Honoré. They decided to walk to the boulevard, as neither had a carriage available. Their conversation turned to the Marquise d’Espard, with Bianchon expressing skepticism about her character. Rastignac, however, was infatuated with her status and beauty, believing she could elevate his social standing and help him pay off debts.

Bianchon warned Rastignac that pursuing the Marquise would be a mistake, comparing her to a “one-eyed horse” and suggesting that her true age was more than what she claimed. He emphasized that a woman’s physical signs reveal her true age, regardless of cosmetics. Rastignac countered that he was drawn to the Marquise for her social power and potential to improve his life, dismissing Bianchon’s concerns about her character.

Bianchon, a physician, shared his disdain for women of fashion, arguing that they often lack genuine emotion and are driven by selfishness. He described the Marquise as a polished woman who hides her true nature behind a facade. Rastignac, however, argued that a woman of fashion could be a valuable ally for an ambitious man, providing social leverage and connections.

Despite Bianchon’s reservations, he agreed to help Rastignac by introducing him to his uncle, Jean-Jules Popinot, a judge who might assist in the Marquise’s affairs. Bianchon was skeptical about Popinot’s willingness to help, given his reputation for being unyielding.

The next morning, Bianchon set out to visit Popinot, who lived in a dilapidated building in a poor part of Paris. The description of the building highlighted its age and the contrast between its former grandeur and current state. Popinot, a judge, was characterized by his unkempt appearance and lack of ambition, which had led to his stagnation in the legal profession. Despite his intelligence and capability, he had been overlooked for promotions and remained a supernumerary judge.

Popinot’s life was marked by a commitment to justice, often at the expense of his own well-being. He was known for his kindness and ability to extract confessions without coercion, demonstrating a blend of compassion and firmness in his role. His character was further explored, revealing a man dedicated to his work, yet burdened by the injustices he witnessed in the legal system.

As Bianchon approached Popinot’s residence, he reflected on the challenge of persuading his uncle to assist Rastignac, aware of the complexities of the legal world and the obstacles that lay ahead.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Twelve years prior to this narrative, in 1816, during a severe food scarcity in France, Popinot was appointed President of the Commission Extraordinary to distribute food to the impoverished in his neighborhood. He had intended to leave the Rue du Fouarre, a place he and his wife disliked. As a prominent lawyer and criminal judge, Popinot had spent five years observing legal outcomes without understanding their causes. His experiences with the destitute led him to develop deep compassion, transforming him into a protector of the poor, akin to a modern-day Saint Vincent de Paul.

This transformation was gradual; charity, like vice, has its temptations. Popinot moved from one act of charity to another, eventually becoming a vital figure in his community, involved in various charitable organizations. He worked tirelessly to prevent crime, provide employment, and assist the vulnerable, all while maintaining a secret life that few in Paris recognized. The gratitude of those he helped was often unexpressed, as they were too burdened by their struggles to acknowledge his kindness.

By the end of his second year of charitable work, Popinot had converted a storeroom in his home into a parlor for the needy. This room, filled with wooden benches and basic furniture, served as a hub for the poor, where they could seek assistance. Popinot meticulously recorded donations and requests for aid, ensuring he could manage his resources effectively. His aide, Lavienne, assisted him by managing pawn tickets and visiting areas most affected by famine.

From early morning until late, the parlor was crowded with women, children, and the destitute seeking help. The atmosphere was often stifling, but the warmth of the crowd negated the need for heating. The poor revered Popinot, often waiting outside his door before dawn, and even criminals respected his home. He dedicated his mornings to the needy, afternoons to legal work, and evenings to his judicial duties.

Popinot's keen observational skills allowed him to discern the hidden virtues in the impoverished, as well as the faintest signs of wrongdoing in others. His modest inherited fortune was supplemented by his late wife's wealth, but his salary as a judge was limited. This financial reality necessitated his frugality, which was further compounded by his selfless dedication to charity. Despite his significant contributions, he remained one of the few judges in the Court of the Seine without the Legion of Honor.

Popinot was tasked by the President of the Second Chamber of the Court to examine the Marquis d’Espard at the request of his wife, who sought a Commission in Lunacy. The Rue du Fouarre, bustling with the needy in the early hours, would become desolate by nine o'clock. Bianchon, Popinot's nephew, rode to find his uncle amidst his charitable work, anticipating the contrast between Popinot's appearance and the Marquise d’Espard's expectations.

Upon arrival, Bianchon was greeted by the familiar sight of the parlor filled with the destitute, each person a testament to the struggles of poverty. The room was a tableau of hardship, with individuals displaying a range of emotions from despair to resilience. Popinot, dressed in a worn nightcap and dressing gown, was deeply engrossed in his work, embodying the dedication of a man committed to alleviating the suffering around him.

As Bianchon approached, he noted the peculiarities of the assembly, where the faces of the poor told stories of their struggles. Popinot, despite his disheveled appearance, was a figure of authority and compassion, diligently attending to each individual’s needs. Lavienne, his assistant, facilitated the process, ensuring that the flow of requests was managed efficiently.

Bianchon interrupted to discuss a matter of urgency with his uncle, who was initially distracted by the ongoing consultations. After a brief exchange, Popinot resumed his work, addressing the needs of a woman in distress who required funds to sustain her grocery business while her husband was imprisoned for debt. Popinot provided her with the necessary assistance, documenting the transaction in his ledger.

As the morning progressed, Bianchon learned that Popinot had seen over two hundred individuals that day, with more waiting. He was instructed to attend to urgent medical matters, and upon his return, he found the parlor empty, the last of the needy having received help.

Popinot's home reflected his chaotic lifestyle, filled with dust and disarray, a testament to his single-minded focus on his charitable endeavors. Papers cluttered every surface, and the remnants of his late wife's possessions lingered, creating an atmosphere of neglect. Despite the disorder, the room was adorned with tokens of gratitude from those he had helped, a poignant reminder of his impact.

As they prepared for breakfast, Bianchon urged Popinot to dress more appropriately for his upcoming meeting with the Marquise d’Espard. Popinot, however, was resistant to the idea of dining with her, citing legal restrictions against magistrates accepting hospitality from parties involved in cases they oversee. Bianchon argued that it would be beneficial for Popinot to meet her in her own environment, where she could express her concerns more comfortably.

After some persuasion, Popinot agreed to visit the Marquise, recognizing the importance of understanding her perspective in the case. He made a note of the appointment, preparing to delve deeper into the complexities surrounding the Marquis d’Espard's mental state and the implications for his family.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next evening at nine, Bianchon climbed his uncle’s dusty staircase to find him working on a complicated judgment. Since the tailor had not sent the coat Lavienne ordered, Popinot wore his old stained coat, which amused those unaware of his private life. Bianchon straightened his uncle's cravat and buttoned his coat, attempting to hide the stains, but the judge's habitual movements soon rucked it up, revealing a gap between his waistcoat and trousers. Bianchon realized this absurdity just as his uncle entered the Marquise’s room.

To understand the ensuing interview, a brief overview of Madame d’Espard is necessary. For seven years, she had been a fashionable figure in Paris, where trends can elevate or diminish individuals. After separating from her husband in 1815, Madame d’Espard, around thirty-three years old, maintained her status as a fashionable woman, despite being a mother of two boys aged fifteen and thirteen. Her ability to remain in vogue was remarkable, as Fashion often favors youth and beauty.

The Marquise, who appeared much younger, employed various methods to maintain her looks, including elaborate hairstyles and a strict regimen of cold baths and minimal food. Her efforts mirrored those of a famous Polish lady who, despite her age, retained the vitality of youth. Madame d’Espard’s complexion was clear, and her figure retained the allure that attracted men, though she remained emotionally detached, knowing neither love nor hatred. She had parted from M. d’Espard with satisfaction, as he took their children, whom she viewed as obstacles to her ambitions.

Living on an income of twenty-six thousand francs a year, the Marquise hesitated before deciding how to manage her life post-separation. Although she had the means to live lavishly, she chose a more subdued lifestyle during the years following her separation. By 1820, she re-emerged into society, hosting gatherings and establishing herself among the elite, eventually taking the place of other prominent women in Paris.

Madame d’Espard cultivated relationships with influential figures, including a duchess and a countess, which enhanced her political clout. Her drawing-room became a hub for political discussions, and she was known for her discretion and ability to navigate the complexities of Parisian society. Among her admirers were ambitious young men, and she skillfully maintained her independence while wielding considerable influence.

Despite her apparent charm, Madame d’Espard was also known for her indiscretion and vanity. She viewed relationships as a means to an end, seeking to expand her influence rather than genuine connections. Her drawing-room was frequented by notable figures, and she adeptly managed her interactions to avoid compromising herself.

Rastignac, a young man seeking to align himself with her, recognized her potential as a powerful ally but found himself challenged by her cunning. The Marquise’s household was grand, reflecting her wealth and status, and when Popinot arrived, he noted the opulence surrounding him. The Marquise welcomed him with feigned weakness, and a third man, the Chevalier d’Espard, stood silently, observing.

Popinot, while appearing simple, was astute and quickly assessed the situation. He engaged Madame d’Espard in conversation, questioning her about her husband’s mental state and their separation. She presented a carefully crafted narrative, claiming her husband’s change in temperament led to their estrangement. Popinot, however, remained skeptical, probing deeper into the circumstances surrounding their separation and the well-being of her children.

As the conversation progressed, Popinot’s inquiries revealed inconsistencies in Madame d’Espard’s story. He noted the lavishness of her lifestyle compared to her claims of financial hardship, suggesting that her motives for seeking control over her husband’s affairs might be self-serving. The Marquise, feeling cornered, attempted to deflect his questions, but Popinot’s persistence forced her to confront the implications of her situation.

The tension in the room escalated as Popinot laid bare the potential consequences of her actions. He emphasized the need for transparency and honesty, warning her that the court would scrutinize her motives. Despite her attempts to maintain composure, the Marquise realized that her carefully constructed facade was at risk of crumbling.

After the meeting, Bianchon and Rastignac discussed Popinot’s character and the implications of the case. They recognized the complexity of the situation and the potential for scandal, with Bianchon expressing concern over the Marquise’s influence. Popinot, meanwhile, resolved to investigate further, planning to summon Madame Jeanrenaud, the woman linked to M. d’Espard, to clarify the situation.

The following day, Madame Jeanrenaud arrived at Popinot’s office, presenting a stark contrast to the Marquise. Described as stout and unrefined, she denied any wrongdoing and expressed concern for M. d’Espard’s well-being. Her honest demeanor and straightforwardness provided a stark contrast to the Marquise’s calculated charm.

Popinot, intrigued by her sincerity, began to piece together the narrative surrounding M. d’Espard’s financial dealings. He recognized that the case was more complicated than it initially appeared, with potential implications for both the Marquise and her husband. As he prepared to confront the complexities of the situation, he understood that the truth would require careful examination of all parties involved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
