Charles stepped back, and they exchanged good-nights with smiles. Both fell asleep in the same dream, and from that moment, Charles began to wear roses with his mourning.

The next day, before breakfast, Madame Grandet found Eugenie in the garden with Charles. He was still sad, burdened by the weight of his misfortune. “My father will not be home until dinner,” Eugenie said, noticing her mother’s anxious look.

The connection between Eugenie and Charles was evident in their shared thoughts and the sweetness of her voice. Charles spent the morning in the hall, his sadness respected by the three women, each occupied with their tasks. Grandet had neglected his affairs, and various tradesmen came to discuss business. Madame Grandet and Eugenie listened to the endless chatter of workmen and farmers, while Nanon prepared the produce they brought.

Around five, Grandet returned from Angers, having made fourteen thousand francs from the exchange on his gold. He had left Cornoiller at Angers to look after the horses, which were nearly foundered.

“I am hungry,” he said upon entering.

Nanon called from the kitchen, “Haven’t you eaten since yesterday?”

“Nothing,” he replied.

Nanon brought in soup just as Des Grassins arrived to take Grandet’s orders. Grandet had not even noticed his nephew. “Go on eating, Grandet,” said the banker. “Do you know what gold is worth in Angers? They have come from Nantes after it? I shall send some of ours.”

“Don’t send any,” Grandet replied. “They have enough. We are such old friends, I ought to save you from a loss of time.”

“But gold is worth thirteen francs fifty centimes.”

“Say _was_ worth—”

“Where have they got any?”

“I went to Angers last night,” Grandet said quietly.

The banker was astonished, and a whispered conversation began between them, often glancing at Charles. Des Grassins looked surprised, likely learning of Grandet’s plans to invest a sum that would yield him a hundred thousand francs a year.

“Monsieur Grandet,” Des Grassins said to Charles, “I am going to Paris; if you have any commissions—”

“None, monsieur, I thank you,” Charles replied.

“Thank him better than that, nephew. Monsieur is going to settle the affairs of the house of Guillaume Grandet.”

“Is there any hope?” Charles asked eagerly.

“What!” exclaimed his uncle with feigned pride. “Are you not my nephew? Your honor is ours. Is not your name Grandet?”

Charles rose, kissed his uncle, turned pale, and left the room. Eugenie looked at her father with admiration.

“Well, good-bye, Des Grassins; it is all in your hands. Decoy those people as best you can; lead them by the nose.”

The two men shook hands, and Grandet returned to his armchair, asking Nanon for some black-currant ratafia. Too excited to sit still, he got up, looked at a portrait, and began to sing, doing what Nanon called his dancing steps.

“Dans les gardes francaises
J’avais un bon papa.”

Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie exchanged silent glances. Grandet’s joviality frightened them when it reached its peak. The evening passed quickly, and Grandet chose to go to bed early, expecting everyone else to follow suit. As for Madame Grandet, she lived according to her husband’s will. During the two hours of digestion, Grandet, more jovial than ever, shared his peculiar sayings, one of which captured his mindset: “You have no sooner put your lips to a glass than it is empty! Such is life. You can’t have and hold. Gold won’t circulate and stay in your purse. If it were not for that, life would be too fine.” He was cheerful and generous, offering Nanon some ratafia, which she gladly accepted.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The following day, the family gathered for breakfast, creating a scene of domestic intimacy. Grief had united Madame Grandet, Eugenie, and Charles, while even Nanon felt a sense of sympathy. The old man, preoccupied with his avarice and the prospect of sending Charles away without further expense, remained indifferent to the children’s interactions. He focused on his agricultural tasks, leaving the two young people free to express their feelings under Madame Grandet’s watchful eye.

For Eugenie, love blossomed in the wake of their shared secret. Their relationship deepened, marked by tender glances and gentle words, reminiscent of childhood innocence. Eugenie found joy in comforting Charles, whose pain she sought to alleviate with the sweetness of new love. Their connection, forged in the somber atmosphere of mourning, felt pure and sacred. Charles, having experienced only the tumult of worldly love, now embraced this genuine affection, appreciating the simplicity of their lives together.

After breakfast, while Grandet attended to his fields, Charles enjoyed the pleasure of being with Eugenie and her mother, finding beauty in their modest existence. He had previously thought such morals were only found in Germany, but now he saw them as real and enchanting. Eugenie became his ideal, and he cherished her innocence, while she surrendered to the current of their love, unaware of the impending separation that loomed over them.

Three days after des Grassins’ departure, Grandet took Charles to the Civil courts to renounce his rights to his father’s estate. This act felt like a painful betrayal. Charles also arranged for powers of attorney and a passport, selling his belongings to prepare for his journey. Grandet was pleased with Charles’s new appearance in mourning clothes, seeing him as a man ready to embark on his fortune.

Charles collected his valuables, intending to sell them, but Grandet offered to buy them instead, valuing them at nearly a thousand francs. Charles, grateful for his uncle’s generosity, accepted the arrangement. He also gifted his mother’s thimble to Madame Grandet, who was deeply touched. Grandet, in turn, insisted on paying for Charles’s passage to the Indies, further solidifying their bond.

As Charles prepared to leave, Eugenie expressed her sadness, and he acknowledged the weight of his obligations. He had transformed from a boy into a man, and Eugenie admired his newfound strength. The family donned mourning attire for a Requiem Mass for Guillaume Grandet, and during breakfast, Charles received letters from Paris regarding his affairs, which he shared with Eugenie.

In the garden, Charles revealed that his affairs were well-managed, and he had arranged for his belongings to be sent to Nantes. He expressed the reality of their impending separation, urging Eugenie not to tie her future to his uncertain fate. Despite this, their love remained strong, and they shared a passionate kiss, affirming their bond.

As Charles announced his departure, Grandet showed unexpected interest, taking care to pack Charles’s belongings himself and ensuring they were sent to Nantes. The day of departure was filled with emotion, as the family escorted Charles to the diligence. Grandet offered words of encouragement, and Charles expressed gratitude for his uncle’s support.

After Charles left, the house felt empty to Eugenie, who wished to keep his room as it was. She and Nanon conspired to maintain the status quo, hoping for his return. Eugenie’s beauty transformed as she embraced her love, becoming more radiant and dignified. She followed Charles’s journey on a map, longing for his well-being and reflecting on their shared moments.

Eugenie’s love became a constant presence in her life, filling her thoughts and shaping her daily existence. While she maintained a facade of cheerfulness around her father, she confided in Nanon about her feelings for Charles. Nanon, despite her own unfulfilled desires, empathized with Eugenie’s plight, recognizing the depth of her mistress’s love and the pain of separation.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Two months passed, and the once monotonous domestic life of Eugenie and her mother was now filled with the intense secret that connected them. Charles lived in their thoughts, and Eugenie often gazed at her aunt's portrait, searching for his features. One Sunday, her mother discovered the truth about the gold Eugenie had given Charles, leading to a moment of panic. 

“What will you say to your father on New Year’s Day when he asks to see your gold?” Madame Grandet exclaimed, terrified. The two women spent a morning in dread, missing high Mass and fearing the impending drama that awaited them. 

Madame Grandet lamented, “What will become of us?” Her anxiety had left her unprepared for the winter, and she felt a chill from her husband’s impending wrath. She suggested they could have consulted Monsieur des Grassins in Paris for help, but Eugenie insisted it was too late. 

On January 1, 1820, the cold winter morning prompted Madame Grandet to request a fire in her room, hoping to avoid a confrontation with her husband. Grandet, in a surprisingly good mood, agreed, revealing he had received a large sum of money from his investments. 

As he prepared for breakfast, he boasted about his financial success, oblivious to the tension in the room. The two women wished him a happy New Year, but their hearts sank as he continued to talk about money and investments. 

After breakfast, Grandet demanded to see Eugenie’s gold, insisting she return it to him. Eugenie, feeling cornered, claimed she no longer had it. Grandet’s anger escalated as he accused her of deceit, demanding to know what she had done with the gold. 

Eugenie defended her actions, asserting her independence as an adult. Grandet, furious, threatened to punish her by confining her to her room on bread and water until she submitted to him. Eugenie, heartbroken, fled to her mother, who was also suffering from the strain of the situation. 

Grandet, still seething, accused Eugenie of giving her gold to Charles, whom he deemed unworthy. Madame Grandet pleaded for her husband to forgive their daughter, but Grandet remained obstinate, insisting on his authority. 

Eugenie, feeling the weight of her father’s anger, resolved to stand firm in her love for Charles. Nanon, the servant, worried about Eugenie’s fate and vowed to support her. 

That evening, Grandet dined alone for the first time in years, while the usual guests arrived, surprised by the absence of Madame Grandet and Eugenie. Grandet dismissed their concerns, but the tension in the household was palpable. 

Later, Nanon secretly brought Eugenie a hare pâté, determined that she would not live on bread alone. Their bond deepened as they navigated the challenges posed by Grandet’s harshness and the looming threat of his wrath.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For several months, the old wine-grower visited his wife’s room at all hours, never mentioning their daughter, Eugenie, who remained confined. Madame Grandet's health deteriorated, and Grandet became increasingly harsh and unyielding, even making mistakes in his business dealings. The townspeople speculated about the Grandet family, and it soon became known that Eugenie was being kept in her room on bread and water by her father's orders, with only Nanon providing her with secret meals. The community condemned Grandet, and when Eugenie attended Mass, her gentle demeanor drew curiosity and sympathy.

Eugenie’s primary sorrow was her mother’s declining health. She often blamed herself for her mother’s suffering, and though Madame Grandet tried to comfort her, she longed for her daughter’s happiness. The mother expressed a desire for reconciliation between father and daughter, urging Grandet to bring Eugenie back into the family fold. However, Grandet remained indifferent, even as his wife’s condition worsened.

As spring approached, Madame Grandet confided her worries to the Cruchots, who were appalled by Eugenie’s treatment. They discussed the legal implications of her confinement, but Eugenie, overhearing them, insisted on her obedience to her father and requested their silence on the matter. She believed that criticizing her father would tarnish their family honor.

One day, Grandet, while walking in the garden, hesitated between his stubbornness and the desire to embrace his daughter. Meanwhile, the notary Cruchot visited Grandet, expressing concern for his wife’s health and the potential consequences of her death on Eugenie’s inheritance. Grandet, realizing the legal ramifications, became agitated and distressed at the thought of losing control over his wealth.

Cruchot advised Grandet to treat Eugenie kindly, as her inheritance could force him to sell his property. Grandet, feeling cornered, began to reconsider his harsh treatment of Eugenie. He returned home, determined to reconcile with her, but upon entering, he found Eugenie and her mother admiring a dressing-case filled with gold that had been given to Eugenie by Charles. Grandet, seeing the gold, became possessive and aggressive, leading to a confrontation where Eugenie pleaded for the gold to be returned to its rightful owner.

In a moment of desperation, Eugenie threatened to harm herself if her father did not relent. This shocked Grandet, who ultimately backed down, allowing Eugenie to keep the dressing-case. He then attempted to show affection towards both his wife and daughter, scattering coins on the bed and promising to be kinder.

Despite his newfound tenderness, Madame Grandet’s health continued to decline. She grew weaker, and her death seemed imminent. In October 1822, she passed away peacefully, leaving behind a legacy of love and patience. Her last words to Eugenie emphasized the importance of seeking happiness in heaven, expressing her concern for her daughter’s future in a selfish world.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On the day after her father's death, Eugenie felt a renewed attachment to her childhood home, where she had endured much suffering, including the loss of her mother. The sight of familiar objects brought her to tears, and she was surprised by her father's tender care, which seemed out of character for him. He took her to breakfast and watched her with an almost kind gaze, leading the household staff to believe he was losing his faculties due to old age. However, during the family’s mourning dinner, he revealed his true intentions.

“Dear child,” he said after the meal, “you are now your mother’s heiress, and we have some matters to settle.” He insisted on discussing the inheritance that evening, despite Eugenie's reluctance. The notary, Maitre Cruchot, explained that Grandet did not want to divide the property or pay taxes on the money he might have. To avoid this, he needed Eugenie to renounce her rights to her mother’s estate, allowing him to retain control over their shared property.

Eugenie, confused, asked for the deed to sign. Grandet, sweating with emotion, suggested that instead of signing, she could simply trust him and receive a monthly allowance of a hundred francs. She agreed, and he pressed her hand, declaring they were quits. The next morning, she signed the papers, effectively giving up her inheritance. However, a year later, Grandet had not given her the promised money. When she reminded him, he awkwardly offered her some jewels instead.

Over the next two years, Grandet trained Eugenie in managing the household and finances, instilling his frugal habits in her. By the third year, she had become the mistress of the house, and their monotonous life continued for five years without interruption. Eugenie's sadness was well-known, but she never spoke of it, and her only company was the Cruchots and their friends, who played whist with her father.

In 1827, Grandet, now eighty-two, suffered a stroke. As he deteriorated, Eugenie devoted herself to caring for him, feeling increasingly isolated. His death was unremarkable; he remained fixated on his hidden wealth until the end. When the priest came to administer last rites, Grandet's avarice was evident in his final words, urging Eugenie to take care of his riches.

After his death, Eugenie was left alone with only Nanon, her loyal servant, who was her sole confidante. Maitre Cruchot informed her of her substantial inheritance, totaling seventeen million francs. Despite her wealth, her thoughts were consumed with finding her cousin Charles. In the empty house, she and Nanon lamented their isolation, while news of her fortune spread throughout the province.

Eugenie’s first act was to secure an annuity for Nanon, who soon married Antoine Cornoiller, becoming housekeeper for Eugenie. Cornoiller managed the estate, and life continued without disruption for the farmers. At thirty, Eugenie had never experienced joy; her childhood was marked by her mother’s suffering and her own unfulfilled love for Charles, who had left her with only memories.

Her mourning period delayed any thoughts of marriage, and the Cruchots surrounded her with attention, hoping to win her favor. They praised her beauty, and she grew accustomed to the flattery, which became a daily occurrence. Monsieur de Bonfons, a prominent figure in the circle, sought to ingratiate himself with her, and the Cruchots discussed their financial prospects, all while Eugenie remained the center of attention.

As spring approached, Madame des Grassins attempted to stir interest in a potential match between Eugenie and the Marquis de Froidfond, emphasizing his noble lineage. However, Eugenie’s indifference to the idea was misinterpreted as interest, leading to speculation about her future. Despite the attention, she remained haunted by the absence of Charles, who had not contacted her in seven years.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
While events unfolded in Saumur, Charles was thriving in the Indies. His commercial ventures yielded six thousand dollars, and he quickly realized that buying and selling people was the most lucrative business. He traveled to Africa, purchasing enslaved individuals and combining this trade with other profitable goods. His relentless ambition was fueled by the desire to return to Paris with a significant fortune and reclaim a more prestigious position than before.

Through his experiences, Charles's views shifted; he became skeptical and lost his moral compass, recognizing that what was deemed a crime in one culture could be celebrated in another. His heart hardened, and he became ruthless, engaging in various exploitative trades, including selling people and defrauding customs. He bought pirate-captured goods at low prices and sold them for profit. Initially, he carried the memory of Eugenie with him, but as he indulged in various escapades, he distanced himself from his past, viewing his family with disdain and regarding Eugenie merely as a creditor.

This transformation explains Charles Grandet's silence. In the Indies and elsewhere, he adopted the alias "Shepherd" to protect his identity while pursuing wealth through unscrupulous means. His efforts paid off, and by 1827, he returned to Bordeaux aboard the "Marie Caroline," bringing with him gold-dust worth nearly two million francs, expecting to profit further at the Paris mint.

On the ship, he encountered Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman connected to the royal court, who was returning from the Indies with his family. The d’Aubrions, once a prominent family, were now struggling financially, and Madame d’Aubrion was eager to marry off her unattractive daughter to a wealthy suitor. She saw Charles as a potential son-in-law and promised to help him gain a noble title and a significant income by marrying her daughter. This union would elevate both families' status, allowing them to live comfortably in Paris.

Charles, intoxicated by ambition, entertained the idea of marrying Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, seeing it as a path to social prominence. He visited Annette, who encouraged him to pursue the marriage, recognizing the potential benefits of securing a wealthy and influential husband for her friend. Charles, now more confident and assertive after his time in the Indies, felt ready to embrace this new life.

Des Grassins, aware of Charles's return and impending marriage, approached him about settling his father's debts. Charles, now a man of means, dismissed Des Grassins's concerns, asserting that his father's financial troubles were no longer his responsibility. He was focused on his new identity as the Comte d’Aubrion, believing that wealth would shield him from his father's failures.

In August, Eugenie sat on the bench where she and Charles had once pledged their love, reflecting on their past. She received a letter from him, which she struggled to open, filled with anticipation and dread. The letter revealed Charles's success and his new life, but it also conveyed a painful message: he was engaged to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, a marriage of convenience that would secure his social ambitions. He expressed that love in marriage was a delusion and that he could not pursue their youthful dreams due to the differences in their lives.

Eugenie was devastated by the letter, feeling abandoned and heartbroken. She realized that her love for Charles was profound and enduring, contrasting sharply with his pragmatic approach to marriage. As she processed the news, she recalled her mother's prophetic words about suffering and enduring pain. In her sorrow, she resolved to live a life of prayer and acceptance, recognizing that her dreams had been shattered.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Eugenie returned slowly from the garden, avoiding the corridor where memories of her cousin lingered. This day was significant, marked by the visit of the parish priest, who was related to the Cruchots and aligned with Monsieur de Bonfons. The priest had been urged to discuss the importance of marriage with Eugenie, who was preoccupied with her own unhappiness. She expressed her desire to retreat from the world and live solely for God, but the priest cautioned her against such a drastic decision, emphasizing her responsibilities to society and the poor.

Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by a mix of vengeance and despair. She revealed that Charles Grandet had returned from the Indies and was engaged to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Eugenie, taken aback, maintained her composure despite the emotional turmoil. Madame des Grassins presented a letter from her husband detailing Charles's situation, including his father's bankruptcy and the implications for his marriage prospects. Eugenie, feeling a mix of contempt and sorrow, returned the letter without finishing it.

Eugenie then asked the priest if it would be a sin to remain a virgin after marriage, indicating her conflicted feelings. After the priest left, she isolated herself in her father's secret room, avoiding dinner and the company of guests. When she finally joined them, the atmosphere was charged with curiosity about her situation. As the evening progressed, Eugenie made a surprising declaration to Monsieur de Bonfons, asking him to stay, which was interpreted as a promise of marriage.

In a private moment, Eugenie made it clear to the president that she could only offer him friendship and requested that he help her settle her uncle's debts in exchange for her hand. The president, eager to please, agreed to her terms. After he left, Eugenie broke down in tears, realizing the gravity of her situation.

The president quickly traveled to Paris, where he met with the creditors and arranged for the payment of all debts, including interest. He then delivered a letter from Eugenie to Charles, explaining her decision and the conditions of their relationship. Charles, initially excited, was shocked to learn of Eugenie's wealth and the implications of her actions.

Meanwhile, Madame d’Aubrion, unaware of the president's dealings, expressed her confidence in the marriage. The president returned to Saumur, announcing his marriage to Eugenie, who remained devoted to charitable works despite her wealth. After their marriage, he pursued a political career, but his ambitions were cut short when he died shortly after being elected as a deputy.

Eugenie, now a widow, lived a life of piety and charity, dedicating her wealth to the community while maintaining a simple lifestyle. Despite her fortune, she remained detached from worldly desires, focusing on her spiritual life and the well-being of others. Rumors of a potential new marriage circulated, particularly regarding the Marquis de Froidfond, but Eugenie remained resolute in her independence, supported by her loyal servants, Nanon and Cornoiller.

Eugenie's story reflects the tension between wealth and personal fulfillment, illustrating her struggle to navigate societal expectations while remaining true to her values. She became a figure of reverence in her community, embodying both the burdens and blessings of her inheritance.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

PIERRETTE

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:

Dear Child,--You, the joy of the household, whose pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of Wierzschovnia, how can I dedicate to _you_ a story full of melancholy? Yet, should not sorrows be spoken of to a young girl like you, since the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is difficult to find a subject worthy of your eyes, so I have no choice; perhaps you will feel fortunate when you read this story sent to you by your old friend, De Balzac.

PIERRETTE

I. THE LORRAINS

On an October morning in 1827, a sixteen-year-old boy, dressed in what modern terms call proletarian clothing, stood in a small square of Lower Provins. He observed the houses around him, noting the mills along the river already at work, their sounds echoing in the crisp air. The square, bordered by linden trees, featured simple bourgeois architecture, with few signs of commerce and only a couple of luxurious porte-cocheres, one belonging to Monsieur Martener, a physician.

The young man identified the house he sought, a newly restored white stone structure with gray blinds and yellow-painted iron balconies. The front door was reached by three stone steps, and the house's fresh appearance contrasted with the timeworn exteriors of its neighbors, reflecting the self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.

As he admired the details, a hint of sadness crossed his face. The rising sun illuminated a calico curtain at one of the garret windows, and he sang a Breton ditty, a song traditionally sung to newlyweds, evoking memories of Brittany's customs and the bittersweet nature of life.

As he sang, a girl appeared at the window, signaling him. He revealed a yellow flower, the broom, and they exchanged words. She was Pierrette, and he was Brigaut, her childhood companion. He expressed his desire to settle near her, but she urged him to flee as a woman opened a window below, revealing an old maid who had been awakened by his song.

The old maid, a grotesque figure, searched for the source of the music but saw nothing. Her appearance was unflattering, and she was known for her keen observations, which often led to grave suppositions and domestic dramas.

Pierrette, overwhelmed by Brigaut's visit, reflected on her past, recalling her childhood memories and the joy they brought her. She sat in a shabby armchair, lost in thought, her delicate appearance marred by illness. Her mother, a Demoiselle Auffray, had a complicated family history, with her father marrying twice and leaving behind a tangled inheritance.

Pierrette's mother married a Breton officer, Major Lorrain, who died in battle, leaving her with little means. The Lorrains, Pierrette's grandparents, struggled financially, and after a series of misfortunes, they were unable to care for her. Pierrette was eventually sent to live with her uncle and aunt, the Rogrons, in Provins, who became the unexpected masters of her fate.

The Rogrons, unaware of the complexities of Pierrette's past, would soon play a significant role in her life, setting the stage for the unfolding drama of her existence.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Pere Rogron, an innkeeper in Provins, was a short, fat man with a ruddy face and a penchant for indulgence. He and his equally unattractive wife were selfish and brutish, living off the modest income from their land and the sale of their inn. After selling the inn, they moved into a dilapidated house inherited from old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother. They had two unattractive children, Sylvie and Jerome-Denis, who were poorly raised and neglected, leading to their coarsened features and harsh voices. Rogron sent them to Paris to work in shops, believing in tough love and minimal support.

Sylvie became an apprentice in a shop on rue Saint-Denis, eventually earning her independence and rising to a forewoman position. Jerome followed a similar path, becoming a head-clerk. The siblings met occasionally, enjoying modest outings and pooling their savings to buy a retail shop, the “Family Sister.” Despite their success, they struggled against fierce competition and were known for their harshness and lack of empathy in business.

Sylvie, though not conventionally attractive, was shrewd and managed the business effectively, while Jerome lacked the intellect to grasp the broader aspects of their trade. Their lives revolved around their shop, and they were oblivious to the world beyond it. They were known for their stinginess and poor treatment of employees, leading to a high turnover of clerks.

Sylvie had developed a dual persona: a charming saleswoman and a sour spinster. When they received a letter from the Lorrains regarding their cousin Pierrette, they were in mourning for their father and contemplating their future. They inherited property and had significant assets, which led them to consider selling their business for a comfortable life in Provins.

However, they viewed Pierrette as a potential burden and debated the implications of taking her in. Ultimately, they decided against it, prioritizing their own interests and the prospect of keeping their wealth intact for future generations. Sylvie intended to write a refusal but got sidetracked by a business opportunity, leading to their departure for Provins four years before Brigaut’s arrival, which would significantly impact Pierrette’s life. Their actions in Provins would prove just as consequential for Pierrette as their previous life in Paris.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When a petty shopkeeper returns to his provincial town from Paris, he brings back a few ideas that quickly fade as he immerses himself in provincial life. This transition from shopkeeper to bourgeois often feels like an illness, as the vibrancy of Parisian life is replaced by the stillness of the provinces. With their newfound wealth, these individuals often indulge in long-held desires, whether through travel, political engagement, or hobbies like hunting or fishing. The Rogrons, a brother and sister duo, exemplify this as they remodel their old house into a charming new one, driven by a desire to emulate the wealthier bourgeois of Provins.

The renovations of their home reflect their aspirations, with the contractor meticulously consulting them on every detail, ensuring that their choices align with the tastes of the local elite. The Rogrons' first year back in Provins is consumed by discussions about their home and attempts to integrate into the social fabric of the town. They seek acceptance among the established families, including the Julliards, Guepins, and Guenees, who dominate the local social scene.

Madame Tiphaine, the belle of the town, is particularly ambitious for her husband, hoping to elevate him to a deputy position. She skillfully navigates social dynamics, hosting gatherings that solidify her influence. The Rogrons, however, struggle to fit in. Sylvie, the sister, attempts to learn card games to engage socially, while Rogron remains awkward and silent. Their efforts to impress the local elite are met with disdain, as their lack of refinement and understanding of social norms becomes apparent.

Despite their attempts to host lavish dinners to showcase their wealth, the Rogrons find themselves increasingly isolated. Their invitations are often declined, and they become the subject of ridicule among the townspeople. Sylvie's ambition to establish a social standing leads to frustration as they realize that wealth alone does not guarantee acceptance.

As their social aspirations crumble, Sylvie turns to frugality, dismissing their extravagant dinners and opting for a simpler lifestyle. The brother and sister find themselves in a state of ennui, reminiscing about their past business and feeling the weight of their isolation. Their attempts to engage with the community yield little satisfaction, and they become increasingly resentful of the social elite.

In a bid to regain their social standing, Sylvie decides to invite her cousin Pierrette Lorrain to live with them, hoping to use her as a means to connect with the upper class. However, their plans are thwarted when the townspeople learn of their neglect towards Pierrette, leading to further ostracism. The Rogrons' bitterness towards the local elite grows, and they begin to align themselves with the lower bourgeoisie, seeking to establish a new social order.

Colonel Gouraud and lawyer Vinet, both marginalized figures in Provins, see an opportunity in the Rogrons' discontent. They aim to leverage the Rogrons' wealth and ignorance to further their own political ambitions. As the Rogrons become more entrenched in their resentment, they unwittingly position themselves as a potential center for the liberal opposition in the town.

The arrival of Pierrette becomes a catalyst for the Rogrons' ambitions, as they hope to use her to regain favor in society. However, the dynamics of power and social standing in Provins are complex, and the Rogrons must navigate a treacherous landscape filled with rivalries and ambitions. Their journey reflects the challenges of social mobility and the often harsh realities of provincial life, where wealth does not always translate to acceptance or influence.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
After her grandparents entered a hospital, Pierrette, feeling the weight of living on charity, was relieved to learn of her wealthy relatives. Brigaut, her childhood friend and the son of her mother’s friend, offered her sixty francs for her journey to Paris, which she accepted without thanks, reflecting their deep bond. Brigaut often visited her, trying to console her during her difficult times.

In October 1824, after Pierrette turned eleven, she was entrusted to the diligence conductor for her journey to Paris. Brigaut ran after the coach, tears in his eyes, until he could no longer keep up. Upon arriving in Paris, Pierrette had no money, but the conductor paid for her hotel and arranged for her transfer to Provins, treating her like a package. Four days later, a kind conductor brought her to Mademoiselle Rogron’s home, where her cousins greeted her with astonishment.

Pierrette arrived with only a small bundle of clothes, looking charming in her Breton attire. Sylvie and Rogron, her cousins, were initially taken aback by her appearance. They asked her questions, but Pierrette was shy and overwhelmed. Sylvie and the maid, Adele, took her to her new room, which was modestly furnished but seemed luxurious to Pierrette. Sylvie expressed concern over Pierrette’s lack of clothing and decided they needed to buy her a new wardrobe.

Pierrette, a child of love, had previously known only affection and freedom in Brittany. However, her new life with the Rogrons was starkly different. The warmth she had known was replaced by the coldness of her relatives, leaving her feeling lost and confused. The next morning, she awoke early, exploring the house and garden, but was soon reprimanded by Rogron for her dirty shoes.

As Pierrette settled into her new life, she faced constant criticism from her cousins, who were more concerned about their possessions than her well-being. Sylvie and Rogron’s harshness contrasted sharply with the love she had received in Brittany. Pierrette’s innocence and sensitivity made her acutely aware of their disdain, leading her to suppress her natural joy.

The Rogrons became fixated on Pierrette’s clothing, viewing her as a burden rather than a beloved family member. Their constant fault-finding and harsh reprimands stifled her spirit. Pierrette, who had once been lively and carefree, learned to be passive in their presence, fearing their scorn. When she accidentally tore a dress while playing, she was devastated, knowing the reprimands that awaited her.

As time passed, Pierrette’s life in Provins became increasingly difficult. Sylvie’s refusal to let her visit friends marked the end of any joy she had experienced. The Rogrons’ obsession with appearances and their harsh treatment of Pierrette turned her life into a cycle of fear and repression. They saw her as a means to fill their lives with purpose, but their love was conditional and rooted in self-interest.

The arrival of Vinet and the colonel introduced new dynamics. They encouraged Sylvie to form an opposition to the local social elite, further isolating Pierrette. As her cousins became more consumed by their ambitions, Pierrette was increasingly confined to the house, subjected to constant scrutiny and criticism. The once vibrant child was transformed into a shadow of her former self, struggling to navigate the harsh realities of her new life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One evening, marking a new phase in Pierrette’s life with her wealthy cousins, she entered the room to bid goodnight. Sylvie coldly turned her cheek away, causing Pierrette to tear up. When questioned, she expressed that her rich cousin was not as kind as her poor grandmother had been. Sylvie dismissed her feelings, stating that her grandmother had taken her money, while her cousin would leave her hers. Pierrette, feeling unloved, wished for affection over wealth.

Sylvie accused Pierrette of mischief, claiming she broke her watch and ignored instructions. Pierrette, ashamed, left the room. Rogron, her cousin, expressed a desire to discipline her, while Madame Vinet suggested sending her to school. The colonel chimed in, suggesting that teaching might keep Pierrette quiet. Vinet proposed hiring a schoolmaster, emphasizing that Pierrette was a rough diamond needing polishing.

As discussions continued, Pierrette was given a writing master, leading to chaos in the Rogron household. Ink stains and scattered materials were blamed on her, and she was harshly told she must earn her keep. Pierrette suffered under Rogron’s strictness and Sylvie’s indifference, leading to her feeling increasingly isolated and misunderstood. Her natural curiosity and spirit were stifled, and she began to feel ill, often complaining of pains that were dismissed by her cousins.

The only kindness came from Adele, the servant, who secretly warmed Pierrette’s bed. Sylvie believed in hardening children, while Pierrette’s innocent affection was seen as manipulation. The colonel and Vinet supported the Rogrons’ harsh treatment, attributing Pierrette’s behavior to her stubborn Breton nature. Vinet and Rogron invested in a local newspaper, which began to elevate their social standing, while Pierrette’s situation worsened.

As time passed, Pierrette contemplated escaping to her grandparents in Brittany, but two events—a death and Rogron’s appointment as her guardian—prevented her. Vinet hinted at the potential inheritance from Pierrette, leading Rogron to secure her grandmother’s promise of the eight thousand francs upon her death. Pierrette was deeply affected by these developments, especially as she prepared for her first communion, which brought significant changes in the household.

Sylvie insisted Pierrette be taught by a zealous priest, Monsieur Habert, who profoundly influenced her. Pierrette found solace in faith, abandoning thoughts of escape as she embraced the teachings of the Church. Sylvie, noticing Pierrette’s transformation, became curious about the priest’s methods, leading to a newfound piety in her as well.

Meanwhile, the priest’s sister, Mademoiselle Habert, befriended Sylvie, and the two women grew close. Mademoiselle Habert offered to take Pierrette into her school, but the Rogrons insisted on keeping her, claiming they would miss her. The colonel and Vinet, sensing a threat from the priest’s influence, worried that he might push for a convent education for Pierrette.

As political and matrimonial intrigues unfolded, Vinet sought to strengthen his position by bringing in Madame and Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf, who were impoverished but socially advantageous. Their arrival bolstered the liberal faction and alarmed the aristocrats of Provins. Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, beautiful and charming, quickly became a rival for Rogron’s affections, while Sylvie began to see Pierrette as an obstacle to her own marriage prospects.

Sylvie decided to make Pierrette a servant, believing she should learn domestic skills. Pierrette willingly accepted the role, losing her only protector, Adele. Her cousins treated her with less regard than a servant, scolding her for trivial matters. Despite her efforts to please them, she received no praise, only criticism, which deepened her sense of isolation and despair.

As the years passed, Pierrette’s health deteriorated under the relentless pressure and lack of affection from her cousins. She experienced severe internal pains, and her natural growth was stunted. The once vibrant child became a shadow of her former self, suffering in silence as her cousins remained oblivious to her plight.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Before recounting the domestic drama that Jacques Brigaut's arrival would incite in the Rogron family, it is essential to explain how he came to be in Provins, as he plays a significant yet passive role in the unfolding events.

Brigaut fled from the Rogron house, shaken by Pierrette’s distressing transformation. He barely recognized her; her once vibrant spirit was now replaced by illness and sorrow. As he climbed to a vantage point in the Upper town, he gazed mournfully at Pierrette’s home, consumed by questions about her well-being and the depth of his feelings for her. Their bond, though rare for children of opposite sexes, was profound. Brigaut had dedicated himself to improving his life for her sake, learning his trade and moving to Paris with dreams of success to support her. However, after a fortnight in the city, he felt compelled to return to Provins, where he quickly secured a position as a journeyman cabinetmaker, allowing him to stay close to Pierrette.

Brigaut buried his feelings, seeking information about the Rogrons from Madame Frappier, who revealed their dubious acquisition of Pierrette’s grandfather’s property. He often saw Pierrette in the market, where her frail state troubled him. Sundays at church brought him closer to her, but he sensed her need for caution, especially after she signaled him to stay hidden. He was determined to uncover the cause of her pallor and weakness, fearing for her health.

Meanwhile, Pierrette was anxious about the changes in her life since Brigaut’s arrival. She was unaware of the turmoil brewing among the Rogrons, particularly between Sylvie and Mademoiselle Habert, both of whom were vying for marriage. Sylvie, at forty-two, felt the pressure of her age and the fear of dying alone, while Celeste, ten years younger, was encouraged by her brother, a priest, to pursue marriage. The political climate shifted with the rise of the Martignac ministry, emboldening the Liberals in Provins and creating a competitive atmosphere for social standing.

Sylvie’s fears led her to consult a doctor about the risks of marriage at her age, receiving discouraging news that only heightened her anxiety. Celeste, sensing an opportunity, advised Sylvie to wait, hoping to eliminate her rival. Sylvie’s internal struggles intensified, and her jealousy flared when she noticed the colonel’s growing interest in Pierrette. Vinet, a lawyer with ambitions of marrying Bathilde, saw Sylvie’s vulnerability and plotted to use it to his advantage, aiming to drive a wedge between the colonel and Pierrette.

As tensions escalated, Vinet approached the colonel, suggesting he pursue Pierrette instead of Sylvie. He painted a picture of happiness with Pierrette, manipulating the colonel’s feelings while secretly planning to secure the Rogron fortune for Bathilde. The colonel, who had been treating Pierrette with a mix of affection and aloofness, began to seek her out more frequently, further igniting Sylvie’s jealousy.

Sylvie’s suspicions deepened when she overheard Brigaut serenading Pierrette, leading her to believe that the colonel was involved with the girl. Her jealousy clouded her judgment, and she became increasingly paranoid about losing the colonel to Pierrette. This obsession consumed her, distorting her perception of reality and driving her to confront Pierrette.

On a morning filled with tension, Sylvie accused Pierrette of having a secret admirer, mocking her innocence and demanding answers. Pierrette, confused and frightened, remained silent, which only fueled Sylvie’s suspicions. Sylvie’s jealousy morphed into a desire to control Pierrette, leading her to threaten the girl with severe consequences if she did not confess.

As Pierrette navigated the oppressive atmosphere of the Rogron household, she struggled with her health, unaware of the severity of her condition. Her innocence and naivety shielded her from the harsh realities of her situation, but the emotional turmoil and physical labor took a toll on her well-being. Despite her suffering, the presence of Brigaut brought her a flicker of hope and joy, momentarily alleviating her pain.

However, Sylvie’s relentless scrutiny and jealousy created an unbearable environment for Pierrette. The old maid’s bitterness and suspicion only intensified, leading to a confrontation that left Pierrette feeling isolated and desperate. As the tension reached a breaking point, Pierrette’s health deteriorated, and she found herself wishing for escape from her torment.

In the midst of this turmoil, the dynamics of love, jealousy, and ambition intertwined, setting the stage for a tragic unfolding of events that would ultimately impact all involved. The struggle for power, affection, and security within the Rogron household would lead to devastating consequences for Pierrette, whose innocence and vulnerability made her a target in a world rife with envy and desire.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For a month, Rogron had stopped delivering the “Constitutionnel” to Gouraud, who now came to fetch it himself, often engaging in idle gossip and walks with Sylvie. Sylvie, eager to see the colonel, dressed in her most eye-catching attire. When Gouraud arrived, Rogron excused himself, leaving Sylvie alone with the colonel. 

Gouraud, noticing Sylvie's solemn demeanor, inquired about her cousin's health, to which Sylvie replied she was unwell. The colonel, wary of Vinet's influence over the Rogrons, felt uneasy about his position. Vinet seemed to dominate their political endeavors, while Gouraud, a half-pay colonel, felt sidelined. He was also aware of Rogron's growing infatuation with Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, which made him anxious about his own prospects.

During their conversation, Gouraud mentioned his interest in Bathilde, causing Rogron to pale at the thought of competition. The colonel realized that Vinet might be trying to manipulate him into abandoning Sylvie for Pierrette, which he suspected was a ploy to benefit Vinet and Bathilde. 

When left alone with Sylvie, Gouraud noticed her unease and suspected Vinet's involvement. Sylvie, trying to gauge his feelings, brought up Pierrette, and the colonel dismissed any affection for her, claiming he preferred a more mature partner. Sylvie, relieved, decided to keep her intentions hidden and suggested that her brother had considered a match with Pierrette.

Gouraud, sensing Sylvie's interest, professed his desire to marry her, which delighted Rogron. Sylvie began planning their future home but remained suspicious of Pierrette's interactions with the colonel. 

As dinner approached, Sylvie berated Pierrette for a stain on her dress, blaming her for the mishap. Despite her harshness, Sylvie ordered Pierrette to dress nicely for the evening, suspecting her of plotting with Gouraud. 

The Chargeboeuf ladies arrived, with Bathilde dressed to impress. Bathilde, under Vinet's guidance, aimed to use her marriage to Rogron as a stepping stone to power, viewing him as a means to an end rather than a partner. Vinet had instilled in her a desire for independence and influence, encouraging her to leverage her beauty and connections.

As the evening progressed, the atmosphere became charged with tension. Bathilde's condescension towards Pierrette was palpable, and Vinet's remarks only heightened the sense of rivalry. The group played cards, with Sylvie attempting to manipulate the game to her advantage, but Pierrette's innocent comments drew Sylvie's ire.

When Pierrette left the room in distress, Sylvie's anger boiled over, and she resolved to make her pay for the perceived slight. The evening ended with Sylvie feeling frustrated and betrayed, reflecting on the duplicity of men and her own precarious position.

The next day, Pierrette suffered a painful injury from a fall, which Sylvie attributed to her disobedience, showing no sympathy. Rogron suggested care for Pierrette, but Sylvie dismissed it, revealing her coldness and the growing rift between her and her cousin.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The week concluded in relentless torment for Pierrette, who suffered under Sylvie's increasingly cruel tyranny. Pierrette refrained from voicing her pain, both physical and emotional, stemming from her cousin's anger over her silence regarding Brigaut's arrival. Despite her suffering, thoughts of Brigaut brought her solace; she longed for his presence while fearing for his safety. Their connection was palpable, and a single glimpse of him felt like a lifeline.

Brigaut, determined to communicate, devised a plan to exchange letters with Pierrette. One morning, he managed to slip a note to her while pretending to buy fruit. The exchange ignited a deep emotional response in both, leaving them with a wellspring of feelings that transcended ordinary affection. Sylvie remained oblivious to their connection, allowing Pierrette to return home with the note.

That night, Pierrette read Brigaut's heartfelt letter, which expressed his longing for her and his desire to escape their oppressive circumstances. He urged her to let down a string from her window so they could communicate nightly. Pierrette, moved by his words, resolved to respond, despite the difficulty of obtaining writing materials without waking Sylvie. She poured her heart into her letter, revealing her love for Brigaut and her suffering at the hands of her relatives.

At midnight, she successfully sent her letter down to Brigaut, who eagerly awaited her response. Their correspondence continued, filled with hope and plans for a future together, despite the looming threat of Sylvie's jealousy and suspicion. Sylvie, noticing Pierrette's newfound joy, became increasingly watchful, suspecting that Pierrette had seen someone.

As Pierrette's health deteriorated under Sylvie's oppressive scrutiny, she fainted one evening, prompting concern from the colonel and others. Sylvie dismissed her illness as a ruse, but Pierrette's condition worsened. In a moment of desperation, Pierrette cried for help, leading to a violent confrontation with Sylvie, who sought to seize Pierrette's letters. Just as Sylvie's fury reached its peak, Pierrette's grandmother arrived, witnessing the struggle and rushing to her granddaughter's aid.

The grandmother's presence instilled a sense of hope in Pierrette, who had been on the brink of despair. Sylvie, realizing the gravity of her actions, was left in a state of confusion and fear. The grandmother, determined to protect Pierrette, sought medical help, fearing for her granddaughter's life.

Brigaut, meanwhile, wrote to Madame Lorrain, urging her to come and rescue Pierrette from her dire situation. His letters conveyed the urgency of the matter, as he feared for Pierrette's health and well-being. The grandmother, upon receiving Brigaut's letters, was filled with a mix of joy and dread, prompting her to travel to Provins immediately.

Upon her arrival, the grandmother found Pierrette in a state of distress, having endured both physical and emotional trauma. The sight of her injured hand, a result of Sylvie's violent attempts to control her, filled the grandmother with anguish. She vowed to seek justice for Pierrette and ensure her safety.

As Pierrette recovered, the bond between her and her grandmother deepened, providing her with the strength to confront the challenges ahead. The arrival of medical assistance and the promise of a better life with her grandmother and Brigaut offered a glimmer of hope amidst the darkness of her recent experiences. The struggle for freedom and love continued, with Pierrette determined to reclaim her life from the clutches of her oppressive relatives.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At nine o’clock that morning, Monsieur Martener visited Monsieur Tiphaine to discuss the distressing situation involving Pierrette and the Rogrons, who had subjected her to severe mistreatment. This prompted Tiphaine to summon Auffray, a notary and Pierrette’s relative. At that time, tensions between the Vinet and Tiphaine factions were escalating, fueled by damaging rumors about Madame Tiphaine’s family. These truths, though painful, intensified the animosity between the two parties, intertwining personal grievances with political strife.

Tiphaine recognized the opportunity to undermine the Rogrons, who were influential in local opposition to the monarchy. He consulted with the public prosecutor and, along with Auffray and Martener, devised a plan to petition the courts for Auffray to become Pierrette’s guardian. This would allow for a Family Council to be convened, supported by medical testimony, to free Pierrette from the Rogrons’ control.

By midday, the town buzzed with news of Pierrette’s cries heard the previous night, leading to speculation about her condition. Doctor Bianchon and Brigaut arrived to assess her injuries, confirming the Rogrons’ abuse. Vinet, the lawyer, rushed to the Rogrons to inform them of the potential criminal charges they faced. Sylvie, initially evasive, eventually admitted to her jealousy and the truth of the incident, realizing the gravity of their situation.

Vinet exploited their fear, suggesting that a marriage announcement could mitigate the scandal. After much manipulation, the Rogrons agreed to a marriage contract that would benefit them politically. Vinet then filed a petition against Brigaut and the widow Lorrain for abducting Pierrette, positioning the Rogrons as victims.

The court hearing was postponed, heightening the town’s excitement. The Tiphaines, meanwhile, rallied support for Pierrette, who remained unaware of the turmoil surrounding her. She found solace in the care of her grandmother and Brigaut, who were devoted to her recovery. The doctors, horrified by her condition, unanimously condemned the Rogrons’ treatment of Pierrette.

When the court reconvened, Auffray presented the physicians’ report, arguing for Pierrette’s protection. Vinet countered, but the judge ruled in favor of keeping Pierrette away from the Rogrons until a Family Council could be held. This decision was a significant blow to the Rogrons, who were now facing public scrutiny.

The Family Council, composed of Pierrette’s relatives and allies, ultimately decided to remove Rogron as guardian, appointing Auffray instead. Despite Vinet’s lengthy defense of the Rogrons, the evidence against them was overwhelming, leading to a civil suit against them for their neglect and abuse.

As the legal battle unfolded, Pierrette’s health deteriorated. Despite the doctors’ efforts, she faced a dire prognosis. Martener and the Auffray family grew fond of Pierrette, who remained a source of inspiration despite her suffering. Her grandmother’s unwavering support and Brigaut’s visits provided her with comfort.

In March 1828, a critical operation was performed on Pierrette, but the prognosis remained grim. As her condition worsened, she expressed her desire to forgive her cousins, demonstrating her grace even in suffering. Tragically, Pierrette passed away, prompting a funeral attended by the entire town, while the Rogrons, fearing public outrage, stayed away.

Brigaut, devastated by her death, crafted her coffin, pouring his grief into the task. The Rogrons attempted to exploit the situation legally, but their efforts were thwarted by the community’s support for Pierrette. Vinet, seeking to protect his clients, faced backlash as the truth of the Rogrons’ actions became widely known.

In the aftermath, Brigaut left Provins, seeking a new life in the military, while Vinet and the Rogrons continued to thrive politically, their reputations largely intact despite the scandal. The narrative of Pierrette’s tragic fate became distorted, with many in the town believing the Rogrons had acted nobly. Only a few, like Martener and Brigaut, remembered the truth of her suffering and the injustices she faced.

The story concludes with a reflection on the nature of justice and the societal dynamics that allowed such tragedies to occur, emphasizing the need for accountability and the enduring impact of Pierrette’s life and death on those who truly understood her plight.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

**DEDICATION**

To David, Sculptor:

The permanence of the work on which I inscribe your name—twice made illustrious in this century—is very problematical; whereas you have graven mine in bronze which survives nations—if only in their coins. The day may come when numismatists, discovering amid the ashes of Paris existences perpetuated by you, will wonder at the number of heads crowned in your atelier and endeavor to find in them new dynasties.

To you, this divine privilege; to me, gratitude.

De Balzac.

---

**THE VICAR OF TOURS**

I

In the autumn of 1826, Abbe Birotteau, the main character of this story, was caught in a rainstorm while returning home from a friend's house. He hurried across the deserted square known as “The Cloister,” located behind the cathedral of Saint-Gatien in Tours.

The Abbe Birotteau, a short, corpulent man of about sixty, had suffered from gout and was particularly averse to wet shoes. Despite this, he was in good spirits, having won money at cards with Madame de Listomere and feeling optimistic about his long-held ambition to be appointed canon of the cathedral. He was wrapped in thoughts of this promotion, which made him oblivious to the rain and his discomfort.

Once, the Cloister had housed several dignitaries of the Chapter, but after the confiscation of ecclesiastical property, it was transformed into a narrow street, the Rue de la Psalette. The left side of this street featured a single house overshadowed by the cathedral, which had always been occupied by priests and was owned by an old maid named Mademoiselle Gamard. She had taken in priests as boarders, and her devout nature allowed her to retain the property without objection during the Restoration.

Birotteau was heading to this house, where he had lived for two years. His apartment had been a source of envy and a long-held desire. To be Mademoiselle Gamard’s boarder and to become a canon were his two great ambitions, representing the aspirations of a priest who sought comfort and status in life. His desire for the apartment had become a passion, filled with obstacles and hopes.

Mademoiselle Gamard could only accommodate two lodgers, and for twelve years, she had housed the Abbe Troubert and the late Abbe Chapeloud. When Chapeloud died, Birotteau took his place, having admired the apartment and its furnishings during his visits. The late canon had been a close friend, and Birotteau’s desire for the apartment grew alongside his affection for Chapeloud.

Chapeloud had improved the apartment over the years, filling it with books and furniture that Birotteau envied. The vicar’s longing for the comforts of that space became an obsession, and he often found himself wishing for Chapeloud’s death to fulfill his desire. However, he never considered how to influence his friend’s will.

Chapeloud, aware of Birotteau’s desires, forgave him for his unspoken wishes. Just before his death, he told Birotteau, “This time you will certainly get the apartment. I feel it is all over with me now.” After Chapeloud’s passing, Birotteau inherited his library and furniture, which somewhat eased his grief. He mourned his friend but also felt a sense of relief at acquiring the comforts he had long desired.

Birotteau’s first days of mourning were spent organizing his new possessions, and he was content with his new life, even though the canonry he hoped for was given to another. Mademoiselle Gamard welcomed him as a boarder, and he enjoyed the comforts that had once belonged to Chapeloud. He marveled at the attentions Gamard provided, which he had often heard praised by Chapeloud.

However, Birotteau soon found himself longing for the canonry, which became an obsession. His thoughts of promotion consumed him, and he was so preoccupied that he left his umbrella behind in the rain. When he arrived home, he found the door locked and had to ring the bell multiple times before being let in. His servant, Marianne, informed him that Mademoiselle Gamard had assumed he was already home.

Birotteau felt a pang of annoyance at the situation, especially as he noticed a lack of the usual attentions he had come to expect from Gamard. He began to suspect that something was amiss, as he had not received his slippers or a fire in his room. These small neglects led him to believe that Gamard was intentionally slighting him.

As he settled into his room, he reflected on the changes in his treatment by Gamard, realizing that he had been subjected to a subtle form of persecution. He pondered the reasons behind her behavior, convinced that he must have displeased her in some way. Despite his good nature, he was oblivious to the underlying tensions in their relationship.

Birotteau’s innocence and lack of worldly experience left him vulnerable to Gamard’s manipulations. He failed to recognize the signs of her growing resentment, which had developed over the eighteen months he had lived with her. While he believed he had managed their relationship well, he was, in fact, the target of her quiet vengeance.

As he went to bed, he hoped for clarity in the morning, unaware that the reasons for Gamard’s hostility would remain hidden from him. His naivety and lack of self-awareness prevented him from understanding the dynamics at play, and he continued to suffer the consequences of his unintentional selfishness.

The contrast between Birotteau and Chapeloud became evident; the latter had navigated his relationship with Gamard with shrewdness, while Birotteau’s simplicity led him into a web of misunderstandings. Gamard’s desire for social recognition and her ambition to create a vibrant salon were thwarted by Birotteau’s indifference to her expectations.

Despite his initial happiness, Birotteau’s growing discontent with Gamard’s company led him to seek solace elsewhere, spending evenings with friends who offered the social engagement he craved. This shift further alienated Gamard, who felt her ambitions slipping away as Birotteau distanced himself from her.

As the vicar resumed his previous social habits, Gamard’s resentment deepened, and she began to plot her revenge. The neglect he experienced—such as the locked door and the absence of his slippers—was a manifestation of her growing animosity. Birotteau remained oblivious to the brewing storm, believing he had successfully navigated his domestic life.

The events that unfolded in the vicar’s life serve as a prologue to a deeper exploration of the complexities of human relationships, ambition, and the often unseen forces that shape our interactions. The narrative reveals how small grievances can escalate into significant conflicts, illustrating the intricate dance of social dynamics in a provincial setting.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next morning, Birotteau awoke preoccupied with thoughts of his potential canonry, momentarily forgetting the ominous signs he had perceived the night before. He rang for Marianne to light a fire, but when she did not appear, he was about to ring again when Abbe Troubert entered after knocking. This monthly visit was expected, and Troubert immediately noticed the absence of a fire. He called for Marianne, then inquired about Birotteau’s health and any news regarding his canonry. Birotteau, unaware of Troubert's resentment towards Madame de Listomere for not inviting him to her home, shared details of his efforts to secure the position.

The two abbes were starkly different in appearance and demeanor. Troubert was tall, thin, and serious, with a face marked by wrinkles that hinted at sarcasm and contempt, while Birotteau was plump, with a round, cheerful face. Troubert’s calm exterior masked a dullness that some attributed to his subservience to Mademoiselle Gamard, while Birotteau’s jovial nature made him well-liked. Despite their differences, both men were unlikely to rise above their current positions—Troubert due to his ambition and Birotteau due to his amiable disposition.

At fifty, Troubert had managed to dispel fears about his ambitions through a saintly life, and his declining health suggested he might soon be appointed vicar-general. In contrast, Birotteau’s robust health made him appear a strong candidate for longevity. Abbe Chapeloud, a respected figure in the diocese, had quietly opposed Troubert’s elevation, but Troubert treated him with respect, despite Chapeloud’s skepticism.

As Troubert visited Birotteau, he explained that he needed to borrow the “Register of Bishops,” which Birotteau gladly offered. They went downstairs together, each carrying a large book, only to be met with Mademoiselle Gamard’s sharp disapproval. She scolded Birotteau for cluttering her dining room, and he felt the sting of her ill temper, which contrasted sharply with his usual cheerful demeanor.

During breakfast, the atmosphere was tense. Mademoiselle Gamard complained about being disturbed the previous night, and Birotteau, feeling the weight of her displeasure, struggled to engage in conversation. He attempted to break the silence with comments about the coffee and the weather, but his efforts were met with disdain. Mademoiselle Gamard’s demeanor reflected the bitterness often found in old maids, who, feeling unfulfilled and rejected, often harbor resentment towards others.

Old maids like Mademoiselle Gamard often embody a complex mix of societal rejection and self-loathing. Their lives, devoid of romantic fulfillment, lead to bitterness and a lack of charm. Mademoiselle Gamard’s physical appearance mirrored her inner turmoil—her features were sharp, her movements abrupt, and her demeanor ungracious. She had never married, which society often viewed as a failure, and this perception weighed heavily on her.

Birotteau, feeling the oppressive atmosphere, left the dining room, his heart heavy. He wandered the garden, only to find Mademoiselle Gamard and Troubert watching him, which intensified his discomfort. Distracted by their scrutiny, he sought refuge in church activities, momentarily forgetting his troubles. However, when he returned home, he discovered that he had missed dinner, as Mademoiselle Gamard had set the clock ahead to avoid waiting for him. This manipulation exemplified her malicious nature, as she took pleasure in undermining Birotteau’s comfort while maintaining an appearance of propriety.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Eight days after the events began, the dynamics between Abbe Birotteau and Mademoiselle Gamard revealed a plot that had been brewing for six months. Initially, Birotteau was oblivious to Gamard's malicious intentions, but after the incident with the candlestick and the clock, he recognized her animosity. This realization plunged him into despair, as he felt her presence suffocating him. Gamard, reveling in her vengeful emotions, meticulously executed her plan against the unsuspecting priest, who was too preoccupied with his trivial troubles to confide in friends.

Birotteau's life, once filled with mundane activities, turned into a living hell. His anxiety about confronting Gamard worsened, affecting his health. Noticing a physical decline, he decided to seek help from Abbe Troubert, hoping for intervention. When he met Troubert, he felt embarrassed discussing Gamard's provocations with someone so serious. Troubert listened coldly, suppressing a smile at Birotteau's plight, and suggested that the vicar should learn to understand Gamard better, attributing her behavior to her health issues.

Confused and desperate, Birotteau thought that leaving Gamard's house temporarily might ease her hostility. He planned to visit a country house owned by Madame de Listomere, unaware that this would only serve Gamard's interests. After ten days at the Alouette, Birotteau was approached by Monsieur Caron, Gamard's lawyer, who informed him that she was concerned about his extended absence. Birotteau, startled, insisted he had no intention of leaving, but Caron revealed that Gamard wanted clarity on his plans to avoid disputes.

Returning to the dining room, Birotteau shared his troubles with friends, who were intrigued by the drama. Madame de Listomere suggested that Troubert was after Birotteau's apartment. The group analyzed Gamard's motives, with an old landowner warning that Birotteau was only at the beginning of his troubles. Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix arrived with news that the vicar-general was ill and that Troubert was likely to be appointed in his place, which would give him significant power over appointments.

Madame de Listomere urged Birotteau to consider the canonry over his living situation with Gamard. The group rallied around him, suggesting he let Gamard and Troubert have their way, as it might lead to his advancement. However, the struggle soon escalated, and Birotteau's friends sent for Caron again to clarify the situation. Caron returned, asking Birotteau to sign a document relinquishing his residence, which he did under pressure from his friends.

Birotteau's despair deepened as he realized he had signed away his belongings, which Gamard claimed as compensation for the difference in board prices. He confronted Gamard, who coldly reminded him of the agreement. Devastated, Birotteau fled to Madame de Listomere's, where he found his belongings packed. Mademoiselle Salomon, concerned for his mental state, took him back to the Alouette, where he confided in friends about his misfortunes.

Monsieur de Bourbonne examined the deed Birotteau had signed and discovered its exploitative nature. He expressed outrage at Gamard's actions and suggested that Birotteau could pursue legal action. The group debated the merits of a lawsuit, with some advocating for a compromise to avoid scandal. They resolved to support Birotteau in his struggle against Gamard and Troubert, though Bourbonne cautioned that many would abandon him when the situation became dire.

Madame de Listomere's invitation to Birotteau to stay at her house sparked gossip and divided the town. Gamard, feeling slighted, sought to undermine Birotteau's reputation. Meanwhile, Birotteau's friends began to gather evidence for a lawsuit, which was complicated by the political climate and public sentiment against the clergy. The expert appraisal of Birotteau's belongings revealed their true value, suggesting that Gamard's claims were fraudulent.

As the legal battle unfolded, Birotteau's situation became increasingly precarious, with the town's gossip fueling the conflict. The stakes were high, and the outcome uncertain, as Birotteau faced the formidable alliance of Gamard and Troubert, who were determined to maintain their power and influence.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A few days after hostilities began between Birotteau and the old maid, Baron de Listomere, expecting a promotion to captain of a corvette, received a letter warning him of a possible retirement. Shocked, he rushed to Paris to speak with the minister of the Navy, who dismissed his concerns with laughter. However, further inquiries revealed a document confirming his impending retirement, pending the director's signature. 

Desperate, the baron sought help from his uncle, a deputy, who learned that the minister believed the baron had aligned himself with the Radicals in Tours and had offended the influential Abbe Troubert, the vicar-general. His uncle advised him to reconcile with Troubert, emphasizing the importance of maintaining good relations with the clergy for his career. The baron realized the gravity of the situation and resolved to avoid further conflict.

Three days later, the baron returned home and informed his aunt of the family's precarious position due to their support of Birotteau. He sought the counsel of Monsieur de Bourbonne, who advised them to retreat gracefully from their conflict with Troubert. The plan involved appeasing Troubert while distancing themselves from Birotteau, who was seen as a liability.

The baroness approached Birotteau, asking him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and leave her house. Birotteau, shocked and distressed, agreed, believing he was a burden. He requested the return of a portrait of Chapeloud, which belonged to him, and the baroness promised to negotiate with Gamard.

However, Mademoiselle Gamard soon fell ill, and the town buzzed with rumors that Birotteau's lawsuit had caused her distress. The baroness visited Gamard but was unable to see her. She then sought an audience with Troubert, where both parties engaged in a subtle battle of wits, each trying to outmaneuver the other while maintaining a facade of civility.

The baroness presented Birotteau's relinquishment document to Troubert, who feigned indifference but was secretly pleased. He later returned with Gamard's refusal to part with Chapeloud's portrait, which the baroness accepted, hoping to maintain peace. However, Gamard died shortly after, leaving her fortune to Troubert, which further complicated matters for the Listomeres.

At Gamard's funeral, Troubert delivered a grand eulogy, masking his true feelings about her. Birotteau, feeling guilty for her death, mourned in solitude. Meanwhile, the Listomeres navigated the fallout from Gamard's death and Troubert's growing power.

Five months later, Troubert was appointed Bishop of Troyes, while Madame de Listomere passed away, leaving an annuity to Birotteau. Troubert's influence continued to threaten the baron's career, and he manipulated the situation to his advantage, ensuring that Birotteau's legacy was contested.

In the end, Birotteau was exiled to a distant parish, suffering from illness and grief. Troubert's rise to power was marked by cunning and manipulation, reflecting the broader struggles within society and the Church. The narrative concludes with a reflection on the nature of power, individualism, and the moral complexities of human relationships, suggesting that the pursuit of personal ambition often comes at a significant cost to others.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

DEDICATION

To Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy, etc.

Dear Nodier, this book reveals actions hidden behind the closed doors of domestic life, where divine intervention often replaces human justice. The moral lessons within are significant for families and motherhood. We may one day realize the consequences of diminishing paternal authority, which historically served as the only tribunal for domestic crimes. A mother, no matter how loving, cannot replace the patriarchal role. This narrative illustrates the importance of the indissoluble marriage bond and the dangers of unchecked selfishness. May society, built on wealth, recognize the law's impotence in a system that glorifies success at any cost. I hope it returns to Catholicism for the moral uplift of its people through a different kind of education.

In the “Scenes from Military Life,” I will depict noble self-sacrifice while also highlighting the corrupting influence of war on those who act in domestic life as if on a battlefield.

You have keenly observed our times, and your reflections on the political chaos have been insightful. Thus, I entrust this story to your esteemed name, which may shield it from inevitable criticism.

Your sincere admirer,

De Balzac  
Paris, November 1842.

THE TWO BROTHERS

CHAPTER I
