Indeed, is not this proved by the reports in the _Gazette des Tribunaux_—the Police news—in my opinion, one of the worst abuses of the Press? This newspaper, which was started only in 1826 or ‘27, was not in existence when I began my professional career, and the facts of the crime I am about to speak of were not known beyond the limits of the department where it was committed.

“In the quarter of Saint-Pierre-des-Corps at Tours, a woman whose husband had disappeared at the time when the army of the Loire was disbanded, and who had mourned him deeply, was conspicuous for her excess of devotion. When the mission priests went through all the provinces to restore the crosses that had been destroyed and to efface the traces of revolutionary impiety, this widow was one of their most zealous proselytes; she carried a cross and nailed to it a silver heart pierced by an arrow; and, for a long time after, she went every evening to pray at the foot of the cross which was erected behind the Cathedral apse.

“At last, overwhelmed by remorse, she confessed to a horrible crime. She had killed her husband, as Fualdes was murdered, by bleeding him; she had salted the body and packed it in pieces into old casks, exactly as if it had been pork; and for a long time she had taken a piece every morning and thrown it into the Loire. Her confessor consulted his superiors and told her that it would be his duty to inform the public prosecutor. The woman awaited the action of the Law. The public prosecutor and the examining judge, on examining the cellar, found the husband’s head still in pickle in one of the casks.—‘Wretched woman,’ said the judge to the accused, ‘since you were so barbarous as to throw your husband’s body into the river, why did you not get rid of the head? Then there would have been no proof.’

“‘I often tried, monsieur,’ said she, ‘but it was too heavy.’”

“Well, and what became of the woman?” asked the two Parisians.

“She was sentenced and executed at Tours,” replied the lawyer; “but her repentance and piety had attracted interest in spite of her monstrous crime.”

“And do you suppose,” said Bianchon, “that we know all the tragedies that are played out behind the curtain of private life that the public never lifts?—It seems to me that human justice is ill adapted to judge of crimes as between husband and wife. It has every right to intervene as the police; but in equity it knows nothing of the heart of the matter.”

“The victim has in many cases been for so long the tormentor,” said Madame de la Baudraye guilelessly, “that the crime would sometimes seem almost excusable if the accused could tell all.”

This reply, led up to by Bianchon and by the story which Clagny had told, left the two Parisians excessively puzzled as to Dinah’s position.

At bedtime, a council was held, one of those discussions that take place in the passages of old country houses where the bachelors linger, candle in hand, for mysterious conversations.

Monsieur Gravier was now informed of the object in view during this entertaining evening which had brought Madame de la Baudraye’s innocence to light.

“But, after all,” said Lousteau, “our hostess’ serenity may indicate deep depravity instead of the most child-like innocence. The Public Prosecutor looks to me quite capable of suggesting that little La Baudraye should be put in pickle—”

“He is not to return till tomorrow; who knows what may happen in the course of the night?” said Gatien.

“We will know!” cried Monsieur Gravier.

In the life of a country house, a number of practical jokes are considered admissible, some of them odiously treacherous. Monsieur Gravier, who had seen so much of the world, proposed setting seals on the door of Madame de la Baudraye and of the Public Prosecutor. The ducks that denounced the poet Ibycus are as nothing in comparison with the single hair that these country spies fasten across the opening of a door by means of two little flattened pills of wax, fixed so high up, or so low down, that the trick is never suspected. If the gallant comes out of his own door and opens the other, the broken hair tells the tale.

When everybody was supposed to be asleep, the doctor, the journalist, the receiver of taxes, and Gatien came barefoot, like robbers, and silently fastened up the two doors, agreeing to come again at five in the morning to examine the state of the fastenings. Imagine their astonishment and Gatien’s delight when all four, candle in hand, and with hardly any clothes on, came to look at the hairs, and found them in perfect preservation on both doors.

“Is it the same wax?” asked Monsieur Gravier.

“Are they the same hairs?” asked Lousteau.

“Yes,” replied Gatien.

“This quite alters the matter!” cried Lousteau. “You have been beating the bush for a will-o’-the-wisp.”

Monsieur Gravier and Gatien exchanged questioning glances which were meant to convey, “Is there not something offensive to us in that speech? Ought we to laugh or to be angry?”

“If Dinah is virtuous,” said the journalist in a whisper to Bianchon, “she is worth an effort on my part to pluck the fruit of her first love.”

The idea of carrying by storm a fortress that had for nine years stood out against the besiegers of Sancerre smiled on Lousteau.

With this notion in his head, he was the first to go down and into the garden, hoping to meet his hostess. And this chance fell out all the more easily because Madame de la Baudraye on her part wished to converse with her critic. Half such chances are planned.

“You were out shooting yesterday, monsieur,” said Madame de la Baudraye. “This morning I am rather puzzled as to how to find you any new amusement; unless you would like to come to La Baudraye, where you may study more of our provincial life than you can see here, for you have made but one mouthful of my absurdities. However, the saying about the handsomest girl in the world is not less true of the poor provincial woman!”

“That little simpleton Gatien has, I suppose, related to you a speech I made simply to make him confess that he adored you,” said Etienne. “Your silence, during dinner the day before yesterday and throughout the evening, was enough to betray one of those indiscretions which we never commit in Paris.—What can I say? I do not flatter myself that you will understand me. In fact, I laid a plot for the telling of all those stories yesterday solely to see whether I could rouse you and Monsieur de Clagny to a pang of remorse.—Oh! be quite easy; your innocence is fully proved.

“If you had the slightest fancy for that estimable magistrate, you would have lost all your value in my eyes.—I love perfection.

“You do not, you cannot love that cold, dried-up, taciturn little usurer on wine casks and land, who would leave any man in the lurch for twenty-five centimes on a renewal. Oh, I have fully recognized Monsieur de la Baudraye’s similarity to a Parisian bill-discounter; their nature is identical.—At eight-and-twenty, handsome, well conducted, and childless—I assure you, madame, I never saw the problem of virtue more admirably expressed.—The author of _Paquita la Sevillane_ must have dreamed many dreams!

“I can speak of such things without the hypocritical gloss lent them by young men, for I am old before my time. I have no illusions left. Can a man have any illusions in the trade I follow?”

By opening the game in this tone, Lousteau cut out all excursions in the _Pays de Tendre_, where genuine passion beats the bush so long; he went straight to the point and placed himself in a position to force the offer of what women often make a man pray for, for years; witness the hapless Public Prosecutor, to whom the greatest favor had consisted in clasping Dinah’s hand to his heart more tenderly than usual as they walked, happy man!

And Madame de la Baudraye, to be true to her reputation as a Superior Woman, tried to console the Manfred of the Press by prophesying such a future of love as he had not had in his mind.

“You have sought pleasure,” said she, “but you have never loved. Believe me, true love often comes late in life. Remember Monsieur de Gentz, who fell in love in his old age with Fanny Ellsler, and left the Revolution of July to take its course while he attended the dancer’s rehearsals.”

“It seems to me unlikely,” replied Lousteau. “I can still believe in love, but I have ceased to believe in woman. There are in me, I suppose, certain defects which hinder me from being loved, for I have often been thrown over. Perhaps I have too strong a feeling for the ideal—like all men who have looked too closely into reality—”

Madame de la Baudraye at last heard the mind of a man who, flung into the wittiest Parisian circles, represented to her its most daring axioms, its almost artless depravity, its advanced convictions; who, if he were not really superior, acted superiority extremely well. Etienne, performing before Dinah, had all the success of a first night. _Paquita_ of Sancerre scented the storms, the atmosphere of Paris. She spent one of the most delightful days of her life with Lousteau and Bianchon, who told her strange tales about the great men of the day, the anecdotes which will some day form the _Ana_ of our century; sayings and doings that were the common talk of Paris, but quite new to her.

Of course, Lousteau spoke very ill of the great female celebrity of Le Berry, with the obvious intention of flattering Madame de la Baudraye and leading her into literary confidences, by suggesting that she could rival so great a writer. This praise intoxicated Madame de la Baudraye; and Monsieur de Clagny, Monsieur Gravier, and Gatien, all thought her warmer in her manner to Etienne than she had been on the previous day. Dinah’s three _attaches_ greatly regretted having all gone to Sancerre to blow the trumpet in honor of the evening at Anzy; nothing, to hear them, had ever been so brilliant. The Hours had fled on feet so light that none had marked their pace. The two Parisians they spoke of as perfect prodigies.

These exaggerated reports loudly proclaimed on the Mall brought sixteen persons to Anzy that evening, some in family coaches, some in wagonettes, and a few bachelors on hired saddle horses. By about seven o’clock, this provincial company had made a more or less graceful entry into the huge Anzy drawing-room, which Dinah, warned of the invasion, had lighted up, giving it all the luster it was capable of by taking the holland covers off the handsome furniture, for she regarded this assembly as one of her great triumphs. Lousteau, Bianchon, and Dinah exchanged meaningful looks as they studied the attitudes and listened to the speeches of these visitors, attracted by curiosity.

What invalided ribbons, what ancestral laces, what ancient flowers, more imaginative than imitative, were boldly displayed on some perennial caps! The Presidente Boirouge, Bianchon’s cousin, exchanged a few words with the doctor, from whom she extracted some “advice gratis” by expatiating on certain pains in the chest, which she declared were nervous, but which he ascribed to chronic indigestion.

“Simply drink a cup of tea every day an hour after dinner, as the English do, and you will get over it, for what you suffer from is an English malady,” Bianchon replied very gravely.

“He is certainly a great physician,” said the Presidente, coming back to Madame de Clagny, Madame Popinot-Chandier, and Madame Gorju, the Mayor’s wife.

“They say,” replied Madame de Clagny behind her fan, “that Dinah sent for him, not so much with a view to the elections as to ascertain why she has no children.”

In the first excitement of this success, Lousteau introduced the great doctor as the only possible candidate at the ensuing elections. But Bianchon, to the great satisfaction of the new Sous-prefet, remarked that it seemed to him almost impossible to give up science in favor of politics.

“Only a physician without a practice,” said he, “could care to be returned as a deputy. Nominate statesmen, thinkers, men whose knowledge is universal, and who are capable of placing themselves on the high level which a legislator should occupy. That is what is lacking in our Chambers, and what our country needs.”

Two or three young ladies, some of the younger men, and the elder women stared at Lousteau as if he were a mountebank.

“Monsieur Gatien Boirouge declares that Monsieur Lousteau makes twenty thousand francs a year by his writings,” observed the Mayor’s wife to Madame de Clagny. “Can you believe it?”

“Is it possible? Why, a Public Prosecutor gets but a thousand crowns!”

“Monsieur Gatien,” said Madame Chandier, “get Monsieur Lousteau to talk a little louder. I have not heard him yet.”

“What pretty boots he wears,” said Mademoiselle Chandier to her brother, “and how they shine!”

“Yes—patent leather.”

“Why haven’t you the same?”

Lousteau began to feel that he was too much on show and saw in the manners of the good townsfolk indications of the desires that had brought them there.

“What trick can I play them?” thought he.

At this moment, the footman, so called—a farm-servant put into livery—brought in the letters and papers, and among them a packet of proof, which the journalist left for Bianchon; for Madame de la Baudraye, on seeing the parcel, of which the form and string were obviously from the printers, exclaimed:

“What, does literature pursue you even here?”

“Not literature,” replied he, “but a review in which I am now finishing a story to come out ten days hence. I have reached the stage of ‘_To be concluded in our next_,’ so I was obliged to give my address to the printer. Oh, we eat very hard-earned bread at the hands of these speculators in black and white! I will give you a description of these editors of magazines.”

“When will the conversation begin?” Madame de Clagny asked of Dinah, as one might ask, “When do the fireworks go off?”

“I fancied we should hear some amusing stories,” said Madame Popinot to her cousin, the Presidente Boirouge.

At this moment, when the good folks of Sancerre were beginning to murmur like an impatient pit, Lousteau observed that Bianchon was lost in meditation inspired by the wrapper around the proofs.

“What is it?” asked Etienne.

“Why, here is the most fascinating romance possible on some spoiled proof used to wrap yours in. Here, read it. _Olympia, or Roman Revenge_.”

“Let us see,” said Lousteau, taking the sheet the doctor held out to him, and he read aloud as follows:

  240          OLYMPIA

  cavern. Rinaldo, indignant at his
  companions’ cowardice, for they had
  no courage but in the open field, and
  dared not venture into Rome, looked
  at them with scorn.

  “Then I go alone?” said he. He
  seemed to reflect, and then he went
  on: “You are poor wretches. I shall
  proceed alone, and have the rich
  booty to myself.—You hear me!
  Farewell.”

  “My Captain,” said Lamberti, “if
  you should be captured without
  having succeeded?”

  “God protects me!” said Rinaldo,
  pointing to the sky.

  With these words he went out,
  and on his way he met the steward

“That is the end of the page,” said Lousteau, to whom everyone had listened devoutly.

“He is reading his work to us,” said Gatien to Madame Popinot-Chandier’s son.

“From the first word, ladies,” said the journalist, jumping at an opportunity of mystifying the natives, “it is evident that the brigands are in a cave. But how careless romancers of that date were as to details which are nowadays so closely, so elaborately studied under the name of ‘local color.’ If the robbers were in a cavern, instead of pointing to the sky he ought to have pointed to the vault above him.—In spite of this inaccuracy, Rinaldo strikes me as a man of spirit, and his appeal to God is quite Italian. There must have been a touch of local color in this romance. Why, what with brigands, and a cavern, and one Lamberti who could foresee future possibilities—there is a whole melodrama in that page. Add to these elements a little intrigue, a peasant maiden with her hair dressed high, short skirts, and a hundred or so of bad couplets.—Oh! the public will crowd to see it! And then Rinaldo—how well the name suits Lafont! By giving him black whiskers, tightly-fitting trousers, a cloak, a moustache, a pistol, and a peaked hat—if the manager of the Vaudeville Theatre were but bold enough to pay for a few newspaper articles, that would secure fifty performances, and six thousand francs for the author’s rights, if only I were to cry it up in my columns.

“To proceed:—

“Devil take it! Our Rinaldo has vanished!” cried Lousteau. “But a literary man once started by this page would make rapid progress in the comprehension of the plot. The Duchesse Olympia is a lady who could intentionally forget her gloves in a deserted arbor.”

“Unless she may be classed between the oyster and head-clerk of an office, the two creatures nearest to marble in the zoological kingdom, it is impossible to discern in Olympia—” Bianchon began.

“A woman of thirty,” Madame de la Baudraye hastily interposed, fearing some all too medical term.

“Then Adolphe must be two-and-twenty,” the doctor went on, “for an Italian woman at thirty is equivalent to a Parisian of forty.”

“From these two facts, the romance may easily be reconstructed,” said Lousteau. “And this Cavaliere Paluzzi—what a man!—The style is weak in these two passages; the author was perhaps a clerk in the Excise Office, and wrote the novel to pay his tailor!”

“In his time,” said Bianchon, “the censor flourished; you must show as much indulgence to a man who underwent the ordeal by scissors in 1805 as to those who went to the scaffold in 1793.”

“Do you understand in the least?” asked Madame Gorju timidly of Madame de Clagny.

The Public Prosecutor’s wife, who, to use a phrase of Monsieur Gravier’s, might have put a Cossack to flight in 1814, straightened herself in her chair like a horseman in his stirrups, and made a face at her neighbor, conveying, “They are looking at us; we must smile as if we understood.”

“Charming!” said the Mayoress to Gatien. “Pray go on, Monsieur Lousteau.”

Lousteau looked at the two women, two Indian idols, and contrived to keep his countenance. He thought it desirable to say, “Attention!” before going on as follows:—

“For my part,” said Dinah, who had some pity on the eighteen faces gazing up at Lousteau, “I see how the story is progressing. I know it all. I am in Rome; I can see the body of a murdered husband whose wife, as bold as she is wicked, has made her bed on the crater of a volcano. Every night, at every kiss, she says to herself, ‘All will be discovered!’”

“Can you see her,” said Lousteau, “clasping Monsieur Adolphe in her arms, to her heart, throwing her whole life into a kiss?—Adolphe I see as a well-made young man, but not clever—the sort of man an Italian woman likes. Rinaldo hovers behind the scenes of a plot we do not know, but which must be as full of incident as a melodrama by Pixerecourt. Or we can imagine Rinaldo crossing the stage in the background like a figure in one of Victor Hugo’s plays.”

“He, perhaps, is the husband,” exclaimed Madame de la Baudraye.

“Do you understand anything of it all?” Madame Piedefer asked of the Presidente.

“Why, it is charming!” said Dinah to her mother.

All the good folks of Sancerre sat with eyes as large as five-franc pieces.

“Go on, I beg,” said the hostess.

Lousteau went on:—

“And then comes an exquisite galloping goat, a tail-piece drawn by Normand, and cut by Duplat.—the names are signed,” said Lousteau.

“Well, and then?” said such of the audience as understood.

“That is the end of the chapter,” said Lousteau. “The fact of this tailpiece changes my views as to the authorship. To have his book got up, under the Empire, with vignettes engraved on wood, the writer must have been a Councillor of State, or Madame Barthelemy-Hadot, or the late lamented Desforges, or Sewrin.”

“‘Adolphe was silent.’—Ah!” cried Bianchon, “the Duchess must have been under thirty.”

“If there is no more, invent a conclusion,” said Madame de la Baudraye.

“You see,” said Lousteau, “the waste sheet has been printed fair on one side only. In printer’s lingo, it is a back sheet, or, to make it clearer, the other side which would have to be printed is covered all over with pages printed one above another, all experiments in making up. It would take too long to explain to you all the complications of a making-up sheet; but you may understand that it will show no more trace of the first twelve pages that were printed on it than you would in the least remember the first stroke of the bastinado if a Pasha condemned you to have fifty on the soles of your feet.”

“I am quite bewildered,” said Madame Popinot-Chandier to Monsieur Gravier. “I am vainly trying to connect the Councillor of State, the Cardinal, the key, and the making-up—”

“You have not the key to the jest,” said Monsieur Gravier. “Well! no more have I, fair lady, if that can comfort you.”

“But here is another sheet,” said Bianchon, hunting on the table where the proofs had been laid.

“Capital!” said Lousteau, “and it is complete and uninjured. It is signed IV.; J, Second Edition. Ladies, the figure IV. means that this is part of the fourth volume. The letter J, the
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For three days, Dinah’s imagination was filled with romantic notions, influenced by the conversations of her two Parisian visitors. Lousteau observed her growing distraction, waiting for the moment when she would be completely captivated. Dinah wanted to show La Baudraye to her guests, and the farce began with Bianchon’s papers left behind in Anzy. Gatien hurried off to fetch them, while Madame Piedefer went shopping, leaving Dinah alone with Lousteau and Bianchon. Lousteau sat beside Dinah, while Bianchon rode backward, and they discussed the unfortunate circumstances surrounding her life, mocking the local men, which left Dinah feeling defensive.

“I understand your position,” Bianchon said as they crossed the Loire. “You’ve been closed off to love, but now it’s essential for you.” Dinah was taken aback. “Do you prescribe love for me?” she asked. Bianchon warned her that if she continued her current lifestyle, she would become unattractive. Lousteau, half-jokingly, defended Bianchon, claiming his advice was purely for her benefit.

Upon arriving in Cosne, a crowd gathered around the old carriage, adorned with the arms of Louis XIV. Dinah insisted they get out, and she took Bianchon’s arm, walking briskly along the Loire. Bianchon suggested that Lousteau loved her but cautioned that he was frivolous and difficult to keep. He advised Dinah to befriend Lousteau, as he could bring her beauty and happiness. Dinah felt vulnerable under Bianchon’s scrutiny and was unsure about competing with Lousteau’s past lovers.

As they walked, Dinah’s coachman announced Gatien’s arrival, and Bianchon blessed them before they entered town. Lousteau took Dinah’s hand, declaring his love, but she resisted, questioning whether she would reserve her heart for someone who saw her as a fleeting pleasure. Just then, Gatien approached, and Lousteau whispered his passionate intentions in her ear, suggesting she feign resistance to protect his honor. In a moment of reckless abandon, Lousteau crumpled Dinah’s muslin dress, leaving her feeling disheveled.

Gatien, unaware of the tension, rode up, and Lousteau attempted to divert his attention. Dinah urged Gatien to return to the inn for Bianchon’s papers, and he reluctantly complied. Lousteau then insisted they hurry back to La Baudraye, claiming Dinah was unwell. Dinah, feeling humiliated, retreated inside, overwhelmed by emotion. Lousteau followed her, pleading for her forgiveness.

Madame Piedefer entered, concerned about Dinah’s distress. Hearing Gatien’s horse approaching, Dinah fled to her room, while Lousteau confronted Gatien outside, who accused him of trickery. Lousteau dismissed Gatien’s feelings, suggesting that Dinah had played him for a laugh. Meanwhile, Dinah changed into a velvet gown, and when she rejoined the men, Gatien clumsily remarked on her attire.

Lousteau explained her change of dress as a response to the cool air, and Dinah blushed, while her mother glared at Lousteau. The conversation turned to Bianchon, whom Lousteau praised, and Dinah, feeling the weight of the situation, remained silent. Lousteau, determined to win her over, professed his desire to stay in Sancerre and be near her, claiming he had forgotten the vices of Paris.

As they returned to Anzy, Lousteau’s charm began to break through Dinah’s defenses, and she laughed, signaling a shift in her feelings. During dinner, Lousteau remained in high spirits, occasionally lost in thought, reveling in his happiness with Dinah. After coffee, the women left the men to stroll in the gardens, and Lousteau took the opportunity to express his feelings to Dinah, who was now more receptive.

The next day, Lousteau learned that he would have to return to Paris soon. He and Dinah shared a moment with the local artist restoring the chateau, discussing the coat of arms that would replace the old one. Lousteau found humor in the old man’s intentions, while Dinah requested one more day with him before his departure. They exchanged promises of love, and Dinah accompanied him to Cosne, defying societal expectations.

Upon returning to La Baudraye, Dinah spoke highly of Lousteau, claiming he had shown her what true love felt like. Her three admirers, including Monsieur de Clagny, were left to grapple with their feelings for her, while Dinah accepted Clagny as a confidant, showering him with affection as he remained devoted to her despite her newfound happiness.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In Paris once more, Lousteau quickly lost the joyful memories of his time at the Chateau d’Anzy. He lived by his pen, and in this era, especially after the rise of the bourgeoisie, writing was akin to a form of servitude. To live by the pen meant constant creation or the appearance of it, with both demanding significant effort. Lousteau contributed daily to a newspaper and worked for several literary magazines, but he lacked artistic integrity, ranking among the literary hacks of Paris. This hack-work barred him from any literary pretensions, and when he could no longer aspire to advancement, he became a journalist.

Despite this, his life was not unpleasant. He mingled with blue-stockings, aspiring actresses, publishers, and authors, enjoying a lifestyle that required little expense beyond rent. He had theatre boxes, and the sale of books he reviewed covered his minor expenses. His daily life was filled with social engagements, and his writing was the price he paid for this easy existence. Yet, after ten years of struggle, he found himself as financially constrained as when he first arrived in Paris, believing that a monthly income of five hundred francs would make him rich.

Lousteau lived in well-furnished ground-floor rooms in the Rue des Martyrs, which he rented for twelve hundred francs. His financial woes peaked during January, April, July, and October, when rent and other expenses drained his resources. He spent lavishly on cabs, meals, and gifts for his lovers, leaving him perpetually broke despite earning seven to eight hundred francs a month.

Occasionally weary of his literary life, Lousteau would reflect with friends like Nathan and Bixiou about their futures, joking about marriage and their romantic entanglements. Lousteau had a mistress, Madame Schontz, who was proud of having a writer as her lover. She lived nearby and enjoyed teasing her friends about their relationships.

These details of Lousteau’s life are crucial, as his bohemian existence would later impact Dinah’s life. He could easily laugh about his romantic entanglements, including his relationship with Dinah, whom he had almost forgotten. When Bixiou inquired about his time in Sancerre, Lousteau recounted his experiences with three provincial men who were too timid to pursue a local beauty.

Lousteau received a lengthy letter from Dinah, filled with affection, which he read and then burned, dismissing her feelings. He was not particularly concerned about Madame Schontz, who genuinely loved him, but he had also replaced a friend in the affections of a Marquise, who sometimes visited him unexpectedly.

A week later, another letter from Dinah arrived, which he again discarded unread. Madame Schontz, noticing the scented paper, opened hampers sent by Dinah, filled with game and flowers, and suggested they host a dinner. Lousteau wrote back to Dinah, crafting a clever but insincere letter, which he believed would charm her.

By December, Lousteau had stopped reading Dinah’s letters, which piled up in a drawer. Then, Madame Schontz informed him of a potential marriage opportunity. A young woman had fallen into trouble, and her father, a notary, wanted to marry her off quickly, offering a substantial dowry. The young woman’s previous lover had died, and the notary sought a respectable man to marry his daughter.

Madame Schontz encouraged Lousteau to consider the proposal, emphasizing the financial benefits and the potential for a comfortable life. She warned him about the mother-in-law, a strict woman who would scrutinize his past. Despite the challenges, she insisted that marrying the widow would leave him free and financially secure.

Lousteau, initially hesitant, agreed to meet the notary and his family. He flattered the notary, recalling his father, and presented himself as a man of ambition. The notary appreciated Lousteau’s charm and background, and soon Lousteau was introduced to the Cardot family as a prospective son-in-law.

At his first dinner with them, Lousteau found the atmosphere dull, but he noticed Felicie, the notary’s daughter, who seemed shy yet affectionate. She expressed gratitude for his willingness to marry her, which touched Lousteau. Madame Cardot viewed him as a man with a promising future, pleased with his moral standing.

Lousteau, buoyed by his new prospects, began to feel a sense of purpose. He envisioned a life of stability, a charming wife, and a respectable family connection, all while enjoying the financial security that came with the marriage. He was ready to embrace this new chapter, believing it would lead to greater ambitions and opportunities.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame Cardot wanted to see the prints for _Gil Blas_, an illustrated volume, and borrowed it to visit her future son-in-law, Lousteau, unexpectedly. She hoped to learn about his lifestyle through his bachelor rooms. Her sister-in-law, Madame Camusot, was apprehensive about the marriage, having heard unflattering things about Lousteau from her husband, a Supreme Court Councillor.

On a January day in 1837, Madame Cardot and her daughter took a cab to return the book to Lousteau, eager to see his living space. The porter was absent, but his daughter gave them the key after Madame Cardot slipped her a gold coin. Around noon, Lousteau was returning from breakfast when he spotted Dinah, his former lover, arriving in a coach. He was shocked to see her, especially when she revealed she was a mother.

Lousteau, feeling overwhelmed, tried to dismiss Dinah, but she insisted on staying. Meanwhile, Pamela, the child in the apartment, sensed the awkwardness and informed Lousteau that his mother-in-law was inside. When he entered, he found Madame Cardot and Felicie hiding in a corner. Dinah declared her love for Lousteau, but he was concerned about the complications of their situation.

Madame Cardot confronted Lousteau, expressing her disapproval of him as a potential son-in-law. She left, taking Felicie with her, while Dinah remained, believing in Lousteau's reassurances. Lousteau, realizing Dinah's high-mindedness, thought she would sacrifice for his future. He planned to manipulate the situation to his advantage, imagining how he could use her to escape his impending marriage.

As Dinah settled into Lousteau's apartment, she was charmed by its elegance, unaware of the underlying poverty. Pamela, eager to help, went to borrow dinnerware from Madame Schontz, who soon learned of Lousteau's predicament and warned him that Cardot would not pursue the marriage. Lousteau, however, was more focused on his new life with Dinah, enjoying their time together.

When Bixiou, a friend, arrived, he questioned Lousteau about his decision to abandon a wealthy marriage for Dinah. Bixiou warned him that provincial women often struggle to adapt to Parisian life and would eventually bore him. Lousteau dismissed his concerns, insisting he loved Dinah and preferred her over any fortune.

Bixiou continued to argue that Lousteau was making a mistake, emphasizing the importance of marriage for securing legitimacy and stability. Lousteau, however, was resolute in his feelings for Dinah, despite Bixiou's warnings about the potential consequences of their relationship.

As the conversation continued, Lousteau returned to Dinah, who was distressed and questioning her decision to come to Paris. He reassured her of his commitment, but the reality of their situation loomed over them. Madame Schontz later informed Lousteau that Cardot had decided to marry his daughter to another man, effectively ending Lousteau's prospects.

Despite losing his chance at a stable future, Lousteau found himself more entangled with Dinah, who believed in their love. They began to settle into a life together, with Lousteau trying to keep Dinah entertained and dependent on him. Their relationship, initially filled with passion, began to resemble the very provincial romance Lousteau had once mocked.

In the end, both Lousteau and Dinah found themselves caught in a cycle of dependency and illusion, unaware of the challenges that lay ahead in their unconventional union.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
This winter in Paris was for Madame de la Baudraye what October had been in Sancerre. Etienne, eager to introduce Dinah to Parisian life, took her to the theater, where she initially felt self-conscious and feared being recognized by acquaintances from Sancerre. However, Lousteau, who was vain and ambitious, helped her shed her provincial modesty by taking her to fashionable dressmakers and introducing her to the elite of Paris. Dinah quickly adapted to the vibrant life of the city, immersing herself in its culture and entertainment.

One morning, a newspaper article reminded her of her past in Sancerre, mentioning Monsieur le Baron de Clagny's promotion. Dinah joked with Lousteau about his continued affection for her, revealing the depth of their passion, which was still fresh and enchanting. They exchanged exaggerated declarations of love, promising to die for each other if their love faded. Lousteau, however, secretly planned to deceive Dinah when he grew tired of her, viewing her as a valuable asset.

Dinah, despite her love for Lousteau, maintained her dignity and ambition. She was more than just a charming companion; she was intelligent and well-read, capable of holding her own in conversations with the most sophisticated women. Lousteau recognized her worth, seeing in her the potential to be both a muse and a partner.

As Dinah's pregnancy became evident, she prepared to retreat from public life. Before doing so, she attended the premiere of a play by Nathan, where she felt a mix of excitement and anxiety. The evening turned sour when she found herself in a box next to Anna Grossetete, an old acquaintance, and Lousteau abandoned her to socialize with others. Dinah felt the weight of scrutiny and discomfort, unable to mask her unease.

When Clagny visited her at the theater, he expressed concern for her reputation and warned her about the consequences of her relationship with Lousteau. Dinah, however, was resolute in her happiness and the sacrifices she had made for love. Clagny's visit stirred feelings of nostalgia and regret, but she remained committed to her choices.

After the birth of her child, Dinah took on the role of a devoted mother and housekeeper, determined to support Lousteau's career. She pawned her jewelry to help with their finances, believing that her sacrifices would lead to a better life for them both. However, Lousteau's financial irresponsibility and laziness soon became apparent, and Dinah found herself bearing the burden of their household.

Despite her efforts to uplift Lousteau, he continued to struggle with his writing and debts. Dinah's love for him fueled her determination to help him succeed, but she also began to feel the strain of their situation. As she worked tirelessly to maintain their home, she noticed Lousteau's increasing indifference and reliance on her.

The arrival of a second child added to Dinah's challenges, and she found herself working harder than ever. Lousteau's debts mounted, and he became increasingly detached from their domestic life, seeking solace in the company of friends and other women. Dinah, feeling the weight of her sacrifices, began to question the sustainability of their relationship.

As tensions rose, Dinah's jealousy and insecurities surfaced. She attempted to regain control over their lives by confronting Lousteau about his behavior and the state of their finances. However, her efforts often led to conflict, and she struggled to maintain her dignity while navigating the complexities of their love.

The turning point came when Dinah's estranged husband, Monsieur de la Baudraye, re-entered her life, seeking a power of attorney to manage an inheritance. This encounter forced Dinah to confront her past and the reality of her situation. Clagny, now Attorney-General, intervened to protect her interests, negotiating an allowance from her husband in exchange for her cooperation.

As Dinah grappled with her feelings of betrayal and loss, she found herself at a crossroads. Lousteau's lack of ambition and commitment weighed heavily on her, and she began to realize that her sacrifices might not be reciprocated. The love that once felt like a refuge now seemed like a burden, and she questioned whether she could continue to support a man who was unwilling to fight for their future.

In the end, Dinah's journey through love, sacrifice, and self-discovery revealed the complexities of her relationship with Lousteau. She learned that true love requires not only passion but also mutual respect and commitment. As she faced the challenges of motherhood and the realities of her choices, Dinah emerged stronger, determined to reclaim her identity and find her own path in a world that often sought to define her.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
From that day forth, Etienne lived in luxury, and Dinah could hold her own among the best-dressed women in Paris. Lousteau, however, feigned despair among his friends, claiming he was ruined by Madame de la Baudraye. He lamented, “Oh, what would I not give to the friend who would deliver me from Dinah! But no one ever can!” He believed her love was so strong that she would harm herself if he asked.

Despite his complaints, Lousteau was unfaithful, and Monsieur de Clagny, distressed by Dinah's disgrace, warned her of betrayal. Dinah, however, simply replied, “I know it.” When Clagny asked if she still loved Lousteau, she responded passionately, “I would lose my soul for you!” This revelation left Clagny speechless, and tears filled his eyes.

Dinah eventually recognized Lousteau’s true nature. She described him to her mother as a poet, lazy and prone to pleasure, but ultimately someone she could not hate. She felt responsible for his well-being, believing she was his only support. “Oh, my Dinah!” her mother exclaimed, “what a hell you live in! What gives you the strength to persist?” Dinah replied, “I will be a mother to him!”

In her painful situation, Dinah accepted compromises with herself, only feeling the weight of her dishonor when confronted by others. She resolved to emulate Madame de Pompadour to maintain her love, indicating that her affection was becoming a burden. Lousteau’s insincerity and false affection further degraded her, leading her to believe in a revival of his love. She felt like a mere plaything, yet found a twisted joy in it.

Dinah often rehearsed the tragedy of _Le Dernier Jour d’un condamne_, contemplating their separation, but each time a word or gesture from Lousteau would pull her back into love. She even contemplated suicide while walking in her garden, yet her devotion remained unexhausted. The romance of _Adolphe_ became her guide, and she vowed not to become like Ellenore, refusing to express her pain through tears or reproaches.

Meanwhile, Monsieur de la Baudraye, seeking to reclaim his fortune, manipulated his wife’s inheritance, reducing its value while pocketing the difference. He invested in land, expecting significant returns, and considered himself free of debt to Dinah, believing he could restore her fortune later. He envisioned himself as a prominent figure in Sancerre, aspiring for a title and a noble lifestyle.

By early 1842, Dinah felt like a mere reserve for Lousteau, sacrificing herself for his comfort. She donned mourning attire, symbolizing her remorse, and her mother, concerned for her well-being, sought to intervene. The reality of their situation was far from the dramatic conclusions of literature; it was marked by fatigue and disillusionment.

Madame Piedefer, with the help of Monsieur de Clagny, encouraged Dinah to consider a life apart from Lousteau, suggesting that her husband would allow her to keep the children and live in Paris. Dinah left the negotiations to Clagny, who assured her that her husband would support her return to a respectable life.

Monsieur de la Baudraye, meanwhile, had been secretly investing in a luxurious residence in Paris, seeking to elevate his status. He aimed for a peerage and sought to present a respectable image with a well-furnished home. He wrote to Dinah, inviting her to live with him in Paris, promising financial support for their children’s education.

Dinah, now a Countess, felt the weight of her new title and the expectations that came with it. She prepared for a life of social prominence, contrasting sharply with her previous struggles. On a fateful day in May 1842, she paid off her debts and invited Lousteau to dinner, signaling a significant change in their relationship.

When Lousteau arrived, he was taken aback by Dinah’s transformation. She informed him of her new status and declared their separation, expressing her enduring affection but insisting on a calm parting. Lousteau, feeling betrayed, accused her of ruining his life, but Dinah stood firm, asserting her independence.

Their conversation turned bitter, with Lousteau lamenting his lost potential and Dinah refusing to accept blame. She declared her resolve to move on, and their final exchange was marked by a mix of sorrow and defiance. Dinah returned home, feeling both liberated and heartbroken.

In her new life, Dinah transformed her home into a model of comfort, spending more than her husband anticipated. When Monsieur de la Baudraye returned to Paris, he was pleased with her efforts, unaware of the emotional turmoil beneath her polished exterior. Dinah, while charming and fashionable, felt a void in her life, missing the passion and excitement of her past.

As she navigated her new social circle, Dinah found herself haunted by memories of Lousteau. Despite her success, she struggled to connect with other women and felt isolated in her achievements. Monsieur de Clagny, ever supportive, tried to introduce her to influential figures, but she remained unfulfilled.

One evening, after a theater performance, Dinah confided in Clagny about her lingering feelings for Lousteau. He revealed that Lousteau was living with another woman, which stirred conflicting emotions in Dinah. This revelation prompted her to immerse herself in social life, seeking success and recognition.

In March 1843, Dinah was appointed to collect donations for a charitable cause, marking a turning point in her public image. Her efforts were recognized, and she began to regain her standing in society. However, the emotional scars from her past remained, and she often reflected on her choices.

As the seasons changed, Dinah’s life continued to evolve. She returned to Sancerre for the summer, where her husband doted on their children, and she found herself caught between her past and present. The dynamics of her relationships shifted, and she faced the challenges of motherhood and societal expectations.

In June 1844, Monsieur de la Baudraye proudly introduced his children to a cousin, highlighting the family’s newfound status. Dinah, while maintaining her composure, grappled with the complexities of her life, balancing her roles as a mother, a Countess, and a woman shaped by her experiences.

The narrative concludes with a sense of unresolved tension, as Dinah navigates her dual existence, reflecting the intricate interplay of love, ambition, and societal pressures that define her journey.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
