######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

AN OLD MAID

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

To Monsieur Eugene-Auguste-Georges-Louis Midy de la Greneraye Surville, Royal Engineer of the Ponts at Chausses. As a testimony to the affection of his brother-in-law, De Balzac

AN OLD MAID

CHAPTER I. ONE OF MANY CHEVALIERS DE VALOIS

In various provinces of France, many Chevaliers de Valois exist, including one in Alencon, who is the focus of this tale. These chevaliers, while sharing a name, were largely unaware of each other and accepted the Bourbon monarchy, recognizing that Henri IV. ascended the throne due to the lack of a male heir from the Valois line. The Alencon chevalier, like others, was an old gentleman, tall and thin, who had seen better days. He had spent his youth in Paris, where the Revolution interrupted his romantic pursuits.

The Chevalier de Valois of Alencon was well-regarded in the local aristocracy, known for his excellent manners and wit, often sharing anecdotes from the reign of Louis XV. He was a regular at social gatherings, where his charm and discretion made him a favorite. At fifty-eight, he claimed to be fifty, maintaining a youthful appearance despite his age. His notable features included a large nose and a pale complexion, which turned red on one side due to a supposed "hot liver" condition, a claim he used to justify his voracious appetite.

Despite his frail appearance, the chevalier was vigorous and passionate, with a face that bore the marks of a life well-lived. He took great care in his grooming, presenting himself with a youthful elegance that belied his years. His attire, though somewhat worn, was always clean and well-maintained, reflecting a commitment to aristocratic standards. He lived modestly in a rented room, dining out daily and spending little on himself, relying on a modest annuity for his income.

The chevalier was a beloved figure in Alencon, known for his card-playing skills and amiable nature. He was a master of conversation, able to engage with both the high and low of society, and his presence was often sought after at social events. Despite his charm, he was also a figure of intrigue, with rumors circulating about his private life, including a supposed secret marriage to a woman named Cesarine.

Living in a modest apartment above Madame Lardot's laundry, the chevalier's life was marked by a careful balance of social engagement and personal discretion. His financial situation was precarious, yet he managed to maintain a semblance of dignity and respectability. His reputation as a gentleman was upheld by his ability to navigate the complexities of provincial life with grace.

CHAPTER II. SUSANNAH AND THE ELDERS

One spring morning, the chevalier was interrupted by the arrival of Suzanne, a young girl from Madame Lardot's laundry. She brought news that could bring him both pleasure and pain, though she feigned indifference to his rumored connection with Cesarine. Suzanne, ambitious and clever, sought to escape Alencon for Paris, where she believed she could find a better life.

The chevalier, recognizing her charm and ambition, encouraged her to pursue her dreams while maintaining a paternal demeanor. He advised her to seek out Monsieur du Bousquier, a wealthy bachelor who had shown interest in her. The chevalier understood the dynamics of their society and the potential for Suzanne to leverage her beauty and wit to secure a favorable match.

As Suzanne left to pursue her plans, the narrative shifted to du Bousquier, a man of dubious reputation who had once enjoyed wealth and influence. Now returned to Alencon, he harbored ambitions of marrying Mademoiselle Cormon, a wealthy old maid, to restore his fortunes. His past as a purveyor during the Revolution had left him with a tarnished reputation, yet he remained determined to regain his status.

Du Bousquier's character contrasted sharply with that of the chevalier. While the chevalier embodied the elegance of the old regime, du Bousquier represented the more brusque and opportunistic nature of the new political landscape. Their rivalry over Mademoiselle Cormon was subtle yet intense, with each man vying for her favor while navigating the complexities of their respective social standings.

As the story unfolds, Suzanne's ambitions and the machinations of both men intertwine, revealing the intricate dance of desire, ambition, and societal expectations in the provincial setting of Alencon. The characters' interactions reflect the broader themes of class, gender, and the pursuit of happiness in a changing world.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Madame Granson, a widow living on a modest pension and her own property, struggled to support her son, Athanase, who contributed to their income with a small salary from the mayor’s office. Their home in a provincial town was characterized by its simplicity and the weight of poverty, which shaped their lives and morals. Athanase, a pale and introspective young man of twenty-three, harbored a deep passion for his cousin, Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he believed to be wealthy. His feelings were complicated by his awareness of societal expectations and the ridicule he might face for loving an older woman.

Athanase's love for Mademoiselle Cormon began as a practical consideration for securing a better life for himself and his mother, but it evolved into genuine affection. He admired her from afar, feeling both drawn to her and constrained by his circumstances. His talent and potential remained unrecognized in their provincial setting, where ambition often clashed with the harsh realities of life.

Madame Granson, aware of her son’s feelings, saw the potential benefits of a match with Mademoiselle Cormon, despite the obstacles posed by Athanase's lack of fortune and her cousin's age. She remained hopeful and patient, waiting for the right moment to act. Meanwhile, Athanase's thoughts were consumed by his love for Mademoiselle Cormon, and he often fantasized about a future together, despite the barriers that seemed insurmountable.

The arrival of Suzanne, a young woman from a less fortunate background, introduced a new dynamic. She felt an immediate connection to Athanase, sensing his struggles and aspirations. However, her own ambitions led her to consider a different path, one that involved deception to secure a better future. After a brief encounter with Madame Granson, Suzanne left the town, equipped with money and new clothes, while the community mourned her departure.

As Athanase prepared for a dinner with Mademoiselle Cormon, his mother urged him to present himself well, hoping to enhance his chances with her. Athanase, unaware of the full extent of his mother’s plans, navigated his feelings of love and ambition amidst the constraints of their provincial life. The evening promised to be pivotal, as the intertwining fates of Athanase, Mademoiselle Cormon, and Suzanne began to unfold, each character driven by their desires and the societal pressures surrounding them. 

In the end, Suzanne's departure to Paris marked a significant turning point, as she sought to redefine her life away from the constraints of her past. Her journey reflected the struggles of many women of her time, caught between societal expectations and personal aspirations. Meanwhile, Athanase remained in Alencon, grappling with his unfulfilled dreams and the weight of his love for Mademoiselle Cormon, as the complexities of their lives continued to intertwine in the backdrop of provincial society.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In many second-class prefectures of France, there exists a salon that serves as a gathering place for influential yet not elite members of the community. The hosts of these salons are prominent figures in their towns, invited to various events, but they do not mingle with the highest social echelons, who prefer to maintain distance, communicating through formal invitations instead.

This salon, where lesser nobility, clergy, and magistrates convene, wields significant influence. Its members are characterized by their indifference to luxury and fashion, focusing instead on practical matters like land acquisition. They are resistant to outside influences, particularly from Paris, and are known for their gossip, which often takes on the weight of judgment. Young women are rarely present, and when they are, it is typically to seek validation.

In Alencon, the upper classes had gathered at the home of an old maid, Mademoiselle Rose-Marie-Victoire Cormon, whose fortune was coveted by her relatives and two old bachelors. She lived with her uncle, a former grand-vicar, and her family had a long history of serving the nobility. The Cormon family had intermarried with the nobility, giving them a semblance of aristocracy despite their middle-class status.

Mademoiselle Cormon's house, built in the time of Henri IV, was a source of envy due to its prime location and well-maintained condition. The mansion featured a courtyard with a flowerbed, reflecting the meticulous care of its owner, an old maid whose attention to cleanliness was evident. The house's architecture was robust, with decorative elements that showcased its historical significance.

The interior of the house was divided into reception areas, including a salon adorned with family portraits and a dining room that exuded a provincial charm. The salon was a hub for social activities, where card games and conversations about local affairs took place. Mademoiselle Cormon's social circle consisted of about 150 individuals, mostly of mature age, who rarely traveled and had lived their lives in the provinces.

Wednesdays were special occasions when Mademoiselle Cormon hosted dinners, drawing a larger crowd. The women dressed modestly, and the atmosphere was one of familiarity and routine. Conversations often revolved around local gossip, land disputes, and the behaviors of the aristocracy, reflecting the community's values and concerns.

Mademoiselle Cormon, despite her wealth and social standing, remained unmarried, largely due to her desire to marry a nobleman and the societal constraints of her time. The political climate from 1788 to 1815 made it difficult for her to find a suitable match, as many eligible men were either too old or too young, or lacked the qualities she sought. Her fear of being married for her fortune led her to set traps for potential suitors, testing their intentions without their knowledge.

As she aged, her desire for love intensified, yet she remained oblivious to the affections of those around her, including Athanase Granson, one of her suitors. Mademoiselle Cormon's innocence and naivety prevented her from recognizing genuine feelings, leading to a series of missed opportunities. Her character became increasingly rigid, and societal pressures began to weigh heavily on her.

Despite her piety and virtuous nature, Mademoiselle Cormon was often the subject of ridicule in Alencon, her unmarried status becoming a local proverb. She lived modestly, eschewing luxury and maintaining a lifestyle that resonated with the values of her community. Her fortune, while considerable, did not elevate her above the expectations of provincial life, and her attempts at marriage were viewed with skepticism.

As the years passed, Mademoiselle Cormon's hopes for a suitable husband dwindled, and she found herself increasingly isolated in her desires. The return of disbanded soldiers and officers offered a glimmer of hope, yet none met her standards. Her longing for companionship and motherhood became a source of despair, as she grappled with the reality of her situation.

Ultimately, Mademoiselle Cormon's story reflects the complexities of love, societal expectations, and the challenges faced by women in her position. Her life, marked by a desire for connection and a struggle against the constraints of her environment, serves as a poignant commentary on the nature of happiness and fulfillment in a world governed by rigid social norms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
To complete the picture of the household of Mademoiselle Cormon, we must consider her and the Abbe de Sponde alongside their loyal servants: Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who all contributed to the comfort of the uncle and niece.

Jacquelin, a short, stout man with a ruddy complexion, had served the household for twenty-two years. He performed various tasks, from waiting at table to grooming the mare, Penelope, who was cherished by the household. He was in love with Josette, a thirty-year-old maid whom Mademoiselle would dismiss if she married him. They quietly saved their wages, hoping for Mademoiselle’s marriage to free them to wed. Josette, plump and practical, was said to rule her mistress. Mariette, the cook, had been with the family for fifteen years and was skilled in preparing the local delicacies.

Penelope, the old brown-bay mare, was a source of affection and conversation among the household, as Mademoiselle Cormon, lacking children, directed her maternal instincts toward the animal. The four servants, along with Penelope, maintained a routine that reflected their long-standing roles. Mademoiselle Cormon, however, became increasingly difficult to please, fixating on trivial matters due to her idle mind. She often fretted over small details, such as the state of the household linens or the timing of Penelope’s meals, leading to minor disputes that provided her with a sense of purpose.

Mademoiselle Cormon’s life revolved around her household, her uncle, and her religious duties, magnifying the minutiae of her existence. Her health was a constant concern, leading her to obsess over minor ailments and dietary choices. Each season, she would visit her estate at Prebaudet to oversee her apple trees and prepare for the upcoming harvest, marking the occasion with a farewell dinner for her friends, which was a notable event in Alencon.

On the day of her departure, the town buzzed with gossip about her and her potential suitors, particularly Monsieur du Bousquier, whom she had previously rejected. The evening of her farewell dinner was filled with social interactions, where Mademoiselle Cormon attempted to engage her guests in conversation, often struggling to find the right words. Her efforts to be amiable were met with mixed reactions, as her lack of wit became a source of amusement among her more astute guests.

As the evening progressed, the conversation turned to local politics and the controversial figure of du Bousquier, who was rumored to be involved with a young woman named Suzanne. Mademoiselle Cormon, in an attempt to defend du Bousquier, made a remark that unexpectedly garnered laughter and admiration from her guests, showcasing her ability to contribute to the evening’s entertainment, albeit unintentionally.

The dinner concluded with light-hearted banter about du Bousquier’s character, revealing the town’s divided opinions on him. Mademoiselle Cormon’s innocence and naivety were evident as she navigated the complexities of social interactions and the underlying tensions regarding marriage and morality.

As the guests departed, discussions continued about Mademoiselle Cormon’s prospects and the implications of du Bousquier’s actions. The evening left her contemplating her role in the community and her own desires, setting the stage for future developments in her life and the lives of those around her. The dynamics of love, ambition, and societal expectations intertwined, creating a rich tapestry of relationships that would continue to evolve in the small town of Alencon.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The following day, Mademoiselle Cormon, packed into her old carriole with Josette, set off for Prebaudet, unaware that an unexpected event would hasten her marriage. The morning after her arrival, while having breakfast, she was interrupted by Jacquelin, who brought news of a letter from her uncle, the Abbe de Sponde. The letter announced that Monsieur de Troisville, a grandson of one of the abbe's friends, wished to stay in Alencon and needed hospitality. Alarmed by the urgency, Mademoiselle Cormon decided to return to town immediately to help her uncle prepare for the guest.

Despite the rain, she insisted on a quick departure, driven by her excitement and the prospect of marriage. As they arrived in Alencon, her return caused quite a stir, with townspeople speculating about the reasons behind her sudden arrival. Josette and Jacquelin were left to manage the household while Mademoiselle Cormon focused on preparing for Monsieur de Troisville's arrival.

The news of the viscount's impending visit spread quickly, and the townsfolk were abuzz with gossip. Mademoiselle Cormon, filled with hope, began to worry about the state of her home and her appearance. She felt a mix of excitement and anxiety, fearing that her old-fashioned decor might not impress the viscount. As preparations were made, she sacrificed some of her prized liqueurs to impress her guest.

When Monsieur de Troisville finally arrived, he was greeted by the abbe and Mademoiselle Cormon. He was a distinguished-looking man, and Mademoiselle Cormon felt an immediate attraction. Their dinner conversation flowed, with the viscount complimenting her home and expressing admiration for the region. Mademoiselle Cormon felt she was finally being seen as a desirable woman.

However, the evening took a turn when the Chevalier de Valois and other guests arrived, drawn by curiosity about the new visitor. During the introductions, the viscount casually mentioned that he was married, which devastated Mademoiselle Cormon. She fainted, and du Bousquier, a local suitor, caught her and carried her to her room. The townspeople, who had been eagerly watching the events unfold, were left to speculate about the implications of the evening.

As Mademoiselle Cormon recovered, she realized that her hopes for marriage had been dashed. The next day, the news of her fainting and the viscount's marital status spread throughout Alencon, leading to a mix of laughter and sympathy. Mademoiselle Cormon, feeling the weight of her situation, contemplated her options. Both du Bousquier and the Chevalier de Valois sought her attention, each hoping to win her favor.

Ultimately, Mademoiselle Cormon decided to accept du Bousquier's proposal, seeing it as a way to regain her standing in the community. She required him to agree to a lie about their engagement, which he readily accepted. As they prepared to announce their engagement, the Chevalier de Valois arrived, unaware of the developments. The evening ended with Mademoiselle Cormon feeling a mix of triumph and uncertainty, as the town buzzed with the news of her engagement, marking a new chapter in her life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The news of Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage devastated Athanase Granson, though he masked his inner turmoil. He first learned of it while at the home of Chief Justice du Ronceret, where his mother was playing cards. Athanase had long pinned his hopes on Mademoiselle Cormon, and the news felt like a catastrophe. He was a product of the Imperial education system, shaped by a sense of fatalism that left him feeling lost and indifferent. His mother noticed his pallor but attributed it to deep thought about his work.

In the wake of the marriage announcement, Athanase wandered the picturesque Sarthe region, a place often overlooked by locals. He found solace in its beauty, though his heart was heavy with despair. His mother, concerned for him, speculated that he was contemplating a significant project. Athanase withdrew from politics and social life, becoming a figure of little interest to the townspeople, who were unaware of his suffering.

Meanwhile, Suzanne, a young woman inspired by tales of romance and adventure, returned to Alencon with plans to help Athanase financially, though their paths did not cross. The town buzzed with gossip about Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage, with conflicting reports about its legitimacy. Eventually, the marriage contract was signed, and the wedding took place amid ominous signs, including a priest accidentally reading from the De Profundis.

The marriage of Mademoiselle Cormon and du Bousquier was marked by a lack of celebration, and the couple quickly retreated to Prebaudet. Du Bousquier, eager to modernize the Cormon estate, began renovations that alarmed the townsfolk, who feared the loss of their provincial charm. Athanase, meanwhile, was consumed by his grief and ultimately took his own life, leaving a letter for his mother that she misinterpreted as a sign of independence.

The news of Athanase’s suicide shocked Alencon, and his mother was left to mourn in silence, grappling with the depth of her loss. The Chevalier de Valois revealed Athanase’s unrequited love for Mademoiselle Cormon, which only deepened the tragedy in the eyes of the townspeople. Madame Granson’s grief was profound, as she alone understood the true nature of her son’s struggles.

In the aftermath, Madame du Bousquier returned to Alencon, where she encountered Madame Granson, whose sorrow was palpable. The two women’s paths crossed in a moment heavy with unspoken words. Madame Granson sought solace in the church, imploring the rector for a proper burial for her son, which was conducted discreetly to avoid scandal.

As time passed, Madame du Bousquier’s marriage to du Bousquier became a source of societal gossip. Initially, she appeared content, but the reality of her situation soon set in. Du Bousquier, while politically ambitious and outwardly successful, was a tyrant at home, leaving her feeling powerless and unfulfilled. The couple had no children, and Madame du Bousquier’s longing for motherhood became a source of pain.

The Chevalier de Valois, embittered by the marriage, sought revenge against du Bousquier, who had become a figure of ridicule in the community. Despite the initial laughter at Mademoiselle Cormon’s misfortune, sympathy for Madame du Bousquier grew as her struggles became apparent. The chevalier’s machinations only served to deepen the animosity between du Bousquier and the aristocracy.

Madame du Bousquier’s life became a series of contradictions as she navigated her duties as a wife while grappling with her feelings of resentment. She devoted herself to her husband, even as she felt trapped by his oppressive nature. The marriage, while socially advantageous, left her emotionally desolate.

The Chevalier de Valois’s death coincided with significant political changes in France, marking the end of an era. His demise, along with that of Suzanne’s mother, left a void in the lives of those who had known them. Suzanne, now seeking to honor her past, purchased a memento from the chevalier’s estate, a symbol of lost connections.

Ultimately, Madame du Bousquier’s life became a testament to the complexities of love, duty, and societal expectations. She remained a figure of pity and admiration, enduring the consequences of her choices while reflecting on the life she might have had. The narrative serves as a poignant exploration of the human condition, illustrating the interplay between personal desires and societal pressures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

DEDICATION

To Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author of the History of the Ottoman Empire.

Dear Baron, your encouragement for my “History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century” has inspired me to associate your name with my work. As a representative of studious Germany, your approval lends credibility to my efforts. Your dedication to research has motivated me through my own labor on modern civilization. I hope this token of respect finds you well in Dobling, reminding you of your sincere admirer and friend.

DE BALZAC.

THE COLLECTION OF ANTIQUITIES

In a small French town, there stands a house known as the Hotel d’Esgrignon, where the old Marquis, Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol, resided. Initially a jest, the name became earnest over time. The d’Esgrignon family, descended from powerful Northmen, had a proud lineage, never bowing to kings or commoners. They were provincial nobles, revered by the local populace, and had maintained their status despite being neglected by the court for centuries. The family’s history was marked by marriages without dowries and a steadfast refusal to accept titles that diminished their nobility.

During the Revolution, the d’Esgrignons faced peril. The Marquis, respected by the countryside, avoided emigration but had to hide for a time. The family lands were sold off, but Mlle. d’Esgrignon managed to save some properties through the family steward, Chesnel. After the Terror, the Marquis returned to a dilapidated castle, mourning the loss of his heritage. The Hotel d’Esgrignon had been demolished, and Chesnel used the last of the Marquis’ money to purchase a new home for the family, which was humorously named the Hotel d’Esgrignon.

In 1800, some emigres began returning, including the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter, whom the Marquis married. Tragically, she died in childbirth, leaving a son to carry on the family name. The Marquis, now burdened by grief, faced the challenges of his diminished status. Mlle. d’Esgrignon, a beautiful woman, was approached for marriage by a local contractor, but both she and her brother were offended by the proposal. This incident strained their relationship with Chesnel, who had acted on the contractor’s behalf.

Chesnel, a respected notary, had always been more than a servant to the d’Esgrignons, but the shift in their dynamic troubled him. The Marquis, proud of his lineage, viewed Chesnel as a loyal companion, while Chesnel felt the weight of his loyalty to a family in decline. The political landscape shifted as some nobles aligned with Napoleon, while others remained loyal to the monarchy. The d’Esgrignons, however, held steadfast to their principles.

By 1804, many emigres returned, but the d’Esgrignons’ fortunes did not improve. The Marquis, now a symbol of loyalty and nobility, became a leader among the remaining loyalists. His salon, dubbed the “Collection of Antiquities,” became a gathering place for those who clung to the past. The nickname, initially playful, took on a more serious tone as the political climate shifted.

The d’Esgrignons faced challenges from the rising bourgeoisie, represented by du Croisier, who sought to undermine their status. The rivalry intensified as du Croisier’s faction grew in strength, while the d’Esgrignons remained a bastion of old-world values. The political landscape became increasingly polarized, with du Croisier adopting a more aggressive stance against the nobility.

In 1822, the d’Esgrignons’ situation remained precarious. The Marquis, now elderly, found himself at a crossroads, unsure of his role in a changing society. The Restoration had not brought the expected relief, and the family’s financial struggles continued. The d’Esgrignons lived modestly, their income barely covering their needs, while the political tensions in the town simmered beneath the surface.

As the political landscape evolved, the d’Esgrignons clung to their ideals, navigating a world that seemed increasingly hostile to their way of life. The Collection of Antiquities stood as a testament to a bygone era, a reminder of the nobility’s struggles and the enduring legacy of the d’Esgrignon family.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The Marquis was oblivious to the management of his estate, and the idea that his family, the d’Esgrignons, could regain their former glory seemed absurd to those around him. They believed that Victurnien, the handsome heir, would restore the family's prestige by marrying into the nobility. However, the reality was that the d’Esgrignons lacked the political and financial means to reclaim their status, and their historical continuity had been severed by the events of the past forty years.

Chesnel, the notary, was the only one who recognized the futility of their hopes. He understood the changes in society brought about by the Revolution and the new political landscape. Despite his loyalty to the d’Esgrignons, he could not ignore the harsh truths of their situation. He tried to gently inform the Marquis of the need for adaptation, but the old noble remained steadfast in his beliefs, convinced that divine intervention would restore their fortunes.

Victurnien, adored by his father and aunt, was a spoiled child who had never faced opposition. His upbringing, filled with indulgence, left him self-centered and reckless. The Chevalier, a relic of the past, encouraged Victurnien's extravagant lifestyle, sharing tales of the old nobility that only fueled the young man's sense of superiority. This environment led Victurnien to believe he was above the common people, which only exacerbated his flaws.

As Victurnien entered society, he quickly became aware of the discrepancies between his noble upbringing and the realities of the modern world. His behavior alienated the townspeople, and his escapades led to legal troubles that Chesnel had to manage discreetly. Encouraged by his peers, Victurnien's arrogance grew, and he became embroiled in a lifestyle of excess, believing that his noble lineage exempted him from the consequences of his actions.

Du Croisier, a rival, sought to exploit Victurnien's weaknesses, hoping to see the d’Esgrignons fall into ruin. Meanwhile, Victurnien's vanity and desire for pleasure blinded him to the dangers surrounding him. He was drawn into a world of gambling and frivolity, supported by Chesnel's financial sacrifices, which he took for granted.

When Victurnien was finally sent to Paris, he was filled with excitement and expectations. However, he soon realized that the court was not as welcoming as he had hoped. The nobility he encountered were more concerned with their own interests than with reviving the d’Esgrignon name. Despite this, Victurnien was determined to make a name for himself, adopting the extravagant lifestyle of the Parisian elite.

His charm and good looks opened doors, and he quickly became a fixture in high society, particularly drawn to the enchanting Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. She captivated him, and he fell deeply in love, blinded by her beauty and the allure of her world. However, he remained unaware of the financial burdens she carried, believing instead that their love was pure and untainted.

As Victurnien indulged in the pleasures of Paris, he racked up debts that he could not afford. His friends, while enjoying his company, were also aware of his precarious situation. They encouraged his spending, knowing that his downfall would be entertaining. Victurnien's naivety and lack of foresight led him to believe that he could maintain his lavish lifestyle without consequence.

Chesnel, ever loyal, continued to support Victurnien financially, but he grew increasingly concerned about the young man's reckless behavior. Despite his warnings, Victurnien remained oblivious to the reality of his situation, caught up in the fantasy of his new life. The contrast between his noble heritage and the harsh truths of his financial state created a tension that would ultimately lead to his downfall.

As the story unfolds, Victurnien's journey through the world of Parisian high society reveals the dangers of vanity, excess, and the illusion of nobility. His relationships, particularly with the Duchesse, become entangled in a web of deceit and financial ruin, highlighting the fragility of status and the consequences of living beyond one's means. The narrative serves as a cautionary tale about the perils of pride and the importance of recognizing one's limitations in a rapidly changing world.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By the end of 1823, the Kellers had provided Victurnien with two hundred thousand francs, unbeknownst to Chesnel or Mlle. Armande. He had also received two thousand crowns from Chesnel to obscure his financial sources. Victurnien wrote deceitful letters to his father and aunt, who remained blissfully unaware of the impending disaster threatening their noble house. Only du Croisier was aware of the situation, relishing the thought of not just ruining the d’Esgrignons but also dishonoring them. He sensed that his revenge was near, especially as Victurnien's debts became unmanageable.

Du Croisier's first move was to eliminate his enemy, the venerable Chesnel, who lived in a modest house in the Rue du Bercail. One afternoon, while digesting his dinner, Chesnel was interrupted by his housekeeper, who announced du Croisier's arrival. The old lawyer's heart sank at the sight of his adversary. Du Croisier presented a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs, demanding immediate payment or threatening to expose Victurnien's debts. Chesnel, overwhelmed, realized he could only raise the money by mortgaging his own lands. However, du Croisier, sensing an opportunity, proposed to buy Chesnel's property, which he did within two days. Stripped of his lands, Chesnel was left with only his practice and the house he lived in, consumed by worry for Victurnien's future.

Chesnel's thoughts turned to Mlle. Armande, and he dreaded the task of informing her of the family's financial ruin. Meanwhile, Mlle. Armande received a charming yet hypocritical letter from Victurnien, detailing his travels in Italy with the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Enchanted by his words, she was blissfully unaware of the truth. As she read, Chesnel's arrival shattered her reverie. He delivered the grim news: all was lost, and Victurnien risked bringing dishonor to their house. Mlle. Armande was devastated, realizing that they had allowed Victurnien too much freedom.

The Marquis, having read Victurnien's letter, was oblivious to the family's plight. Mlle. Armande and Chesnel shared a silent understanding of their mutual concern for the young Count. They discussed the need to intervene and save him from his reckless lifestyle, fearing he would be left without resources. Mlle. Armande resolved to go to Paris to rescue Victurnien, disguising her journey as a medical consultation to avoid gossip.

Chesnel provided her with his last bag of louis, and she set off, determined to confront the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Du Croisier, meanwhile, had meticulously planned his revenge, ensuring that Victurnien would be cut off from further financial support. He wrote to Victurnien, informing him that the Kellers would no longer advance money without security, timing the letter to coincide with Victurnien's growing desperation.

Victurnien, who had been living a life of luxury, suddenly faced the harsh reality of his debts. Despite his previous successes at gambling, he found himself unable to procure money and felt the weight of impending ruin. In a moment of despair, he contemplated suicide but ultimately decided against it. Instead, he considered fleeing with the Duchess, planning to forge a bill of exchange to finance their escape. However, he hesitated, wanting to ensure the Duchess's agreement before taking such drastic action.

When Victurnien approached the Duchess, he expressed his financial troubles, but she reassured him that money matters could be resolved. Encouraged by her support, he proposed they flee together, and she agreed, eager to escape their current lives. Victurnien, buoyed by her promise, resolved to forge the bill, believing it was the only way to secure their future together.

The next day, he presented a bill for three hundred thousand francs to the Kellers, who advanced the money, unaware of the deception. Du Croisier, upon learning of the transaction, realized his plan for revenge was coming to fruition.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When Victurnien received his money, he brought it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who locked it away and suggested they attend the Opera one last time. Victurnien, however, was troubled, contemplating whether he should confess everything to Chesnel instead. Despite his reservations, the Duchess's enchanting glance led him to stay longer than he intended.

The next day, Victurnien returned to the Hotel de Maufrigneuse to discuss their escape plans. The Duchess, however, was resolute about staying, believing it would be more remarkable to face the storm than to flee. Victurnien felt a mix of admiration and despair, realizing the depth of his feelings for her.

Diane's coldness after their argument left Victurnien distraught. He pleaded for her forgiveness, but she remained unyielding, embodying an angelic indifference that only deepened his anguish. As time passed, Victurnien's despair grew, and he felt utterly lost, unable to think clearly about his next steps.

Meanwhile, the Count had taken Diane's hand, but it felt lifeless to him. Victurnien's emotional turmoil was compounded by the realization that he was in serious trouble due to his financial misdeeds. When Josephin informed him of a warrant for his arrest, Victurnien panicked, believing he was doomed.

Josephin urged him to flee to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine, where Mlle. Armande awaited him. Victurnien, still in shock, embraced his aunt, who was distraught but determined to help him. They discussed hiding and the possibility of sneaking into Chesnel’s house unnoticed.

As they executed their plan, Victurnien unwittingly walked into a trap. Chesnel was in the process of selling his practice to a rival notary, M. Lepressoir, who was eager to capitalize on Victurnien's troubles. When Chesnel learned of Victurnien's situation, he was devastated, fearing for the young man's future.

Chesnel's desperation led him to consider drastic measures to protect Victurnien, including taking the blame for the forgery himself. He resolved to confront du Croisier, Victurnien's creditor, hoping to negotiate a settlement. Meanwhile, du Croisier reveled in his power over the d’Esgrignon family, seeing the situation as an opportunity to assert his dominance.

At a gathering at du Croisier's, the news of Victurnien's arrest spread among the guests, who were both shocked and intrigued. The atmosphere was tense, with du Croisier's allies discussing the implications of the arrest and the potential for a trial.

Chesnel, realizing the gravity of the situation, sought to influence the examining magistrate, M. Camusot, by appealing to his ambitions and connections. He believed that if he could secure Camusot's support, they might be able to mitigate the damage to Victurnien's reputation.

As the legal proceedings unfolded, Chesnel's efforts to protect Victurnien intensified. He sought the assistance of Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who was initially unaware of the full extent of the situation. When she learned of the forgery, she felt compelled to help, recognizing the depth of Victurnien's love and the potential consequences of his actions.

Chesnel's negotiations with du Croisier proved challenging, as the latter was driven by a desire for revenge against the aristocracy. Du Croisier's demands included political favors and social recognition, which Chesnel found difficult to fulfill.

In a moment of desperation, Chesnel offered to pay du Croisier a substantial sum to withdraw the charges against Victurnien. However, du Croisier's pride and ambition made him resistant to compromise. The situation escalated, with both sides unwilling to back down.

As the trial approached, the stakes grew higher. Chesnel's determination to save Victurnien led him to consider morally questionable actions, including lying under oath. He understood that the honor of the d’Esgrignon family was at stake and that the consequences of dishonor could be devastating.

In the end, the legal battle became a reflection of the broader social tensions between the aristocracy and the bourgeoisie. The outcome would not only determine Victurnien's fate but also signal the shifting power dynamics within their society.

Chesnel's unwavering loyalty to the d’Esgrignon family and his willingness to navigate the treacherous waters of provincial politics underscored the complexities of love, honor, and ambition in a world where social status could dictate one's destiny.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Blondet, a knowledgeable judge, was overshadowed by his passion for gardening, particularly for pelargoniums, which consumed his time and ambition. His marriage to a much younger woman, who had an affair with the local prefect, further obscured his career. Despite having a son, Joseph, with his wife, Blondet favored his illegitimate son, Emile, whom he eventually sent to Paris with minimal support after his wife's death.

Blondet's home was a charming brick house with a well-kept garden, reflecting his dedication to horticulture. He intended to leave his estate to Joseph, having disinherited Emile, whom he viewed with disdain. Emile, however, found success in Paris, aided by connections from his mother’s family, particularly Mlle. de Troisville, who had promised to support him.

As Emile gained recognition in literature, Blondet remained unaware of his son's achievements, focusing instead on his legal duties and gardening. His life was marked by strict frugality, with a maid managing the household and ensuring they lived modestly.

Joseph Blondet, groomed to be a judge, spent time with the Blandureau family, while Michu, a young lawyer, navigated social circles, often overshadowing the older judges. The public prosecutor, a skilled politician, was embroiled in a plot against the d’Esgrignons, leading to a charge of forgery against Victurnien d’Esgrignon, a young nobleman.

The President of the Tribunal, seeking personal gain, manipulated the situation to benefit du Croisier, a rival of the d’Esgrignons. The prosecutor, influenced by the President's wife, was persuaded to pursue the case against Victurnien, despite the potential for political fallout.

As the trial approached, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse intervened, bringing funds to secure Victurnien's release. Her efforts, combined with the support of Chesnel, the family notary, aimed to clear the d’Esgrignon name. The trial revealed the truth, exonerating Victurnien and exposing du Croisier's machinations.

In the aftermath, the d’Esgrignons faced societal scrutiny, but the family’s honor was preserved. Victurnien's future, however, remained uncertain, as he struggled with the implications of his past and the expectations of his lineage. The story concludes with reflections on the changing nature of nobility and the enduring bonds of loyalty and service, embodied by characters like Chesnel, who represented the fading ideals of feudal devotion.

Ultimately, the narrative explores themes of ambition, familial loyalty, and the complexities of social standing in a changing France, highlighting the interplay between personal desires and societal expectations.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

THE LILY OF THE VALLEY

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

DEDICATION

To Monsieur J. B. Nacquart, Member of the Royal Academy of Medicine.

Dear Doctor--Here is one of the most carefully hewn stones in the second course of the foundation of a literary edifice which I have slowly and laboriously constructed. I wish to inscribe your name upon it, as much to thank the man whose science once saved me as to honor the friend of my daily life.

De Balzac.

THE LILY OF THE VALLEY

ENVOI

Felix de Vandenesse to Madame la Comtesse Natalie de Manerville:

I yield to your wishes. It is the privilege of the women whom we love more than they love us to make the men who love them ignore the ordinary rules of common-sense. To smooth the frown upon their brow, to soften the pout upon their lips, what obstacles we miraculously overcome! We shed our blood, we risk our future!

You exact the history of my past life; here it is. But remember this, Natalie; in obeying you I crush under foot a reluctance hitherto unconquerable. Why are you jealous of the sudden reveries which overtake me in the midst of our happiness? Why show the pretty anger of a petted woman when silence grasps me? Could you not play upon the contradictions of my character without inquiring into the causes of them? Are there secrets in your heart which seek absolution through a knowledge of mine? Ah! Natalie, you have guessed mine; and it is better you should know the whole truth. Yes, my life is shadowed by a phantom; a word evokes it; it hovers vaguely above me and about me; within my soul are solemn memories, buried in its depths like those marine productions seen in calmest weather and which the storms of ocean cast in fragments on the shore.

The mental labor which the expression of ideas necessitates has revived the old, old feelings which give me so much pain when they come suddenly; and if in this confession of my past they break forth in a way that wounds you, remember that you threatened to punish me if I did not obey your wishes, and do not, therefore, punish my obedience. I would that this, my confidence, might increase your love.

Until we meet,

Felix.

CHAPTER I. TWO CHILDHOODS

To what genius fed on tears shall we some day owe that most touching of all elegies,--the tale of tortures borne silently by souls whose tender roots find stony ground in the domestic soil, whose earliest buds are torn apart by rancorous hands, whose flowers are touched by frost at the moment of their blossoming? What poet will sing the sorrows of the child whose lips must suck a bitter breast, whose smiles are checked by the cruel fire of a stern eye? The tale that tells of such poor hearts, oppressed by beings placed about them to promote the development of their natures, would contain the true history of my childhood.

What vanity could I have wounded,--I a child new-born? What moral or physical infirmity caused by mother’s coldness? Was I the child of duty, whose birth is a mere chance, or was I one whose very life was a reproach? Put to nurse in the country and forgotten by my family for over three years, I was treated with such indifference on my return to the parental roof that even the servants pitied me. I do not know to what feeling or happy accident I owed my rescue from this first neglect; as a child I was ignorant of it, as a man I have not discovered it. Far from easing my lot, my brother and my two sisters found amusement in making me suffer. The compact in virtue of which children hide each other’s peccadilloes, and which early teaches them the principles of honor, was null and void in my case; more than that, I was often punished for my brother’s faults, without being allowed to prove the injustice. The fawning spirit which seems instinctive in children taught my brother and sisters to join in the persecutions to which I was subjected, and thus keep in the good graces of a mother whom they feared as much as I. Was this partly the effect of a childish love of imitation; was it from a need of testing their powers; or was it simply through lack of pity? Perhaps these causes united to deprive me of the sweets of fraternal intercourse.

Disinherited of all affection, I could love nothing; yet nature had made me loving. Is there an angel who garners the sighs of feeling hearts rebuffed incessantly? If in many such hearts the crushed feelings turn to hatred, in mine they condensed and hollowed a depth from which, in after years, they gushed forth upon my life. In many characters the habit of trembling relaxes the fibres and begets fear, and fear ends in submission; hence, a weakness which emasculates a man, and makes him more or less a slave. But in my case these perpetual tortures led to the development of a certain strength, which increased through exercise and predisposed my spirit to the habit of moral resistance. Always in expectation of some new grief--as the martyrs expected some fresh blow--my whole being expressed, I doubt not, a sullen resignation which smothered the grace and gaiety of childhood, and gave me an appearance of idiocy which seemed to justify my mother’s threatening prophecies. The certainty of injustice prematurely roused my pride--that fruit of reason--and thus, no doubt, checked the evil tendencies which an education like mine encouraged.

Though my mother neglected me I was sometimes the object of her solicitude; she occasionally spoke of my education and seemed desirous of attending to it herself. Cold chills ran through me at such times when I thought of the torture a daily intercourse with her would inflict upon me. I blessed the neglect in which I lived, and rejoiced that I could stay alone in the garden and play with the pebbles and watch the insects and gaze into the blueness of the sky. Though my loneliness naturally led me to reverie, my liking for contemplation was first aroused by an incident which will give you an idea of my early troubles. So little notice was taken of me that the governess occasionally forgot to send me to bed. One evening I was peacefully crouching under a fig-tree, watching a star with that passion of curiosity which takes possession of a child’s mind, and to which my precocious melancholy gave a sort of sentimental intuition. My sisters were playing about and laughing; I heard their distant chatter like an accompaniment to my thoughts. After a while the noise ceased and darkness fell. My mother happened to notice my absence. To escape blame, our governess, a terrible Mademoiselle Caroline, worked upon my mother’s fears,--told her I had a horror of my home and would long ago have run away if she had not watched me; that I was not stupid but sullen; and that in all her experience of children she had never known one of so bad a disposition as mine. She pretended to search for me. I answered as soon as I was called, and she came to the fig-tree, where she very well knew I was. “What are you doing there?” she asked. “Watching a star.” “You were not watching a star,” said my mother, who was listening on her balcony; “children of your age know nothing of astronomy.” “Ah, madame,” cried Mademoiselle Caroline, “he has opened the faucet of the reservoir; the garden is inundated!” Then there was a general excitement. The fact was that my sisters had amused themselves by turning the cock to see the water flow, but a sudden spurt wet them all over and frightened them so much that they ran away without closing it. Accused and convicted of this piece of mischief and told that I lied when I denied it, I was severely punished. Worse than all, I was jeered at for my pretended love of the stars and forbidden to stay in the garden after dark.

Such tyrannical restraints intensify a passion in the hearts of children even more than in those of men; children think of nothing but the forbidden thing, which then becomes irresistibly attractive to them. I was often whipped for my star. Unable to confide in my kind, I told it all my troubles in that delicious inward prattle with which we stammer our first ideas, just as once we stammered our first words. At twelve years of age, long after I was at school, I still watched that star with indescribable delight,--so deep and lasting are the impressions we receive in the dawn of life.

My brother Charles, five years older than I and as handsome a boy as he now is a man, was the favorite of my father, the idol of my mother, and consequently the sovereign of the house. He was robust and well-made, and had a tutor. I, puny and even sickly, was sent at five years of age as a day pupil to a school in the town; taken in the morning and brought back at night by my father’s valet. I was sent with a scanty lunch, while my school-fellows brought plenty of good food. This trifling contrast between my privations and their prosperity made me suffer deeply. The famous potted pork prepared at Tours and called “rillettes” and “rillons” was the chief feature of their mid-day meal, between the early breakfast and the parent’s dinner, which was ready when we returned from school. This preparation of meat, much prized by certain gourmands, is seldom seen at Tours on aristocratic tables; if I had ever heard of it before I went to school, I certainly had never had the happiness of seeing that brown mess spread on slices of bread and butter. Nevertheless, my desire for those “rillons” was so great that it grew to be a fixed idea, like the longing of an elegant Parisian duchess for the stews cooked by a porter’s wife,--longings which, being a woman, she found means to satisfy. Children guess each other’s covetousness, just as you are able to read a man’s love, by the look in the eyes; consequently I became an admirable butt for ridicule. My comrades, nearly all belonging to the lower bourgeoisie, would show me their “rillons” and ask if I knew how they were made and where they were sold, and why it was that I never had any. They licked their lips as they talked of them--scraps of pork pressed in their own fat and looking like cooked truffles; they inspected my lunch-basket, and finding nothing better than Olivet cheese or dried fruits, they plagued me with questions: “Is that all you have? have you really nothing else?”--speeches which made me realize the difference between my brother and myself.

This contrast between my own abandonment and the happiness of others nipped the roses of my childhood and blighted my budding youth. The first time that I, mistaking my comrades’ actions for generosity, put forth my hand to take the dainty I had so long coveted and which was now hypocritically held out to me, my tormentor pulled back his slice to the great delight of his comrades who were expecting that result. If noble and distinguished minds are, as we often find them, capable of vanity, can we blame the child who weeps when despised and jeered at? Under such a trial many boys would have turned into gluttons and cringing beggars. I fought to escape my persecutors. The courage of despair made me formidable; but I was hated, and thus had no protection against treachery. One evening as I left school I was struck in the back by a handful of small stones tied in a handkerchief. When the valet, who punished the perpetrator, told this to my mother she exclaimed: “That dreadful child! he will always be a torment to us.”

Finding that I inspired in my schoolmates the same repulsion that was felt for me by my family, I sank into a horrible distrust of myself. A second fall of snow checked the seeds that were germinating in my soul. The boys whom I most liked were notorious scamps; this fact roused my pride and I held aloof. Again I was shut up within myself and had no vent for the feelings with which my heart was full. The master of the school, observing that I was gloomy, disliked by my comrades, and always alone, confirmed the family verdict as to my sulky temper. As soon as I could read and write, my mother transferred me to Pont-le-Voy, a school in charge of Oratorians who took boys of my age into a form called the “class of the Latin steps” where dull lads with torpid brains were apt to linger.

There I remained eight years without seeing my family; living the life of a pariah,--partly for the following reason. I received but three francs a month pocket-money, a sum barely sufficient to buy the pens, ink, paper, knives, and rules which we were forced to supply ourselves. Unable to buy stilts or skipping-ropes, or any of the things that were used in the playground, I was driven out of the games; to gain admission on suffrage I should have had to toady the rich and flatter the strong of my division. My heart rose against either of these meannesses, which, however, most children readily employ. I lived under a tree, lost in dejected thought, or reading the books distributed to us monthly by the librarian. How many griefs were in the shadow of that solitude; what genuine anguish filled my neglected life! Imagine what my sore heart felt when, at the first distribution of prizes,--of which I obtained the two most valued, namely, for theme and for translation,--neither my father nor my mother was present in the theatre when I came forward to receive the awards amid general acclamations, although the building was filled with the relatives of all my comrades. Instead of kissing the distributor, according to custom, I burst into tears and threw myself on his breast. That night I burned my crowns in the stove. The parents of the other boys were in town for a whole week preceding the distribution of the prizes, and my comrades departed joyfully the next day; while I, whose father and mother were only a few miles distant, remained at the school with the “outremers,”--a name given to scholars whose families were in the colonies or in foreign countries.

You will notice throughout how my unhappiness increased in proportion as the social spheres on which I entered widened. God knows what efforts I made to weaken the decree which condemned me to live within myself! What hopes, long cherished with eagerness of soul, were doomed to perish in a day! To persuade my parents to come and see me, I wrote them letters full of feeling, too emphatically worded, it may be; but surely such letters ought not to have drawn upon me my mother’s reprimand, coupled with ironical reproaches for my style. Not discouraged even then, I implored the help of my sisters, to whom I always wrote on their birthdays and fete-days with the persistence of a neglected child; but it was all in vain. As the day for the distribution of prizes approached I redoubled my entreaties, and told of my expected triumphs. Misled by my parents’ silence, I expected them with a beating heart. I told my schoolfellows they were coming; and then, when the old porter’s step sounded in the corridors as he called my happy comrades one by one to receive their friends, I was sick with expectation. Never did that old man call my name!

One day, when I accused myself to my confessor of having cursed my life, he pointed to the skies, where grew, he said, the promised palm for the “Beati qui lugent” of the Saviour. From the period of my first communion I flung myself into the mysterious depths of prayer, attracted to religious ideas whose moral fairyland so fascinates young spirits. Burning with ardent faith, I prayed to God to renew in my behalf the miracles I had read of in martyrology. At five years of age I fled to my star; at twelve I took refuge in the sanctuary. My ecstasy brought dreams unspeakable, which fed my imagination, fostered my susceptibilities, and strengthened my thinking powers. I have often attributed those sublime visions to the guardian angel charged with moulding my spirit to its divine destiny; they endowed my soul with the faculty of seeing the inner soul of things; they prepared my heart for the magic craft which makes a man a poet when the fatal power is his to compare what he feels within him with reality,--the great things aimed for with the small things gained. Those visions wrote upon my brain a book in which I read that which I must voice; they laid upon my lips the coal of utterance.

My father having conceived some doubts as to the tendency of the Oratorian teachings, took me from Pont-le-Voy, and sent me to Paris to an institution in the Marais. I was then fifteen. When examined as to my capacity, I, who was in the rhetoric class at Pont-le-Voy, was pronounced worthy of the third class. The sufferings I had endured in my family and in school were continued under another form during my stay at the Lepitre Academy. My father gave me no money; I was to be fed, clothed, and stuffed with Latin and Greek, for a sum agreed on. During my school life I came in contact with over a thousand comrades; but I never met with such an instance of neglect and indifference as mine. Monsieur Lepitre, who was fanatically attached to the Bourbons, had had relations with my father at the time when all devoted royalists were endeavoring to bring about the escape of Marie Antoinette from the Temple. They had lately renewed acquaintance; and Monsieur Lepitre thought himself obliged to repair my father’s oversight, and to give me a small sum monthly. But not being authorized to do so, the amount was small indeed.

The Lepitre establishment was in the old Joyeuse mansion where, as in all seignorial houses, there was a porter’s lodge. During a recess, which preceded the hour when the man-of-all-work took us to the Charlemagne Lyceum, the well-to-do pupils used to breakfast with the porter, named Doisy. Monsieur Lepitre was either ignorant of the fact or he connived at this arrangement with Doisy, a regular smuggler whom it was the pupils’ interest to protect,--he being the secret guardian of their pranks, the safe confidant of their late returns and their intermediary for obtaining forbidden books. Breakfast on a cup of “cafe-au-lait” is an aristocratic habit, explained by the high prices to which colonial products rose under Napoleon. If the use of sugar and coffee was a luxury to our parents, with us it was the sign of self-conscious superiority. Doisy gave credit, for he reckoned on the sisters and aunts of the pupils, who made it a point of honor to pay their debts. I resisted the blandishments of his place for a long time. If my judges knew the strength of its seduction, the heroic efforts I made after stoicism, the repressed desires of my long resistance, they would pardon my final overthrow. But, child as I was, could I have the grandeur of soul that scorns the scorn of others? Moreover, I may have felt the promptings of several social vices whose power was increased by my longings.

About the end of the second year my father and mother came to Paris. My brother had written me the day of their arrival. He lived in Paris, but had never been to see me. My sisters, he said, were of the party; we were all to see Paris together. The first day we were to dine in the Palais-Royal, so as to be near the Theatre-Francais. In spite of the intoxication such a programme of unhoped-for delights excited, my joy was dampened by the wind of a coming storm, which those who are used to unhappiness apprehend instinctively. I was forced to own a debt of a hundred francs to the Sieur Doisy, who threatened to ask my parents himself for the money. I bethought me of making my brother the emissary of Doisy, the mouth-piece of my repentance and the mediator of pardon. My father inclined to forgiveness, but my mother was pitiless; her dark blue eye froze me; she fulminated cruel prophecies: “What should I be later if at seventeen years of age I committed such follies? Was I really a son of hers? Did I mean to ruin my family? Did I think myself the only child of the house? My brother Charles’s career, already begun, required large outlay, amply deserved by his conduct which did honor to the family, while mine would always disgrace it. Did I know nothing of the value of money, and what I cost them? Of what use were coffee and sugar to my education? Such conduct was the first step into all the vices.”

After enduring the shock of this torrent which rasped my soul, I was sent back to school in charge of my brother. I lost the dinner at the Freres Provencaux, and was deprived of seeing Talma in Britannicus. Such was my first interview with my mother after a separation of twelve years.

When I had finished school my father left me under the guardianship of Monsieur Lepitre. I was to study the higher mathematics, follow a course of law for one year, and begin philosophy. Allowed to study in my own room and released from the classes, I expected a truce with trouble. But, in spite of my nineteen years, perhaps because of them, my father persisted in the system which had sent me to school without food, to an academy without pocket-money, and had driven me into debt to Doisy. Very little money was allowed to me, and what can you do in Paris without money? Moreover, my freedom was carefully chained up. Monsieur Lepitre sent me to the law school accompanied by a man-of-all-work who handed me over to the professor and fetched me home again. A young girl would have been treated with less precaution than my mother’s fears insisted on for me. Paris alarmed my parents, and justly. Students are secretly engaged in the same occupation which fills the minds of young ladies in their boarding-schools. Do what you will, nothing can prevent the latter from talking of lovers, or the former of women. But in Paris, and especially at this particular time, such talk among young lads was influenced by the oriental and sultanic atmosphere and customs of the Palais-Royal.

The Palais-Royal was an Eldorado of love where the ingots melted away in coin; there virgin doubts were over; there curiosity was appeased. The Palais-Royal and I were two asymptotes bearing one towards the other, yet unable to meet. Fate miscarried all my attempts. My father had presented me to one of my aunts who lived in the Ile St. Louis. With her I was to dine on Sundays and Thursdays, escorted to the house by either Monsieur or Madame Lepitre, who went out themselves on those days and were to call for me on their way home. Singular amusement for a young lad! My aunt, the Marquise de Listomere, was a great lady, of ceremonious habits, who would never have dreamed of offering me money. Old as a cathedral, painted like a miniature, sumptuous in dress, she lived in her great house as though Louis XV. were not dead, and saw none but old women and men of a past day,--a fossil society which made me think I was in a graveyard. No one spoke to me and I had not the courage to speak first. Cold and alien looks made me ashamed of my youth, which seemed to annoy them. I counted on this indifference to aid me in certain plans; I was resolved to escape some day directly after dinner and rush to the Palais-Royal. Once seated at whist my aunt would pay no attention to me. Jean, the footman, cared little for Monsieur Lepitre and would have aided me; but on the day I chose for my adventure that luckless dinner was longer than usual,--either because the jaws employed were worn out or the false teeth more imperfect. At last, between eight and nine o’clock, I reached the staircase, my heart beating like that of Bianca Capello on the day of her flight; but when the porter pulled the cord I beheld in the street before me Monsieur Lepitre’s hackney-coach, and I heard his pursy voice demanding me!

Three times did fate interpose between the hell of the Palais-Royal and the heaven of my youth. On the day when I, ashamed at twenty years of age of my own ignorance, determined to risk all dangers to put an end to it, at the very moment when I was about to run away from Monsieur Lepitre as he got into the coach,--a difficult process, for he was as fat as Louis XVIII. and club-footed,--well, can you believe it, my mother arrived in a post-chaise! Her glance arrested me; I stood still, like a bird before a snake. What fate had brought her there? The simplest thing in the world. Napoleon was then making his last efforts. My father, who foresaw the return of the Bourbons, had come to Paris with my mother to advise my brother, who was employed in the imperial diplomatic service. My mother was to take me back with her, out of the way of dangers which seemed, to those who followed the march of events intelligently, to threaten the capital. In a few minutes, as it were, I was taken out of Paris, at the very moment when my life there was about to become fatal to me.

The tortures of imagination excited by repressed desires, the weariness of a life depressed by constant privations had driven me to study, just as men, weary of fate, confine themselves in a cloister. To me, study had become a passion, which might even be fatal to my health by imprisoning me at a period of life when young men ought to yield to the bewitching activities of their springtide youth.

This slight sketch of my boyhood, in which you, Natalie, can readily perceive innumerable songs of woe, was needful to explain to you its influence on my future life. At twenty years of age, and affected by many morbid elements, I was still small and thin and pale. My soul, filled with the will to do, struggled with a body that seemed weakly, but which, in the words of an old physician at Tours, was undergoing its final fusion into a temperament of iron. Child in body and old in mind, I had read and thought so much that I knew life metaphysically at its highest reaches at the moment when I was about to enter the tortuous difficulties of its defiles and the sandy roads of its plains. A strange chance had held me long in that delightful period when the soul awakes to its first tumults, to its desires for joy, and the savor of life is fresh. I stood in the period between puberty and manhood,--the one prolonged by my excessive study, the other tardily developing its living shoots. No young man was ever more thoroughly prepared to feel and to love. To understand my history, let your mind dwell on that pure time of youth when the mouth is innocent of falsehood; when the glance of the eye is honest, though veiled by lids which droop from timidity contradicting desire; when the soul bends not to worldly Jesuitism, and the heart throbs as violently from trepidation as from the generous impulses of young emotion.

I need say nothing of the journey I made with my mother from Paris to Tours. The coldness of her behavior repressed me. At each relay I tried to speak; but a look, a word from her frightened away the speeches I had been meditating. At Orleans, where we had passed the night, my mother complained of my silence. I threw myself at her feet and clasped her knees; with tears I opened my heart. I tried to touch hers by the eloquence of my hungry love in accents that might have moved a stepmother. She replied that I was playing comedy. I complained that she had abandoned me. She called me an unnatural child. My whole nature was so wrung that at Blois I went upon the bridge to drown myself in the Loire. The height of the parapet prevented my suicide.

When I reached home, my two sisters, who did not know me, showed more surprise than tenderness. Afterwards, however, they seemed, by comparison, to be full of kindness towards me. I was given a room on the third story. You will understand the extent of my hardships when I tell you that my mother left me, a young man of twenty, without other linen than my miserable school outfit, or any other outside clothes than those I had long worn in Paris. If I ran from one end of the room to the other to pick up her handkerchief, she took it with the cold thanks a lady gives to her footman. Driven to watch her to find if there were any soft spot where I could fasten the rootlets of affection, I came to see her as she was,--a tall, spare woman, given to cards, egotistical and insolent, like all the Listomeres, who count insolence as part of their dowry. She saw nothing in life except duties to be fulfilled. All cold women whom I have known made, as she did, a religion of duty; she received our homage as a priest receives the incense of the mass. My elder brother appeared to absorb the trifling sentiment of maternity which was in her nature. She stabbed us constantly with her sharp irony,--the weapon of those who have no heart,--and which she used against us, who could make her no reply.

Notwithstanding these thorny hindrances, the instinctive sentiments have so many roots, the religious fear inspired by a mother whom it is dangerous to displease holds by so many threads, that the sublime mistake--if I may so call it--of our love for our mother lasted until the day, much later in our lives, when we judged her finally. This terrible despotism drove from my mind all thoughts of the voluptuous enjoyments I had dreamed of finding at Tours. In despair I took refuge in my father’s library, where I set myself to read every book I did not know. These long periods of hard study saved me from contact with my mother; but they aggravated the dangers of my moral condition. Sometimes my eldest sister--she who afterwards married our cousin, the Marquis de Listomere--tried to comfort me, without, however, being able to calm the irritation to which I was a victim. I desired to die.

Great events, of which I knew nothing, were then in preparation. The Duc d’Angouleme, who had left Bordeaux to join Louis XVIII. in Paris, was received in every town through which he passed with ovations inspired by the enthusiasm felt throughout old France at the return of the Bourbons. Touraine was aroused for its legitimate princes; the town itself was in a flutter, every window decorated, the inhabitants in their Sunday clothes, a festival in preparation, and that nameless excitement in the air which intoxicates, and which gave me a strong desire to be present at the ball given by the duke. When I summoned courage to make this request of my mother, who was too ill to go herself, she became extremely angry. “Had I come from Congo?” she inquired. “How could I suppose that our family would not be represented at the ball? In the absence of my father and brother, of course it was my duty to be present. Had I no mother? Was she not always thinking of the welfare of her children?”

In a moment the semi-disinherited son had become a personage! I was more dumfounded by my importance than by the deluge of ironical reasoning with which my mother received my request. I questioned my sisters, and then discovered that my mother, who liked such theatrical plots, was already attending to my clothes. The tailors in Tours were fully occupied by the sudden demands of their regular customers, and my mother was forced to employ her usual seamstress, who--according to provincial custom--could do all kinds of sewing. A bottle-blue coat had been secretly made for me, after a fashion, and silk stockings and pumps provided; waistcoats were then worn short, so that I could wear one of my father’s; and for the first time in my life I had a shirt with a frill, the pleatings of which puffed out my chest and were gathered in to the knot of my cravat. When dressed in this apparel I looked so little like myself that my sister’s compliments nerved me to face all Touraine at the ball. But it was a bold enterprise. Thanks to my slimness I slipped into a tent set up in the gardens of the Papion house, and found a place close to the armchair in which the duke was seated. Instantly I was suffocated by the heat, and dazzled by the lights, the scarlet draperies, the gilded ornaments, the dresses, and the diamonds of the first public ball I had ever witnessed. I was pushed hither and thither by a mass of men and women, who hustled each other in a cloud of dust. The brazen clash of military music was drowned in the hurrahs and acclamations of “Long live the Duc d’Angouleme! Long live the King! Long live the Bourbons!” The ball was an outburst of pent-up enthusiasm, where each man endeavored to outdo the rest in his fierce haste to worship the rising sun,--an exhibition of partisan greed which left me unmoved, or rather, it disgusted me and drove me back within myself.

Swept onward like a straw in the whirlwind, I was seized with a childish desire to be the Duc d’Angouleme himself, to be one of these princes parading before an awed assemblage. This silly fancy of a Tourangean lad roused an ambition to which my nature and the surrounding circumstances lent dignity. Who would not envy such worship?--a magnificent repetition of which I saw a few months later, when all Paris rushed to the feet of the Emperor on his return from Elba. The sense of this dominion exercised over the masses, whose feelings and whose very life are thus merged into one soul, dedicated me then and thenceforth to glory, that priestess who slaughters the Frenchmen of to-day as the Druidess once sacrificed the Gauls.

Suddenly I met the woman who was destined to spur these ambitious desires and to crown them by sending me into the heart of royalty. Too timid to ask any one to dance,--fearing, moreover, to confuse the figures,--I naturally became very awkward, and did not know what to do with my arms and legs. Just as I was suffering severely from the pressure of the crowd an officer stepped on my feet, swollen by the new leather of my shoes as well as by the heat. This disgusted me with the whole affair. It was impossible to get away; but I took refuge in a corner of a room at the end of an empty bench, where I sat with fixed eyes, motionless and sullen. Misled by my puny appearance, a woman--taking me for a sleepy child--slid softly into the place beside me, with the motion of a bird as she drops upon her nest. Instantly I breathed the woman-atmosphere, which irradiated my soul as, in after days, oriental poesy has shone there. I looked at my neighbor, and was more dazzled by that vision than I had been by the scene of the fete.

If you have understood this history of my early life you will guess the feelings which now welled up within me. My eyes rested suddenly on white, rounded shoulders where I would fain have laid my head,--shoulders faintly rosy, which seemed to blush as if uncovered for the first time; modest shoulders, that possessed a soul, and reflected light from their satin surface as from a silken texture. These shoulders were parted by a line along which my eyes wandered. I raised myself to see the bust and was spell-bound by the beauty of the bosom, chastely covered with gauze, where blue-veined globes of perfect outline were softly hidden in waves of lace. The slightest details of the head were each and all enchantments which awakened infinite delights within me; the brilliancy of the hair laid smoothly above a neck as soft and velvety as a child’s, the white lines drawn by the comb where my imagination ran as along a dewy path,--all these things put me, as it were, beside myself. Glancing round to be sure that no one saw me, I threw myself upon those shoulders as a child upon the breast of its mother, kissing them as I laid my head there.
